The administrative area of the city, known as Antananarivo-Renivohitra, is the capital of Analamanga region. The city sits at 1,280 m (4,199 ft) above sea level in the center of the island, making it the highest national capital by elevation among the island countries. It has been the country's largest population center since at least the 18th century.
01-26.location: Capital and largest city of Madagascar

01-26.title: 2015 Los Llanos Air Base crash
01-26.subtitle: A jet fighter crashed at Los Llanos Air Base in Albacete, Spain, killing 11 people and injuring 21 others.
01-26.text: On 26 January 2015, an F-16D Fighting Falcon jet fighter of the Hellenic Air Force crashed into a flight line shortly after take-off at Los Llanos Air Base in Albacete, Spain, killing 11 people: the two crew members and nine personnel on the ground. Thirty-three others, all on the ground, were injured.
01-26.location: Fighter jet crash in Albacete, Spain

01-26.title: Sikorsky S-76
01-26.subtitle: A Sikorsky S-76B flying from John Wayne Airport to Camarillo Airport crashes in Calabasas, 30 miles west of Los Angeles,...
01-26.text: A Sikorsky S-76B flying from John Wayne Airport to Camarillo Airport crashes in Calabasas, 30 miles west of Los Angeles, killing all nine people on board, including five-time NBA champion Kobe Bryant and his daughter Gianna Bryant.

01-27.title: Trajan Becomes Emperor
01-27.subtitle: Rome's greatest conqueror takes the throne
01-27.text: Upon the death of his adoptive father Nerva, Trajan ascended to the throne of the Roman Empire. Under his rule, the empire would reach its maximum territorial extent, stretching from the misty shores of Britain to the sunbaked deserts of Mesopotamia. Trajan's military conquests, monumental building projects, and generous social programs earned him the Senate's honorific as Optimus Princeps, the best of rulers, a title that endured long after his death.

01-27.title: Hyman Rickover Was Born
01-27.subtitle: The father of the nuclear navy arrives
01-27.text: Hyman George Rickover was born in what is now Poland, a future admiral who would revolutionize naval warfare. Emigrating to America as a child, he rose through the ranks of the U.S. Navy to become the driving force behind nuclear-powered submarines and ships. His relentless pursuit of excellence and safety created a naval nuclear program that remains unmatched. Rickover's legacy includes two Congressional Gold Medals and the world's first commercial nuclear power plant.

01-27.title: Cold War
01-27.subtitle: Cold War: The Soviet Union, the United States, and the United Kingdom sign the Outer Space Treaty in Washington, D.
01-27.text: The Cold War was a period of international geopolitical rivalry between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) and their respective allies, the capitalist Western Bloc and communist Eastern Bloc, which began in the aftermath of the Second World War and ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
01-27.location: 1947–1991 geopolitical rivalry between US and USSR

01-27.title: Paris Peace Accords
01-27.subtitle: The Paris Peace Accords officially ends the Vietnam War.
01-27.text: The Paris Peace Accords officially ends the Vietnam War. Colonel William Nolde is killed in action becoming the conflict's last recorded American combat casualty.

01-27.title: Iran hostage crisis
01-27.subtitle: Six American diplomats who had evaded capture in the Iran hostage crisis escaped with the help of Canadian government of...
01-27.text: The Iran hostage crisis began on November 4, 1979, when 66 Americans, including diplomats and other civilian personnel, were taken hostage at the Embassy of the United States in Tehran, with 52 of them being held until January 20, 1981. The incident occurred after the Muslim Student Followers of the Imam's Line stormed and occupied the building in the months following the Iranian Revolution.

01-27.title: Mahamane Ousmane
01-27.subtitle: Mahamane Ousmane, the first democratically elected president of Niger, was deposed by Colonel Ibrahim Baré Maïnassara in a military coup d'état.
01-27.text: Mahamane Ousmane is a Nigerien politician. Elected as the fourth President of Niger at 43 years old, he is the youngest elected president in Africa. He was also the first democratically elected president of his country, serving from 16 April 1993 until he was deposed in a military coup d'état on 27 January 1996.

01-27.title: Morgan Gibbs-White Born
01-27.subtitle: Nottingham Forest finds a future star
01-27.text: Morgan Anthony Gibbs-White was born in England, a future attacking midfielder who would light up the Premier League. Developing through Wolverhampton Wanderers' academy, Gibbs-White honed his craft before becoming a cornerstone of Nottingham Forest's return to top-flight football. His creativity, work rate, and eye for goal made him a fan favorite and a key part of Forest's ambitions to reestablish themselves among English football's elite.

01-27.title: National Recording Registry
01-27.subtitle: The first selections for the National Recording Registry are announced by the Library of Congress.
01-27.text: The first selections for the National Recording Registry are announced by the Library of Congress.

01-27.title: Porfirio Lobo Sosa
01-27.subtitle: Porfirio Lobo Sosa became the new president of Honduras, ending a constitutional crisis that had begun in 2009 when Manu...
01-27.text: Porfirio Lobo Sosa also known by his nickname, Pepe Lobo, is a Honduran former politician, business administrator, and agricultural landowner who served as 37th President of Honduras from 27 January 2010 to 27 January 2014. A member of the conservative National Party and a former deputy in the National Congress of Honduras from 25 January 1990, he was president of the National Congress of Honduras from 25 January 2002 to 25 January 2006.

01-27.title: Arab Spring
01-27.subtitle: Arab Spring: The Yemeni Revolution began as more than 16,000 protesters demonstrated in Sanaa to demand governmental changes.
01-27.text: The Arab Spring was a series of pro-democracy anti-government protests, uprisings, and armed rebellions that spread across much of the Arab world in the early 2010s. It began in Tunisia in response to the death of Mohamed Bouazizi by self-immolation. From Tunisia, the protests initially spread to five other countries: Libya, Egypt, Yemen, Syria and Bahrain.
01-27.location: 2010s protests and revolutions in the Arab world

01-28.title: Trajan Declared in Cologne
01-28.subtitle: A new emperor proclaimed on the Rhine
01-28.text: In the city of Cologne, the seat of his government in lower Germany, Trajan received word of Emperor Nerva's death and was formally declared Roman emperor. Unlike many of his predecessors, Trajan did not rush to Rome to claim his throne. Instead, he spent months securing the northern frontier, demonstrating the steady confidence and military discipline that would define his reign as one of Rome's greatest.

01-28.title: Alice Neel Was Born
01-28.subtitle: A painter who captured the soul on canvas
01-28.text: Alice Neel was born in Pennsylvania, destined to become one of America's greatest portraitists. For six decades, she painted friends, family, lovers, poets, and strangers with an unflinching honesty that captured not just likeness but essence. Working through the Depression, the McCarthy era, and the social upheavals of the 1960s, Neel documented American life on canvas with a raw emotional power that few artists have matched.

01-28.title: World War II
01-28.subtitle: World War II: Supplies begin to reach the Republic of China over the newly reopened Burma Road.
01-28.text: World War II: Supplies begin to reach the Republic of China over the newly reopened Burma Road.

01-28.title: Elvis Presley
01-28.subtitle: Elvis Presley makes his first national television appearance.
01-28.text: Elvis Aaron Presley was an American singer and actor. Referred to as the "King of Rock and Roll", he is widely regarded as one of the most culturally significant figures of the 20th century. Presley's energetic and sexually provocative performance style, combined with a mix of influences across color lines during a transformative era in race relations, brought both great success and initial controversy.

01-28.title: Muhanga
01-28.subtitle: In Gitarama, Rwanda, local and other government officials initiated a coup to overthrow the monarchy and establish a republic.
01-28.text: In Gitarama, Rwanda, local and other government officials initiated a coup to overthrow the monarchy and establish a republic.

01-28.title: North American Sabreliner
01-28.subtitle: Three U.
01-28.text: Three U.S. Air Force pilots aboard an unarmed T-39 Sabreliner were killed when the aircraft was shot down over Erfurt, East Germany, by a Soviet MiG-19.

01-28.title: Blizzard of 1977
01-28.subtitle: The first day of the Great Lakes Blizzard of 1977, which dumps 3 metres (10 ft) of snow in one day in Upstate New York.
01-28.text: The first day of the Great Lakes Blizzard of 1977, which dumps 3 metres (10 ft) of snow in one day in Upstate New York. Buffalo, Syracuse, Watertown, and surrounding areas are most affected.

01-28.title: Ronald Reagan
01-28.subtitle: U.
01-28.text: U.S. president Ronald Reagan lifted price controls from petroleum products, contributing to the 1980s oil glut.

01-28.title: Tropical Storm Domoina
01-28.subtitle: Tropical Storm Domoina made landfall in southern Mozambique, causing some of the most severe flooding recorded in the region.
01-28.text: Tropical Storm Domoina made landfall in southern Mozambique, causing some of the most severe flooding recorded in the region.

01-28.title: We Are the World
01-28.subtitle: Supergroup USA for Africa (United Support of Artists for Africa) records the hit single We Are the World, to help raise ...
01-28.text: Supergroup USA for Africa (United Support of Artists for Africa) records the hit single We Are the World, to help raise funds for Ethiopian famine relief.

01-28.title: Space Shuttle program
01-28.subtitle: Space Shuttle program: STS-51-L mission: Space Shuttle Challenger disintegrates after liftoff, killing all seven astronauts on board.
01-28.text: The Space Shuttle program was the fourth human spaceflight program carried out by the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), which accomplished routine transportation for Earth-to-orbit crew and cargo from 1981 to 2011. Its official program name was Space Transportation System (STS), taken from a 1969 plan for a system of reusable spacecraft where it was the only item funded for development, as a proposed nuclear shuttle in the plan was cancelled in 1972.

01-28.title: Abel Ruiz Was Born
01-28.subtitle: Spain's next striking talent arrives
01-28.text: Abel Ruiz Ortega was born in Spain, a country that produces strikers the way vineyards produce wine. Developed through Barcelona's legendary La Masia academy, Ruiz moved to Sporting Braga in Portugal before joining Girona in La Liga. His sharp movement and clinical finishing earned him a place on the Spanish national team, adding another chapter to Spain's seemingly endless production line of world-class forward talent.

01-29.title: An Lushan Assassinated
01-29.subtitle: A rebel emperor betrayed by his own son
01-29.text: In one of the most dramatic episodes of the Tang dynasty's crisis, An Lushan, the rebel general who had proclaimed himself emperor of the rival Yan state, was assassinated in a palace plot orchestrated by his own son, An Qingxu. The rebellion An Lushan had launched against the Tang had plunged China into years of devastating warfare and cost millions of lives. His murder did not end the conflict, but it marked the beginning of the revolt's slow unraveling.

01-29.title: Allen DuMont Was Born
01-29.subtitle: The man who brought television to your home
01-29.text: Allen Balcom DuMont was born in Brooklyn, New York, a future inventor who would transform how the world consumed entertainment. He improved the cathode-ray tube for television receivers and manufactured the first commercially practical TV set in 1938. In 1946, he founded the DuMont Television Network, the first licensed television network, pioneering the medium that would become the dominant cultural force of the twentieth century.

01-29.title: World War II
01-29.subtitle: World War II: In Bologna, Italy, the Anatomical theatre of the Archiginnasio is completely destroyed in an air-raid.
01-29.text: World War II: In Bologna, Italy, the Anatomical theatre of the Archiginnasio is completely destroyed in an air-raid.

01-29.title: Mantra-Rock Dance
01-29.subtitle: The Mantra-Rock Dance (poster pictured), called the "ultimate high" of the hippie era, took place in San Francisco, feat...
01-29.text: The Mantra-Rock Dance was a counterculture music event held on January 29, 1967, at the Avalon Ballroom in San Francisco. It was organized by followers of the International Society for Krishna Consciousness (ISKCON) as an opportunity for its founder, A. C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada, to address a wider public. It was also a promotional and fundraising effort for their first center on the West Coast of the United States.

01-29.title: Cold War
01-29.subtitle: Cold War: Hungary establishes diplomatic relations with South Korea, making it the first Eastern Bloc nation to do so.
01-29.text: The Cold War was a period of international geopolitical rivalry between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) and their respective allies, the capitalist Western Bloc and communist Eastern Bloc, which began in the aftermath of the Second World War and ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
01-29.location: 1947–1991 geopolitical rivalry between US and USSR

01-29.title: Gulf War
01-29.subtitle: Gulf War: The Battle of Khafji, the first major ground engagement of the war, as well as its deadliest, begins between Iraq and Saudi Arabia.
01-29.text: The Gulf War was an armed conflict between Iraq and a 42-country coalition led by the United States. The coalition's efforts were in two phases: Operation Desert Shield, which marked the military buildup from August 1990 to January 1991; and Operation Desert Storm, from the bombing campaign against Iraq on 17 January until the American-led liberation of Kuwait on 28 February.

01-29.title: Indonesian Students Storm Parliament
01-29.subtitle: Thousands demand a president's resignation
01-29.text: Thousands of student protesters stormed the Indonesian parliament building in Jakarta, demanding that President Abdurrahman Wahid resign over alleged involvement in corruption scandals. The dramatic confrontation between young idealists and an embattled president echoed the student movements that had toppled Suharto just years before. Indonesia's fragile democracy was being tested again, its streets transformed into theaters of political struggle.

01-29.title: 2002 State of the Union Address
01-29.subtitle: In his State of the Union address, President George W.
01-29.text: In his State of the Union address, President George W. Bush describes "regimes that sponsor terror" as an Axis of evil, in which he includes Iraq, Iran and North Korea.

01-29.title: Mainland China
01-29.subtitle: The first direct commercial flights from mainland China (from Guangzhou) to Taiwan since 1949 arrived in Taipei.
01-29.text: The first direct commercial flights from mainland China (from Guangzhou) to Taiwan since 1949 arrived in Taipei. Shortly afterwards, a China Airlines flight lands in Beijing.

01-29.title: India national cricket team
01-29.subtitle: India's Irfan Pathan became the first bowler to take a Test cricket hat-trick in the opening over of a match.
01-29.text: The India men's national cricket team represents India in international cricket. It is governed by the Board of Control for Cricket in India (BCCI) and is a full member nation of the International Cricket Council with Test, One Day International and Twenty20 International status. India are the current holders of the T20 World Cup, the ICC Champions Trophy and the Asia Cup.

01-29.title: Supreme Constitutional Court
01-29.subtitle: The Supreme Constitutional Court of Egypt ruled that people who did not adhere to one of the three government-recognised...
01-29.text: The Supreme Constitutional Court of Egypt ruled that people who did not adhere to one of the three government-recognised religions were also eligible to receive government identity documents.

01-29.title: Quebec City mosque shooting
01-29.subtitle: A lone gunman carried out a mass shooting at a mosque in Quebec City, Canada, killing six people and injuring up to nineteen others.
01-29.text: A lone gunman carried out a mass shooting at a mosque in Quebec City, Canada, killing six people and injuring up to nineteen others.

01-30.title: Livia Was Born
01-30.subtitle: Rome's most powerful woman enters the world
01-30.text: Livia Drusilla was born into the Roman aristocracy, destined to become the most influential woman in the history of the Roman Empire. As the wife of Augustus, she wielded extraordinary behind-the-scenes power for over half a century. Her political acumen, her influence over her husband's decisions, and her role in securing the succession made her a figure of both admiration and suspicion, a woman who helped shape an empire from the shadows.

01-30.title: Rudolf Caracciola Was Born
01-30.subtitle: The Rainmaster of European racing
01-30.text: Rudolf Caracciola was born in Germany, a future racing legend who would dominate European motorsport in the 1930s. Known as the Regenmeister for his uncanny ability to race in wet conditions, Caracciola won the European Drivers' Championship an unsurpassed three times. Racing for Mercedes-Benz during their legendary Silver Arrows period, he set speed records and defined an era of motorsport that combined elegance with breathtaking danger.

01-30.title: Nguyễn Khánh
01-30.subtitle: In a bloodless coup, Nguyễn Khánh overthrew Dương Văn Minh's military junta in South Vietnam, less than three months after Minh's own coup.
01-30.text: In a bloodless coup, Nguyễn Khánh overthrew Dương Văn Minh's military junta in South Vietnam, less than three months after Minh's own coup.

01-30.title: Vietnam War
01-30.subtitle: Vietnam War: Forces of the Viet Cong and the Vietnamese People's Army launched the Tet Offensive to strike military and ...
01-30.text: The Vietnam War was an armed conflict in Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia fought between North Vietnam and South Vietnam and their allies. North Vietnam was supported by the Soviet Union and China, while South Vietnam was supported by the United States and other anti-communist nations. The conflict was the second of the Indochina wars and a proxy war of the Cold War between the Soviet Union and US.
01-30.location: War in Southeast Asia from 1955 to 1975

01-30.title: The Beatles
01-30.subtitle: The Beatles' last public performance, on the roof of Apple Records in London.
01-30.text: The Beatles' last public performance, on the roof of Apple Records in London. The impromptu concert is broken up by the police.

01-30.title: The Troubles
01-30.subtitle: The Troubles: On Bloody Sunday, members of the British Parachute Regiment shot 26 civil-rights protesters in Derry, Nort...
01-30.text: The Troubles were an ethno-nationalist conflict in Northern Ireland that lasted for about 30 years from the late 1960s to 1998. Also known internationally as the Northern Ireland conflict, it began in the late 1960s and is usually deemed to have ended with the Good Friday Agreement of 1998. Although the Troubles mostly took place in Northern Ireland, at times violence spilled over into parts of the Republic of Ireland, England, and mainland Europe.

01-30.title: Monitor National Marine Sanctuary
01-30.subtitle: The Monitor National Marine Sanctuary is established as the first United States National Marine Sanctuary.
01-30.text: The Monitor National Marine Sanctuary is established as the first United States National Marine Sanctuary.

01-30.title: Rich Skrenta
01-30.subtitle: Richard Skrenta writes the first PC virus code, which is 400 lines long and disguised as an Apple boot program called "Elk Cloner".
01-30.text: Richard Skrenta writes the first PC virus code, which is 400 lines long and disguised as an Apple boot program called "Elk Cloner".

01-30.title: Kenya Airways Flight 431
01-30.subtitle: Darkness swallows a flight over the Atlantic
01-30.text: Shortly after taking off from Abidjan, Ivory Coast, Kenya Airways Flight 431 plunged into the Atlantic Ocean, killing 169 of the 179 people on board. The Airbus A310, bound for Lagos and Nairobi, disappeared into the night in one of Africa's deadliest aviation disasters. The ten survivors, pulled from the dark waters, bore witness to a tragedy that exposed the vulnerabilities of air travel across the developing world.
01-30.location: Off the coast of Ivory Coast

01-30.title: Microsoft
01-30.subtitle: Microsoft Corporation releases Windows Vista, a major release of the operating system Microsoft Windows and the NT based kernel.
01-30.text: Microsoft Corporation releases Windows Vista, a major release of the operating system Microsoft Windows and the NT based kernel.

01-30.title: Naro-1
01-30.subtitle: Naro-1 becomes the first carrier rocket launched by South Korea.
01-30.text: Naro-1 becomes the first carrier rocket launched by South Korea.

01-30.title: World Health Organization
01-30.subtitle: The World Health Organization declared the COVID-19 pandemic to be a public health emergency of international concern.
01-30.text: The World Health Organization declared the COVID-19 pandemic to be a public health emergency of international concern.

01-31.title: Pope Sylvester I Begins
01-31.subtitle: A papacy shaped by Constantine's conversion
01-31.text: Sylvester I became Bishop of Rome at one of the most transformative moments in Christian history. His pontificate coincided with Emperor Constantine the Great's embrace of Christianity, and during his reign many of Rome's greatest churches were constructed with imperial patronage. Though history records little about Sylvester himself, the era he presided over fundamentally reshaped the relationship between church and state in the Western world.

01-31.title: Mat Salleh Rebellion Ends
01-31.subtitle: A rebel chief falls but his cause endures
01-31.text: In the remote highlands of Tambunan in North Borneo, Datu Muhammad Salleh, leader of a sustained rebellion against the British North Borneo Chartered Company, was shot dead. His uprising, which had begun in 1894, challenged colonial authority across the territory. But his death did not end the resistance. His followers continued fighting for five more years, a testament to the fierce desire for independence that burned in the hearts of the Borneo people.

01-31.title: Cold War
01-31.subtitle: Cold War: Yugoslavia's new constitution, modeling that of the Soviet Union, establishes six constituent republics (Bosni...
01-31.text: The Cold War was a period of international geopolitical rivalry between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) and their respective allies, the capitalist Western Bloc and communist Eastern Bloc, which began in the aftermath of the Second World War and ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
01-31.location: 1947–1991 geopolitical rivalry between US and USSR

01-31.title: Douglas DC-7
01-31.subtitle: A DC-7B operated by Douglas Aircraft collided in mid-air with a U.
01-31.text: A DC-7B operated by Douglas Aircraft collided in mid-air with a U.S. Air Force F-89 and crashed into a schoolyard in Pacoima, California.

01-31.title: Cold War
01-31.subtitle: Cold War: Space Race: The Explorer 1, the first successful American satellite, detects the Van Allen radiation belt.
01-31.text: The Space Race was a 20th-century competition between the Cold War rivals, the United States and the Soviet Union, to achieve superior spaceflight capability. It had its origins in the ballistic missile-based nuclear arms race between the two nations following World War II and the onset of the Cold War.

01-31.title: NASA
01-31.subtitle: Aboard NASA's Mercury-Redstone 2, the chimpanzee Ham became the first hominid to be launched into outer space.
01-31.text: The National Aeronautics and Space Administration is an independent agency of the U.S. federal government responsible for the United States' civil space program and for research in aeronautics and space exploration. Headquartered in Washington, D.C., NASA operates ten field centers across the US and is organized into mission directorates for Science, Space Operations, Exploration Systems Development, Space Technology, Aeronautics Research, and Mission Support.

01-31.title: Soviet Union
01-31.subtitle: The Soviet Union launches the unmanned Luna 9 spacecraft as part of the Luna program.
01-31.text: The Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (USSR), also known as the Soviet Union, was a transcontinental country that spanned much of Eurasia from 1922 until its dissolution in 1991. It was the world's third-most populous country, largest by area, and bordered twelve countries. A diverse multinational state, it was organized as a federal union of national republics, the largest and most populous being the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic. In practice, its government and economy were highly centralized.

01-31.title: Apollo program
01-31.subtitle: Apollo program: Apollo 14: Astronauts Alan Shepard, Stuart Roosa, and Edgar Mitchell, aboard a Saturn V, lift off for a ...
01-31.text: The Apollo program, also known as Project Apollo, was the United States human spaceflight program led by NASA, which landed the first humans on the Moon in 1969. Apollo was conceived during Project Mercury and executed after Project Gemini. It was conceived in 1960 as a three-person spacecraft during the presidency of Dwight D. Eisenhower. Apollo was later dedicated to President John F.

01-31.title: Doug Williams
01-31.subtitle: Doug Williams becomes the first African American quarterback to play in a Super Bowl and leads the Washington Redskins to victory in Super Bowl XXII.
01-31.text: Douglas Lee Williams is an American professional football executive and former quarterback and coach who is a senior advisor for the Washington Commanders of the National Football League (NFL). Williams was the first black quarterback to both start and win a Super Bowl, doing so with Washington in Super Bowl XXII when they were known as the Redskins. He was named Super Bowl MVP after throwing four touchdowns in a single quarter.
01-31.location: American football player, coach, and executive (born 1955)

01-31.title: Alaska Airlines Flight 261
01-31.subtitle: A stabilizer fails and a plane falls from the sky
01-31.text: Alaska Airlines Flight 261, en route from Puerto Vallarta to Seattle, suffered a catastrophic failure of its horizontal stabilizer system over the Pacific Ocean. The McDonnell Douglas MD-83 crashed near Anacapa Island, California, killing all eighty-eight people on board. The disaster, caused by inadequate maintenance of a critical flight component, led to sweeping reforms in aviation maintenance procedures and remains one of the most studied accidents in aviation history.
01-31.location: Pacific Ocean, off California, United States

01-31.title: Aqua Teen Hunger Force
01-31.subtitle: Emergency officials in Boston mistakenly identified LED placards depicting characters from Aqua Teen Hunger Force as IEDs, causing a panic.
01-31.text: Aqua Teen Hunger Force is an American adult animated television series created by Dave Willis and Matt Maiellaro for Cartoon Network's late night programming block Adult Swim. Produced by Williams Street, it documents the surreal adventures and antics of three anthropomorphic fast food items: Master Shake, Meatwad, and Frylock, who live together as roommates and frequently interact with their cranky human next-door neighbor, Carl.

01-31.title: Torre Ejecutiva Pemex
01-31.subtitle: A gas leak underneath the Pemex Executive Tower in Mexico City caused an explosion (aftermath pictured) that killed at l...
01-31.text: A gas leak underneath the Pemex Executive Tower in Mexico City caused an explosion (aftermath pictured) that killed at least 37 people and injured another 121.

02-01.title: Walter de Stapledon Born
02-01.subtitle: A bishop who built and was betrayed
02-01.text: Walter de Stapledon was born in Devon, England, destined to become one of medieval England's most accomplished yet tragically fated clerics. As Bishop of Exeter and twice Lord High Treasurer, he founded what became Exeter College at Oxford and contributed lavishly to the rebuilding of Exeter Cathedral. His service to the crown, however, made him a target during the London uprising of 1326, where he was killed by a mob in the streets.

02-01.title: Lord Roberts Takes Command
02-01.subtitle: Britain sends its best general to Africa
02-01.text: Reeling from a series of humiliating defeats at the hands of Boer forces, Great Britain turned to one of its most celebrated military figures. Field Marshal Lord Roberts was named commander of British forces in South Africa, bringing a lifetime of imperial military experience to a war that had exposed the limits of British power. His appointment signaled a dramatic escalation that would eventually turn the tide of the Second Boer War.

02-01.title: Civil rights movement
02-01.subtitle: Civil rights movement: Four African-American students staged the first of more than five months of sit-ins at an F.
02-01.text: The civil rights movement was a social movement in the United States from 1954 to 1968 which aimed to abolish legalized racial segregation, discrimination, and disenfranchisement in the country, which most commonly affected African Americans. The movement had origins in the Reconstruction era in the late 19th century, and modern roots in the 1940s.

02-01.title: The Beatles
02-01.subtitle: The Beatles have their first number one hit in the United States with "I Want to Hold Your Hand".
02-01.text: The Beatles have their first number one hit in the United States with "I Want to Hold Your Hand".

02-01.title: Vietnam War
02-01.subtitle: Vietnam War: The execution of Viet Cong officer Nguyễn Văn Lém by South Vietnamese National Police Chief Nguyễn Ngọc Loa...
02-01.text: The Vietnam War was an armed conflict in Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia fought between North Vietnam and South Vietnam and their allies. North Vietnam was supported by the Soviet Union and China, while South Vietnam was supported by the United States and other anti-communist nations. The conflict was the second of the Indochina wars and a proxy war of the Cold War between the Soviet Union and US.
02-01.location: War in Southeast Asia from 1955 to 1975

02-01.title: Intel 80286
02-01.subtitle: The Intel 80286 is released, which introduced protected mode memory.
02-01.text: The Intel 80286 is released, which introduced protected mode memory. The IBM PC/AT and its clones used this CPU.

02-01.title: Talanoa Hufanga Was Born
02-01.subtitle: A Polynesian warrior for the gridiron
02-01.text: Talanoa Hufanga was born into a family with deep Polynesian roots and a passion for football. The hard-hitting safety would star at USC before being drafted by the San Francisco 49ers. His ferocious tackling and instinctive playmaking quickly made him one of the NFL's most feared defenders, carrying the warrior spirit of his heritage onto the gridiron every Sunday.

02-01.title: Space Shuttle Columbia
02-01.subtitle: Space Shuttle Columbia disintegrated during the reentry of mission STS-107 into the Earth's atmosphere, killing all seven astronauts aboard.
02-01.text: Space Shuttle Columbia (OV-102) was a Space Shuttle orbiter manufactured by Rockwell International and operated by NASA. Named after the first American ship to circumnavigate the globe, and the female personification of the United States, Columbia was the first of five Space Shuttle orbiters to fly in space, debuting the Space Shuttle launch vehicle on its maiden flight on April 12, 1981 and becoming the first spacecraft to be re-used after its first flight when it launched on STS-2 on

02-01.title: Halftime show
02-01.subtitle: During the halftime show of Super Bowl XXXVIII, Janet Jackson's breast was exposed by Justin Timberlake in what was late...
02-01.text: During the halftime show of Super Bowl XXXVIII, Janet Jackson's breast was exposed by Justin Timberlake in what was later referred to as a "wardrobe malfunction", resulting in an immediate crackdown and widespread debate on perceived indecency in U.S. broadcasting.

02-01.title: Gyanendra of Nepal
02-01.subtitle: King Gyanendra of Nepal carries out a coup d'état to capture the democracy, becoming Chairman of the Councils of ministers.
02-01.text: King Gyanendra of Nepal carries out a coup d'état to capture the democracy, becoming Chairman of the Councils of ministers.

02-01.title: Egyptian Premier League
02-01.subtitle: Following an Egyptian Premier League match in Port Said, Al Masry fans rioted and violently attacked Al Ahly supporters, resulting in 74 deaths.
02-01.text: Following an Egyptian Premier League match in Port Said, Al Masry fans rioted and violently attacked Al Ahly supporters, resulting in 74 deaths.

02-01.title: Tatmadaw
02-01.subtitle: The Burmese military staged a coup d'état that deposed the democratically elected government of Aung San Suu Kyi, sparki...
02-01.text: The Burmese military staged a coup d'état that deposed the democratically elected government of Aung San Suu Kyi, sparking nationwide protests and a civil war.

02-02.title: Justin I Was Born
02-02.subtitle: From peasant boy to Byzantine emperor
02-02.text: Justin was born to a peasant family in the Roman province of Dardania, with nothing to suggest the extraordinary destiny awaiting him. Through sheer determination, he rose through the ranks of the Byzantine military to become commander of the imperial guard. When Emperor Anastasius I died, the nearly seventy-year-old Justin outmaneuvered younger rivals to claim the throne, founding the Justinian dynasty that would produce some of Byzantium's greatest rulers.

02-02.title: Willie Kamm Was Born
02-02.subtitle: A master of the hot corner in Chicago
02-02.text: William Edward Kamm was born in San Francisco, destined for a distinguished career at third base in Major League Baseball. Playing primarily for the Chicago White Sox, Kamm became the dominant defensive third baseman in the American League during the 1920s and 1930s. His sure hands and quick reflexes at the hot corner set a standard that few of his contemporaries could match.

02-02.title: The Troubles
02-02.subtitle: The Troubles: Protestors burned the British Embassy in Ireland following the massacre of 14 civilians in Derry by British forces.
02-02.text: The Troubles were an ethno-nationalist conflict in Northern Ireland that lasted for about 30 years from the late 1960s to 1998. Also known internationally as the Northern Ireland conflict, it began in the late 1960s and is usually deemed to have ended with the Good Friday Agreement of 1998. Although the Troubles mostly took place in Northern Ireland, at times violence spilled over into parts of the Republic of Ireland, England, and mainland Europe.

02-02.title: General Dynamics F-16 Fighting Falcon
02-02.subtitle: The F-16 Fighting Falcon (pictured), the most numerous fixed-wing aircraft currently in military service, made its first flight.
02-02.text: The General Dynamics F-16 Fighting Falcon is an American single-engine supersonic multirole fourth-generation fighter aircraft developed by General Dynamics and produced by multiple companies, including General Dynamics until 1993 and Lockheed Martin until 2017. Designed as an air superiority day fighter, it evolved into a successful all-weather multirole aircraft with over 4,600 built since 1976.

02-02.title: People Power Revolution
02-02.subtitle: After the 1986 People Power Revolution, the Philippines enacts a new constitution.
02-02.text: After the 1986 People Power Revolution, the Philippines enacts a new constitution.

02-02.title: Apartheid
02-02.subtitle: Apartheid: F.
02-02.text: Apartheid was a system of institutionalised racial segregation that existed in South Africa and South West Africa from 1948 to the early 1990s. It was characterised by an authoritarian political culture based on baasskap, which ensured that South Africa was dominated politically, socially, and economically by the nation's minority white population. Under this minoritarian system, white citizens held the highest status, followed by Indians, Coloureds and black Africans, in that order.
02-02.location: South African system of racial segregation

02-02.title: Digital Cinema Comes to Europe
02-02.subtitle: Paris witnesses the future of film projection
02-02.text: In a Parisian cinema, Philippe Binant realized the first digital cinema projection in Europe using Texas Instruments' DLP Cinema technology. The flickering of celluloid film, which had defined moviegoing for over a century, was giving way to pixels and digital light. This milestone moment in the City of Light marked the beginning of a technological revolution that would fundamentally transform how films are distributed and experienced worldwide.

02-02.title: Tennis
02-02.subtitle: Swiss tennis player Roger Federer becomes the No.
02-02.text: Swiss tennis player Roger Federer becomes the No. 1 ranked men's singles player, a position he will hold for a record 237 weeks.

02-02.title: MV Rabaul Queen
02-02.subtitle: The passenger ferry MV Rabaul Queen capsized and sank in rough conditions in the Solomon Sea, resulting in at least 88 deaths.
02-02.text: The passenger ferry MV Rabaul Queen capsized and sank in rough conditions in the Solomon Sea, resulting in at least 88 deaths.

02-02.title: Tatmadaw
02-02.subtitle: The Burmese military establishes the State Administration Council, the military junta, after deposing the democratically...
02-02.text: The Burmese military establishes the State Administration Council, the military junta, after deposing the democratically elected government in the 2021 Myanmar coup d'état.

02-02.title: Slovenia
02-02.subtitle: Slovenian NBA player Luka Doncic is traded from the Dallas Mavericks to the Los Angeles Lakers in exchange for Anthony D...
02-02.text: Slovenian NBA player Luka Doncic is traded from the Dallas Mavericks to the Los Angeles Lakers in exchange for Anthony Davis in one of the largest trades in american sports history.

02-03.title: Normans Gain Imperial Favor
02-03.subtitle: The Holy Roman Emperor legitimizes Norse warriors
02-03.text: Emperor Henry III declared Drogo of Hauteville to be count of all Apulian and Calabrian Normans, granting imperial legitimacy to the Norse warriors who had carved out territories in southern Italy. This recognition transformed the Normans from adventurous mercenaries into legitimate feudal lords, setting the stage for the eventual Norman conquest of southern Italy and Sicily that would reshape the Mediterranean world.

02-03.title: Portuguese Empire
02-03.subtitle: The Portuguese navy defeats a joint fleet of the Ottoman Empire, the Republic of Venice, the Sultan of Gujarat, the Maml...
02-03.text: The Portuguese navy defeats a joint fleet of the Ottoman Empire, the Republic of Venice, the Sultan of Gujarat, the Mamlûk Burji Sultanate of Egypt, the Zamorin of Calicut, and the Republic of Ragusa at the Battle of Diu in Diu, India.

02-03.title: Mabel Mercer Was Born
02-03.subtitle: The singer who taught Sinatra how to phrase
02-03.text: Mabel Mercer was born in Staffordshire, England, a future cabaret legend whose artistry would influence the greatest singers of the twentieth century. Performing at Chez Bricktop in Paris and the finest clubs of New York, Mercer's intimate storytelling style captivated audiences and artists alike. Frank Sinatra himself acknowledged her as his greatest influence, attending her shows regularly and emulating her masterful phrasing.

02-03.title: Soviet Union
02-03.subtitle: The Soviet Union's Luna 9 becomes the first spacecraft to make a soft landing on the Moon, and the first spacecraft to t...
02-03.text: The Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (USSR), also known as the Soviet Union, was a transcontinental country that spanned much of Eurasia from 1922 until its dissolution in 1991. It was the world's third-most populous country, largest by area, and bordered twelve countries. A diverse multinational state, it was organized as a federal union of national republics, the largest and most populous being the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic. In practice, its government and economy were highly centralized.

02-03.title: Space Shuttle program
02-03.subtitle: Space Shuttle program: STS-41-B is launched using Space Shuttle Challenger.
02-03.text: The Space Shuttle program was the fourth human spaceflight program carried out by the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), which accomplished routine transportation for Earth-to-orbit crew and cargo from 1981 to 2011. Its official program name was Space Transportation System (STS), taken from a 1969 plan for a system of reusable spacecraft where it was the only item funded for development, as a proposed nuclear shuttle in the plan was cancelled in 1972.

02-03.title: Steve Jobs
02-03.subtitle: Steve Jobs purchased Pixar from Lucasfilm and launched it as an independent computer-animation studio.
02-03.text: Steve Jobs purchased Pixar from Lucasfilm and launched it as an independent computer-animation studio.

02-03.title: Space Shuttle program
02-03.subtitle: Space Shuttle program: STS-60 is launched, carrying Sergei Krikalev, the first Russian cosmonaut to fly aboard the Shuttle.
02-03.text: The Space Shuttle program was the fourth human spaceflight program carried out by the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), which accomplished routine transportation for Earth-to-orbit crew and cargo from 1981 to 2011. Its official program name was Space Transportation System (STS), taken from a 1969 plan for a system of reusable spacecraft where it was the only item funded for development, as a proposed nuclear shuttle in the plan was cancelled in 1972.

02-03.title: Eileen Collins
02-03.subtitle: Astronaut Eileen Collins becomes the first woman to pilot the Space Shuttle as mission STS-63 gets underway from Kennedy Space Center in Florida.
02-03.text: Astronaut Eileen Collins becomes the first woman to pilot the Space Shuttle as mission STS-63 gets underway from Kennedy Space Center in Florida.

02-03.title: Tre Mann Was Born
02-03.subtitle: A hoops talent blooms for the Hornets
02-03.text: Tre'shaun Albert Mann was born in the United States, a future basketball talent who would shine at the University of Florida before joining the NBA. Selected by the Oklahoma City Thunder, Mann's explosive scoring ability and creative playmaking earned him a role with the Charlotte Hornets. His game, built on speed, skill, and fearlessness, exemplifies the modern NBA guard who can score from anywhere on the court.

02-03.title: Al-Qaeda insurgency in Yemen
02-03.subtitle: Al-Qaeda insurgency in Yemen: 23 convicts, including several militants associated with al-Qaeda, escaped from a prison a...
02-03.text: The Al-Qaeda insurgency in Yemen is an ongoing armed conflict between the Yemeni government, the United States and their allies, and al-Qaeda-affiliated groups in Yemen. It is a part of the Global War on Terrorism.

02-03.title: School shooting
02-03.subtitle: Russia's first school shooting took place when a student opened fire at School No.
02-03.text: Russia's first school shooting took place when a student opened fire at School No. 263 in Moscow, resulting in the deaths of a teacher and a police officer.

02-03.title: East Palestine, Ohio, train derailment
02-03.subtitle: A freight train derailed in East Palestine, Ohio, releasing hazardous materials into the surrounding area.
02-03.text: A freight train derailed in East Palestine, Ohio, releasing hazardous materials into the surrounding area.

02-04.title: Septimius Severus Dies
02-04.subtitle: An emperor's last wish goes unheeded
02-04.text: At Eboracum, modern-day York in northern England, Emperor Septimius Severus breathed his last while preparing to campaign against the Caledonians of Scotland. Born in Roman Africa, he had fought his way to the throne and held it with iron determination. On his deathbed, he instructed his quarreling sons Caracalla and Geta to make peace and rule together. They ignored him, and within a year Caracalla murdered his brother in their mother's arms.

02-04.title: Slavery
02-04.subtitle: The French legislature abolishes slavery throughout all territories of the French First Republic.
02-04.text: The French legislature abolishes slavery throughout all territories of the French First Republic. It would be reestablished in the French West Indies in 1802.

02-04.title: Jacques Prevert Was Born
02-04.subtitle: France's poet of the everyday and eternal
02-04.text: Jacques Prevert was born in Neuilly-sur-Seine, France, destined to become one of the most beloved poets in the French language. His verses, accessible and deeply humane, became staples of French school curricula. As a screenwriter, he crafted the poetic realist masterpiece Les Enfants du Paradis. Prevert's gift was making the ordinary extraordinary, finding in the streets of Paris and the faces of lovers a poetry that spoke to everyone.

02-04.title: Lunar Orbiter program
02-04.subtitle: Lunar Orbiter program: Lunar Orbiter 3 lifts off from Cape Canaveral's Launch Complex 13 on its mission to identify poss...
02-04.text: Lunar Orbiter program: Lunar Orbiter 3 lifts off from Cape Canaveral's Launch Complex 13 on its mission to identify possible landing sites for the Surveyor and Apollo spacecraft.

02-04.title: Symbionese Liberation Army
02-04.subtitle: The Symbionese Liberation Army kidnaps Patty Hearst in Berkeley, California.
02-04.text: The Symbionese Liberation Army kidnaps Patty Hearst in Berkeley, California.

02-04.title: New Carissa
02-04.subtitle: The Philippine-flagged freighter New Carissa ran aground near Coos Bay, Oregon, causing one of the worst oil spills in the state's history.
02-04.text: The Philippine-flagged freighter New Carissa ran aground near Coos Bay, Oregon, causing one of the worst oil spills in the state's history.

02-04.title: World Cancer Day Created
02-04.subtitle: A global pledge signed in the City of Light
02-04.text: At the World Summit Against Cancer for the New Millennium, French President Jacques Chirac and UNESCO Director General Koichiro Matsuura signed the Charter of Paris, establishing World Cancer Day on February fourth. The declaration united world leaders in a commitment to raise awareness, improve prevention, and advance research against a disease that touches every nation and family. The annual observance became a global rallying point in the fight against cancer.

02-04.title: Serbia and Montenegro
02-04.subtitle: The Federal Republic of Yugoslavia adopts a new constitution, becoming a loose confederacy between Montenegro and Serbia.
02-04.text: The Federal Republic of Yugoslavia adopts a new constitution, becoming a loose confederacy between Montenegro and Serbia.

02-04.title: Facebook
02-04.subtitle: Facebook, a mainstream online social networking site, is founded by Mark Zuckerberg and Eduardo Saverin.
02-04.text: Facebook, a mainstream online social networking site, is founded by Mark Zuckerberg and Eduardo Saverin.

02-04.title: London low emission zone
02-04.subtitle: The London low emission zone (sign pictured), charging certain diesel-powered commercial vehicles to enter Greater London, came into operation.
02-04.text: The London low emission zone (sign pictured), charging certain diesel-powered commercial vehicles to enter Greater London, came into operation.

02-04.title: Songshan Airport
02-04.subtitle: Shortly after takeoff from Taipei Songshan Airport, the crew of TransAsia Airways Flight 235 shut down the wrong engine ...
02-04.text: Shortly after takeoff from Taipei Songshan Airport, the crew of TransAsia Airways Flight 235 shut down the wrong engine in response to a flameout, leading to a crash that resulted in 43 deaths.

02-05.title: Augustus Named Father of Country
02-05.subtitle: The Senate honors Rome's founding emperor
02-05.text: In a solemn ceremony in the Roman Senate, Caesar Augustus received the title pater patriae, Father of the Country. It was the ultimate recognition for the man who had transformed Rome from a war-torn republic into a stable empire. The title, previously given only to Cicero, acknowledged Augustus's role in bringing peace and prosperity to a world exhausted by civil war. It became the crowning honor of his extraordinary political career.

02-05.title: Adlai Stevenson II Born
02-05.subtitle: An eloquent voice for American diplomacy
02-05.text: Adlai Ewing Stevenson II was born in Los Angeles, heir to a distinguished political family. He would serve as Governor of Illinois, twice run for president against Eisenhower, and cap his career as America's ambassador to the United Nations. Known for his wit, intellect, and moral eloquence, Stevenson became the conscience of the Democratic Party, even as electoral victory eluded him. His speeches remain models of political rhetoric.

02-05.title: Gamal Abdel Nasser
02-05.subtitle: Gamal Abdel Nasser is nominated to be the first president of the United Arab Republic.
02-05.text: Gamal Abdel Nasser is nominated to be the first president of the United Arab Republic.

02-05.title: Cultural Revolution
02-05.subtitle: Cultural Revolution: The Shanghai People's Commune is formally proclaimed, with Yao Wenyuan and Zhang Chunqiao being appointed as its leaders.
02-05.text: The Cultural Revolution, formally known as the Great Proletarian Cultural Revolution, was a sociopolitical movement in the People's Republic of China (PRC). It was launched by CCP chairman Mao Zedong in 1966 and lasted until his death in 1976. Its stated goal was to preserve Chinese communism by purging remnants of capitalist and traditional elements from Chinese society.
02-05.location: Period of sociopolitical turmoil in China (1966–1976)

02-05.title: Apollo 14
02-05.subtitle: Astronauts land on the Moon in the Apollo 14 mission.
02-05.text: Astronauts land on the Moon in the Apollo 14 mission.

02-05.title: Ugo Vetere
02-05.subtitle: Ugo Vetere, then the mayor of Rome, and Chedli Klibi, then the mayor of Carthage, meet in Tunis to sign a treaty of frie...
02-05.text: Ugo Vetere, then the mayor of Rome, and Chedli Klibi, then the mayor of Carthage, meet in Tunis to sign a treaty of friendship officially ending the Third Punic War which lasted 2,131 years.

02-05.title: Novye Aldi Massacre
02-05.subtitle: Civilians slaughtered in a Chechen suburb
02-05.text: As the Battle of Grozny neared its end during the Second Chechen War, Russian forces committed one of the conflict's worst atrocities. In the suburb of Novye Aldi, soldiers summarily executed at least sixty civilians in a brutal sweep through the neighborhood. The massacre, which drew international condemnation, laid bare the human cost of Russia's campaign to crush Chechen separatism and darkened Moscow's claims of a legitimate counter-terrorism operation.

02-05.title: Common cockle
02-05.subtitle: At least 21 cockle-gatherers were drowned by an incoming tide in Morecambe Bay, England, prompting the establishment of ...
02-05.text: At least 21 cockle-gatherers were drowned by an incoming tide in Morecambe Bay, England, prompting the establishment of the British government's Gangmasters Licensing Authority.

02-05.title: United States Navy
02-05.subtitle: The United States Navy guided missile cruiser  Port Royal ran aground (pictured) on a coral reef off the island of Oahu.
02-05.text: The United States Navy guided missile cruiser  Port Royal ran aground (pictured) on a coral reef off the island of Oahu.

02-05.title: Pope Francis
02-05.subtitle: Pope Francis becomes the first Pope in history to visit and perform papal mass in the Arabian Peninsula during his visit to Abu Dhabi.
02-05.text: Pope Francis becomes the first Pope in history to visit and perform papal mass in the Arabian Peninsula during his visit to Abu Dhabi.

02-06.title: Pope Julius I Elected
02-06.subtitle: A pope who set Christmas on December 25
02-06.text: The Christian community of Rome elected Julius I as their new bishop and pope. His pontificate would be defined by bold assertions of papal authority, particularly in his defense of Athanasius against the powerful Arian bishops of the East. Julius is also credited with establishing December 25 as the official date of Christ's birth, a decision that would shape the rhythm of the Western calendar for all centuries to come.

02-06.title: Court of Arbitration Created
02-06.subtitle: A palace of peace rises in The Hague
02-06.text: The Permanent Court of Arbitration came into being when the Dutch Senate ratified the decree of the 1899 peace conference. Housed in the grand Peace Palace at The Hague, the court offered nations an alternative to war for settling disputes. It was one of the earliest institutions of international law, a hopeful gesture toward a world where justice might prevail over force, even as the twentieth century's wars loomed on the horizon.

02-06.title: World War I
02-06.subtitle: World War I: More than 65,000 workers in Seattle began a five-day general strike to gain higher wages after two years of...
02-06.text: World War I, or the First World War, also known as the Great War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Central Powers. Major areas of conflict included Europe and the Middle East, as well as parts of Africa and the Asia-Pacific. The war saw important developments in weaponry including tanks, aircraft, artillery, machine guns, and chemical weapons.

02-06.title: Woodbridge train derailment
02-06.subtitle: A train derailed while crossing a temporary wooden trestle in Woodbridge, New Jersey, causing 85 deaths.
02-06.text: A train derailed while crossing a temporary wooden trestle in Woodbridge, New Jersey, causing 85 deaths.

02-06.title: Elizabeth II
02-06.subtitle: Elizabeth II becomes Queen of the United Kingdom and her other Realms and Territories and Head of the Commonwealth upon ...
02-06.text: Elizabeth II was Queen of the United Kingdom and other Commonwealth realms from 6 February 1952 until her death in 2022. She was queen regnant of 32 sovereign states during her lifetime and was the monarch of 15 realms at her death. Her reign of 70 years and 214 days is the longest of any British monarch, the second-longest of any sovereign state, and the longest of any queen regnant in history.
02-06.location: Queen of the United Kingdom from 1952 to 2022

02-06.title: Manchester United F.C.
02-06.subtitle: The aircraft carrying the Manchester United football team crashed while attempting to take off from Munich-Riem Airport ...
02-06.text: The aircraft carrying the Manchester United football team crashed while attempting to take off from Munich-Riem Airport in West Germany, killing 8 players and 23 people in total (news reel featured).

02-06.title: Jack Kilby
02-06.subtitle: Jack Kilby of Texas Instruments files the first patent for an integrated circuit.
02-06.text: Jack Kilby of Texas Instruments files the first patent for an integrated circuit.

02-06.title: Lockheed Corporation
02-06.subtitle: Lockheed Corporation president Carl Kotchian admitted that the company had paid out approximately US$3 million in bribes...
02-06.text: The Lockheed Corporation was an American aerospace manufacturer. Lockheed was founded in 1926 and merged in 1995 with Martin Marietta to form Lockheed Martin. Its founder, Allan Lockheed, had earlier founded the similarly named but otherwise-unrelated Loughead Aircraft Manufacturing Company, which was operational from 1912 to 1920.

02-06.title: Mary Gaudron
02-06.subtitle: Mary Gaudron became the first woman to be appointed a justice of the High Court of Australia.
02-06.text: Mary Gaudron became the first woman to be appointed a justice of the High Court of Australia.

02-06.title: Russia Captures Grozny
02-06.subtitle: A shattered city falls to Russian forces
02-06.text: After months of devastating bombardment and fierce street-to-street fighting, Russian forces captured Grozny, the capital of Chechnya. The separatist government of the Chechen Republic of Ichkeria was forced into exile, and the city lay in ruins. The fall of Grozny marked a turning point in the Second Chechen War, but the smoldering rubble and displaced populations foretold years of continued insurgency and suffering.

02-06.title: SpaceX
02-06.subtitle: SpaceX's Falcon Heavy, a super heavy launch vehicle, makes its maiden flight.
02-06.text: SpaceX's Falcon Heavy, a super heavy launch vehicle, makes its maiden flight.

02-06.title: 2023 Turkey–Syria earthquakes
02-06.subtitle: Two earthquakes measuring Mww 7.
02-06.text: Two earthquakes measuring Mww 7.8 and 7.5 struck near the border between Turkey and Syria with a maximum Mercalli intensity of XII (Extreme). The earthquakes resulted in numerous aftershocks and a death toll of 57,658 people.

02-07.title: Emperor Leo I Crowned
02-07.subtitle: A Thracian soldier claims the Eastern throne
02-07.text: Leo I, a native of Dacia Aureliana near historic Thrace, was crowned Byzantine emperor, beginning a reign that would last nearly two decades. Rising from humble origins, Leo navigated the treacherous politics of Constantinople with skill, asserting imperial authority against powerful generals and barbarian threats. His reign stabilized the Eastern Empire at a time when the Western half was crumbling, earning him the epithet 'the Great.'

02-07.title: Plague Hits San Francisco
02-07.subtitle: The Black Death arrives in the New World
02-07.text: In San Francisco's Chinatown, a Chinese immigrant fell ill with the bubonic plague, marking the first epidemic of the disease in the continental United States. The outbreak, initially met with denial and racist scapegoating, exposed deep fractures in American public health and racial politics. The city's struggle to contain the plague became a defining moment in the history of American epidemiology and the fight against infectious disease.

02-07.title: The Beatles
02-07.subtitle: The Beatles land in the United States for the first time, at the newly renamed John F.
02-07.text: The Beatles land in the United States for the first time, at the newly renamed John F. Kennedy International Airport.

02-07.title: Pluto
02-07.subtitle: Pluto moves inside Neptune's orbit for the first time since either was discovered.
02-07.text: Pluto moves inside Neptune's orbit for the first time since either was discovered.

02-07.title: Space Shuttle program
02-07.subtitle: Space Shuttle program: STS-41-B Mission: astronauts Bruce McCandless II and Robert L.
02-07.text: The Space Shuttle program was the fourth human spaceflight program carried out by the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), which accomplished routine transportation for Earth-to-orbit crew and cargo from 1981 to 2011. Its official program name was Space Transportation System (STS), taken from a 1969 plan for a system of reusable spacecraft where it was the only item funded for development, as a proposed nuclear shuttle in the plan was cancelled in 1972.

02-07.title: The Troubles
02-07.subtitle: The Troubles: The Provisional Irish Republican Army shelled 10 Downing Street with mortars in a failed attempt to assass...
02-07.text: The Troubles were an ethno-nationalist conflict in Northern Ireland that lasted for about 30 years from the late 1960s to 1998. Also known internationally as the Northern Ireland conflict, it began in the late 1960s and is usually deemed to have ended with the Good Friday Agreement of 1998. Although the Troubles mostly took place in Northern Ireland, at times violence spilled over into parts of the Republic of Ireland, England, and mainland Europe.

02-07.title: Ramzi Yousef
02-07.subtitle: Ramzi Yousef, one of the main perpetrators of the 1993 World Trade Center bombing and the bombing of Philippine Airlines...
02-07.text: Ramzi Yousef, one of the main perpetrators of the 1993 World Trade Center bombing and the bombing of Philippine Airlines Flight 434, was arrested in Islamabad, Pakistan.

02-07.title: Atlantis Carries Destiny
02-07.subtitle: A space laboratory soars to its new home
02-07.text: Space Shuttle Atlantis thundered into orbit on mission STS-98, carrying the Destiny laboratory module to the International Space Station. The American-built module would become the primary research facility on the station, a cornerstone of scientific discovery in microgravity. The successful installation marked a milestone in international cooperation in space and expanded humanity's ability to conduct experiments beyond the bounds of Earth.
02-07.location: Kennedy Space Center, Florida, United States

02-07.title: Ilham Aliyev
02-07.subtitle: President Ilham Aliyev issued a decree on the redenomination of Azerbaijan's currency, with 1 new manat equal to 5000 old manats.
02-07.text: President Ilham Aliyev issued a decree on the redenomination of Azerbaijan's currency, with 1 new manat equal to 5000 old manats.

02-07.title: Mississippi
02-07.subtitle: The U.
02-07.text: The U.S. state of Mississippi officially certifies the Thirteenth Amendment, becoming the last state to approve the abolition of slavery. The Thirteenth Amendment was formally ratified by Mississippi in 1995.

02-07.title: Happisburgh footprints
02-07.subtitle: Researchers announced the discovery of the Happisburgh footprints in Norfolk, England, the oldest known hominid footprin...
02-07.text: The Happisburgh footprints were a set of fossilized hominid footprints that date to the end of the Early Pleistocene, around 850–950,000 years ago. They were discovered in May 2013 in a newly uncovered sediment layer of the Cromer Forest Bed on a beach at Happisburgh in Norfolk, England, and carefully photographed in 3D before being destroyed by the tide shortly afterwards.
02-07.location: Fossilized hominid footprints in Norfolk, England

02-08.title: Proclus Was Born
02-08.subtitle: The last great mind of classical antiquity
02-08.text: In Constantinople, Proclus Lycius was born, the philosopher who would become the final towering figure of classical Neoplatonism. His elaborate philosophical system synthesized centuries of Greek thought and profoundly influenced Byzantine, Islamic, and medieval European philosophy. Hegel himself called Proclus's work the true turning point between ancient and modern thought, a bridge spanning the intellectual chasm between the classical world and the one that followed.

02-08.title: Demchugdongrub Was Born
02-08.subtitle: A Mongol prince caught between empires
02-08.text: Demchugdongrub was born a Mongol prince of the Borjigin imperial clan, the same lineage as Genghis Khan. He would become the leader of an Inner Mongolian independence movement and later head of the Japanese puppet state of Mengjiang during World War II. His legacy remains deeply contested: some see him as a Mongol nationalist pursuing Pan-Mongolism, while others regard him as a collaborator who served Tokyo's imperial ambitions.

02-08.title: Groundbreaking
02-08.subtitle: The official groundbreaking of the Walk of Fame took place in Hollywood, Los Angeles, in California.
02-08.text: The official groundbreaking of the Walk of Fame took place in Hollywood, Los Angeles, in California.

02-08.title: Charonne subway massacre
02-08.subtitle: Nine protestors are killed at Charonne station, Paris, by French police under the command of ex-Vichy official and Paris...
02-08.text: Nine protestors are killed at Charonne station, Paris, by French police under the command of ex-Vichy official and Parisian Prefect of Police Maurice Papon.

02-08.title: John F. Kennedy International Airport
02-08.subtitle: After taking evasive action to avoid a mid-air collision just after taking off from New York's John F.
02-08.text: After taking evasive action to avoid a mid-air collision just after taking off from New York's John F. Kennedy International Airport, Eastern Air Lines Flight 663 crashed into the Atlantic Ocean, killing all 84 people on board.

02-08.title: American civil rights movement
02-08.subtitle: American civil rights movement: An attack on Black students from South Carolina State University who are protesting raci...
02-08.text: American civil rights movement: An attack on Black students from South Carolina State University who are protesting racial segregation leaves three dead and 28 injured in Orangeburg, South Carolina.

02-08.title: Nasdaq
02-08.subtitle: The NASDAQ stock market index opens for the first time.
02-08.text: The NASDAQ stock market index opens for the first time.

02-08.title: Skylab 4
02-08.subtitle: The crew of Skylab 4, the last mission to visit the American space station Skylab, returns to Earth after 84 days in space.
02-08.text: The crew of Skylab 4, the last mission to visit the American space station Skylab, returns to Earth after 84 days in space.

02-08.title: Denis Sassou Nguesso
02-08.subtitle: Denis Sassou Nguesso (pictured) was chosen as the new President of the Republic of the Congo after Joachim Yhombi-Opango was forced from power.
02-08.text: Denis Sassou Nguesso (pictured) was chosen as the new President of the Republic of the Congo after Joachim Yhombi-Opango was forced from power.

02-08.title: Rhodesian Bush War
02-08.subtitle: Rhodesian Bush War: Zimbabwe African National Liberation Army cadres in Gwelo attacked Zimbabwe People's Revolutionary A...
02-08.text: Rhodesian Bush War: Zimbabwe African National Liberation Army cadres in Gwelo attacked Zimbabwe People's Revolutionary Army troops, killing over 60 of them and starting the 1981 Entumbane uprising.

02-08.title: Iran Airtour
02-08.subtitle: An Iran Air Tours Tupolev Tu-154 and an Iranian Air Force Sukhoi Su-24 collide mid-air near Qods, Iran, killing all 133 people onboard both aircraft.
02-08.text: An Iran Air Tours Tupolev Tu-154 and an Iranian Air Force Sukhoi Su-24 collide mid-air near Qods, Iran, killing all 133 people onboard both aircraft.

02-08.title: I.N Was Born
02-08.subtitle: The youngest voice of Stray Kids arrives
02-08.text: Yang Jeong-in, who would become known by his stage name I.N, was born in South Korea. As the youngest member of the boy band Stray Kids, formed by JYP Entertainment in 2017, he brought youthful energy and vocal talent to one of K-pop's most innovative groups. His bright personality and growing artistic confidence have made him a beloved figure among fans worldwide as Stray Kids rose to global prominence.

02-09.title: Zeno Crowned Co-Emperor
02-09.subtitle: An Isaurian rises to share Rome's throne
02-09.text: Zeno, born in the rugged province of Isauria in modern-day Turkey, was crowned co-emperor of the Eastern Roman Empire. His path to power was anything but smooth, marked by palace coups and religious controversies. Yet Zeno proved remarkably resilient, surviving a usurpation and returning to rule for fifteen years. He presided over the fall of the Western Roman Empire while stabilizing the East, a feat of political survival against extraordinary odds.

02-09.title: Davis Cup Established
02-09.subtitle: Tennis gets its World Cup of nations
02-09.text: The Davis Cup was established, creating the premier international team competition in men's tennis. What began as a challenge match between Great Britain and the United States would grow into the largest annual international team sporting competition in the world, with over 150 nations participating. The silver trophy donated by Dwight Davis, a Harvard student with a passion for tennis, became one of sport's most coveted prizes.

02-09.title: Korean War
02-09.subtitle: Korean War: The two-day Geochang massacre begins as a battalion of the 11th Division of the South Korean Army kills 719 ...
02-09.text: The Korean War was an armed conflict on the Korean peninsula between North Korea and South Korea and their allies. North Korea was supported by China and the Soviet Union, while South Korea was supported by the United Nations Command (UNC) led by the United States. The war, which ended in stalemate, was the first great clash of arms of the Cold War and was one of, if not its deadliest proxy war.

02-09.title: The Beatles at the Cavern Club
02-09.subtitle: The Beatles at the Cavern Club: Lunchtime – The Beatles perform under this name at The Cavern Club for the first time fo...
02-09.text: The Beatles at the Cavern Club: Lunchtime – The Beatles perform under this name at The Cavern Club for the first time following their return to Liverpool from Hamburg.

02-09.title: The Beatles
02-09.subtitle: The Beatles make their first appearance on The Ed Sullivan Show, performing before a record-setting audience of 73 milli...
02-09.text: The Beatles were an English rock band formed in Liverpool in 1960. The core lineup of the band comprised John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison and Ringo Starr. They are widely regarded as the most influential band in popular music and were integral to the development of 1960s counterculture and the recognition of popular music as an art form.

02-09.title: Apollo program
02-09.subtitle: Apollo program: Apollo 14 returns to Earth after the third human Moon landing.
02-09.text: The Apollo program, also known as Project Apollo, was the United States human spaceflight program led by NASA, which landed the first humans on the Moon in 1969. Apollo was conceived during Project Mercury and executed after Project Gemini. It was conceived in 1960 as a three-person spacecraft during the presidency of Dwight D. Eisenhower. Apollo was later dedicated to President John F.

02-09.title: Australian Defence Force
02-09.subtitle: The Australian Defence Force (ensign pictured) was formed by the integration of the Australian Army, the Royal Australia...
02-09.text: The Australian Defence Force (ensign pictured) was formed by the integration of the Australian Army, the Royal Australian Navy, and the Royal Australian Air Force.

02-09.title: USS Greeneville Collision
02-09.subtitle: A submarine surfaces into tragedy
02-09.text: The American nuclear submarine USS Greeneville collided with the Ehime Maru, a Japanese high school training vessel, during an emergency surfacing drill off the coast of Hawaii. The collision sank the Japanese ship and killed nine people, including four high school students. The disaster strained U.S.-Japan relations and raised serious questions about Navy procedures during civilian observation cruises aboard military submarines.
02-09.location: Off the coast of Hawaii, United States

02-09.title: Figure skating
02-09.subtitle: Japanese figure skater Yuzuru Hanyu won the Four Continents Championships to become the only man to complete a Super Slam.
02-09.text: Figure skating is a sport in which individuals, pairs, or groups perform on figure skates on ice. It was the first winter sport to be included in the Olympic Games, with its introduction occurring at the 1908 Olympics in London. The Olympic disciplines are men's singles, women's singles, pair skating, and ice dance; the four individual disciplines are also combined into a team event, which was first included in the Winter Olympics in 2014.
02-09.location: Ice sport performed on figure skates

02-10.title: Genghis Khan Takes Bukhara
02-10.subtitle: The Mongol storm engulfs Central Asia
02-10.text: The great city of Bukhara, jewel of the Khwarazmian Empire and one of the Islamic world's most learned centers, fell to the Mongol armies of Genghis Khan. The conqueror's forces looted the city with devastating thoroughness, destroying libraries, mosques, and centuries of accumulated knowledge. The fall of Bukhara was a harbinger of the wider devastation to come as the Mongol horde swept across Central Asia, reshaping the entire region.

02-10.title: Stella Adler Was Born
02-10.subtitle: The teacher who reshaped American acting
02-10.text: Stella Adler was born in New York City to a famous Yiddish theater family. She would become one of America's most influential acting teachers, training generations of performers including Marlon Brando, Robert De Niro, and Warren Beatty. Her approach, which emphasized imagination and given circumstances over emotional memory, revolutionized the craft of acting and made her name synonymous with the highest standards of theatrical artistry.

02-10.title: Cold War
02-10.subtitle: Cold War: Captured American U2 spy-plane pilot Gary Powers is exchanged for captured Soviet spy Rudolf Abel.
02-10.text: The Cold War was a period of international geopolitical rivalry between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) and their respective allies, the capitalist Western Bloc and communist Eastern Bloc, which began in the aftermath of the Second World War and ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
02-10.location: 1947–1991 geopolitical rivalry between US and USSR

02-10.title: Ron Brown
02-10.subtitle: Ron Brown is elected chairman of the Democratic National Committee, becoming the first African American to lead a major American political party.
02-10.text: Ron Brown is elected chairman of the Democratic National Committee, becoming the first African American to lead a major American political party.

02-10.title: IBM
02-10.subtitle: IBM supercomputer Deep Blue defeats Garry Kasparov in chess for the first time.
02-10.text: International Business Machines Corporation, doing business as IBM, is an American multinational technology company headquartered in Armonk, New York, and present in over 175 countries. It is a publicly traded company and one of the 30 companies in the Dow Jones Industrial Average. IBM is the largest industrial research organization in the world, with 19 research facilities across a dozen countries; for 29 consecutive years, from 1993 to 2021, it held the record for most annual U.S.

02-10.title: Maria Carle Was Born
02-10.subtitle: Argentina's tennis talent blooms
02-10.text: Maria Lourdes Carle was born in Argentina, a nation with a rich but evolving tennis tradition. The young player would develop her game on the clay courts of South America before competing on the WTA Tour, reaching a career-high singles ranking of seventy-one. Her journey from Argentine junior circuits to the world stage represents the growing depth of women's tennis talent emerging from Latin America.

02-10.title: Namdaemun
02-10.subtitle: The Namdaemun gate in Seoul, the first of South Korea's National Treasures, was severely damaged by arson (damage pictured).
02-10.text: The Namdaemun gate in Seoul, the first of South Korea's National Treasures, was severely damaged by arson (damage pictured).

02-10.title: Communications satellite
02-10.subtitle: The communications satellites Iridium 33 and Kosmos 2251 collide in orbit, destroying both.
02-10.text: The communications satellites Iridium 33 and Kosmos 2251 collide in orbit, destroying both.

02-10.title: Rio Carnival
02-10.subtitle: The traditional Carnival in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil is canceled for the first time because of the COVID-19 pandemic.
02-10.text: The traditional Carnival in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil is canceled for the first time because of the COVID-19 pandemic.

02-11.title: Japan's Mythical Founding
02-11.subtitle: A sun goddess's descendant builds a nation
02-11.text: According to ancient Japanese tradition, Emperor Jimmu founded the nation of Japan on this day, descending from the sun goddess Amaterasu herself. While historians regard Jimmu as legendary rather than historical, the story holds immense cultural significance. His mythical journey from Hyuga to Yamato and his establishment of the imperial line are celebrated every year on National Foundation Day, connecting modern Japan to its deepest mythological roots.

02-11.title: Hans-Georg Gadamer Born
02-11.subtitle: A philosopher who lived past a hundred
02-11.text: Hans-Georg Gadamer was born in Marburg, Germany, destined to become one of the twentieth century's most important philosophers. His magnum opus, Truth and Method, revolutionized hermeneutics and challenged the dominance of scientific methodology in the humanities. Living to the remarkable age of one hundred and two, Gadamer embodied the life of the mind, continuously engaging with students, critics, and ideas until his final days.

02-11.title: The Beatles
02-11.subtitle: The Beatles record their first album, Please Please Me.
02-11.text: The Beatles were an English rock band formed in Liverpool in 1960. The core lineup of the band comprised John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison and Ringo Starr. They are widely regarded as the most influential band in popular music and were integral to the development of 1960s counterculture and the recognition of popular music as an art form.

02-11.title: Cold War
02-11.subtitle: Cold War: The Seabed Arms Control Treaty opens for signature, outlawing nuclear weapons on the ocean floor in international waters.
02-11.text: The Seabed Arms Control Treaty is a multilateral agreement between the United States, Soviet Union, United Kingdom, and 91 other countries banning the emplacement of nuclear weapons or "weapons of mass destruction" on the seabed and the ocean floor beyond a 12 nautical miles coastal zone. It allows signatories to observe all seabed "activities" of any other signatory beyond the 12-mile zone to ensure compliance.
02-11.location: 1971 international agreement limiting nuclear weapons on the sea floor

02-11.title: Frente de Liberación Homosexual
02-11.subtitle: The Frente de Liberación Homosexual made their final public appearance, shortly before the group's dissolution due to po...
02-11.text: The Frente de Liberación Homosexual made their final public appearance, shortly before the group's dissolution due to political repression after the Argentine coup d'état.

02-11.title: Nelson Mandela
02-11.subtitle: Anti-apartheid activist Nelson Mandela, having been a political prisoner for 27 years, was released from Victor Verster ...
02-11.text: Anti-apartheid activist Nelson Mandela, having been a political prisoner for 27 years, was released from Victor Verster Prison near Paarl, South Africa.

02-11.title: Space Shuttle Discovery
02-11.subtitle: Space Shuttle Discovery is launched on a mission to service the Hubble Space Telescope.
02-11.text: Space Shuttle Discovery is launched on a mission to service the Hubble Space Telescope.

02-11.title: Endeavour Maps the Earth
02-11.subtitle: A shuttle launches to scan every mountain
02-11.text: Space Shuttle Endeavour soared into orbit on mission STS-99, carrying the instruments for the Shuttle Radar Topography Mission. Over eleven days, the shuttle mapped eighty percent of Earth's land surface with unprecedented precision, creating the most complete high-resolution digital topographic database of our planet. The data transformed fields from geology to urban planning, giving humanity its most detailed three-dimensional portrait of the world.
02-11.location: Kennedy Space Center, Florida, United States

02-11.title: Computer worm
02-11.subtitle: The computer worm Anna Kournikova, which would affect millions of users worldwide, was released by a 20-year-old Dutch student.
