North Vietnam was supported by the Soviet Union and China, while South Vietnam was supported by the United States and other anti-communist nations. The conflict was the second of the Indochina wars and a proxy war of the Cold War between the Soviet Union and US.
03-16.location: War in Southeast Asia from 1955 to 1975

03-16.title: Amoco Cadiz
03-16.subtitle: Supertanker Amoco Cadiz splits in two after running aground on the Portsall Rocks, three miles off the coast of Brittany...
03-16.text: Supertanker Amoco Cadiz splits in two after running aground on the Portsall Rocks, three miles off the coast of Brittany, resulting in the largest oil spill in history at that time.

03-16.title: William Francis Buckley
03-16.subtitle: William Buckley, the U.
03-16.text: William Buckley, the U.S. Central Intelligence Agency station chief in Beirut, Lebanon, was kidnapped by Islamic fundamentalists.

03-16.title: Iran–Iraq War
03-16.subtitle: Iran–Iraq War: Iraqi forces began attacking the Kurdish town of Halabja with chemical weapons, killing up to 5,000 people.
03-16.text: Iran–Iraq War: Iraqi forces began attacking the Kurdish town of Halabja with chemical weapons, killing up to 5,000 people.

03-16.title: Mississippi
03-16.subtitle: Mississippi formally ratifies the Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution, becoming the last state to approve the abolition of slavery.
03-16.text: Mississippi formally ratifies the Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution, becoming the last state to approve the abolition of slavery. The Thirteenth Amendment was officially ratified in 1865.

03-16.title: Jalen Smith Was Born
03-16.subtitle: A young basketball talent enters the world
03-16.text: Jalen Smith was born in the United States, a future basketball player whose height, athleticism, and developing skill set would carry him from the University of Maryland to the NBA. Selected in the first round of the 2020 draft, Smith's journey through multiple NBA teams showcased the patience and perseverance required to establish oneself at the highest level of professional basketball.

03-16.title: Rachel Corrie
03-16.subtitle: American peace activist Rachel Corrie was crushed to death by an Israel Defense Forces armored bulldozer in Rafah while ...
03-16.text: American peace activist Rachel Corrie was crushed to death by an Israel Defense Forces armored bulldozer in Rafah while protesting the demolition of a Palestinian house.

03-16.title: 2020 stock market crash
03-16.subtitle: The Dow Jones Industrial Average falls by 2,997.
03-16.text: The Dow Jones Industrial Average falls by 2,997.10, the single largest point drop in history and the second-largest percentage drop ever at 12.93%, an even greater crash than Black Monday (1929). This follows the U.S. Federal Reserve announcing that it will cut its target interest rate to 0–0.25%.

03-17.title: Caesar's Final Victory
03-17.subtitle: The last battle of Rome's civil war
03-17.text: In the dusty fields of Munda in southern Spain, Julius Caesar won his last and hardest-fought victory, defeating the Pompeian forces of Titus Labienus and Pompey the Younger. The Battle of Munda was a savage, desperate affair in which Caesar reportedly told his soldiers he had often fought for victory but now fought for his life. The triumph at Munda ended Rome's civil war and left Caesar master of the entire Roman world.

03-17.title: Alfred Newman Was Born
03-17.subtitle: Hollywood's maestro of the movie score
03-17.text: Alfred Newman was born in New Haven, Connecticut, destined to become one of Hollywood's most prolific and celebrated film composers. Over a career spanning four decades, he composed the scores for over two hundred films and won nine Academy Awards, more than any other person in the music categories. His iconic 20th Century Fox fanfare remains one of the most recognizable pieces of music in cinema history.

03-17.title: Belgium
03-17.subtitle: Belgium, France, Luxembourg, the Netherlands and the United Kingdom sign the Treaty of Brussels, a precursor to the Nort...
03-17.text: Belgium, France, Luxembourg, the Netherlands and the United Kingdom sign the Treaty of Brussels, a precursor to the North Atlantic Treaty establishing NATO.

03-17.title: Ice hockey
03-17.subtitle: Ice hockey fans in Montreal rioted to protest the suspension of Montreal Canadiens star Maurice Richard for hitting an official.
03-17.text: Ice hockey fans in Montreal rioted to protest the suspension of Montreal Canadiens star Maurice Richard for hitting an official.

03-17.title: Vanguard 1
03-17.subtitle: The United States launches the first solar-powered satellite, which is also the first satellite to achieve a long-term orbit.
03-17.text: Vanguard 1 is an American satellite that was the fourth artificial Earth-orbiting satellite to be successfully launched, following Sputnik 1, Sputnik 2, and Explorer 1. It was launched 17 March 1958. Vanguard 1 was the first satellite to have solar electric power. Although communications with the satellite were lost in 1964, it remains the oldest artificial object still in orbit, together with the upper stage of its launch vehicle.
03-17.location: American satellite launched in 1958; oldest manmade object currently in Earth orbit

03-17.title: Dugway Proving Ground
03-17.subtitle: Six thousand sheep were killed on ranches near Dugway Proving Ground in Utah as a result of the U.
03-17.text: Six thousand sheep were killed on ranches near Dugway Proving Ground in Utah as a result of the U.S. Army spraying a nerve agent.

03-17.title: Pulitzer Prize
03-17.subtitle: The Pulitzer Prize–winning photograph Burst of Joy is taken, depicting a former prisoner of war being reunited with his ...
03-17.text: The Pulitzer Prize–winning photograph Burst of Joy is taken, depicting a former prisoner of war being reunited with his family, which came to symbolize the end of United States involvement in the Vietnam War.

03-17.title: Eritrean War of Independence
03-17.subtitle: Eritrean War of Independence: The Nadew Command, an Ethiopian army corps in Eritrea, is attacked on three sides by milit...
03-17.text: Eritrean War of Independence: The Nadew Command, an Ethiopian army corps in Eritrea, is attacked on three sides by military units of the Eritrean People's Liberation Front in the opening action of the Battle of Afabet.

03-17.title: 1992 South African apartheid referendum
03-17.subtitle: A referendum to end apartheid in South Africa is passed 68.
03-17.text: A referendum to end apartheid in South Africa is passed 68.7% to 31.2%.

03-17.title: Ugandan Cult Mass Death
03-17.subtitle: Five hundred perish in a doomsday cult fire
03-17.text: In the remote Kanungu district of Uganda, five hundred and thirty members of the Movement for the Restoration of the Ten Commandments of God perished in a church fire that investigators determined was a mass murder disguised as a mass suicide. The cult's leaders had prophesied the end of the world and, when it failed to arrive, killed their followers to conceal years of fraud and abuse. The tragedy remains one of the deadliest cult events in history.

03-17.title: First Libyan Civil War
03-17.subtitle: First Libyan Civil War: The United Nations Security Council adopted Resolution 1973, authorizing military intervention in Libya to protect civilians.
03-17.text: First Libyan Civil War: The United Nations Security Council adopted Resolution 1973, authorizing military intervention in Libya to protect civilians.

03-18.title: Caligula Becomes Emperor
03-18.subtitle: Little Boots ascends to rule the Roman world
03-18.text: Following the death of Emperor Tiberius, the Roman Senate annulled his will and proclaimed Gaius Julius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, known by his childhood nickname Caligula, meaning 'Little Boots,' as the new emperor. The young ruler was initially welcomed with joy by a populace tired of Tiberius's dark reign. But Caligula's rule would descend into cruelty and madness, making his very name a byword for tyrannical excess.

03-18.title: House with Chimaeras Built
03-18.subtitle: Kyiv gets its most fantastical building
03-18.text: Construction began on the House with Chimaeras in Kyiv, one of the most extraordinary examples of Art Nouveau architecture in Eastern Europe. Designed by architect Vladislav Horodetsky, the building was adorned with sculptural decorations of mythical creatures, hunting scenes, and exotic animals. The fantastical facade would become one of Kyiv's most beloved landmarks, a testament to the creativity and ambition of the city's early twentieth-century cultural flowering.

03-18.title: Tibetan uprising
03-18.subtitle: Tibetan uprising: After the 14th Dalai Lama sought refuge at Sera Monastery in Lhasa, Tibet, Chinese forces bombarded th...
03-18.text: Tibetan uprising: After the 14th Dalai Lama sought refuge at Sera Monastery in Lhasa, Tibet, Chinese forces bombarded the monastery, inflicting severe destruction and killing hundreds of Buddhist monks.

03-18.title: Astronaut
03-18.subtitle: Cosmonaut Alexei Leonov, leaving his spacecraft Voskhod 2 for 12 minutes, becomes the first person to walk in space.
03-18.text: An astronaut is a person trained, equipped, and deployed by a human spaceflight program to serve as a commander or crew member of a spacecraft. Although generally reserved for professional space travelers, the term is sometimes applied to anyone who travels into space, including scientists, politicians, journalists, and space tourists. In the United States, it is a designated term used by three agencies: NASA, the FAA, and the military.

03-18.title: Vietnam War
03-18.subtitle: Vietnam War: The United States began secretly bombing the Sihanouk Trail in Cambodia, used by communist forces to infiltrate South Vietnam.
03-18.text: The Vietnam War was an armed conflict in Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia fought between North Vietnam and South Vietnam and their allies. North Vietnam was supported by the Soviet Union and China, while South Vietnam was supported by the United States and other anti-communist nations. The conflict was the second of the Indochina wars and a proxy war of the Cold War between the Soviet Union and US.
03-18.location: War in Southeast Asia from 1955 to 1975

03-18.title: Vostok-2M
03-18.subtitle: A Vostok-2M rocket at Plesetsk Cosmodrome Site 43 explodes during a fueling operation, killing 48 people.
03-18.text: A Vostok-2M rocket at Plesetsk Cosmodrome Site 43 explodes during a fueling operation, killing 48 people.

03-18.title: Soap opera
03-18.subtitle: The first episode of the soap opera Neighbours was broadcast on the Seven Network, later becoming the longest-running dr...
03-18.text: The first episode of the soap opera Neighbours was broadcast on the Seven Network, later becoming the longest-running drama in Australian television history.

03-18.title: East Germany
03-18.subtitle: Germans in the German Democratic Republic vote in the first democratic elections in the former communist dictatorship.
03-18.text: Germans in the German Democratic Republic vote in the first democratic elections in the former communist dictatorship.

03-18.title: Bosnia and Herzegovina
03-18.subtitle: Bosnia's Bosniaks and Croats sign the Washington Agreement, ending war between the Croatian Republic of Herzeg-Bosnia an...
03-18.text: Bosnia's Bosniaks and Croats sign the Washington Agreement, ending war between the Croatian Republic of Herzeg-Bosnia and the Republic of Bosnia and Herzegovina, and establishing the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina.

03-18.title: Terri Schiavo's Feeding Tube Removed
03-18.subtitle: America confronts the right to die
03-18.text: Following a prolonged legal battle, the feeding tube of Terri Schiavo, an American woman who had suffered severe brain damage fifteen years earlier, was removed pursuant to a court order. The case ignited a fierce national debate over the right to die, the sanctity of life, and the limits of governmental authority over medical decisions. Schiavo's case became a cultural lightning rod, dividing families, politicians, and the nation itself.

03-18.title: Syrian civil war
03-18.subtitle: Syrian civil war: The U.
03-18.text: The Syrian civil war was an armed conflict that began with the Syrian revolution in March 2011, when popular discontent with the Ba'athist regime ruled by Bashar al-Assad triggered large-scale protests and pro-democracy rallies across Syria, as part of the wider Arab Spring. The Assad regime responded to the protests with lethal force, which led to a series of defections, the emergence of armed opposition groups, and the civilian uprising descending into a civil war.

03-19.title: Guyuk Khan Was Born
03-19.subtitle: A Mongol prince destined for the Great Khanate
03-19.text: Guyuk Khan was born into the Mongol royal family, the eldest son of Ogedei Khan and grandson of Genghis Khan. He would eventually become the third Great Khan of the Mongol Empire, ruling a dominion that stretched from Korea to Hungary. Though his reign lasted only two years before his sudden death, Guyuk presided over one of the largest empires in human history, a legacy of conquest that reshaped the entire Eurasian continent.

03-19.title: Carmen Carbonell Was Born
03-19.subtitle: Spain's beloved stage and screen actress
03-19.text: Carmen Carbonell was born in Spain, a future actress who would grace both the theatrical stages and film screens of her homeland for decades. Her talent and versatility made her one of Spain's most respected performers during the mid-twentieth century, a time of profound social and political change in the Iberian Peninsula. Carbonell's career reflected the resilience of Spanish cultural life through decades of dictatorship and transformation.

03-19.title: Irish War of Independence
03-19.subtitle: Irish War of Independence: One of the biggest engagements of the war takes place at Crossbarry, County Cork.
03-19.text: Irish War of Independence: One of the biggest engagements of the war takes place at Crossbarry, County Cork. About 100 Irish Republican Army (IRA) volunteers escape an attempt by over 1,300 British forces to encircle them.

03-19.title: Bob Dylan
03-19.subtitle: Influential American musician Bob Dylan  released his eponymous debut album, mainly comprising traditional folk, blues and gospel songs.
03-19.text: Bob Dylan is an American singer-songwriter. Described as one of the greatest songwriters of all time, Dylan has been a major figure in popular culture over his 69-year career. With an estimated 125 million records sold worldwide, he is one of the best-selling musicians. Dylan added increasingly sophisticated lyrical techniques to the folk music of the early 1960s, infusing it "with the intellectualism of classic literature and poetry".

03-19.title: Televangelism
03-19.subtitle: American televangelist Jim Bakker resigned as the host of The PTL Club in the midst of a sex scandal.
03-19.text: American televangelist Jim Bakker resigned as the host of The PTL Club in the midst of a sex scandal.

03-19.title: Egypt
03-19.subtitle: The Egyptian flag is raised at Taba, marking the end of Israeli occupation since the Six Days War in 1967 and the Egypt–Israel peace treaty in 1979.
03-19.text: The Egyptian flag is raised at Taba, marking the end of Israeli occupation since the Six Days War in 1967 and the Egypt–Israel peace treaty in 1979.

03-19.title: Bush Announces Iraq Invasion
03-19.subtitle: A president addresses the nation for war
03-19.text: President George W. Bush addressed the American people from the Oval Office, announcing the invasion of Iraq. Citing weapons of mass destruction and links to terrorism, Bush launched a military operation that would topple Saddam Hussein but ignite years of insurgency and sectarian violence. The decision to invade Iraq became one of the most controversial foreign policy actions in American history, reshaping the Middle East and American politics for decades.

03-19.title: Gamma-ray burst
03-19.subtitle: The gamma-ray burst GRB 080319B (artist's impression pictured), the farthest object from Earth that was observable by the naked eye, was observed.
03-19.text: The gamma-ray burst GRB 080319B (artist's impression pictured), the farthest object from Earth that was observable by the naked eye, was observed.

03-19.title: First Libyan Civil War
03-19.subtitle: First Libyan Civil War: The French Air Force launched Opération Harmattan, beginning foreign military intervention in Libya.
03-19.text: First Libyan Civil War: The French Air Force launched Opération Harmattan, beginning foreign military intervention in Libya.

03-19.title: Flydubai Flight 981
03-19.subtitle: Flydubai Flight 981 crashed during an aborted landing at Rostov-on-Don Airport, Russia, resulting in the deaths of all 6...
03-19.text: Flydubai Flight 981 (FZ981/FDB981) was a scheduled international passenger flight from Dubai, United Arab Emirates, to Rostov-on-Don, Russia. On 19 March 2016, the Boeing 737-800 aircraft operating the flight crashed during a go-around, killing all 62 passengers and crew on board.

03-19.title: President of Kazakhstan
03-19.subtitle: The first President of Kazakhstan, Nursultan Nazarbayev, resigns from office after nearly three decades, leaving Senate ...
03-19.text: The first President of Kazakhstan, Nursultan Nazarbayev, resigns from office after nearly three decades, leaving Senate Chairman Kassym-Jomart Tokayev as the acting President and successor.

03-20.title: Ovid Was Born
03-20.subtitle: Rome's poet of love and transformation
03-20.text: In the Apennine town of Sulmo, Publius Ovidius Naso was born, the poet who would give the world the Metamorphoses, one of the most influential works in all of Western literature. Ovid's verses, filled with stories of gods and mortals transformed by love, desire, and fate, have inspired artists from Bernini to Shakespeare. His eventual exile by Augustus for mysterious reasons only deepened the mystique surrounding Rome's most imaginative storyteller.

03-20.title: Bolivia's First Female Doctor
03-20.subtitle: Amelia Chopitea breaks a medical barrier
03-20.text: Amelia Chopitea Villa was born in Bolivia, destined to become the country's first female physician. In a society where women were largely excluded from professional life, Chopitea's determination to practice medicine broke barriers and opened doors for future generations of Bolivian women. Her pioneering career, though cut short by her death in 1942, demonstrated that talent and dedication can overcome even the most entrenched social obstacles.

03-20.title: Albert Einstein
03-20.subtitle: Albert Einstein submits his paper, "The Foundation of the General Theory of Relativity", which establishes his general t...
03-20.text: Albert Einstein submits his paper, "The Foundation of the General Theory of Relativity", which establishes his general theory of relativity, to the journal Annalen der Physik.

03-20.title: World War II
03-20.subtitle: World War II: After being forced to flee the Philippines for Australia, US Army general Douglas MacArthur (pictured) ann...
03-20.text: World War II or the Second World War was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers. Nearly all of the world's countries participated. Tanks and aircraft played major roles, the latter enabling the strategic bombing of cities and delivery of the only nuclear weapons used in war. World War II was the deadliest conflict in history, causing the death of 60 to 75 million people.

03-20.title: World War II
03-20.subtitle: World War II: U.
03-20.text: World War II or the Second World War was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers. Nearly all of the world's countries participated. Tanks and aircraft played major roles, the latter enabling the strategic bombing of cities and delivery of the only nuclear weapons used in war. World War II was the deadliest conflict in history, causing the death of 60 to 75 million people.

03-20.title: European Space Agency
03-20.subtitle: The precursor of the European Space Agency, ESRO (European Space Research Organisation) is established per an agreement signed on June 14, 1962.
03-20.text: The European Space Agency (ESA), pronounced 'ee-sah', is a 23-member international organisation devoted to space exploration. It has its headquarters in Paris and a staff of around 2,547 people globally as of 2023. ESA was founded in 1975 in the context of European integration. Its 2026 annual budget was around US$10 billion.
03-20.location: European organisation dedicated to space exploration

03-20.title: The Troubles
03-20.subtitle: The Troubles: The first car bombing by the Provisional IRA in Belfast kills seven people and injures 148 others in Northern Ireland.
03-20.text: The Troubles: The first car bombing by the Provisional IRA in Belfast kills seven people and injures 148 others in Northern Ireland.

03-20.title: Antiviral drug
03-20.subtitle: The antiretroviral drug zidovudine (chemical structure pictured) became the first treatment approved by the U.
03-20.text: The antiretroviral drug zidovudine (chemical structure pictured) became the first treatment approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration for HIV/AIDS.

03-20.title: Eritrean War of Independence
03-20.subtitle: Eritrean War of Independence: Having defeated the Nadew Command, the Eritrean People's Liberation Front enters the town ...
03-20.text: Eritrean War of Independence: Having defeated the Nadew Command, the Eritrean People's Liberation Front enters the town of Afabet, victoriously concluding the Battle of Afabet.

03-20.title: The Troubles
03-20.subtitle: The Troubles: Two children were killed by the second of two bomb attacks by the Provisional Irish Republican Army in Warrington, England.
03-20.text: The Troubles were an ethno-nationalist conflict in Northern Ireland that lasted for about 30 years from the late 1960s to 1998. Also known internationally as the Northern Ireland conflict, it began in the late 1960s and is usually deemed to have ended with the Good Friday Agreement of 1998. Although the Troubles mostly took place in Northern Ireland, at times violence spilled over into parts of the Republic of Ireland, England, and mainland Europe.

03-20.title: H. Rap Brown Captured
03-20.subtitle: A former Black Panther faces justice for murder
03-20.text: Jamil Abdullah Al-Amin, formerly known as H. Rap Brown of the Black Panthers, was captured after murdering a Georgia sheriff's deputy. Once a prominent figure in the Black Power movement of the 1960s, Al-Amin's transformation from revolutionary activist to convicted murderer marked a dramatic and tragic arc. His capture closed one chapter of a life that had already lived through multiple reinventions, from civil rights firebrand to Islamic leader to fugitive.

03-20.title: Taliban
03-20.subtitle: Taliban militants killed nine civilians in a mass shooting at the Kabul Serena Hotel in Afghanistan.
03-20.text: The Taliban, which also refers to itself by its state name, the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan, is an Afghan political and militant organization with an ideology comprising elements of the Deobandi movement of Islamic fundamentalism and Pashtun nationalism. It ruled approximately 90% of Afghanistan from 1996 to 2001, before it was overthrown by an American-led invasion after the September 11 attacks carried out by the Taliban's ally al-Qaeda.
03-20.location: Islamist militant organization in control of Afghanistan

03-20.title: Solar eclipse of March 20, 2015
03-20.subtitle: A Solar eclipse, equinox, and a supermoon all occur on the same day.
03-20.text: A total solar eclipse occurred at the Moon's descending node of orbit on Friday, March 20, 2015, with a magnitude of 1.0445. A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon passes between Earth and the Sun, thereby totally or partly obscuring the image of the Sun for a viewer on Earth. A total solar eclipse occurs when the Moon's apparent diameter is larger than the Sun's, blocking all direct sunlight, turning day into darkness.

03-21.title: Vitiges Assaults Rome
03-21.subtitle: Ostrogoth warriors storm the ancient walls
03-21.text: King Vitiges led his Ostrogothic warriors in a furious assault against the northern and eastern walls of Rome, attempting to break through the city's ancient defenses. The Byzantine garrison, commanded by the legendary general Belisarius, repulsed the attackers at the Praenestine Gate in fierce hand-to-hand combat. The failed assault demonstrated that Rome's walls, built centuries earlier, could still hold against a determined enemy when defended by skilled soldiers.

03-21.title: Karl Arnold Was Born
03-21.subtitle: A man who would lead postwar Germany
03-21.text: Karl Arnold was born in Germany, a future businessman and politician who would serve as President of the German Bundesrat after World War II. His career in postwar politics reflected the enormous challenges of rebuilding a nation from the rubble of total defeat. Arnold's leadership helped establish the democratic institutions that would guide West Germany through the Cold War and toward the economic miracle that transformed it into Europe's powerhouse.

03-21.title: Hungarian Soviet Republic
03-21.subtitle: The Hungarian Soviet Republic is established becoming the first Communist government to be formed in Europe after the October Revolution in Russia.
03-21.text: The Hungarian Soviet Republic is established becoming the first Communist government to be formed in Europe after the October Revolution in Russia.

03-21.title: Alan Freed
03-21.subtitle: Alan Freed presents the Moondog Coronation Ball, the first rock and roll concert, in Cleveland, Ohio.
03-21.text: Alan Freed presents the Moondog Coronation Ball, the first rock and roll concert, in Cleveland, Ohio.

03-21.title: Alcatraz Federal Penitentiary
03-21.subtitle: Alcatraz Federal Penitentiary on Alcatraz Island in San Francisco Bay, California—one of the world's most notorious and best known prisons—was closed.
03-21.text: Alcatraz Federal Penitentiary on Alcatraz Island in San Francisco Bay, California—one of the world's most notorious and best known prisons—was closed.

03-21.title: Ranger program
03-21.subtitle: Ranger program: NASA launches Ranger 9, the last in a series of uncrewed lunar space probes.
03-21.text: Ranger program: NASA launches Ranger 9, the last in a series of uncrewed lunar space probes.

03-21.title: War of Attrition
03-21.subtitle: War of Attrition: The Battle of Karameh took place between the Israel Defense Forces and allied troops of the Palestine ...
03-21.text: War of Attrition: The Battle of Karameh took place between the Israel Defense Forces and allied troops of the Palestine Liberation Organization and the Jordanian Armed Forces.

03-21.title: Cold War
03-21.subtitle: Cold War: American President Jimmy Carter announces a United States boycott of the 1980 Summer Olympics in Moscow to protest the Soviet–Afghan War.
03-21.text: The Cold War was a period of international geopolitical rivalry between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) and their respective allies, the capitalist Western Bloc and communist Eastern Bloc, which began in the aftermath of the Second World War and ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
03-21.location: 1947–1991 geopolitical rivalry between US and USSR

03-21.title: Pope Visits Israel
03-21.subtitle: A historic pilgrimage to the Holy Land
03-21.text: Pope John Paul II made his first ever pontifical visit to Israel, a pilgrimage of profound spiritual and political significance. The pope prayed at the Western Wall, visited Yad Vashem, and met with Israeli and Palestinian leaders. The visit was a powerful gesture of reconciliation between Christianity and Judaism, acknowledging centuries of pain while seeking a path toward mutual understanding in one of the world's most contested and sacred landscapes.

03-21.title: Social media
03-21.subtitle: The social media site Twitter (now officially named X) is founded.
03-21.text: The social media site Twitter (now officially named X) is founded.

03-22.title: Bostran Era Begins
03-22.subtitle: A Roman province marks a new epoch
03-22.text: The Bostran era, the official chronological system of the Roman province of Arabia Petraea, began, marking a new epoch for the diverse peoples of the region. The era, named after the provincial capital Bostra, served as the standard dating system for one of Rome's most fascinating frontier provinces, where Greco-Roman civilization met the ancient cultures of the Arabian desert in a rich tapestry of commerce and exchange.

03-22.title: Johannes Brinkman Was Born
03-22.subtitle: The architect behind a modernist masterpiece
03-22.text: Johannes Brinkman was born in Rotterdam, Netherlands, a future architect whose masterpiece, the Van Nelle Factory, would become one of the most celebrated examples of modernist architecture in the world. The factory's revolutionary design, featuring glass curtain walls and open floor plans, embodied the functionalist ideals of the modern movement. Now a UNESCO World Heritage site, the building stands as a monument to Dutch architectural innovation.

03-22.title: The Beatles
03-22.subtitle: The Beatles release their debut album Please Please Me.
03-22.text: The Beatles were an English rock band formed in Liverpool in 1960. The core lineup of the band comprised John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison and Ringo Starr. They are widely regarded as the most influential band in popular music and were integral to the development of 1960s counterculture and the recognition of popular music as an art form.

03-22.title: NASA
03-22.subtitle: NASA's Space Shuttle Columbia is launched from the Kennedy Space Center on its third mission, STS-3.
03-22.text: The National Aeronautics and Space Administration is an independent agency of the U.S. federal government responsible for the United States' civil space program and for research in aeronautics and space exploration. Headquartered in Washington, D.C., NASA operates ten field centers across the US and is organized into mission directorates for Science, Space Operations, Exploration Systems Development, Space Technology, Aeronautics Research, and Mission Support.

03-22.title: Manhattan Beach, California
03-22.subtitle: Teachers at a preschool in Manhattan Beach, California, were falsely charged with the sexual abuse of schoolchildren, le...
03-22.text: Teachers at a preschool in Manhattan Beach, California, were falsely charged with the sexual abuse of schoolchildren, leading to the longest and costliest criminal trial in United States history.

03-22.title: USAir Flight 405
03-22.subtitle: USAir Flight 405 crashed in Flushing Bay shortly after takeoff from New York City's LaGuardia Airport, killing 27 people...
03-22.text: USAir Flight 405 crashed in Flushing Bay shortly after takeoff from New York City's LaGuardia Airport, killing 27 people, and leading to studies into the effects of ice on aircraft.

03-22.title: Intel
03-22.subtitle: The Intel Corporation ships the first Pentium chips (80586), featuring a 60 MHz clock speed, 100+ MIPS, and a 64 bit data path.
03-22.text: The Intel Corporation ships the first Pentium chips (80586), featuring a 60 MHz clock speed, 100+ MIPS, and a 64 bit data path.

03-22.title: Astronaut
03-22.subtitle: Cosmonaut Valeri Polyakov returns to earth after setting a record of 438 days in space.
03-22.text: An astronaut is a person trained, equipped, and deployed by a human spaceflight program to serve as a commander or crew member of a spacecraft. Although generally reserved for professional space travelers, the term is sometimes applied to anyone who travels into space, including scientists, politicians, journalists, and space tourists. In the United States, it is a designated term used by three agencies: NASA, the FAA, and the military.

03-22.title: Space Shuttle Atlantis
03-22.subtitle: NASA's Space Shuttle Atlantis is launched on its 16th mission, STS-76.
03-22.text: Space Shuttle Atlantis is a retired Space Shuttle orbiter vehicle which belongs to NASA, the spaceflight and space exploration agency of the United States. Atlantis was manufactured by the Rockwell International company in Southern California and was delivered to the Kennedy Space Center in Eastern Florida in April 1985. Atlantis is the fourth operational and the second-to-last Space Shuttle built. Its maiden flight was STS-51-J made from October 3 to 7, 1985.

03-22.title: Arturs Silovs Was Born
03-22.subtitle: Latvia's ice hockey talent begins to develop
03-22.text: Arturs Silovs was born in Latvia, a small Baltic nation with an outsized passion for ice hockey. The young goaltender would develop his skills through the Latvian and North American hockey systems, eventually earning a roster spot in the NHL. His journey from a hockey-mad nation of fewer than two million people to the world's premier professional league embodies the global reach and competitive depth of modern ice hockey.

03-22.title: Imam
03-22.subtitle: Palestinian imam Sheikh Ahmed Yassin, a founder and the spiritual leader of Hamas, was killed by an Israeli missile as he left early morning prayers.
03-22.text: Palestinian imam Sheikh Ahmed Yassin, a founder and the spiritual leader of Hamas, was killed by an Israeli missile as he left early morning prayers.

03-22.title: 2014 Oso landslide
03-22.subtitle: A massive landslide in Oso, Washington, killed 43 people after engulfing a rural neighborhood, the largest death toll for a standalone landslide in U.
03-22.text: A massive landslide in Oso, Washington, killed 43 people after engulfing a rural neighborhood, the largest death toll for a standalone landslide in U.S. history.

03-23.title: Battle of Uhud
03-23.subtitle: The Prophet's army faces a bitter setback
03-23.text: At the foot of Mount Uhud near Medina, the Muslim army under the Prophet Muhammad suffered a painful defeat at the hands of the Quraysh of Mecca. The battle, which saw Muhammad himself wounded, was a sobering reversal after the Muslim victory at Badr the previous year. Uhud taught the early Muslim community lessons about discipline and faith in adversity that became foundational to Islamic tradition and military thought.

03-23.title: Erich Fromm Was Born
03-23.subtitle: A psychologist who probed humanity's deepest fears
03-23.text: Erich Fromm was born in Frankfurt, Germany, a future psychologist and social philosopher whose writings would challenge how humanity understood freedom, love, and alienation. Fleeing Nazi Germany, Fromm settled in America and produced works like Escape from Freedom and The Art of Loving that blended psychoanalysis with social criticism. His insights into authoritarianism and human nature remain disturbingly relevant in every era.

03-23.title: Pakistan
03-23.subtitle: Pakistan becomes the first Islamic republic in the world.
03-23.text: Pakistan becomes the first Islamic republic in the world. This date is now celebrated as Republic Day in Pakistan.

03-23.title: NASA
03-23.subtitle: NASA launches Gemini 3, the United States' first two-man space flight (crew: Gus Grissom and John Young).
03-23.text: The National Aeronautics and Space Administration is an independent agency of the U.S. federal government responsible for the United States' civil space program and for research in aeronautics and space exploration. Headquartered in Washington, D.C., NASA operates ten field centers across the US and is organized into mission directorates for Science, Space Operations, Exploration Systems Development, Space Technology, Aeronautics Research, and Mission Support.

03-23.title: David Frost's interviews with Richard Nixon
03-23.subtitle: The first of The Nixon Interviews (12 will be recorded over four weeks) is videotaped with British journalist David Fros...
03-23.text: The first of The Nixon Interviews (12 will be recorded over four weeks) is videotaped with British journalist David Frost interviewing former United States President Richard Nixon about the Watergate scandal and the Nixon tapes.

03-23.title: United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon
03-23.subtitle: The first UNIFIL troops arrived in Lebanon for peacekeeping mission along the Blue Line.
03-23.text: The first UNIFIL troops arrived in Lebanon for peacekeeping mission along the Blue Line.

03-23.title: Revolutionary United Front
03-23.subtitle: The Revolutionary United Front, with support from the special forces of Charles Taylor's National Patriotic Front of Lib...
03-23.text: The Revolutionary United Front, with support from the special forces of Charles Taylor's National Patriotic Front of Liberia, invades Sierra Leone in an attempt to overthrow Joseph Saidu Momoh, sparking the 11-year Sierra Leone Civil War.

03-23.title: Aeroflot Flight 593
03-23.subtitle: Aeroflot Flight 593 crashed into a hillside in Russia's Kemerovo Oblast, killing all 75 people on board, after the pilot...
03-23.text: Aeroflot Flight 593 was a scheduled international passenger flight from Sheremetyevo International Airport, Moscow, Russia, to Kai Tak Airport in Hong Kong. On 23 March 1994, the aircraft operating the route, an Airbus A310-304 flown by Aeroflot, crashed into the Kuznetsk Alatau mountain range in Kemerovo Oblast, killing all 63 passengers and 12 crew members on board.

03-23.title: Lee Teng-hui
03-23.subtitle: Lee Teng-hui (pictured) was elected President of the Republic of China in the first direct presidential election in Taiwan.
03-23.text: Lee Teng-hui was a Taiwanese statesman, economist, and agronomist who served as the president of the Republic of China and chairman of the Kuomintang from 1988 to 2000. He was the first president to be born in Taiwan, the last to be indirectly elected, and the first to be directly elected.
03-23.location: President of the Republic of China from 1988 to 2000

03-23.title: Mir Space Station Falls
03-23.subtitle: A Soviet icon meets its fiery end
03-23.text: The Russian space station Mir, which had orbited the Earth for fifteen years and hosted astronauts from twelve nations, was deliberately de-orbited and fell into the southern Pacific Ocean. As chunks of the 130-ton station blazed through the atmosphere, an era of space exploration came to a spectacular close. Mir's legacy of scientific achievement and international cooperation paved the way for the International Space Station that succeeded it.

03-23.title: Texas City refinery explosion
03-23.subtitle: A fire and explosion at an oil refinery in Texas City, the third-largest in the United States, killed 15 workers.
03-23.text: A fire and explosion at an oil refinery in Texas City, the third-largest in the United States, killed 15 workers.

03-24.title: Yue Fei Was Born
03-24.subtitle: China's eternal symbol of patriotic loyalty
03-24.text: Yue Fei was born in Tangyin, China, destined to become the most celebrated military general in Chinese history and the supreme symbol of patriotic loyalty. His campaigns against the Jurchen Jin dynasty during the Southern Song period were marked by brilliant tactics and unwavering devotion to his country. Betrayed by a corrupt official and executed at the age of thirty-nine, Yue Fei became a national hero whose story has inspired the Chinese people for nearly a millennium.

03-24.title: New York Breaks Ground on Subway
03-24.subtitle: A city starts digging its underground future
03-24.text: Mayor Robert Anderson Van Wyck broke ground for New York City's new underground Rapid Transit Railroad, the system that would become the iconic New York City Subway. The ambitious project aimed to move millions of passengers beneath the congested streets of Manhattan. The subway's construction transformed New York into a truly modern metropolis, enabling the city's explosive growth and establishing the template for urban mass transit systems worldwide.

03-24.title: Royal assent
03-24.subtitle: Royal assent was given to Prince Edward Island's Provincial Flag Act, which outlined the design of its provincial flag (pictured).
03-24.text: Royal assent was given to Prince Edward Island's Provincial Flag Act, which outlined the design of its provincial flag (pictured).

03-24.title: Argentina
03-24.subtitle: In Argentina, the armed forces overthrow the constitutional government of President Isabel Perón and start a seven-year ...
03-24.text: In Argentina, the armed forces overthrow the constitutional government of President Isabel Perón and start a seven-year dictatorial period self-styled the National Reorganization Process.

03-24.title: Revolutionary Government Junta of El Salvador
03-24.subtitle: One day after making a plea to Salvadoran soldiers to stop carrying out the government's repression, Archbishop Óscar Ro...
03-24.text: One day after making a plea to Salvadoran soldiers to stop carrying out the government's repression, Archbishop Óscar Romero was assassinated while celebrating Mass in San Salvador.

03-24.title: Abdus Sattar
03-24.subtitle: Bangladeshi President Abdus Sattar is deposed in a bloodless coup led by Army Chief Lieutenant general Hussain Muhammad ...
03-24.text: Bangladeshi President Abdus Sattar is deposed in a bloodless coup led by Army Chief Lieutenant general Hussain Muhammad Ershad, who suspends the Constitution and imposes martial law.

03-24.title: Kosovo War
03-24.subtitle: Kosovo War: NATO began attacks on Yugoslavia without United Nations Security Council (UNSC) approval, marking the first ...
03-24.text: Kosovo War: NATO began attacks on Yugoslavia without United Nations Security Council (UNSC) approval, marking the first time NATO has attacked a sovereign country.

03-24.title: Clara Burel Was Born
03-24.subtitle: France's tennis future continues to bloom
03-24.text: Clara Burel was born in France, part of a new wave of French tennis talent. The young player would develop her game through the French tennis federation's elite training programs before competing on the WTA Tour. Her journey from junior circuits to the professional game reflects France's enduring commitment to producing competitive tennis players who can challenge the world's best on any surface.

03-24.title: Hannah Montana
03-24.subtitle: Hannah Montana, starring Miley Cyrus as an actress whose alter ego is the titular character, premiered.
03-24.text: Hannah Montana is an American teen sitcom created by Michael Poryes, Rich Correll, and Barry O'Brien that aired on Disney Channel for four seasons between March 2006 and January 2011. The series centers on Miley Stewart, a teenage girl living a double life as famous pop singer Hannah Montana, an alter ego she adopted so she could maintain her anonymity and live a normal life as a typical teenager.

03-24.title: Bhutan
03-24.subtitle: Bhutan officially becomes a democracy, with its first ever general election.
03-24.text: Bhutan officially becomes a democracy, with its first ever general election.

03-24.title: Germanwings Flight 9525
03-24.subtitle: The co-pilot of Germanwings Flight 9525 deliberately crashed the aircraft in a mass murder–suicide in the French Alps, k...
03-24.text: The co-pilot of Germanwings Flight 9525 deliberately crashed the aircraft in a mass murder–suicide in the French Alps, killing all 150 people on board.

03-25.title: Southern Yan Dynasty Falls
03-25.subtitle: A rogue kingdom crumbles before Jin forces
03-25.text: The capital of Guanggu fell to the Jin dynasty general Liu Yu, bringing the Southern Yan dynasty to a decisive end. The Southern Yan, a Xianbei-led state in northern China, had survived only a few tumultuous years before being overwhelmed by Liu Yu's military genius. The conquest consolidated Jin control over a wider swath of China and demonstrated Liu Yu's growing power, foreshadowing his eventual founding of the Liu Song dynasty.

03-25.title: Ed Begley Was Born
03-25.subtitle: An Oscar-winning character actor arrives
03-25.text: Ed Begley was born in Hartford, Connecticut, a future actor whose powerful performances would earn him an Academy Award. Best known for his Oscar-winning role in Sweet Bird of Youth, Begley brought a blue-collar intensity and emotional honesty to every part. His career, spanning theater, film, and early television, helped define the golden age of American character acting, proving that stars need not have leading-man looks to command the screen.

03-25.title: Mehmed Reshid
03-25.subtitle: The appointment of Mehmed Reshid as governor of Diyarbekir, Turkey intensified the genocide of Syriac Christians by pro-Ottoman forces.
03-25.text: The appointment of Mehmed Reshid as governor of Diyarbekir, Turkey intensified the genocide of Syriac Christians by pro-Ottoman forces.

03-25.title: Enrico Fermi
03-25.subtitle: Enrico Fermi (pictured) published his discovery of neutron-induced radioactivity, for which he was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics.
03-25.text: Enrico Fermi (pictured) published his discovery of neutron-induced radioactivity, for which he was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics.

03-25.title: Tinker Air Force Base
03-25.subtitle: Meteorologists at Tinker Air Force Base in Oklahoma City, United States, issued the world's first tornado forecast after...
03-25.text: Meteorologists at Tinker Air Force Base in Oklahoma City, United States, issued the world's first tornado forecast after noticing conditions similar to another tornado that had struck five days earlier.

03-25.title: Operation Priboi
03-25.subtitle: The Soviet Union began mass deportations of more than 90,000 "undesirable" people from the Baltic states to Siberia.
03-25.text: Operation Priboi was the code name for the biggest Stalin-era Soviet mass deportation from the Baltic states on 25–28 March 1949. Also known as the March deportation. More than 90,000 Estonians, Latvians and Lithuanians, labeled as "enemies of the state", were deported to forced settlements in inhospitable Siberian areas of the Soviet Union. Over 70% of the deportees were either women, or children under the age of 16.
03-25.location: Code name for mass deportations by the Soviet Union in 1949

03-25.title: European Economic Community
03-25.subtitle: The European Economic Community is established with West Germany, France, Italy, Belgium, Netherlands and Luxembourg as the first members.
03-25.text: The European Economic Community is established with West Germany, France, Italy, Belgium, Netherlands and Luxembourg as the first members.

03-25.title: Civil rights movement
03-25.subtitle: Civil rights activists led by Martin Luther King Jr.
03-25.text: Civil rights activists led by Martin Luther King Jr. successfully complete their 4-day 50-mile (80 km) march from Selma to the capitol in Montgomery, Alabama.

03-25.title: Army of the Republic of Vietnam
03-25.subtitle: The Army of the Republic of Vietnam abandon an attempt to cut off the Ho Chi Minh trail in Laos.
03-25.text: The Army of the Republic of Vietnam abandon an attempt to cut off the Ho Chi Minh trail in Laos.

03-25.title: Faisal of Saudi Arabia
03-25.subtitle: King Faisal of Saudi Arabia was shot and killed by his nephew Faisal bin Musaid.
03-25.text: Faisal bin Abdulaziz Al Saud was King of Saudi Arabia from 1964 until his assassination in 1975. Before his ascension, he served as Crown Prince of Saudi Arabia from 1953 to 1964, and he was briefly regent to his half-brother King Saud in 1964. He was prime minister from 1954 to 1960 and from 1962 to 1975. Faisal was the third son of King Abdulaziz, the founder of modern Saudi Arabia.
03-25.location: King of Saudi Arabia from 1964 to 1975

03-25.title: Space Shuttle
03-25.subtitle: The first fully functional Space Shuttle orbiter, Columbia, is delivered to the John F.
03-25.text: The first fully functional Space Shuttle orbiter, Columbia, is delivered to the John F. Kennedy Space Center to be prepared for its first launch.

03-25.title: WikiWikiWeb
03-25.subtitle: WikiWikiWeb, the world's first wiki, and part of the Portland Pattern Repository, is made public by Ward Cunningham.
03-25.text: WikiWikiWeb, the world's first wiki, and part of the Portland Pattern Repository, is made public by Ward Cunningham.

03-25.title: Ozan Kabak Was Born
03-25.subtitle: Turkey's defensive prodigy takes first breath
03-25.text: Ozan Kabak was born in Turkey, a future centre-back whose precocious talent would bring him to the attention of Europe's top clubs at a remarkably young age. Moving from the Turkish Superlig to the Bundesliga and eventually the Premier League on loan, Kabak's rapid rise reflected both his own ability and the growing global reach of Turkish football's talent development system.

03-26.title: Theodosius Named Co-Emperor
03-26.subtitle: A father secures his dynasty's future
03-26.text: Byzantine Emperor Maurice proclaimed his young son Theodosius as co-emperor, a time-honored strategy for securing dynastic succession in the volatile world of imperial politics. The move was designed to ensure a smooth transition of power, but Maurice's reign would end in tragedy when he and his sons were overthrown and executed by the usurper Phocas. The fate of Maurice's dynasty became one of the most poignant tales of Byzantine political violence.

03-26.title: Joseph Campbell Was Born
03-26.subtitle: The man who found the hero in every myth
03-26.text: Joseph Campbell was born in White Plains, New York, a future mythologist and author whose work would transform how humanity understands its own storytelling traditions. His concept of the Hero's Journey, articulated in The Hero with a Thousand Faces, influenced filmmakers from George Lucas to countless screenwriters. Campbell's insight that all myths share a common structure gave the world a new lens through which to understand art, religion, and the human experience.
03-26.location: White Plains, New York, United States

03-26.title: Jonas Salk
03-26.subtitle: Jonas Salk announced the successful test of his polio vaccine on a small group of adults and children.
03-26.text: Jonas Salk announced the successful test of his polio vaccine on a small group of adults and children.

03-26.title: Nuclear weapons testing
03-26.subtitle: Nuclear weapons testing: The Romeo shot of Operation Castle is detonated at Bikini Atoll.
03-26.text: Nuclear weapons tests are experiments carried out to determine the performance of nuclear weapons and the effects of their explosion. Over 2,000 nuclear weapons tests have been carried out since 1945. Nuclear testing is a sensitive political issue. Governments have often performed tests to signal strength. Because of their destruction and fallout, testing has seen opposition by civilians as well as governments, with international bans having been agreed on.
03-26.location: Controlled detonation of nuclear weapons for scientific or political purposes

03-26.title: East Pakistan
03-26.subtitle: East Pakistan, then province of Pakistan, declares its independence from Pakistan to form Bangladesh; the Bangladesh's War of Independence begins.
03-26.text: East Pakistan, then province of Pakistan, declares its independence from Pakistan to form Bangladesh; the Bangladesh's War of Independence begins.

03-26.title: Anwar Sadat
03-26.subtitle: Anwar al-Sadat, Menachem Begin and Jimmy Carter sign the Egypt–Israel peace treaty in Washington, D.
03-26.text: Anwar al-Sadat, Menachem Begin and Jimmy Carter sign the Egypt–Israel peace treaty in Washington, D.C.

03-26.title: Argentina
03-26.subtitle: Argentina, Brazil, Uruguay and Paraguay sign the Treaty of Asunción, establishing Mercosur, the South Common Market.
03-26.text: Argentina, Brazil, Uruguay and Paraguay sign the Treaty of Asunción, establishing Mercosur, the South Common Market.

03-26.title: Rancho Santa Fe, California
03-26.subtitle: Police in Rancho Santa Fe, California, discovered the bodies of 39 members of the religious group Heaven's Gate who had ...
03-26.text: Police in Rancho Santa Fe, California, discovered the bodies of 39 members of the religious group Heaven's Gate who had died in an apparent cult suicide.

03-26.title: Jack Kevorkian
03-26.subtitle: A jury began deliberations in the trial of Jack Kevorkian, an American practitioner of physician-assisted suicide who wa...
03-26.text: Murad Jacob Kevorkian was an American pathologist and euthanasia proponent. He publicly championed a terminal patient's right to die by physician-assisted suicide, embodied in his quote, "Dying is not a crime". Kevorkian said that he assisted at least 130 patients to that end. He was convicted of murder in 1999 and was often portrayed in the media with the name of "Dr. Death".

03-26.title: Gefen Primo Was Born
03-26.subtitle: An Israeli judoka enters the world
03-26.text: Gefen Primo was born in Israel, a nation with a strong tradition in competitive judo. The young athlete would develop through Israel's judo training programs, competing on the international circuit. Her dedication to the martial art reflects Israel's investment in Olympic sports and the country's ability to produce world-class athletes despite its small population and unique security challenges.

03-26.title: Tesla Model S
03-26.subtitle: A prototype of the Tesla Model S, one of the most influential electric cars in history, was unveiled.
03-26.text: A prototype of the Tesla Model S, one of the most influential electric cars in history, was unveiled.

03-26.title: Torpedo
03-26.subtitle: An explosion, allegedly caused by a North Korean torpedo, sank the South Korean warship ROKS Cheonan near Baengnyeongdo ...
03-26.text: A torpedo is an underwater ranged weapon launched above or below the water surface, self-propelled towards a target, with an explosive warhead designed to detonate either on contact with or in proximity to the target. Historically, such a device was called an automotive, automobile, locomotive, or fish torpedo; colloquially, a fish.

03-26.title: Francis Scott Key Bridge
03-26.subtitle: The Francis Scott Key Bridge collapses following a collision between the MV Dali container ship and one of the bridge's ...
03-26.text: The Francis Scott Key Bridge collapses following a collision between the MV Dali container ship and one of the bridge's support pillars, killing 6 people.

03-27.title: Pope Excommunicates Venice
03-27.subtitle: A pontiff wages economic war on a maritime republic
03-27.text: Pope Clement V imposed excommunication and interdiction upon the Republic of Venice, forbidding all commercial intercourse with the powerful maritime state. The papal sanctions were a dramatic exercise of spiritual authority against one of Europe's wealthiest and most independent cities. Venice's merchants, accustomed to trading freely across the Mediterranean, found themselves cut off by the pope's decree, demonstrating the immense economic power the papacy could still wield.

03-27.title: Aguinaldo Captured
03-27.subtitle: America catches the Philippines' rebel president
03-27.text: Emilio Aguinaldo, the leader of the First Philippine Republic and symbol of Filipino resistance to American colonization, was captured by American forces. His arrest came after years of guerrilla warfare against U.S. troops following the Philippine-American War. Aguinaldo's capture effectively ended organized Filipino resistance, though it could not extinguish the desire for independence that would eventually be realized decades later.

03-27.title: 1964 Alaska earthquake
03-27.subtitle: The Good Friday earthquake, the most powerful earthquake recorded in North American history at a magnitude of 9.
03-27.text: The Good Friday earthquake, the most powerful earthquake recorded in North American history at a magnitude of 9.2 strikes Southcentral Alaska, killing 125 people and inflicting massive damage to the city of Anchorage.

03-27.title: Nelson Bunker Hunt
03-27.subtitle: Brothers Nelson Bunker Hunt and William Herbert Hunt failed in their attempt to corner the world silver market, causing ...
03-27.text: Brothers Nelson Bunker Hunt and William Herbert Hunt failed in their attempt to corner the world silver market, causing panic in commodity and futures exchanges.

03-27.title: Christian Democracy
03-27.subtitle: Italian former minister and Christian Democracy leader Giulio Andreotti is accused of mafia allegiance by the tribunal of Palermo.
03-27.text: Italian former minister and Christian Democracy leader Giulio Andreotti is accused of mafia allegiance by the tribunal of Palermo.

03-27.title: Food and Drug Administration
03-27.subtitle: The Food and Drug Administration approved the drug sildenafil (chemical structure pictured), better known by the trade n...
03-27.text: The Food and Drug Administration approved the drug sildenafil (chemical structure pictured), better known by the trade name Viagra, as the first treatment approved in the United States for erectile dysfunction.

03-27.title: Kosovo War
03-27.subtitle: Kosovo War: An American Lockheed F-117A Nighthawk is shot down by a Yugoslav Army SAM, the first and only Nighthawk to be lost in combat.
03-27.text: Kosovo War: An American Lockheed F-117A Nighthawk is shot down by a Yugoslav Army SAM, the first and only Nighthawk to be lost in combat.

03-27.title: Phillips Plant Explosion
03-27.subtitle: A chemical plant erupts in Texas
03-27.text: A massive explosion rocked a Phillips Petroleum plant in Pasadena, Texas, killing one person and injuring seventy-one others. The blast, which sent a fireball into the sky visible for miles, exposed the dangers inherent in the petrochemical industry concentrated along the Texas Gulf Coast. The incident prompted investigations into plant safety procedures and renewed calls for stricter regulation of the chemical manufacturing industry.

03-27.title: Passover massacre
03-27.subtitle: Passover massacre: A Palestinian suicide bomber kills 29 people at a Passover seder in Netanya, Israel.
03-27.text: Passover massacre: A Palestinian suicide bomber kills 29 people at a Passover seder in Netanya, Israel.

03-27.title: Situ Gintung
03-27.subtitle: A failure of the dam holding Situ Gintung, an artificial lake in Tangerang, Indonesia, caused floods that killed at least 100 people.
03-27.text: A failure of the dam holding Situ Gintung, an artificial lake in Tangerang, Indonesia, caused floods that killed at least 100 people.

03-27.title: Government of the Philippines
03-27.subtitle: Philippines signs a peace accord with the largest Muslim rebel group, the Moro Islamic Liberation Front, ending decades of conflict.
03-27.text: Philippines signs a peace accord with the largest Muslim rebel group, the Moro Islamic Liberation Front, ending decades of conflict.

03-28.title: Caligula Accepts the Principate
03-28.subtitle: A young emperor embraces his absolute power
03-28.text: The Roman Senate bestowed upon Emperor Caligula the full titles and powers of the Principate, formalizing his authority over the Roman world. The young emperor, grandson of the beloved Germanicus, was greeted with popular enthusiasm and high hopes. No one could yet foresee the cruelty and excess that would characterize his brief reign, making the joyful ceremony an ironic prelude to one of Rome's most turbulent imperial chapters.

03-28.title: Flora Robson Was Born
03-28.subtitle: A dame of British stage and screen
03-28.text: Flora Robson was born in South Shields, England, a future actress whose commanding presence and versatility would earn her a place among the greatest performers in British theatrical history. She was made a Dame Commander of the Order of the British Empire for her contributions to drama. From Shakespearean stages to Hollywood films, Robson brought intelligence, power, and subtlety to every role she inhabited across a career spanning five decades.

03-28.title: Civil rights movement
03-28.subtitle: Civil rights movement: Over one hundred high school students conducted a sit-in protest in Rome, Georgia.
03-28.text: The civil rights movement was a social movement in the United States from 1954 to 1968 which aimed to abolish legalized racial segregation, discrimination, and disenfranchisement in the country, which most commonly affected African Americans. The movement had origins in the Reconstruction era in the late 19th century, and modern roots in the 1940s.

03-28.title: Nobel Prize in Literature
03-28.subtitle: Greek poet and Nobel Prize laureate Giorgos Seferis makes a famous statement on the BBC World Service opposing the junta in Greece.
03-28.text: The Nobel Prize in Literature, here meaning for Literature, is a Swedish literature prize that is awarded annually to an author from any country who has, in the words of Alfred Nobel, "in the field of literature, produced the most outstanding work in an idealistic direction". Though individual works are sometimes cited as being particularly noteworthy, the award is based on an author's body of work as a whole. The Swedish Academy decides who, if anyone, will receive the prize.
03-28.location: Prize established in 1895 by Alfred Nobel

03-28.title: Three Mile Island Nuclear Generating Station
03-28.subtitle: A coolant leak at the Three Mile Island's Unit 2 nuclear reactor outside Harrisburg, Pennsylvania leads to the core over...
03-28.text: A coolant leak at the Three Mile Island's Unit 2 nuclear reactor outside Harrisburg, Pennsylvania leads to the core overheating and a partial meltdown.

03-28.title: African National Congress
03-28.subtitle: In South Africa, African National Congress security guards kill dozens of Inkatha Freedom Party protesters.
03-28.text: The African National Congress (ANC) is a political party in South Africa. It originated as a liberation movement known for its opposition to apartheid and has governed the country since 1994, when the first post-apartheid election resulted in Nelson Mandela being elected as President of South Africa. Cyril Ramaphosa, the incumbent national president, has served as president of the ANC since 18 December 2017.

03-28.title: Tragedy of Otranto
03-28.subtitle: In the Tragedy of Otranto, the Italian Navy vessel Sibilla collided with the Albanian civilian vessel Kateri i Radës, ca...
03-28.text: In the Tragedy of Otranto, the Italian Navy vessel Sibilla collided with the Albanian civilian vessel Kateri i Radës, causing the latter to sink and leading to dozens of deaths.

03-28.title: Kosovo War
03-28.subtitle: Kosovo War: Serbian police and special forces killed around 93 Kosovo Albanians in the village of Izbica.
03-28.text: Kosovo War: Serbian police and special forces killed around 93 Kosovo Albanians in the village of Izbica.

03-28.title: Athens Airport Opens
03-28.subtitle: Greece gets a modern gateway to the world
03-28.text: Athens International Airport Eleftherios Venizelos began operations, replacing the aging Hellinikon Airport as Greece's primary international gateway. Named after the visionary Greek statesman, the new airport was designed to handle the growing demands of modern air travel and serve as a hub connecting Europe, the Middle East, and Africa. Its opening symbolized Greece's commitment to modernization as the country prepared to host the 2004 Olympic Games.

03-28.title: Makka al-Mukarama hotel attack
03-28.subtitle: A siege of a hotel in Mogadishu by al-Shabaab militants, which began the previous day and killed at least 20 people, end...
03-28.text: A siege of a hotel in Mogadishu by al-Shabaab militants, which began the previous day and killed at least 20 people, ended with the Somali Armed Forces recapturing the premises.

03-28.title: 2025 Myanmar earthquake
03-28.subtitle: An earthquake strikes close to Mandalay, Myanmar with a magnitude of 7.
03-28.text: An earthquake strikes close to Mandalay, Myanmar with a magnitude of 7.7, killing over 100 people.

03-29.title: Vikings Plunder Paris
03-29.subtitle: Norse raiders conquer the City of Light
03-29.text: A fleet of Viking longships sailed up the Seine and fell upon Paris, possibly led by the legendary Ragnar Lodbrok. The Norse raiders plundered and occupied the city, exacting a massive ransom in silver from the terrified Frankish king Charles the Bald. The sack of Paris demonstrated the devastating reach of Viking expansion and the vulnerability of even Europe's greatest cities to these fearsome warriors from the north.

03-29.title: John McEwen Was Born
03-29.subtitle: Australia's farmer prime minister arrives
03-29.text: John McEwen was born in rural Victoria, Australia, a future farmer and politician who would serve as the eighteenth Prime Minister of Australia. His deep understanding of agricultural issues and trade policy made him a powerful voice for rural Australia in Canberra. McEwen's brief tenure as prime minister and his long career in the Country Party shaped Australian agricultural and trade policy for a generation.

03-29.title: Arturo Frondizi
03-29.subtitle: Arturo Frondizi, the president of Argentina, is overthrown in a military coup by Argentina's armed forces, ending an 11+1⁄2 day constitutional crisis.
03-29.text: Arturo Frondizi, the president of Argentina, is overthrown in a military coup by Argentina's armed forces, ending an 11+1⁄2 day constitutional crisis.

03-29.title: NASA
03-29.subtitle: NASA's Mariner 10 becomes the first space probe to fly by Mercury.
03-29.text: The National Aeronautics and Space Administration is an independent agency of the U.S. federal government responsible for the United States' civil space program and for research in aeronautics and space exploration. Headquartered in Washington, D.C., NASA operates ten field centers across the US and is organized into mission directorates for Science, Space Operations, Exploration Systems Development, Space Technology, Aeronautics Research, and Mission Support.

03-29.title: Jeff Beck
03-29.subtitle: Jeff Beck released Blow by Blow, his most successful album in the U.
03-29.text: Geoffrey Arnold Beck was an English guitarist. He rose to prominence as a member of the rock band the Yardbirds, and afterwards founded and fronted the Jeff Beck Group and Beck, Bogert & Appice. In 1975, he switched to an instrumental style with focus on an innovative sound, and his releases spanned genres and styles ranging from blues rock, hard rock, jazz fusion and a blend of guitar-rock and electronica.

03-29.title: Dick Beardsley
03-29.subtitle: Dick Beardsley and Inge Simonsen jointly won the Men's Elite Race and Joyce Smith won the Women's Elite Race in the inaugural London Marathon.
03-29.text: Dick Beardsley and Inge Simonsen jointly won the Men's Elite Race and Joyce Smith won the Women's Elite Race in the inaugural London Marathon.

03-29.title: Elizabeth II
03-29.subtitle: Queen Elizabeth II granted royal assent to the Canada Act 1982, which ended any remaining constitutional dependence of C...
03-29.text: Elizabeth II was Queen of the United Kingdom and other Commonwealth realms from 6 February 1952 until her death in 2022. She was queen regnant of 32 sovereign states during her lifetime and was the monarch of 15 realms at her death. Her reign of 70 years and 214 days is the longest of any British monarch, the second-longest of any sovereign state, and the longest of any queen regnant in history.
03-29.location: Queen of the United Kingdom from 1952 to 2022

03-29.title: Aspen Plane Crash
03-29.subtitle: A charter jet crashes in the Colorado mountains
03-29.text: A Gulfstream III corporate jet crashed on approach to Aspen/Pitkin County Airport in Colorado, killing all eighteen people on board. The aircraft, attempting to land at one of America's most challenging mountain airports, went down in terrain that allows no margin for error. The tragedy underscored the unique dangers of flying into mountain airports and prompted renewed scrutiny of approach procedures at high-altitude airfields.

03-29.title: Passover massacre
03-29.subtitle: In reaction to the Passover massacre two days prior, Israel launches Operation Defensive Shield against Palestinian mili...
03-29.text: In reaction to the Passover massacre two days prior, Israel launches Operation Defensive Shield against Palestinian militants, its largest military operation in the West Bank since the 1967 Six-Day War.

03-29.title: Bulgaria
03-29.subtitle: Bulgaria, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Romania, Slovakia, and Slovenia join NATO as full members.
03-29.text: Bulgaria, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Romania, Slovakia, and Slovenia join NATO as full members.

03-29.title: Caucasus Emirate
03-29.subtitle: Islamist separatists of the Caucasus Emirate detonated two bombs on the Moscow Metro, killing 40 people and injuring 102 others.
03-29.text: Islamist separatists of the Caucasus Emirate detonated two bombs on the Moscow Metro, killing 40 people and injuring 102 others.

03-29.title: Prime Minister of the United Kingdom
03-29.subtitle: Prime Minister Theresa May invokes Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union, formally beginning the United Kingdom's w...
03-29.text: The prime minister of the United Kingdom is the head of government of the United Kingdom. The prime minister advises the sovereign on the exercise of much of the royal prerogative, chairs the Cabinet, and selects its ministers. Modern prime ministers hold office by virtue of their ability to command the confidence of the House of Commons, so they are invariably members of Parliament.
03-29.location: Head of government of the United Kingdom

03-30.title: Avars Retreat from Tomis
03-30.subtitle: A Byzantine fortress withstands the siege
03-30.text: The Avar siege of the Byzantine stronghold of Tomis was lifted as their leader Bayan I retreated northward across the Danube. The fortress, perched on the shores of the Black Sea in modern-day Romania, had withstood the Avar assault, demonstrating the resilience of Byzantine frontier defenses. The Avar retreat offered Constantinople a brief reprieve from the relentless pressure of barbarian incursions along its Danubian border.

03-30.title: Linear A Discovered
03-30.subtitle: Archaeologists unearth a mysterious ancient script
03-30.text: Archaeologists working at Knossos on the island of Crete discovered the first clay tablets inscribed with hieroglyphic writing in a script that would later be called Linear A. The discovery opened a window into the mysterious Minoan civilization, the first advanced society in Europe. Linear A remains undeciphered to this day, a tantalizing puzzle that guards the secrets of a people who built palaces and commanded the seas four thousand years ago.

03-30.title: Cold War
03-30.subtitle: Cold War: A riot breaks out in Austurvöllur square in Reykjavík, when Iceland joins NATO.
03-30.text: The Cold War was a period of international geopolitical rivalry between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) and their respective allies, the capitalist Western Bloc and communist Eastern Bloc, which began in the aftermath of the Second World War and ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
03-30.location: 1947–1991 geopolitical rivalry between US and USSR

03-30.title: Vietnam War
03-30.subtitle: Vietnam War: North Vietnamese forces began the Easter Offensive in an attempt to gain as much territory and destroy as m...
03-30.text: The Vietnam War was an armed conflict in Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia fought between North Vietnam and South Vietnam and their allies. North Vietnam was supported by the Soviet Union and China, while South Vietnam was supported by the United States and other anti-communist nations. The conflict was the second of the Indochina wars and a proxy war of the Cold War between the Soviet Union and US.
03-30.location: War in Southeast Asia from 1955 to 1975

03-30.title: Annie Hall
03-30.subtitle: Annie Hall had its first screening at the LA Film Festival; it was voted the funniest screenplay ever by members of the Writers Guild of America.
03-30.text: Annie Hall had its first screening at the LA Film Festival; it was voted the funniest screenplay ever by members of the Writers Guild of America.

03-30.title: Airey Neave
03-30.subtitle: Airey Neave, a British Member of Parliament (MP), is killed by a car bomb as he exits the Palace of Westminster.
03-30.text: Airey Neave, a British Member of Parliament (MP), is killed by a car bomb as he exits the Palace of Westminster. The Irish National Liberation Army claims responsibility.
03-30.location: British politician, military officer and lawyer (1916–1979)

03-30.title: John Hinckley Jr.
03-30.subtitle: John Hinckley Jr.
03-30.text: John Hinckley Jr. shot and wounded U.S. president Ronald Reagan and three others outside the Washington Hilton (immediate aftermath pictured).

03-30.title: Space Shuttle program
03-30.subtitle: Space Shuttle program: STS-3 mission is completed with the landing of Columbia at White Sands Missile Range, New Mexico.
03-30.text: The Space Shuttle program was the fourth human spaceflight program carried out by the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), which accomplished routine transportation for Earth-to-orbit crew and cargo from 1981 to 2011. Its official program name was Space Transportation System (STS), taken from a 1969 plan for a system of reusable spacecraft where it was the only item funded for development, as a proposed nuclear shuttle in the plan was cancelled in 1972.

03-30.title: Colton Herta Was Born
03-30.subtitle: American racing's next generation arrives
03-30.text: Colton Herta was born into an American racing family, the son of former IndyCar driver Bryan Herta. He would become the youngest race winner in IndyCar Series history, demonstrating a speed and racecraft that seemed almost genetic. His rapid ascent through American open-wheel racing positioned him as one of the most exciting young talents in motorsport, carrying the family tradition into a new era of competition.

03-30.title: Queen Elizabeth the Queen Mother
03-30.subtitle: Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother, widow of King George VI and mother of Queen Elizabeth II, died at Royal Lodge aged 101.
03-30.text: Elizabeth Angela Marguerite Bowes-Lyon was Queen of the United Kingdom and the Dominions of the British Commonwealth from 11 December 1936 to 6 February 1952 as the wife of King George VI. She was also the last Empress of India from 1936 until the British Raj was dissolved on 15 August 1947. After her husband died, she was officially known as Queen Elizabeth the Queen Mother to avoid confusion with her daughter Queen Elizabeth II.
03-30.location: Queen of the United Kingdom from 1936 to 1952

03-30.title: Lahore
03-30.subtitle: The Manawan Police Academy in Lahore, Pakistan, was attacked and held for several hours by 12 gunmen, resulting in 16 deaths and 95 injuries.
03-30.text: The Manawan Police Academy in Lahore, Pakistan, was attacked and held for several hours by 12 gunmen, resulting in 16 deaths and 95 injuries.

03-30.title: SpaceX
03-30.subtitle: SpaceX conducts the world's first reflight of an orbital class rocket.
03-30.text: SpaceX conducts the world's first reflight of an orbital class rocket.

03-30.title: Donald Trump
03-30.subtitle: Donald Trump becomes the first former United States president to be indicted by a grand jury.
03-30.text: Donald John Trump is an American politician, media personality, and businessman who is the 47th president of the United States. A member of the Republican Party, he served as the 45th president from 2017 to 2021.
03-30.location: President of the United States (2017–2021; since 2025)

03-31.title: Constantine Marries Fausta
03-31.subtitle: A political marriage seals an imperial alliance
03-31.text: After divorcing his first wife Minervina, Emperor Constantine married Fausta, the daughter of retired Emperor Maximian. The marriage was a calculated political alliance designed to strengthen Constantine's claim to imperial legitimacy. But the union would end in tragedy: Constantine later ordered Fausta's execution under circumstances that remain debated by historians. Their marriage, born of ambition, became one of Roman history's most mysterious and disturbing domestic tales.

03-31.title: Prince Henry Was Born
03-31.subtitle: A royal duke for the House of Windsor
03-31.text: Prince Henry, Duke of Gloucester, was born as the third son of King George V of the United Kingdom. Though never expected to reign, he served the crown with quiet dedication as a military officer and later as Governor-General of Australia. His life, lived in the shadow of his brothers Edward VIII and George VI, represented the steadfast service and understated duty that characterized the British royal family during the turbulent twentieth century.

03-31.title: Remington Rand
03-31.subtitle: Remington Rand delivers the first UNIVAC I computer to the United States Census Bureau.
03-31.text: Remington Rand delivers the first UNIVAC I computer to the United States Census Bureau.

03-31.title: Olímpio Mourão Filho
03-31.subtitle: Brazilian General Olímpio Mourão Filho orders his troops to move towards Rio de Janeiro, beginning the coup d'état and 2...
03-31.text: Olímpio Mourão Filho was a Brazilian military officer known as the author of the Cohen Plan, a forged document used to justify the Estado Novo coup in 1937, and, as head of the 4th Military Region/Infantry Division, as the precipitator of the 1964 coup d'état that installed the military dictatorship in Brazil. He reached the rank of army general and ended his career presiding over the Superior Military Court (STM) from 1967 to 1969.
03-31.location: 20th-century Brazilian general and Integralist; leader in the 1964 coup

03-31.title: Soviet Union
03-31.subtitle: The Soviet Union launches Luna 10 which later becomes the first space probe to enter orbit around the Moon.
03-31.text: The Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (USSR), also known as the Soviet Union, was a transcontinental country that spanned much of Eurasia from 1922 until its dissolution in 1991. It was the world's third-most populous country, largest by area, and bordered twelve countries. A diverse multinational state, it was organized as a federal union of national republics, the largest and most populous being the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic. In practice, its government and economy were highly centralized.

03-31.title: USS Missouri
03-31.subtitle: USS Missouri, the last active United States Navy battleship, was decommissioned in Long Beach, California.
03-31.text: USS Missouri, the last active United States Navy battleship, was decommissioned in Long Beach, California.

03-31.title: Selena
03-31.subtitle: American singer-songwriter Selena, known as the "queen of Tejano music", was murdered by the president of her fan club, ...
03-31.text: American singer-songwriter Selena, known as the "queen of Tejano music", was murdered by the president of her fan club, Yolanda Saldívar, in Corpus Christi, Texas, deeply affecting the Latino community.

03-31.title: Singapore's Old Library Closes
03-31.subtitle: Protests cannot save a beloved building
03-31.text: The Old National Library Building in Singapore was closed to make way for a road tunnel, despite widespread public protests. The red-brick colonial building had served as the nation's main library for decades and held deep sentimental value for generations of Singaporeans. Its demolition sparked a national conversation about heritage preservation versus development, highlighting the tensions inherent in a rapidly modernizing city-state's relationship with its past.

03-31.title: Dwarf planet
03-31.subtitle: The dwarf planet Makemake is discovered by a team led by astronomer Michael E.
03-31.text: A dwarf planet is a small planetary-mass object that is in direct orbit around the Sun, massive enough to be gravitationally rounded, but insufficient to achieve orbital dominance like the eight classical planets of the Solar System.
