04-17.text: Ustaše-affiliated Croatian separatists attacked the Yugoslav embassy in Stockholm, fatally shooting the ambassador, Vladimir Rolović.

04-17.title: Cambodian Civil War
04-17.subtitle: The Cambodian Civil War ends and the Cambodian Genocide begins.
04-17.text: The Cambodian Civil War ends and the Cambodian Genocide begins. The Khmer Rouge captures the capital Phnom Penh and Cambodian government forces surrender.

04-17.title: Metropolitan Police
04-17.subtitle: Metropolitan Police officer Yvonne Fletcher was shot and killed while on duty during a protest outside the Libyan embass...
04-17.text: The Metropolitan Police Service (MPS), commonly known as the Metropolitan Police, Met Police, or the "Met", is the territorial police force responsible for law enforcement and crime prevention within Greater London. In addition, it is responsible for specialised tasks throughout the United Kingdom, such as dealing with counter-terrorism throughout the UK, and the protection of certain individuals, including the monarch, royal family, governmental officials, and other designated figures.
04-17.location: Territorial police force of Greater London

04-17.title: Three Hundred and Thirty Five Years'
04-17.subtitle: Having supposedly been at war for 335 years without a single shot having been fired and no casualties incurred, the Neth...
04-17.text: Having supposedly been at war for 335 years without a single shot having been fired and no casualties incurred, the Netherlands and the Isles of Scilly declared peace.

04-17.title: Space Shuttle Columbia
04-17.subtitle: Space Shuttle Columbia is launched on STS-90, the final Spacelab mission.
04-17.text: Space Shuttle Columbia (OV-102) was a Space Shuttle orbiter manufactured by Rockwell International and operated by NASA. Named after the first American ship to circumnavigate the globe, and the female personification of the United States, Columbia was the first of five Space Shuttle orbiters to fly in space, debuting the Space Shuttle launch vehicle on its maiden flight on April 12, 1981 and becoming the first spacecraft to be re-used after its first flight when it launched on STS-2 on

04-17.title: Ryujin Was Born
04-17.subtitle: K-pop's fierce new force takes shape
04-17.text: In Seoul, South Korea, Shin Ryujin was born into a culture that was rapidly becoming the epicenter of global pop music. Selected through rigorous training at JYP Entertainment, she emerged as a member of the girl group Itzy in 2019, quickly establishing herself as one of K-pop's most magnetic performers. Her razor-sharp rapping and powerful dance moves helped Itzy become one of the defining acts of fourth-generation K-pop.

04-17.title: NASA
04-17.subtitle: NASA's Kepler space telescope confirms the discovery of the first Earth-size planet in the habitable zone of another star.
04-17.text: The National Aeronautics and Space Administration is an independent agency of the U.S. federal government responsible for the United States' civil space program and for research in aeronautics and space exploration. Headquartered in Washington, D.C., NASA operates ten field centers across the US and is organized into mission directorates for Science, Space Operations, Exploration Systems Development, Space Technology, Aeronautics Research, and Mission Support.

04-18.title: Emperor Gratian Was Born
04-18.subtitle: A boy emperor inherits a crumbling west
04-18.text: In the garrison town of Sirmium, the infant Gratian was born to Emperor Valentinian I. He would be raised to the rank of Augustus as a child of just eight years, inheriting the Western Roman Empire upon his father's sudden death. Gratian proved a capable ruler in his youth, defeating the Alamanni and appointing Theodosius to govern the East. But his favoritism toward Christian orthodoxy and Gothic bodyguards would ultimately turn his own troops against him.

04-18.title: Republic of Venice
04-18.subtitle: Peace of Ferrara between Republic of Venice, Duchy of Milan, Republic of Florence and House of Gonzaga: ending of the se...
04-18.text: Peace of Ferrara between Republic of Venice, Duchy of Milan, Republic of Florence and House of Gonzaga: ending of the second campaign of the Wars in Lombardy fought until the Treaty of Lodi in 1454, which will then guarantee the conditions for the development of the Italian Renaissance.

04-18.title: War of the First Coalition
04-18.subtitle: War of the First Coalition: The Peace of Leoben is signed by Napoleon Bonaparte and Maximilian, Count of Merveldt, creat...
04-18.text: The War of the First Coalition was a set of wars between a coalition of several European powers and France fought between 1792 and 1797. The coalition was only loosely allied and fought without much coordination; each power wanted to annex a different part of France should they defeat the French, something that never occurred.
04-18.location: 1792–1797 battles between French revolutionaries and neighbouring monarchies

04-18.title: Laszlo Nemeth Was Born
04-18.subtitle: A dentist who healed Hungary through words
04-18.text: In Budapest, Laszlo Nemeth was born -- a man who would become one of Hungary's most versatile intellectuals. Trained as a dentist, he abandoned medicine for literature, producing novels, dramas, and essays that grappled with the spiritual crisis of twentieth-century Europe. His works explored the tension between Western modernism and Hungarian tradition, earning him a devoted readership and establishing him as one of the most important Hungarian writers of his generation.

04-18.title: Superman
04-18.subtitle: Superman, created by Jerry Siegel and Joe Shuster of DC Comics, made his debut in Action Comics #1, the first true superhero comic book.
04-18.text: Superman, created by Jerry Siegel and Joe Shuster of DC Comics, made his debut in Action Comics #1, the first true superhero comic book.

04-18.title: Ezra Pound
04-18.subtitle: Controversial American poet Ezra Pound was released from St.
04-18.text: Controversial American poet Ezra Pound was released from St. Elizabeths Hospital, a psychiatric hospital in Washington, D.C., in which he had been incarcerated for twelve years.

04-18.title: Zimbabwe
04-18.subtitle: The Republic of Zimbabwe (formerly Rhodesia) comes into being, with Canaan Banana as the country's first President.
04-18.text: The Republic of Zimbabwe (formerly Rhodesia) comes into being, with Canaan Banana as the country's first President. The Zimbabwean dollar replaces the Rhodesian dollar as the official currency.

04-18.title: Operation Praying Mantis
04-18.subtitle: The United States launches Operation Praying Mantis against Iranian naval forces in the largest naval battle since World War II.
04-18.text: The United States launches Operation Praying Mantis against Iranian naval forces in the largest naval battle since World War II.

04-18.title: Qana massacre
04-18.subtitle: The Israeli military commits the Qana massacre in a deliberate shelling of a United Nations compound near the village of...
04-18.text: The Israeli military commits the Qana massacre in a deliberate shelling of a United Nations compound near the village of Qana in southern Lebanon, killing 106 Lebanese civilians who were taking shelter there and wounding over 100 more.

04-18.title: Molten Steel Disaster
04-18.subtitle: Thirty tonnes of liquid death spill in seconds
04-18.text: In a steel factory in Qinghe District, Liaoning Province, a ladle carrying thirty tonnes of molten steel separated from its overhead rail and spilled its superheated contents onto the factory floor below. Thirty-two workers were killed instantly in a catastrophe that lasted mere seconds. The Qinghe Special Steel Corporation disaster exposed the deadly consequences of inadequate industrial safety standards and became a rallying point for workplace safety reform in China.

04-18.title: United States Department of Justice
04-18.subtitle: The United States Department of Justice released a redacted version of the Mueller report about the investigation of Russian influence on the U.
04-18.text: The United States Department of Justice released a redacted version of the Mueller report about the investigation of Russian influence on the U.S. presidential election to Congress and the public.

04-19.title: Piso's Plot Betrayed
04-19.subtitle: A freedman's whisper dooms Rome's conspirators
04-19.text: In the imperial capital of Rome, the freedman Milichus betrayed the conspiracy of Gaius Calpurnius Piso to assassinate Emperor Nero. The plot, which had drawn in senators, knights, and even members of the Praetorian Guard, collapsed overnight as the conspirators were arrested one by one. Nero's vengeance was swift and terrible -- the philosopher Seneca and the poet Lucan were among those forced to take their own lives in the purge that followed.

04-19.title: Iracema de Alencar Was Born
04-19.subtitle: Brazil's silver screen pioneer enters the world
04-19.text: In Brazil, Iracema de Alencar was born -- a woman who would become one of the country's earliest film stars. Making her debut as the lead in the 1917 silent film 'Iracema,' she captivated audiences with her beauty and dramatic presence. After years on the theater stage, she returned to cinema later in life, appearing in several more films. Her career spanned the entire arc of Brazilian cinema's development, from silent pictures to the modern era.

04-19.title: Sierra Leone
04-19.subtitle: Sierra Leone becomes a republic, and Siaka Stevens the president.
04-19.text: Sierra Leone becomes a republic, and Siaka Stevens the president.

04-19.title: India
04-19.subtitle: India's first satellite Aryabhata launched in orbit from Kapustin Yar, Russia.
04-19.text: India's first satellite Aryabhata launched in orbit from Kapustin Yar, Russia.

04-19.title: Advance Australia Fair
04-19.subtitle: "Advance Australia Fair", written by Scottish-born composer Peter Dodds McCormick, officially replaced "God Save the Que...
04-19.text: "Advance Australia Fair" is the national anthem of Australia. Written by Scottish-born Australian composer Peter Dodds McCormick, the song was first performed as a patriotic song in Australia in 1878. It replaced "God Save the Queen" as the official national anthem by the Whitlam government in 1974, following an indicative opinion survey.

04-19.title: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and
04-19.subtitle: Two hundred ATF and FBI agents lay siege to the compound of the white supremacist survivalist group The Covenant, the Sw...
04-19.text: Two hundred ATF and FBI agents lay siege to the compound of the white supremacist survivalist group The Covenant, the Sword, and the Arm of the Lord in Arkansas; the CSA surrenders two days later.

04-19.title: The Simpsons
04-19.subtitle: The Simpsons first appear as a series of shorts on The Tracey Ullman Show, first starting with "Good Night".
04-19.text: The Simpsons first appear as a series of shorts on The Tracey Ullman Show, first starting with "Good Night".

04-19.title: Oklahoma City bombing
04-19.subtitle: A truck bombing destroyed much of the Alfred P.
04-19.text: A truck bombing destroyed much of the Alfred P. Murrah Federal Building (aftermath pictured) in Oklahoma City, killing 168 people and injuring more than 680 others.

04-19.title: Air Philippines 541 Crash
04-19.subtitle: A mountain swallows 131 souls near Davao
04-19.text: On approach to Francisco Bangoy International Airport in Davao City, Air Philippines Flight 541 descended below its glide slope and struck a coconut tree, disintegrating on impact and killing all 131 people aboard. The Boeing 737, carrying passengers from Manila, crashed into mountainous terrain south of the city in what remains the deadliest aviation disaster in Philippine history. The tragedy prompted urgent calls for improved approach procedures and terrain awareness systems.

04-19.title: Space Shuttle Endeavour
04-19.subtitle: Space Shuttle Endeavour is launched on STS-100 carrying the Canadarm2 to the International Space Station.
04-19.text: Space Shuttle Endeavour is a retired orbiter from NASA's Space Shuttle program and the fifth and final operational Shuttle built. It embarked on its first mission, STS-49, in May 1992 and its 25th and final mission, STS-134, in May 2011. STS-134 was expected to be the final mission of the Space Shuttle program, but with the authorization of STS-135 by the United States Congress, Atlantis became the last shuttle to fly.

04-19.title: Killing of Freddie Gray
04-19.subtitle: Freddie Gray, a 25-year-old African American, died of injuries sustained while in the custody of the Baltimore Police Department.
04-19.text: Freddie Gray, a 25-year-old African American, died of injuries sustained while in the custody of the Baltimore Police Department.

04-20.title: Baldwin III Takes Jerusalem
04-20.subtitle: A king wrests power from his own mother
04-20.text: After eight years of bitter political struggle, Baldwin III of Jerusalem finally won sole control of the Crusader kingdom from his formidable mother, Queen Melisende. The young king's determination to rule independently had divided the court and nearly sparked civil war. Once in power, he proved himself a capable ruler, expanding the kingdom's borders and defending the other Crusader states. His reign, though cut short by early death, was among the most consequential in Crusader history.

04-20.title: The Curies Refine Radium
04-20.subtitle: Science glows with a dangerous new light
04-20.text: In their modest laboratory in Paris, Pierre and Marie Curie achieved a milestone that would change the course of science: they successfully refined radium chloride to an unprecedented level of purity. This breakthrough, achieved through years of backbreaking work processing tonnes of pitchblende ore, confirmed radium as a new element and opened the door to the atomic age. The couple's shared Nobel Prize the following year would make the Curie name synonymous with scientific discovery.

04-20.title: Enoch Powell
04-20.subtitle: Enoch Powell (pictured), a British Conservative member of Parliament, made a controversial speech in opposition to immig...
04-20.text: Enoch Powell (pictured), a British Conservative member of Parliament, made a controversial speech in opposition to immigration and anti-discrimination legislation, resulting in his removal from the shadow cabinet.

04-20.title: Apollo program
04-20.subtitle: Apollo program: Apollo 16 Lunar Module, commanded by John Young and piloted by Charles Duke, lands on the Moon.
04-20.text: The Apollo program, also known as Project Apollo, was the United States human spaceflight program led by NASA, which landed the first humans on the Moon in 1969. Apollo was conceived during Project Mercury and executed after Project Gemini. It was conceived in 1960 as a three-person spacecraft during the presidency of Dwight D. Eisenhower. Apollo was later dedicated to President John F.

04-20.title: University of California, Riverside 1985 laboratory
04-20.subtitle: University of California, Riverside 1985 laboratory raid: Animal Liberation Front rescues 467 animals being tested in a ...
04-20.text: University of California, Riverside 1985 laboratory raid: Animal Liberation Front rescues 467 animals being tested in a lab at University of California, Riverside in Riverside, California, causing $700,000 in damages to the laboratory in advocation for Animal rights.

04-20.title: Eric Harris and Dylan Klebold
04-20.subtitle: Students Eric Harris and Dylan Klebold carried out a massacre at Columbine High School, killing 13 people before committing suicide.
04-20.text: Students Eric Harris and Dylan Klebold carried out a massacre at Columbine High School, killing 13 people before committing suicide.

04-20.title: Nicoll Highway Collapse
04-20.subtitle: A tunnel caves in beneath Singapore's streets
04-20.text: In the heart of Singapore's bustling Downtown Core, an incomplete tunnel leading to the planned Nicoll Highway MRT station collapsed without warning, killing four construction workers and sending shockwaves through the engineering world. The disaster, caused by structural failures in the retaining walls, forced the relocation of the entire station and triggered one of Singapore's most extensive engineering investigations. It remains a cautionary tale in civil engineering curricula worldwide.

04-20.title: Johnson Space Center shooting
04-20.subtitle: Johnson Space Center shooting: William Phillips barricades himself with a handgun in NASA's Johnson Space Center in Hous...
04-20.text: Johnson Space Center shooting: William Phillips barricades himself with a handgun in NASA's Johnson Space Center in Houston, Texas before killing a male hostage and himself.

04-20.title: Danica Patrick
04-20.subtitle: American racing driver Danica Patrick won the Indy Japan 300, becoming the first woman to win an IndyCar auto race.
04-20.text: American racing driver Danica Patrick won the Indy Japan 300, becoming the first woman to win an IndyCar auto race.

04-20.title: Deepwater Horizon explosion
04-20.subtitle: An explosion on Deepwater Horizon, an offshore rig in the Gulf of Mexico, resulted in the largest marine oil spill in history.
04-20.text: On April 20, 2010, an explosion and fire occurred on the Deepwater Horizon semi-submersible mobile offshore drilling unit, which was owned and operated by Transocean and drilling for BP in the Macondo Prospect oil field about 40 miles (64 km) southeast off the Louisiana coast. The explosion and subsequent fire resulted in the sinking of the Deepwater Horizon and the deaths of 11 workers; 17 others were injured.
04-20.location: 2010 oil disaster in the Gulf of Mexico

04-20.title: Garowe attack
04-20.subtitle: Ten people are killed in a bomb attack on a convoy carrying food supplies to a United Nations compound in Garowe in the Somali region of Puntland.
04-20.text: Ten people are killed in a bomb attack on a convoy carrying food supplies to a United Nations compound in Garowe in the Somali region of Puntland.

04-20.title: SpaceX Starship
04-20.subtitle: SpaceX's Starship rocket, the largest and most powerful rocket ever built, launches for the first time.
04-20.text: SpaceX's Starship rocket, the largest and most powerful rocket ever built, launches for the first time. It explodes four minutes into flight.

04-21.title: Rome Is Founded
04-21.subtitle: Twin brothers and a she-wolf change history
04-21.text: According to the ancient calculations of the scholar Varro, this was the day Romulus and Remus -- twin brothers suckled by a she-wolf in their infancy -- legendarily founded the city of Rome. After a fratricidal quarrel in which Romulus slew his brother, the surviving twin gave his name to what would become the greatest city of the ancient world. From this mythic beginning on the banks of the Tiber, an empire would rise that shaped Western civilization itself.

04-21.title: Luis Saslavsky Was Born
04-21.subtitle: Argentine cinema's golden-age architect
04-21.text: In Buenos Aires, Luis Saslavsky was born -- a filmmaker who would help define the classical era of Argentine cinema. As a director, screenwriter, and producer, he crafted sophisticated films that earned international recognition and elevated Argentine filmmaking to new heights. His work during the 1930s and 1940s captured the elegance and complexity of Buenos Aires society, establishing a cinematic tradition that continues to influence Latin American filmmakers today.

04-21.title: Century 21 Exposition
04-21.subtitle: The Seattle World's Fair (Century 21 Exposition) opens.
04-21.text: The Century 21 Exposition was a world's fair held April 21, 1962, to October 21, 1962, in Seattle, Washington, United States. Nearly 10 million people attended the fair during its six-month run.
04-21.location: World's fair held in Seattle, Washington

04-21.title: Transit
04-21.subtitle: A Transit-5bn satellite fails to reach orbit after launch; as it re-enters the atmosphere, 2.
04-21.text: A Transit-5bn satellite fails to reach orbit after launch; as it re-enters the atmosphere, 2.1 pounds (0.95 kg) of radioactive plutonium in its SNAP RTG power source is widely dispersed.

04-21.title: Western Australia
04-21.subtitle: In response to a dispute over wheat production quotas, Leonard Casley declared his 75 km2 (29 sq mi) farm in Western Aus...
04-21.text: Western Australia (WA) is the westernmost state of Australia. It is bounded by the Indian Ocean to the north and west, the Southern Ocean to the south, the Northern Territory to the north-east, and South Australia to the south-east. Western Australia is Australia's largest state, with a land area of 2,527,013 square kilometres (975,685 sq mi), and is also the second-largest subdivision of any country on Earth.

04-21.title: John Young
04-21.subtitle: Astronauts John Young and Charles Duke fly Apollo 16's Apollo Lunar Module to the Moon's surface, the fifth NASA Apollo Program crewed lunar landing.
04-21.text: Astronauts John Young and Charles Duke fly Apollo 16's Apollo Lunar Module to the Moon's surface, the fifth NASA Apollo Program crewed lunar landing.

04-21.title: South Vietnam
04-21.subtitle: South Vietnamese president Nguyễn Văn Thiệu resigned on hearing of the fall of Xuân Lộc, the last battle of the Vietnam War.
04-21.text: South Vietnamese president Nguyễn Văn Thiệu resigned on hearing of the fall of Xuân Lộc, the last battle of the Vietnam War.

04-21.title: Baseball
04-21.subtitle: Baseball: Rollie Fingers of the Milwaukee Brewers becomes the first pitcher to record 300 saves.
04-21.text: Baseball: Rollie Fingers of the Milwaukee Brewers becomes the first pitcher to record 300 saves.

04-21.title: Xavi Simons Was Born
04-21.subtitle: A prodigy named for greatness
04-21.text: In Amsterdam, a boy named after the legendary Barcelona midfielder Xavi Hernandez entered the world. Xavi Quentin Shay Simons would live up to his namesake's legacy, joining Barcelona's famed La Masia academy as a child before embarking on a journey through European football's elite clubs. His dazzling technical ability and creative vision as an attacking midfielder have made him one of the most exciting young talents in world football.

04-21.title: Kharkiv Pact
04-21.subtitle: The controversial Kharkiv Pact (Russian Ukrainian Naval Base for Gas Treaty) is signed in Kharkiv, Ukraine, by Ukrainian...
04-21.text: The controversial Kharkiv Pact (Russian Ukrainian Naval Base for Gas Treaty) is signed in Kharkiv, Ukraine, by Ukrainian president Viktor Yanukovych and Russian president Dmitry Medvedev; it was unilaterally terminated by Russia on March 31, 2014.

04-21.title: Flint, Michigan
04-21.subtitle: The city of Flint, Michigan, U.
04-21.text: The city of Flint, Michigan, U.S., changed its water source to the Flint River, which exposed residents to lead poisoning.

04-22.title: Reinhard III Was Born
04-22.subtitle: A brief flame in Hanau's noble line
04-22.text: In the medieval county of Hanau, Reinhard III was born to Count Reinhard II and Catherine of Nassau-Beilstein. His life would be a brief chapter in the long saga of German noble families -- ascending to the countship in 1451, he ruled for barely a year before his death. Yet his birth connected two powerful dynasties, the Houses of Hanau and Nassau, weaving together the complex tapestry of alliances that defined medieval German politics.

04-22.title: Nellie Beer Was Born
04-22.subtitle: Manchester would one day call her Lord Mayor
04-22.text: In the industrial heartland of England, Nellie Beer was born into a world where women in politics were rare indeed. She would spend thirty-five years on the Manchester City Council as a Conservative member, rising to become Lord Mayor of Manchester from 1966 to 1967. Awarded an OBE for her public service, she dedicated her career to the civic life of one of England's greatest cities, proving that persistence and dedication could break through even the most stubborn barriers.

04-22.title: Korean War
04-22.subtitle: Korean War: The Chinese People's Volunteer Army attacked positions occupied mainly by Australian and Canadian forces, starting the Battle of Kapyong.
04-22.text: The Korean War was an armed conflict on the Korean peninsula between North Korea and South Korea and their allies. North Korea was supported by China and the Soviet Union, while South Korea was supported by the United Nations Command (UNC) led by the United States. The war, which ended in stalemate, was the first great clash of arms of the Cold War and was one of, if not its deadliest proxy war.

04-22.title: Red Scare
04-22.subtitle: Red Scare: Witnesses begin testifying and live television coverage of the Army–McCarthy hearings begins.
04-22.text: Red Scare: Witnesses begin testifying and live television coverage of the Army–McCarthy hearings begins.

04-22.title: Robin Knox-Johnston
04-22.subtitle: British yachtsman Sir Robin Knox-Johnston wins the Sunday Times Golden Globe Race and completes the first solo non-stop circumnavigation of the world.
04-22.text: British yachtsman Sir Robin Knox-Johnston wins the Sunday Times Golden Globe Race and completes the first solo non-stop circumnavigation of the world.

04-22.title: Optical fiber
04-22.subtitle: Optical fiber is first used to carry live telephone traffic.
04-22.text: Optical fiber is first used to carry live telephone traffic.

04-22.title: Black British people
04-22.subtitle: Stephen Lawrence, a black British teenager, was murdered while waiting for a bus in Eltham, London, leading to cultural ...
04-22.text: Stephen Lawrence, a black British teenager, was murdered while waiting for a bus in Eltham, London, leading to cultural changes of attitudes on racism and the police, and to the law and police practice.
04-22.location: British people of sub-Saharan African descent

04-22.title: Elian Gonzalez Seized
04-22.subtitle: A pre-dawn raid divides two nations
04-22.text: In the pre-dawn darkness of Miami's Little Havana, armed federal agents burst into a modest home and seized six-year-old Elian Gonzalez from his relatives, reuniting him with his Cuban father. The dramatic raid ended a months-long international custody battle that had become a proxy war between Washington and Havana, between exile communities and the Castro regime. The iconic photograph of a terrified Elian confronting an armed agent became one of the defining images of the era.

04-22.title: Junichiro Koizumi
04-22.subtitle: Japan's Prime Minister Junichiro Koizumi apologizes for Japan's war record.
04-22.text: Japan's Prime Minister Junichiro Koizumi apologizes for Japan's war record.

04-22.title: Royal Canadian Mounted Police
04-22.subtitle: The Royal Canadian Mounted Police arrested two men who were plotting to commit terrorist attacks against Via Rail operations.
04-22.text: The Royal Canadian Mounted Police arrested two men who were plotting to commit terrorist attacks against Via Rail operations.

04-22.title: Paris Agreement
04-22.subtitle: The Paris Agreement, an international treaty on climate change, opened for signature and was signed by 175 parties.
04-22.text: The Paris Agreement is an international treaty on climate change that was signed in 2016. The treaty covers climate change mitigation, adaptation, and finance. The Paris Agreement was negotiated by 196 parties at the 2015 United Nations Climate Change Conference near Paris, France. As of January 2026, 194 members of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) are parties to the agreement.

04-22.title: Self-immolation of Wynn Bruce
04-22.subtitle: American climate activist Wynn Bruce set himself on fire outside the United States Supreme Court Building in an apparent...
04-22.text: American climate activist Wynn Bruce set himself on fire outside the United States Supreme Court Building in an apparent protest against the climate crisis.

04-23.title: Temple of Venus Erected
04-23.subtitle: Rome builds beauty from the ashes of defeat
04-23.text: On the sacred Capitoline Hill, the Romans dedicated a magnificent temple to Venus Erycina -- not in celebration, but in atonement. The devastating Roman defeat at Lake Trasimene, where Hannibal's Carthaginian forces had annihilated an entire Roman army, demanded divine intercession. By raising this temple to the goddess of love and beauty, Rome sought to restore the favor of the gods and fortify the spirit of a republic fighting for its survival.

04-23.title: Jim Bottomley Was Born
04-23.subtitle: A Cardinal destined for Cooperstown
04-23.text: In Oglesby, Illinois, James Leroy Bottomley was born -- the future first baseman who would become one of the St. Louis Cardinals' greatest players. Over a fifteen-year career, 'Sunny Jim' helped lead the Cardinals to four National League pennants and two World Series titles. His powerful bat and sunny disposition made him a fan favorite, and his election to the Baseball Hall of Fame in 1974 cemented his place among the sport's immortals.

04-23.title: Batting
04-23.subtitle: Batting for the Milwaukee Braves against Vic Raschi of the St.
04-23.text: Batting for the Milwaukee Braves against Vic Raschi of the St. Louis Cardinals, Hank Aaron hit the first of his 755 home runs in Major League Baseball.

04-23.title: Soviet space program
04-23.subtitle: Soviet space program: Soyuz 1 (Russian: Союз 1, Union 1) a crewed spaceflight carrying cosmonaut Colonel Vladimir Komarov is launched into orbit.
04-23.text: Soviet space program: Soyuz 1 (Russian: Союз 1, Union 1) a crewed spaceflight carrying cosmonaut Colonel Vladimir Komarov is launched into orbit.

04-23.title: Bangladesh Liberation War
04-23.subtitle: Bangladesh Liberation War: The Pakistan Army and Razakars massacre approximately 3,000 Hindu emigrants in the Jathibhang...
04-23.text: The Bangladesh Liberation War, also known as the Bangladesh War of Independence, was an armed conflict sparked by the rise of the Bengali nationalist and self-determination movement in East Pakistan, which resulted in the independence of Bangladesh with the help of India. The war began when the Pakistani military junta based in West Pakistan—under the orders of Yahya Khan—launched Operation Searchlight against East Pakistanis on the night of 25 March 1971, initiating the Bangladesh genocide.

04-23.title: Death of Blair Peach
04-23.subtitle: Blair Peach, a New Zealand teacher, was fatally injured while taking part in an Anti-Nazi League demonstration against a...
04-23.text: Blair Peach, a New Zealand teacher, was fatally injured while taking part in an Anti-Nazi League demonstration against a National Front election meeting in Southall, London.

04-23.title: Namibia
04-23.subtitle: Namibia becomes the 160th member of the United Nations and the 50th member of the Commonwealth of Nations.
04-23.text: Namibia becomes the 160th member of the United Nations and the 50th member of the Commonwealth of Nations.

04-23.title: Eritrea
04-23.subtitle: Eritreans vote overwhelmingly for independence from Ethiopia in a United Nations-monitored referendum.
04-23.text: Eritreans vote overwhelmingly for independence from Ethiopia in a United Nations-monitored referendum.

04-23.title: NATO
04-23.subtitle: NATO bombs the headquarters of Radio Television of Serbia, as part of their aerial campaign against the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia.
04-23.text: NATO bombs the headquarters of Radio Television of Serbia, as part of their aerial campaign against the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia.

04-23.title: Chloe Kim Was Born
04-23.subtitle: A future Olympic champion enters the halfpipe
04-23.text: In Long Beach, California, Chloe Kim was born to Korean immigrant parents who could scarcely imagine their daughter would one day soar above Olympic halfpipes. At seventeen, she became the youngest woman to win an Olympic snowboarding gold medal at the 2018 Pyeongchang Games, throwing back-to-back 1080s with a perfection that left the world breathless. She defended her title in 2022, cementing her status as the greatest halfpipe rider in women's snowboarding history.

04-23.title: Gamma-ray burst
04-23.subtitle: The gamma-ray burst GRB 090423 was detected coming from the most distant astronomical object of any kind known at the time.
04-23.text: The gamma-ray burst GRB 090423 was detected coming from the most distant astronomical object of any kind known at the time.

04-23.title: Jan Brewer
04-23.subtitle: Arizona governor Jan Brewer signed into law the controversial anti–illegal immigration bill SB 1070, much of which was later struck down by the U.
04-23.text: Janice Kay Brewer is an American politician who served as the 22nd governor of Arizona from 2009 to 2015, as a member of the Republican Party. Prior to this, Brewer was a member of the Arizona House of Representatives, Arizona Senate, and Maricopa County Board of Supervisors, and served as Secretary of State of Arizona from 2003 to 2009.

04-23.title: 2018 Toronto van attack
04-23.subtitle: A man intentionally struck pedestrians with a van on Yonge Street in Toronto, Canada, leading to 11 deaths.
04-23.text: A man intentionally struck pedestrians with a van on Yonge Street in Toronto, Canada, leading to 11 deaths.

04-24.title: Thutmose III Takes Throne
04-24.subtitle: Egypt's greatest warrior waits in the wings
04-24.text: In the gilded palaces along the Nile, the young Thutmose III ascended to the throne of Egypt -- though real power immediately shifted to his stepmother, the formidable Hatshepsut. For two decades he waited in her shadow, but once he finally ruled alone, he became Egypt's greatest warrior pharaoh. Seventeen military campaigns expanded the empire to its widest extent, earning him comparisons to Napoleon. Historians now regard him as one of the most brilliant military commanders of all time.

04-24.title: Falange's Founder Was Born
04-24.subtitle: Spain's darkest political chapter takes root
04-24.text: In Madrid, Jose Antonio Primo de Rivera was born into political aristocracy -- his father had been the dictator of Spain. The younger Primo de Rivera would found the Falange Espanola, a fascist political movement that sought to reshape Spain through national syndicalism. Executed by Republican forces at the outbreak of the Spanish Civil War in 1936, he became a martyr figure for Franco's regime, his legacy forever entangled with Spain's most painful century.

04-24.title: Suez Crisis
04-24.subtitle: Suez Crisis: The Suez Canal is reopened following the introduction of UNEF peacekeepers to the region.
04-24.text: Suez Crisis: The Suez Canal is reopened following the introduction of UNEF peacekeepers to the region.

04-24.title: Dominican Civil War
04-24.subtitle: Civil war breaks out in the Dominican Republic when Colonel Francisco Caamaño overthrows the triumvirate that had been i...
04-24.text: Civil war breaks out in the Dominican Republic when Colonel Francisco Caamaño overthrows the triumvirate that had been in power since the coup d'état against Juan Bosch.

04-24.title: Astronaut
04-24.subtitle: Cosmonaut Vladimir Komarov dies in Soyuz 1 when its parachute fails to open.
04-24.text: An astronaut is a person trained, equipped, and deployed by a human spaceflight program to serve as a commander or crew member of a spacecraft. Although generally reserved for professional space travelers, the term is sometimes applied to anyone who travels into space, including scientists, politicians, journalists, and space tourists. In the United States, it is a designated term used by three agencies: NASA, the FAA, and the military.

04-24.title: The Gambia
04-24.subtitle: The Gambia becomes a republic within the Commonwealth of Nations, with Dawda Jawara as its first President.
04-24.text: The Gambia becomes a republic within the Commonwealth of Nations, with Dawda Jawara as its first President.

04-24.title: STS-31
04-24.subtitle: STS-31: The Hubble Space Telescope is launched from the Space Shuttle Discovery.
04-24.text: STS-31: The Hubble Space Telescope is launched from the Space Shuttle Discovery.

04-24.title: Provisional Irish Republican Army
04-24.subtitle: The Provisional Irish Republican Army detonated a truck bomb in London's financial district in Bishopsgate, killing one ...
04-24.text: The Provisional Irish Republican Army detonated a truck bomb in London's financial district in Bishopsgate, killing one person, injuring forty-four others, and causing damage that cost £350 million to repair.

04-24.title: Olivia Gadecki Was Born
04-24.subtitle: Australia's next tennis hope takes her first swing
04-24.text: In Queensland, Australia, Olivia Gadecki was born into a nation with a proud tennis tradition stretching from Rod Laver to Ash Barty. She would grow into a versatile player capable of competing at the highest levels of singles and doubles, winning mixed doubles titles at the Australian Open in back-to-back years. Her rise through the WTA rankings represents the continuing strength of Australian tennis and the promise of a new generation.

04-24.title: Backhoe
04-24.subtitle: A backhoe breached a pipeline in Toronto, Canada, which caused a gas explosion that killed seven people.
04-24.text: A backhoe breached a pipeline in Toronto, Canada, which caused a gas explosion that killed seven people.

04-24.title: Savar Upazila
04-24.subtitle: A building in the Savar Upazila of Dhaka, Bangladesh, collapsed, killing 1,134 people, making it the deadliest accidenta...
04-24.text: Savar is an upazila of Dhaka District in the division of Dhaka, Bangladesh and is located at a distance of about 24 kilometers (15 mi) to the northwest of Dhaka city. Savar is mostly famous for the National Martyrs' Memorial, the national monument for the martyrs of the Liberation War of Bangladesh.

04-25.title: Athens Falls to Sparta
04-25.subtitle: The great war between Greeks finally ends
04-25.text: After twenty-seven years of devastating conflict, the Peloponnesian War reached its climax as Admiral Lysander and King Pausanias of Sparta blockaded the city of Athens by land and sea. Starving and besieged, the Athenians were forced to surrender, accepting humiliating terms that included the destruction of their Long Walls and the disbanding of their fleet. The golden age of Athenian democracy was over, and Spartan hegemony over Greece had begun.

04-25.title: Gladwyn Jebb Was Born
04-25.subtitle: The man who held the world together briefly
04-25.text: In the English county of Yorkshire, Hubert Miles Gladwyn Jebb was born into a world that would soon be torn apart by two world wars. He would help put it back together as acting Secretary-General of the United Nations from 1945 to 1946, shepherding the fledgling international body through its critical first months. His diplomatic career, spanning decades of British foreign service, made him one of the twentieth century's most influential figures in international relations.

04-25.title: First World War
04-25.subtitle: First World War: The Australian and New Zealand Army Corps landed at Anzac Cove while British and French troops landed a...
04-25.text: World War I, or the First World War, also known as the Great War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Central Powers. Major areas of conflict included Europe and the Middle East, as well as parts of Africa and the Asia-Pacific. The war saw important developments in weaponry including tanks, aircraft, artillery, machine guns, and chemical weapons.

04-25.title: Gladys Elinor Watkins
04-25.subtitle: Gladys Elinor Watkins consecrated the carillon of the National War Memorial in Wellington, New Zealand (dedication pictured).
04-25.text: Gladys Elinor Watkins consecrated the carillon of the National War Memorial in Wellington, New Zealand (dedication pictured).

04-25.title: World War II
04-25.subtitle: World War II: Liberation Day (Italy): The National Liberation Committee for Northern Italy calls for a general uprising ...
04-25.text: World War II: Liberation Day (Italy): The National Liberation Committee for Northern Italy calls for a general uprising against the German occupation and the Italian Social Republic.

04-25.title: Francis Crick
04-25.subtitle: Francis Crick and James Watson publish "Molecular Structure of Nucleic Acids: A Structure for Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid" ...
04-25.text: Francis Crick and James Watson publish "Molecular Structure of Nucleic Acids: A Structure for Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid" describing the double helix structure of DNA.

04-25.title: Solar cell
04-25.subtitle: The first practical solar cell is publicly demonstrated by Bell Telephone Laboratories.
04-25.text: The first practical solar cell is publicly demonstrated by Bell Telephone Laboratories.

04-25.title: United States Navy
04-25.subtitle: The U.
04-25.text: The U.S. Navy submarine Triton (pictured) completed the first submerged circumnavigation of the world.

04-25.title: Carnation Revolution
04-25.subtitle: Carnation Revolution: A leftist military coup in Portugal overthrows the authoritarian-conservative Estado Novo regime.
04-25.text: Carnation Revolution: A leftist military coup in Portugal overthrows the authoritarian-conservative Estado Novo regime.

04-25.title: Margaret Thatcher
04-25.subtitle: British prime minister Margaret Thatcher told journalists in Downing Street to "rejoice" upon hearing news of the succes...
04-25.text: Margaret Hilda Thatcher, Baroness Thatcher, was a British stateswoman who served as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom from 1979 to 1990 and Leader of the Conservative Party from 1975 to 1990. She was the longest-serving British prime minister of the 20th century and the first woman to hold the position. As prime minister, she implemented policies that came to be known as Thatcherism.
04-25.location: Prime Minister of the United Kingdom from 1979 to 1990

04-25.title: Cold War
04-25.subtitle: Cold War: American schoolgirl Samantha Smith is invited to visit the Soviet Union by its leader Yuri Andropov after he r...
04-25.text: The Cold War was a period of international geopolitical rivalry between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) and their respective allies, the capitalist Western Bloc and communist Eastern Bloc, which began in the aftermath of the Second World War and ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
04-25.location: 1947–1991 geopolitical rivalry between US and USSR

04-25.title: Violeta Chamorro
04-25.subtitle: Violeta Chamorro took office as President of Nicaragua, becoming the first female head of state in the Americas to have been elected in her own right.
04-25.text: Violeta Chamorro took office as President of Nicaragua, becoming the first female head of state in the Americas to have been elected in her own right.

04-25.title: Dejan Kulusevski Was Born
04-25.subtitle: Swedish-Macedonian flair meets English football
04-25.text: In Stockholm, Dejan Kulusevski was born to parents of North Macedonian heritage, inheriting a blend of Scandinavian discipline and Balkan creativity that would define his playing style. Rising through Swedish youth football before starring in Italy's Serie A, he found his home at Tottenham Hotspur, where his vision, work rate, and technical brilliance made him one of the Premier League's most complete attacking players.

04-26.title: Marcus Aurelius Was Born
04-26.subtitle: The philosopher who would rule the world
04-26.text: In the heart of Rome, Marcus Aurelius was born into a prominent family during the reign of Emperor Hadrian. He would become the last of the Five Good Emperors and history's most famous philosopher-king. His 'Meditations,' written in his tent during military campaigns along the Danube, remains one of the most profound works of Stoic philosophy ever composed. In an age of plague, war, and political intrigue, he sought to govern with wisdom, justice, and compassion.

04-26.title: Charles Richter Was Born
04-26.subtitle: He taught the world to measure earthquakes
04-26.text: On a farm near Hamilton, Ohio, Charles Francis Richter was born -- the seismologist who would give the world a universal language for describing earthquakes. Working at Caltech alongside Beno Gutenberg, he developed the Richter scale in 1935, transforming seismology from a qualitative art into a precise science. Though later superseded by the moment magnitude scale, his name remains synonymous with earthquake measurement around the globe.

04-26.title: Jonas Salk
04-26.subtitle: The first clinical trials of Jonas Salk's polio vaccine begin in Fairfax County, Virginia.
04-26.text: The first clinical trials of Jonas Salk's polio vaccine begin in Fairfax County, Virginia.

04-26.title: Baltimore and Ohio Railroad
04-26.subtitle: Service ended on the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad's Royal Blue Line, one of the first major electrified train lines in the U.
04-26.text: Service ended on the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad's Royal Blue Line, one of the first major electrified train lines in the U.S. (steam train pictured).

04-26.title: NASA
04-26.subtitle: NASA's Ranger 4 spacecraft crashes into the Moon.
04-26.text: The National Aeronautics and Space Administration is an independent agency of the U.S. federal government responsible for the United States' civil space program and for research in aeronautics and space exploration. Headquartered in Washington, D.C., NASA operates ten field centers across the US and is organized into mission directorates for Science, Space Operations, Exploration Systems Development, Space Technology, Aeronautics Research, and Mission Support.

04-26.title: 1966 Tashkent earthquake
04-26.subtitle: The magnitude 5.
04-26.text: The magnitude 5.1 Tashkent earthquake affects the largest city in Soviet Central Asia with a maximum MSK intensity of VII (Very strong). Tashkent is mostly destroyed and 15–200 are killed.

04-26.title: Daulatpur–Saturia tornado
04-26.subtitle: A tornado struck the Manikganj District of Bangladesh and killed an estimated 1,300 people, making it the deadliest tornado in history.
04-26.text: A tornado struck the Manikganj District of Bangladesh and killed an estimated 1,300 people, making it the deadliest tornado in history.

04-26.title: Space Shuttle Columbia
04-26.subtitle: The Space Shuttle Columbia is launched on mission STS-55 to conduct experiments aboard the Spacelab module.
04-26.text: Space Shuttle Columbia (OV-102) was a Space Shuttle orbiter manufactured by Rockwell International and operated by NASA. Named after the first American ship to circumnavigate the globe, and the female personification of the United States, Columbia was the first of five Space Shuttle orbiters to fly in space, debuting the Space Shuttle launch vehicle on its maiden flight on April 12, 1981 and becoming the first spacecraft to be re-used after its first flight when it launched on STS-2 on

04-26.title: Nagoya Airfield
04-26.subtitle: Just before landing at Nagoya Airport, Japan, the copilot of China Airlines Flight 140 inadvertently triggered the takeo...
04-26.text: Just before landing at Nagoya Airport, Japan, the copilot of China Airlines Flight 140 inadvertently triggered the takeoff/go-around switch, causing the aircraft to crash and killing 264 of the 271 people on board.

04-26.title: Thiago Almada Was Born
04-26.subtitle: Argentine magic finds a European stage
04-26.text: In the working-class neighborhoods of Buenos Aires, Thiago Ezequiel Almada was born with a football at his feet and dreams of glory. His dazzling dribbling and creative vision caught the eye of scouts worldwide, launching him on a journey from Argentine youth football to MLS stardom with Atlanta United, and ultimately to Atletico Madrid in La Liga. A World Cup winner with Argentina in 2022, Almada embodies the fearless creativity that defines South American football.

04-26.title: International Hydrographic Organization
04-26.subtitle: The International Hydrographic Organization abandoned South Korean-led 'attempts to rename the Sea of Japan.
04-26.text: The International Hydrographic Organization abandoned South Korean-led 'attempts to rename the Sea of Japan.

04-27.title: Decimus Brutus Was Born
04-27.subtitle: Caesar's trusted friend, future betrayer
04-27.text: In Rome, Decimus Junius Brutus Albinus was born into the tumultuous final century of the Roman Republic. He would become one of Julius Caesar's most trusted generals, fighting alongside him in Gaul and against Pompey. Yet on the Ides of March in 44 BC, he would be among those who plunged their daggers into Caesar -- making his betrayal perhaps the most painful of all, for it was Decimus, not the more famous Marcus Brutus, whom Caesar had loved like a son.

04-27.title: August Koern Was Born
04-27.subtitle: A diplomat for a nation that ceased to exist
04-27.text: In Estonia, August Koern was born into a small Baltic nation that would soon win its independence, only to lose it again. When the Soviet Union swallowed Estonia in 1940, Koern refused to accept the occupation. For nearly two decades, he served as Estonian foreign minister in exile, keeping the flame of Estonian sovereignty alive through the darkest years of the Cold War -- a testament to the power of diplomatic persistence in the face of seemingly impossible odds.

04-27.title: Milton Margai
04-27.subtitle: Prime Minister Milton Margai led the Sierra Leone Colony and Protectorate to independence from the United Kingdom.
04-27.text: Prime Minister Milton Margai led the Sierra Leone Colony and Protectorate to independence from the United Kingdom.

04-27.title: Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation
04-27.subtitle: Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation: British forces repelled a surprise Indonesian attack on a base at Plaman Mapu in Sarawak.
04-27.text: Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation: British forces repelled a surprise Indonesian attack on a base at Plaman Mapu in Sarawak.

04-27.title: Expo 67
04-27.subtitle: The Expo 67 world's fair opened in Montreal, with 62 nations participating and more than 50 million visitors ultimately attending.
04-27.text: The Expo 67 world's fair opened in Montreal, with 62 nations participating and more than 50 million visitors ultimately attending.

04-27.title: Saur Revolution
04-27.subtitle: The Saur Revolution begins in Afghanistan, ending the following morning with the murder of Afghan President Mohammed Dao...
04-27.text: The Saur Revolution begins in Afghanistan, ending the following morning with the murder of Afghan President Mohammed Daoud Khan and the establishment of the Democratic Republic of Afghanistan.

04-27.title: 1985 World Snooker Championship final
04-27.subtitle: The black-ball final, one of the most famous snooker matches in history, began between Steve Davis and Dennis Taylor (pictured).
04-27.text: The black-ball final, one of the most famous snooker matches in history, began between Steve Davis and Dennis Taylor (pictured).

04-27.title: Russia
04-27.subtitle: The Russian Federation and 12 other former Soviet republics become members of the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank.
04-27.text: The Russian Federation and 12 other former Soviet republics become members of the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank.

04-27.title: South African general election
04-27.subtitle: South African general election: The first democratic general election in South Africa, in which black citizens could vote.
04-27.text: South African general election: The first democratic general election in South Africa, in which black citizens could vote. The Interim Constitution comes into force.

04-27.title: Anthony Elanga Was Born
04-27.subtitle: A Swedish-Cameroonian talent takes shape
04-27.text: In Hyllie, Sweden, Anthony David Junior Elanga was born to a Cameroonian father who had himself been a professional footballer. The younger Elanga would follow in his father's footsteps, developing his pace and directness at Manchester United's academy before establishing himself as a Premier League forward. His move to Newcastle United and selection for the Swedish national team reflect the increasingly global nature of modern football talent.

04-27.title: Airbus A380
04-27.subtitle: The Airbus A380, the world's largest passenger airliner, made its maiden flight from Toulouse, France.
04-27.text: The Airbus A380 is a large wide-body airliner, developed and produced by Airbus until 2021. It is the world's largest passenger airliner and the only full-length double-deck jet airliner.

04-28.title: Emperor Otho Was Born
04-28.subtitle: A ruler born for tragedy and a brief reign
04-28.text: In the Italian town of Ferentium, Marcus Salvius Otho was born -- a man whose life would read like a Roman tragedy. Once a close friend of Emperor Nero, he was exiled when Nero stole his wife Poppaea. In the chaos following Nero's death, Otho seized the throne but held it for only ninety-one days before defeat at the Battle of Bedriacum. Rather than prolong civil war, he took his own life -- an act of courage that even his enemies admired.

04-28.title: Alice Berry Was Born
04-28.subtitle: A champion for women in the Australian bush
04-28.text: In Australia, Alice Miriam Berry was born -- a woman who would dedicate her life to improving the lives of women and children in the vast, isolated rural regions of the Australian outback. Created a Dame for her tireless activism, she worked to bring healthcare, education, and social services to communities that the rest of the country had forgotten. Her legacy lives on in the countless lives she touched across decades of service.

04-28.title: Treaty of Taipei
04-28.subtitle: The Sino-Japanese Peace Treaty (Treaty of Taipei) is signed in Taipei, Taiwan between Japan and the Republic of China to...
04-28.text: The Sino-Japanese Peace Treaty (Treaty of Taipei) is signed in Taipei, Taiwan between Japan and the Republic of China to officially end the Second Sino-Japanese War.

04-28.title: Sibley Commission
04-28.subtitle: The Sibley Commission issued their final report, urging the government of the U.
04-28.text: The Sibley Commission issued their final report, urging the government of the U.S. state of Georgia to abandon its strategy of massive resistance and legalize some instances of school integration.

04-28.title: Jimmy Reid
04-28.subtitle: Scottish trade unionist Jimmy Reid condemned the capitalist "rat race" in his inaugural address as Rector of the University of Glasgow.
04-28.text: Scottish trade unionist Jimmy Reid condemned the capitalist "rat race" in his inaugural address as Rector of the University of Glasgow.

04-28.title: The Dark Side of the Moon
04-28.subtitle: The Dark Side of the Moon by Pink Floyd, recorded in Abbey Road Studios goes to number one on the US Billboard chart, be...
04-28.text: The Dark Side of the Moon by Pink Floyd, recorded in Abbey Road Studios goes to number one on the US Billboard chart, beginning a record-breaking 741-week chart run.

04-28.title: Chernobyl disaster
04-28.subtitle: High levels of radiation resulting from the Chernobyl disaster are detected at Forsmark Nuclear Power Plant in Sweden, l...
04-28.text: High levels of radiation resulting from the Chernobyl disaster are detected at Forsmark Nuclear Power Plant in Sweden, leading Soviet authorities to publicly announce the accident.

04-28.title: Aloha Airlines Flight 243
04-28.subtitle: Aloha Airlines Flight 243 experienced explosive decompression while airborne between Hilo and Honolulu, Hawaii, with one...
04-28.text: Aloha Airlines Flight 243 experienced explosive decompression while airborne between Hilo and Honolulu, Hawaii, with one fatality as a flight attendant was ejected from the aircraft.

04-28.title: Space Shuttle Discovery
04-28.subtitle: Space Shuttle Discovery launches on STS-39, the first unclassified shuttle mission for the United States Department of Defense.
04-28.text: Space Shuttle Discovery launches on STS-39, the first unclassified shuttle mission for the United States Department of Defense.

04-28.title: Victoria De Angelis Was Born
04-28.subtitle: Rome's rock rebel joins the world
04-28.text: In Rome, Victoria De Angelis was born -- the bass-playing powerhouse who would help bring Italian rock music back to the global stage. In 2016, she founded Maneskin alongside three fellow Roman teenagers. Five years later, the band won the Eurovision Song Contest with 'Zitti e buoni,' electrifying audiences worldwide with their unapologetic rock energy. Victoria's thunderous bass lines and fearless stage presence made her an icon of a new generation of rock musicians.

04-29.title: Earthquake Shakes Rome
04-29.subtitle: The earth trembles beneath ancient basilicas
04-29.text: A powerful earthquake originating in the Central Apennines shook the Italian peninsula, rattling the walls of Rome and the city of Spoleto. The tremor damaged the great basilica of San Paolo Fuori le Mura, one of the most sacred churches in Christendom. Recorded independently by both Einhard's Royal Frankish Annals and the Liber Pontificalis, this seismic event left archaeological traces that scientists continue to study more than twelve centuries later.

04-29.title: Amelia Best Was Born
04-29.subtitle: Tasmania would soon hear her voice
04-29.text: In Tasmania, Amelia Martha Best was born -- a woman who would become one of the first two women elected to the Tasmanian House of Assembly, breaking barriers in Australian politics. Awarded an MBE for her public service, she championed the rights of ordinary Tasmanians and proved that women could not only participate in democratic governance but excel at it. Her pioneering career opened doors that generations of women politicians would walk through.

04-29.title: World War I
04-29.subtitle: World War I: The UK's 6th Indian Division surrenders to Ottoman Forces at the Siege of Kut in one of the largest surrend...
04-29.text: World War I, or the First World War, also known as the Great War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Central Powers. Major areas of conflict included Europe and the Middle East, as well as parts of Africa and the Asia-Pacific. The war saw important developments in weaponry including tanks, aircraft, artillery, machine guns, and chemical weapons.

04-29.title: 3D television
04-29.subtitle: The first U.
04-29.text: The first U.S. experimental 3D television broadcast shows an episode of Space Patrol on Los Angeles ABC affiliate KECA-TV.

04-29.title: Broadway theatre
04-29.subtitle: The controversial Broadway musical Hair, a product of the counterculture of the 1960s, opened, with its songs becoming a...
04-29.text: The controversial Broadway musical Hair, a product of the counterculture of the 1960s, opened, with its songs becoming anthems of the anti-Vietnam War movement.

04-29.title: Vietnam War
04-29.subtitle: Vietnam War: South Vietnamese forces began the Cambodian campaign, aiming to attack North Vietnamese jungle bases.
04-29.text: The Vietnam War was an armed conflict in Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia fought between North Vietnam and South Vietnam and their allies. North Vietnam was supported by the Soviet Union and China, while South Vietnam was supported by the United States and other anti-communist nations. The conflict was the second of the Indochina wars and a proxy war of the Cold War between the Soviet Union and US.
04-29.location: War in Southeast Asia from 1955 to 1975

04-29.title: Space Shuttle Challenger
04-29.subtitle: Space Shuttle Challenger is launched on STS-51-B.
04-29.text: Space Shuttle Challenger is launched on STS-51-B.

04-29.title: Sikhs
04-29.subtitle: An assembly of Sikhs, known as a Sarbat Khalsa, officially declared independence for a state of Khalistan.
04-29.text: An assembly of Sikhs, known as a Sarbat Khalsa, officially declared independence for a state of Khalistan.

04-29.title: Rungrado 1st of May Stadium
04-29.subtitle: Before a crowd of about 165,000 at the Rungrado 1st of May Stadium, Ric Flair and Antonio Inoki competed in the main eve...
04-29.text: Before a crowd of about 165,000 at the Rungrado 1st of May Stadium, Ric Flair and Antonio Inoki competed in the main event of Collision in Korea, the highest attended professional wrestling event of all time.

04-29.title: Chemical Weapons Convention
04-29.subtitle: The Chemical Weapons Convention entered into force, outlawing the production, stockpiling and use of chemical weapons in...
04-29.text: The Chemical Weapons Convention (CWC), officially the Convention on the Prohibition of the Development, Production, Stockpiling and Use of Chemical Weapons and on their Destruction, is an arms control treaty administered by the Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (OPCW), an intergovernmental organization based in The Hague, Netherlands. The treaty entered into force on 29 April 1997.
04-29.location: Multilateral treaty prohibiting the production, stockpiling, and use of chemical weapons

04-29.title: Danilo Was Born
04-29.subtitle: A Brazilian midfielder begins his journey
04-29.text: In Brazil, Danilo dos Santos de Oliveira was born into the country that has produced more footballing geniuses than any other. Rising through the academy system, he developed into a dynamic midfielder with an eye for goal and a relentless engine. His performances in the Campeonato Brasileiro earned him a devoted following, and his journey from Brazilian youth football to the top flight embodies the endless pipeline of talent that flows from the fields of South America.

04-29.title: William, Prince of Wales
04-29.subtitle: Watched by a worldwide television audience of tens of millions, Prince William and Catherine Middleton were married at Westminster Abbey in London.
04-29.text: Watched by a worldwide television audience of tens of millions, Prince William and Catherine Middleton were married at Westminster Abbey in London.

04-30.title: Christian Persecution Ends
04-30.subtitle: An empire relents after years of bloodshed
04-30.text: After nearly a decade of the most systematic persecution Christians had ever faced, the Diocletianic Persecution officially ended in the eastern Roman Empire. Emperor Galerius, himself gravely ill, issued an edict of toleration that acknowledged the failure of the campaign to stamp out Christianity. Churches could be rebuilt, congregations could gather openly, and Christians could once again worship without fear. The edict marked the beginning of Christianity's transformation from persecuted faith to state religion.

04-30.title: J. J. Thomson
04-30.subtitle: J.
04-30.text: J. J. Thomson of the Cavendish Laboratory announces his discovery of the electron as a subatomic particle, over 1,800 times smaller than a proton (in the atomic nucleus), at a lecture at the Royal Institution in London.

04-30.title: Hawaii Becomes Territory
04-30.subtitle: Pacific islands become American soil
04-30.text: With the stroke of a pen, the Hawaiian Islands were formally organized as a territory of the United States, with Sanford B. Dole installed as governor. The annexation, which followed the overthrow of the Hawaiian monarchy and years of political maneuvering by American sugar planters, transformed the archipelago from an independent Pacific kingdom into American territory. The move would prove strategically vital four decades later, when Pearl Harbor thrust Hawaii into the center of world history.

04-30.title: Soviet submarine K-19
04-30.subtitle: K-19, the first Soviet nuclear submarine equipped with nuclear missiles, is commissioned.
04-30.text: K-19, the first Soviet nuclear submarine equipped with nuclear missiles, is commissioned.

04-30.title: Bristol Omnibus Company
04-30.subtitle: A refusal by the Bristol Omnibus Company and the Transport and General Workers' Union to permit the employment of black ...
04-30.text: A refusal by the Bristol Omnibus Company and the Transport and General Workers' Union to permit the employment of black bus crews led to a bus boycott in Bristol, England.

04-30.title: Fall of Saigon
04-30.subtitle: Fall of Saigon: Communist forces gain control of Saigon.
04-30.text: Saigon, the capital of South Vietnam, was captured by North Vietnam on 30 April 1975. This caused the collapse of South Vietnam to communism and the evacuation of thousands of civilians and U.S. personnel, and ended the Vietnam War. The aftermath ushered in a transition period under the Provisional Revolutionary Government of the Republic of South Vietnam until the formal reunification in 1976.
04-30.location: End of the Vietnam War, 30 April 1975

04-30.title: Saint Faustina Canonized
04-30.subtitle: A humble nun becomes a messenger of mercy
04-30.text: In St. Peter's Square, Pope John Paul II canonized Faustina Kowalska, a Polish nun whose mystical visions of Jesus had inspired the worldwide Catholic devotion to Divine Mercy. Born into poverty in rural Poland, she had entered the convent as a young woman and recorded her spiritual experiences in a diary that would become one of the most widely read Catholic texts of the twentieth century. Her canonization on the newly declared Divine Mercy Sunday was a moment of profound significance for the global Church.

04-30.title: The New Yorker
04-30.subtitle: The New Yorker published an online article and photographs detailing accounts of torture and abuse by American military ...
04-30.text: The New Yorker published an online article and photographs detailing accounts of torture and abuse by American military personnel of Iraqi prisoners held at Abu Ghraib prison in Baghdad.

04-30.title: 2009 attack on the Dutch royal
04-30.subtitle: Seven civilians and the perpetrator are killed and another ten injured at a Queen's Day parade in Apeldoorn, Netherlands...
04-30.text: Seven civilians and the perpetrator are killed and another ten injured at a Queen's Day parade in Apeldoorn, Netherlands in an attempted assassination on Queen Beatrix.

04-30.title: 2021 Meron crowd crush
04-30.subtitle: A crowd crush killed 45 people during the annual pilgrimage to the tomb of Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai in Israel.
04-30.text: A crowd crush killed 45 people during the annual pilgrimage to the tomb of Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai in Israel.

05-01.title: Diocletian Abdicates
05-01.subtitle: The only Roman emperor to retire
05-01.text: In a ceremony without precedent in Roman history, Emperor Diocletian voluntarily removed his purple robes and abdicated the throne, accompanied by his co-ruler Maximian. The man who had rescued the empire from the Crisis of the Third Century, reformed its government, and invented the Tetrarchy chose retirement in his palace at Split over the intoxication of power. It was the only voluntary abdication by a Roman emperor, and Diocletian spent his remaining years famously tending his cabbages.

05-01.title: Scofield Mine Explosion
05-01.subtitle: Two hundred miners never come home
05-01.text: Deep beneath the mountains of Scofield, Utah, a catastrophic dust explosion ripped through a coal mine, killing at least two hundred miners in one of America's worst industrial disasters. The blast, triggered when coal dust was ignited by a blasting charge, sent shockwaves through the mining community and the nation. The tragedy became a catalyst for mine safety reform, though decades would pass before meaningful protections were enacted for the workers who fueled America's industrial growth.

05-01.title: Salvatore Giuliano
05-01.subtitle: Sicilian separatist Salvatore Giuliano and his gang fired into a crowd of May Day marchers near Piana degli Albanesi, Si...
05-01.text: Sicilian separatist Salvatore Giuliano and his gang fired into a crowd of May Day marchers near Piana degli Albanesi, Sicily, killing 11 and wounding 33.

05-01.title: Gwendolyn Brooks
05-01.subtitle: Gwendolyn Brooks became the first African American to receive the Pulitzer Prize for Poetry.
05-01.text: Gwendolyn Brooks became the first African American to receive the Pulitzer Prize for Poetry.

05-01.title: Juan Perón
05-01.subtitle: Argentine president Juan Perón expelled Montoneros from a demonstration in the Plaza de Mayo in Buenos Aires, forcing th...
05-01.text: Argentine president Juan Perón expelled Montoneros from a demonstration in the Plaza de Mayo in Buenos Aires, forcing the group to become a clandestine organization.

05-01.title: Major League Baseball
05-01.subtitle: In Major League Baseball, Rickey Henderson broke the record for stolen bases on the same night that Nolan Ryan broke his own record for no-hitters.
05-01.text: In Major League Baseball, Rickey Henderson broke the record for stolen bases on the same night that Nolan Ryan broke his own record for no-hitters.

05-01.title: Rema Was Born
05-01.subtitle: Nigeria's Afrobeats prince enters the scene
05-01.text: In Benin City, Nigeria, Divine Ikubor was born -- the artist who would become Rema and help carry Afrobeats to every corner of the globe. His 2022 single 'Calm Down,' remixed with Selena Gomez, peaked at number three on the Billboard Hot 100 and topped the Billboard Afrobeats chart for a record-shattering fifty-eight weeks. At just twenty-two, Rema had become one of the most streamed African artists in history, proving that Nigeria's musical influence knows no borders.

05-01.title: 2016 Fort McMurray wildfire
05-01.subtitle: The evacuation of nearly 88,000 people began when a wildfire swept through Fort McMurray, Alberta, Canada, and burned fo...
05-01.text: The evacuation of nearly 88,000 people began when a wildfire swept through Fort McMurray, Alberta, Canada, and burned for another 14 months, becoming the costliest disaster in Canadian history.

05-02.title: Portsmouth Gets Its Charter
05-02.subtitle: The Lionheart builds a naval legacy
05-02.text: King Richard I of England, the warrior-king known as the Lionheart, granted the city of Portsmouth its first royal charter, recognizing its strategic importance as a naval port. This act of royal patronage transformed a modest coastal settlement into one of England's premier maritime bases -- a role it would fulfill for centuries as the home of the Royal Navy. From this harbor, fleets would sail to fight the Spanish Armada, conquer an empire, and defend Britain in two world wars.

05-02.title: Brian Aherne Was Born
05-02.subtitle: A gentleman of stage and screen
05-02.text: In King's Norton, Worcestershire, Brian Aherne was born -- an English actor whose aristocratic bearing and polished performances would grace both sides of the Atlantic for half a century. Moving seamlessly between the London stage and Hollywood studios, he appeared alongside the era's greatest stars. His career embodied the golden age of transatlantic entertainment, when a cultivated English accent could open doors from the West End to Beverly Hills.

05-02.title: World War II
05-02.subtitle: World War II: Following the coup d'état against Iraq Crown Prince 'Abd al-Ilah earlier that year, the United Kingdom lau...
05-02.text: World War II: Following the coup d'état against Iraq Crown Prince 'Abd al-Ilah earlier that year, the United Kingdom launches the Anglo-Iraqi War to restore him to power.

05-02.title: First ascent
05-02.subtitle: First ascent of Shishapangma, the fourteenth highest mountain in the world and the lowest of the Eight-thousanders.
05-02.text: First ascent of Shishapangma, the fourteenth highest mountain in the world and the lowest of the Eight-thousanders.

05-02.title: Falklands War
05-02.subtitle: Falklands War: The British nuclear submarine HMS Conqueror sinks the Argentine cruiser ARA General Belgrano.
05-02.text: The Falklands War was a ten-week undeclared war between Argentina and the United Kingdom in 1982 over two British Overseas Territories in the South Atlantic: the Falkland Islands and its territorial dependency, South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands. The conflict began on 2 April 1982, when Argentina invaded and occupied the Falkland Islands, followed by the invasion of South Georgia the next day.

05-02.title: Croatian War of Independence
05-02.subtitle: During the Croatian War of Independence, the Army of the Republic of Serb Krajina fires cluster bombs at Zagreb, killing...
05-02.text: During the Croatian War of Independence, the Army of the Republic of Serb Krajina fires cluster bombs at Zagreb, killing seven and wounding over 175 civilians.

05-02.title: GPS Goes Civilian
05-02.subtitle: A president unlocks the satellites for everyone
05-02.text: President Bill Clinton announced that the U.S. military would end Selective Availability, the deliberate degradation of GPS signals for civilian users. Overnight, the accuracy available to ordinary GPS receivers improved tenfold, from a hundred meters to about ten. This single policy decision unleashed a revolution in navigation, mapping, and location-based services that would eventually power everything from ride-sharing apps to precision agriculture, transforming daily life for billions worldwide.

05-02.title: Pastel
05-02.subtitle: A pastel version of The Scream, by Norwegian painter Edvard Munch, sells for $120 million in a New York City auction, se...
05-02.text: A pastel version of The Scream, by Norwegian painter Edvard Munch, sells for $120 million in a New York City auction, setting a new world record for a work of art at auction.

05-03.title: Maya Ruler Born
05-03.subtitle: A future king of Palenque draws first breath
05-03.text: In the Maya lowlands, K'an Joy Chitam I was born -- a prince destined to rule the magnificent city-state of Palenque. He would ascend to the throne in 529 AD at the age of thirty-four, ending an interregnum that had left the city without a ruler for four years. Under his stewardship, Palenque would continue to flourish as one of the greatest centers of Maya civilization, its temples and palaces rising above the jungle canopy in architectural splendor.

05-03.title: Jacksonville's Great Fire
05-03.subtitle: Florida's largest city burns to the ground
05-03.text: A small kitchen fire in a mattress factory in Jacksonville, Florida, leaped out of control and consumed the downtown core in one of the worst urban conflagrations in American history. The Great Fire of 1901 destroyed nearly one hundred fifty city blocks, left almost ten thousand people homeless, and caused millions in damages. Yet Jacksonville rose from the ashes with remarkable speed, rebuilding in brick and stone and emerging as one of the South's most modern cities.

05-03.title: World War II
05-03.subtitle: World War II: Japanese naval troops invade Tulagi Island in the Solomon Islands during the first part of Operation Mo th...
05-03.text: World War II or the Second World War was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers. Nearly all of the world's countries participated. Tanks and aircraft played major roles, the latter enabling the strategic bombing of cities and delivery of the only nuclear weapons used in war. World War II was the deadliest conflict in history, causing the death of 60 to 75 million people.

05-03.title: Semi-trailer
05-03.subtitle: Two men are rescued from a semitrailer that crashed over the side of the Pit River Bridge before it fell into the Sacramento River.
05-03.text: Two men are rescued from a semitrailer that crashed over the side of the Pit River Bridge before it fell into the Sacramento River. Amateur photographer Virginia Schau photographs "Rescue on Pit River Bridge", the first and only winning submission for the Pulitzer Prize for Photography to have been taken by a woman.

05-03.title: Birmingham, Alabama
05-03.subtitle: Police in Birmingham, Alabama, used high-pressure water hoses and dogs against civil-rights protesters, bringing scrutin...
05-03.text: Police in Birmingham, Alabama, used high-pressure water hoses and dogs against civil-rights protesters, bringing scrutiny on racial segregation in the southern United States.

05-03.title: Erich Honecker
05-03.subtitle: Erich Honecker became First Secretary of the Socialist Unity Party of Germany, the governing party of East Germany.
05-03.text: Erich Honecker became First Secretary of the Socialist Unity Party of Germany, the governing party of East Germany.

05-03.title: Email
05-03.subtitle: The first unsolicited bulk commercial email (which would later become known as "spam") is sent by a Digital Equipment Co...
05-03.text: The first unsolicited bulk commercial email (which would later become known as "spam") is sent by a Digital Equipment Corporation marketing representative to every ARPANET address on the west coast of the United States.

05-03.title: Oklahoma City
05-03.subtitle: The southwestern portion of Oklahoma City is devastated by an F5 tornado, killing forty-five people, injuring 665, and causing $1 billion in damage.
05-03.text: The southwestern portion of Oklahoma City is devastated by an F5 tornado, killing forty-five people, injuring 665, and causing $1 billion in damage. The tornado is one of 66 from the 1999 Oklahoma tornado outbreak. This tornado also produces the highest wind speed ever recorded, measured at 484 ± 32 kilometres per hour (301 ± 20 mph). In meteorology, the term "May 3" is synonymous with the F5 tornado.

05-03.title: Geocaching Is Born
05-03.subtitle: The world becomes a treasure hunt
05-03.text: On a quiet day in Beavercreek, Oregon, a GPS enthusiast named Dave Ulmer placed a bucket of trinkets in the woods and posted its coordinates on the internet, launching the sport of geocaching. What began as a single hidden container has grown into a worldwide phenomenon with over three million active caches hidden on every continent. The simple idea of using satellite technology to find hidden treasures transformed outdoor recreation and created a global community of modern treasure hunters.

05-03.title: Mexico City Metro
05-03.subtitle: Twenty-six people are killed and ninety-eight are injured after an elevated section of the Mexico City Metro collapses.
05-03.text: Twenty-six people are killed and ninety-eight are injured after an elevated section of the Mexico City Metro collapses.

05-04.title: Sage of Herat Was Born
05-04.subtitle: A Sufi mystic and poet awakens
05-04.text: In the ancient city of Herat, Abdullah Ansari was born -- the man who would become one of Islam's most revered Sufi saints and poets. Known as the 'Sage of Herat,' he composed sublime mystical poetry in Persian and produced profound commentaries on the Quran. His work 'Munajat' -- intimate conversations with God -- remains among the most beautiful expressions of Islamic mysticism, recited by devotees across the Persian-speaking world more than a millennium after his birth.

05-04.title: Luther Adler Was Born
05-04.subtitle: A great stage family gains another star
05-04.text: In New York City, Luther Adler was born into the most famous acting family in American theater. The son of legendary Yiddish theater star Jacob Adler, he became a founding member of the Group Theatre, the revolutionary company that transformed American acting. His intense, naturalistic performances on Broadway and in Hollywood films like 'D.O.A.' carried the torch of his family's theatrical legacy into the modern era, bridging the worlds of Yiddish theater and mainstream American entertainment.

05-04.title: American civil rights movement
05-04.subtitle: American civil rights movement: The "Freedom Riders" begin a bus trip through the South.
05-04.text: American civil rights movement: The "Freedom Riders" begin a bus trip through the South.

05-04.title: Manaslu
05-04.subtitle: An all-female Japanese team reached the summit of Manaslu in the Himalayas, becoming the first women to climb a peak hig...
05-04.text: An all-female Japanese team reached the summit of Manaslu in the Himalayas, becoming the first women to climb a peak higher than 8,000 metres (26,247 ft) above sea level.

05-04.title: Margaret Thatcher
