05-04.subtitle: Margaret Thatcher became the first female prime minister of the United Kingdom.
05-04.text: Margaret Hilda Thatcher, Baroness Thatcher, was a British stateswoman who served as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom from 1979 to 1990 and Leader of the Conservative Party from 1975 to 1990. She was the longest-serving British prime minister of the 20th century and the first woman to hold the position. As prime minister, she implemented policies that came to be known as Thatcherism.
05-04.location: Prime Minister of the United Kingdom from 1979 to 1990

05-04.title: Falklands War
05-04.subtitle: Falklands War: HMS Sheffield was struck by an Exocet missile, killing 20 sailors and leading to its sinking six days lat...
05-04.text: The Falklands War was a ten-week undeclared war between Argentina and the United Kingdom in 1982 over two British Overseas Territories in the South Atlantic: the Falkland Islands and its territorial dependency, South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands. The conflict began on 2 April 1982, when Argentina invaded and occupied the Falkland Islands, followed by the invasion of South Georgia the next day.

05-04.title: PEPCON disaster
05-04.subtitle: The PEPCON disaster rocks Henderson, Nevada, as tons of Space Shuttle fuel detonate during a fire.
05-04.text: The PEPCON disaster rocks Henderson, Nevada, as tons of Space Shuttle fuel detonate during a fire.

05-04.title: Space Shuttle Atlantis
05-04.subtitle: Space Shuttle Atlantis launches on mission STS-30 to deploy the Venus-bound Magellan space probe.
05-04.text: Space Shuttle Atlantis is a retired Space Shuttle orbiter vehicle which belongs to NASA, the spaceflight and space exploration agency of the United States. Atlantis was manufactured by the Rockwell International company in Southern California and was delivered to the Kennedy Space Center in Eastern Florida in April 1985. Atlantis is the fourth operational and the second-to-last Space Shuttle built. Its maiden flight was STS-51-J made from October 3 to 7, 1985.

05-04.title: Prime minister
05-04.subtitle: Israeli Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin and PLO leader Yasser Arafat sign a peace accord, granting self-rule in the Gaza Strip and Jericho.
05-04.text: Israeli Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin and PLO leader Yasser Arafat sign a peace accord, granting self-rule in the Gaza Strip and Jericho.

05-04.title: London Gets Its First Mayor
05-04.subtitle: A socialist maverick wins a historic vote
05-04.text: Ken Livingstone, the maverick left-wing politician who had once led the Greater London Council, took office as the first directly elected Mayor of London. Running as an independent after being denied the Labour nomination, he won a decisive victory that demonstrated the power of personal politics over party machinery. His creation marked the restoration of city-wide government to London after fourteen years without it, giving the world's greatest city a single democratic voice.

05-04.title: Parliament of Malta
05-04.subtitle: The Parliament of Malta moved from the Grandmaster's Palace to the purpose-built Parliament House (pictured).
05-04.text: The Parliament of Malta moved from the Grandmaster's Palace to the purpose-built Parliament House (pictured).

05-05.title: Constantinople Council Opens
05-05.subtitle: Bishops gather to settle the faith's disputes
05-05.text: Under the soaring domes of Constantinople, Emperor Justinian I convened the Second Council of Constantinople, the fifth ecumenical council of the Christian Church. One hundred sixty-five bishops gathered to resolve the bitter Three Chapters controversy that threatened to split the Church between East and West. Their deliberations, lasting nearly a month, produced condemnations of specific theological writings and reinforced imperial authority over church doctrine, shaping Christianity for centuries.

05-05.title: Helen Redfield Was Born
05-05.subtitle: A geneticist who mapped life's hidden code
05-05.text: In the American heartland, Helen Redfield was born -- a geneticist whose pioneering research would illuminate the mechanisms of heredity. After earning her PhD from UC Berkeley at just twenty-one, she joined the faculty at Stanford and became a National Research Fellow at Columbia. Her decades-long career in genetics, maintaining her maiden name in an era when that was rare, contributed to the foundational understanding of chromosomal inheritance that underpins modern biology.

05-05.title: Bonn–Paris conventions
05-05.subtitle: The General Treaty, by which France, Britain and the United States recognize the sovereignty of West Germany, comes into effect.
05-05.text: The General Treaty, by which France, Britain and the United States recognize the sovereignty of West Germany, comes into effect.

05-05.title: Netherlands Carillon
05-05.subtitle: The Netherlands Carillon was inaugurated in Arlington, Virginia, on the 15th anniversary of Dutch Liberation Day.
05-05.text: The Netherlands Carillon was inaugurated in Arlington, Virginia, on the 15th anniversary of Dutch Liberation Day.

05-05.title: Project Mercury
05-05.subtitle: Project Mercury: Alan Shepard becomes the first American to travel into outer space, on a sub-orbital flight.
05-05.text: Project Mercury was the first human spaceflight program of the United States, running from 1958 through 1963. An early highlight of the Space Race, its goal was to put a man into Earth orbit and return him safely, ideally before the Soviet Union. Taken over from the US Air Force by the newly created civilian space agency NASA, it conducted 20 uncrewed developmental flights, and six successful flights by astronauts.

05-05.title: Secretariat
05-05.subtitle: Secretariat wins the 1973 Kentucky Derby in 1:59.
05-05.text: Secretariat wins the 1973 Kentucky Derby in 1:59.4, an as-yet-unbeaten record.

05-05.title: Special Air Service
05-05.subtitle: The British Special Air Service recaptured the Iranian embassy in London following a six-day siege after Iranian Arab separatists had seized it.
05-05.text: The British Special Air Service recaptured the Iranian embassy in London following a six-day siege after Iranian Arab separatists had seized it.

05-05.title: Carlos Alcaraz Was Born
05-05.subtitle: Tennis meets its youngest-ever Grand Slam master
05-05.text: In El Palmar, Murcia, Carlos Alcaraz Garfia was born into a family of tennis enthusiasts. By nineteen, he had become the youngest world number one in ATP history. By twenty-two, he had completed the Career Grand Slam -- the youngest man ever to do so. His explosive athleticism, creative shot-making, and indomitable competitive spirit have drawn comparisons to every great champion, yet his game is entirely his own. A new era of tennis has begun.

05-05.title: Sudan
05-05.subtitle: The government of Sudan signs an accord with the Sudan Liberation Army.
05-05.text: The government of Sudan signs an accord with the Sudan Liberation Army.

05-05.title: Aeroflot Flight 1492
05-05.subtitle: Aeroflot Flight 1492 was struck by lightning after leaving Moscow's Sheremetyevo International Airport and caught fire d...
05-05.text: Aeroflot Flight 1492 was struck by lightning after leaving Moscow's Sheremetyevo International Airport and caught fire during the subsequent emergency landing attempt, killing 41 people on board.

05-05.title: ABS-CBN
05-05.subtitle: Philippine broadcast network ABS-CBN was forced to go off-air by the National Telecommunications Commission after Congre...
05-05.text: ABS-CBN is a Philippine media and content distribution company. It serves as the flagship media brand of ABS-CBN Corporation, a subsidiary of Lopez Holdings Corporation. Formerly the Philippines' largest free-to-air television network, ABS-CBN has since evolved into a multi-platform content producer and distributor following the expiration and non-renewal of its broadcast franchise in 2020. The company currently syndicates its programming across various platforms, including partner networks, cable channels, streaming services, and digital platforms.

05-05.title: World Health Organization
05-05.subtitle: The World Health Organization declares the end of the COVID-19 pandemic as a global health emergency.
05-05.text: The World Health Organization declares the end of the COVID-19 pandemic as a global health emergency.

05-06.title: Henry II Was Born
05-06.subtitle: The last Ottonian emperor arrives
05-06.text: In the Bavarian town of Abbach, Henry was born -- the man who would become the last ruler of the Ottonian dynasty and Holy Roman Emperor. Ascending to the German throne after the sudden death of his cousin Otto III in 1002, he spent his reign consolidating imperial power and reforming the Church. Crowned emperor by Pope Benedict VIII in 1014, he died without an heir in 1024, ending a dynasty that had shaped Europe for over a century. He was later canonized as a saint.

05-06.title: Gorkhapatra Published
05-06.subtitle: Nepal's oldest newspaper rolls off the press
05-06.text: In Kathmandu, the first issue of Gorkhapatra rolled off the press, launching what would become the oldest continuously running state-owned newspaper in Nepal. Beginning as a modest weekly publication, it would grow into the nation's paper of record, eventually becoming a daily in 1961. For more than a century, Gorkhapatra has documented Nepal's transformation from an isolated Himalayan kingdom to a modern republic, serving as the written conscience of a nation in perpetual change.

05-06.title: Oxford
05-06.subtitle: At Oxford's Iffley Road Track, English runner Roger Bannister became the first person to run the mile in under four minutes.
05-06.text: At Oxford's Iffley Road Track, English runner Roger Bannister became the first person to run the mile in under four minutes.

05-06.title: Wedding of Princess Margaret and Antony
05-06.subtitle: More than 20 million viewers watch the first televised royal wedding when Princess Margaret marries Antony Armstrong-Jones at Westminster Abbey.
05-06.text: The wedding of Princess Margaret and Antony Armstrong-Jones took place on Friday, 6 May 1960 at Westminster Abbey in London. Princess Margaret was the younger sister of Queen Elizabeth II, while Antony Armstrong-Jones was a noted society photographer.

05-06.title: Time
05-06.subtitle: Time magazine published the article "The Thriving Cult of Greed and Power" by Richard Behar criticizing the Church of Sc...
05-06.text: Time magazine published the article "The Thriving Cult of Greed and Power" by Richard Behar criticizing the Church of Scientology, leading to years of legal conflict.

05-06.title: Devolution
05-06.subtitle: The first elections to the devolved Scottish Parliament and Welsh Assembly are held.
05-06.text: The first elections to the devolved Scottish Parliament and Welsh Assembly are held.

05-06.title: Pope Enters a Mosque
05-06.subtitle: An unprecedented gesture of interfaith peace
05-06.text: During a historic visit to Syria, Pope John Paul II removed his shoes and stepped inside the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus, becoming the first pope in history to enter a Muslim house of worship. The gesture stunned the world and electrified the faithful of both religions. Inside the mosque, which houses a shrine to John the Baptist, the Pope prayed alongside Muslim clerics in a powerful symbol of interfaith dialogue that resonated far beyond the ancient walls of Damascus.

05-06.title: Pim Fortuyn
05-06.subtitle: Dutch politician Pim Fortuyn is assassinated following a radio-interview at the Mediapark in Hilversum.
05-06.text: Dutch politician Pim Fortuyn is assassinated following a radio-interview at the Mediapark in Hilversum.

05-06.title: Barrister
05-06.subtitle: British barrister Mark Saunders was shot dead by police after a five-hour siege at his home in Chelsea, London.
05-06.text: A barrister is a type of lawyer in common law jurisdictions that originated from the Inns of Court in the medieval English legal system. Barristers mostly specialise in courtroom advocacy and litigation. Their tasks include arguing cases in courts and tribunals, drafting legal pleadings, researching the law and giving legal opinions.

05-06.title: High-frequency trading
05-06.subtitle: Exacerbated by high-frequency traders using strategies that have since been banned, major U.
05-06.text: Exacerbated by high-frequency traders using strategies that have since been banned, major U.S. stock indices dropped nearly 9 percent and quickly rebounded.

05-06.title: Coronation of Charles III and Camilla
05-06.subtitle: The coronation of Charles III and Camilla as King and Queen of the United Kingdom and the other Commonwealth realms is h...
05-06.text: The coronation of Charles III and Camilla as King and Queen of the United Kingdom and the other Commonwealth realms is held in Westminster Abbey, London.

05-07.title: Jewish Revolt Erupts
05-07.subtitle: Palestine rises against a Roman Caesar
05-07.text: In the Roman province of Syria Palaestina, Jewish communities erupted in revolt against the rule of Constantius Gallus, Caesar of the East. The uprising, sparked by instability across the empire during a wider civil war, saw rebels seize several towns before Roman forces under General Ursicinus crushed the rebellion with characteristic brutality. The revolt was one of the last major Jewish uprisings against Roman rule, a desperate bid for autonomy in an increasingly hostile imperial landscape.

05-07.title: Gary Cooper Was Born
05-07.subtitle: The strong, silent type enters the world
05-07.text: In Helena, Montana, Gary Cooper was born -- the actor who would define the American screen hero for an entire generation. With his tall frame, quiet intensity, and natural charisma, he embodied the ideal of the strong, silent man in films like 'High Noon,' 'Sergeant York,' and 'Mr. Deeds Goes to Town.' Two Academy Awards and a twenty-three-year run as a top-ten box office draw made him the most admired actor of Hollywood's golden age.

05-07.title: World War I
05-07.subtitle: World War I: German submarine U-20 sinks RMS Lusitania, killing 1,199 people, including 128 Americans.
05-07.text: World War I, or the First World War, also known as the Great War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Central Powers. Major areas of conflict included Europe and the Middle East, as well as parts of Africa and the Asia-Pacific. The war saw important developments in weaponry including tanks, aircraft, artillery, machine guns, and chemical weapons.

05-07.title: Treaty of Moscow
05-07.subtitle: Treaty of Moscow: Soviet Russia recognizes the independence of the Democratic Republic of Georgia only to invade the country six months later.
05-07.text: Treaty of Moscow: Soviet Russia recognizes the independence of the Democratic Republic of Georgia only to invade the country six months later.

05-07.title: Integrated circuit
05-07.subtitle: The concept of the integrated circuit, the basis for all modern computers, is first published by Geoffrey Dummer.
05-07.text: The concept of the integrated circuit, the basis for all modern computers, is first published by Geoffrey Dummer.

05-07.title: Dungannon
05-07.subtitle: A convoy of circus wagons carrying lions, horses, ponies and an ass lost control and overturned into some shop fronts in Dungannon, Northern Ireland.
05-07.text: A convoy of circus wagons carrying lions, horses, ponies and an ass lost control and overturned into some shop fronts in Dungannon, Northern Ireland.

05-07.title: Cold War
05-07.subtitle: Cold War: U-2 Crisis of 1960: Soviet leader Nikita Khrushchev announces that his nation is holding American U-2 pilot Gary Powers.
05-07.text: The Cold War was a period of international geopolitical rivalry between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) and their respective allies, the capitalist Western Bloc and communist Eastern Bloc, which began in the aftermath of the Second World War and ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
05-07.location: 1947–1991 geopolitical rivalry between US and USSR

05-07.title: Space Shuttle program
05-07.subtitle: Space Shuttle program: The Space Shuttle Endeavour is launched on its first mission, STS-49.
05-07.text: The Space Shuttle program was the fourth human spaceflight program carried out by the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), which accomplished routine transportation for Earth-to-orbit crew and cargo from 1981 to 2011. Its official program name was Space Transportation System (STS), taken from a 1969 plan for a system of reusable spacecraft where it was the only item funded for development, as a proposed nuclear shuttle in the plan was cancelled in 1972.

05-07.title: Kosovo War
05-07.subtitle: Kosovo War: NATO bombing of Yugoslavia, the United States bombed the Chinese embassy in Belgrade.
05-07.text: Kosovo War: NATO bombing of Yugoslavia, the United States bombed the Chinese embassy in Belgrade.

05-07.title: Putin Inaugurated President
05-07.subtitle: Russia's new strongman takes the oath
05-07.text: In the gilded halls of the Kremlin, Vladimir Putin was inaugurated as president of Russia, completing a meteoric rise from obscure KGB officer to leader of the world's largest country. Having been appointed prime minister just months earlier by an ailing Boris Yeltsin, Putin won the presidential election promising stability and order after the chaos of the 1990s. His inauguration began a period of centralized power that would reshape Russia and its role in the world.

05-07.title: Napier, New Zealand
05-07.subtitle: Police in Napier, New Zealand, began a 40-hour siege of the home of a former New Zealand Army member who had shot at off...
05-07.text: Police in Napier, New Zealand, began a 40-hour siege of the home of a former New Zealand Army member who had shot at officers during the routine execution of a search warrant.

05-07.title: Neanderthal
05-07.subtitle: A draft sequence of the Neanderthal genome was published, demonstrating that today's humans have Neanderthal ancestors.
05-07.text: A draft sequence of the Neanderthal genome was published, demonstrating that today's humans have Neanderthal ancestors.

05-08.title: Battle of Jinyang Ends
05-08.subtitle: A Chinese power struggle reshapes an era
05-08.text: In the waning days of China's Spring and Autumn period, the house of Zhao defeated the powerful house of Zhi at the Battle of Jinyang, ending a siege that had lasted years. The victors breached the dikes of the Jin River, flooding Zhi's encampment and destroying his army. This decisive clash between the elite families of the State of Jin paved the way for the Warring States period, one of the most transformative -- and violent -- eras in Chinese history.

05-08.title: Turkey Stearnes Was Born
05-08.subtitle: The Negro leagues' greatest outfielder arrives
05-08.text: In Nashville, Tennessee, Norman Thomas Stearnes was born -- the man who would become one of the greatest players in Negro League history. Nicknamed 'Turkey' for his distinctive running style, he patrolled center field with supernatural grace for eighteen seasons, most memorably with the Detroit Stars. His powerful bat and elegant play made him a legend in a parallel baseball universe. His belated induction into the Baseball Hall of Fame in 2000 brought overdue recognition to a forgotten giant.

05-08.title: Tollund Man
05-08.subtitle: The Tollund Man (pictured), a naturally mummified corpse, was discovered in a peat bog near Silkeborg, Denmark.
05-08.text: The Tollund Man (pictured), a naturally mummified corpse, was discovered in a peat bog near Silkeborg, Denmark.

05-08.title: South Vietnam
05-08.subtitle: South Vietnamese president Ngô Đình Diệm began a state visit to the United States, his regime's main sponsor.
05-08.text: South Vietnamese president Ngô Đình Diệm began a state visit to the United States, his regime's main sponsor.

05-08.title: Huế
05-08.subtitle: In Huế, South Vietnam, soldiers opened fire into a crowd of Buddhists protesting against a government ban on the flying ...
05-08.text: In Huế, South Vietnam, soldiers opened fire into a crowd of Buddhists protesting against a government ban on the flying of the Buddhist flag on Phật Đản, killing nine and sparking the Buddhist crisis.

05-08.title: Hard Hat Riot
05-08.subtitle: Construction workers attacked students and others who were protesting the Vietnam War in New York City.
05-08.text: Construction workers attacked students and others who were protesting the Vietnam War in New York City.

05-08.title: The New Revolution
05-08.subtitle: The rollercoaster The New Revolution, the first steel coaster with a vertical loop, opens at Six Flags Magic Mountain.
05-08.text: The rollercoaster The New Revolution, the first steel coaster with a vertical loop, opens at Six Flags Magic Mountain.

05-08.title: Mount Everest
05-08.subtitle: The first ascent of Mount Everest without supplemental oxygen, by Reinhold Messner and Peter Habeler.
05-08.text: The first ascent of Mount Everest without supplemental oxygen, by Reinhold Messner and Peter Habeler.

05-08.title: Thames Barrier
05-08.subtitle: The Thames Barrier is officially opened, preventing the floodplain of most of Greater London from being flooded except under extreme circumstances.
05-08.text: The Thames Barrier is a retractable barrier system built to protect the floodplain of most of Greater London from exceptionally high tides and storm surges moving up from the North Sea. It has been operational since 1982. When needed, it is closed (raised) during high tide; at low tide, it can be opened to restore the river's flow towards the sea.
05-08.location: Flood defence system for London, England

05-08.title: Special Air Service
05-08.subtitle: The SAS kills eight Provisional Irish Republican Army volunteers and a civilian during an ambush in Loughgall, Northern Ireland.
05-08.text: The SAS kills eight Provisional Irish Republican Army volunteers and a civilian during an ambush in Loughgall, Northern Ireland.

05-08.title: Jordyn Huitema Was Born
05-08.subtitle: Canada's next soccer star takes shape
05-08.text: In Chilliwack, British Columbia, Jordyn Pamela Huitema was born into a family of athletes. She would develop into one of Canada's most exciting soccer forwards, earning caps for the national team while still a teenager. Her journey from the youth fields of British Columbia to professional football in Europe and the NWSL represents the growing global reach of women's football and Canada's emergence as a force in the international game.

05-09.title: Athanasius Leads Alexandria
05-09.subtitle: Christianity's fiercest defender takes charge
05-09.text: In the ancient Egyptian metropolis of Alexandria, Athanasius was elected patriarch, beginning one of the most turbulent and consequential episcopacies in Christian history. Over forty-five years, he would be exiled five times by four different Roman emperors for his unyielding defense of Trinitarian doctrine against Arianism. His theological writings and fierce personal courage earned him the title 'Athanasius Contra Mundum' -- Athanasius against the world -- and helped establish orthodox Christian theology.

05-09.title: Maria Malicka Was Born
05-09.subtitle: A star of Polish stage and screen
05-09.text: In Poland, Maria Malicka was born -- an actress whose career would span the entire arc of Polish cinema from the silent era to the post-war period. Appearing in ten films between 1927 and 1966, she moved gracefully between stage and screen, embodying the resilience of Polish culture through decades of occupation, war, and reconstruction. Her four-decade career made her a living link between the lost world of interwar Poland and the country's rebirth.

05-09.title: Food and Drug Administration
05-09.subtitle: The Food and Drug Administration announces it will approve birth control as an additional indication for Searle's Enovid...
05-09.text: The Food and Drug Administration announces it will approve birth control as an additional indication for Searle's Enovid, making Enovid the world's first approved oral contraceptive pill.

05-09.title: Ngô Đình Cẩn
05-09.subtitle: Ngô Đình Cẩn, a younger brother and the confidant of South Vietnam's first president, Ngô Đình Diệm, was executed by Nguyễn Khánh's military junta.
05-09.text: Ngô Đình Cẩn was the younger brother and confidant of South Vietnam's first president, Ngô Đình Diệm, and an important member of the Diệm government. Diệm put Cẩn in charge of central Vietnam, stretching from Phan Thiết in the south to the border at the 17th parallel, with Cẩn ruling the region as a virtual dictator.

05-09.title: Carlos Lamarca
05-09.subtitle: Carlos Lamarca leads the first urban guerrilla action against the military dictatorship of Brazil in São Paulo, by robbing two banks.
05-09.text: Carlos Lamarca leads the first urban guerrilla action against the military dictatorship of Brazil in São Paulo, by robbing two banks.

05-09.title: Florida
05-09.subtitle: In Florida, United States, Liberian freighter MV Summit Venture collides with the Sunshine Skyway Bridge over Tampa Bay,...
05-09.text: In Florida, United States, Liberian freighter MV Summit Venture collides with the Sunshine Skyway Bridge over Tampa Bay, making a 430-meter (1,400 ft) section of the southbound span collapse. Thirty-five people in six cars and a Greyhound bus fall 46 metres (150 ft) into the water and die.

05-09.title: Armenia
05-09.subtitle: Armenian forces capture Shusha, marking a major turning point in the First Nagorno-Karabakh War.
05-09.text: Armenian forces capture Shusha, marking a major turning point in the First Nagorno-Karabakh War.

05-09.title: Accra Stadium Stampede
05-09.subtitle: Tear gas turns a football match into a tragedy
05-09.text: At the Ohene Djan Stadium in Accra, Ghana, police fired tear gas into the stands to quell unrest during a football match between Hearts of Oak and Asante Kotoko. The gas triggered a massive stampede as panicked spectators rushed for the exits, crushing 126 people to death in one of the worst stadium disasters in African history. The tragedy exposed the deadly consequences of heavy-handed crowd control and prompted soul-searching about safety standards in African football.

05-09.title: Pope Benedict XVI
05-09.subtitle: Pope Benedict XVI began the beatification process for his predecessor Pope John Paul II, waiving the standard five years...
05-09.text: Pope Benedict XVI was head of the Catholic Church and sovereign of Vatican City from 2005 until his resignation in 2013. Following his resignation, he chose to be known as "pope emeritus", a title he held until his death on 31 December 2022.
05-09.location: Head of the Catholic Church from 2005 to 2013

05-09.title: Yakovlev SJ-100
05-09.subtitle: The pilots of a Sukhoi Superjet, ignoring alerts from the terrain warning system, crashed the aircraft into Mount Salak ...
05-09.text: The pilots of a Sukhoi Superjet, ignoring alerts from the terrain warning system, crashed the aircraft into Mount Salak in Indonesia, resulting in the deaths of all 45 people on board.

05-09.title: Russo-Ukrainian War
05-09.subtitle: Russo-Ukrainian War: United States President Joe Biden signs the 2022 Lend-Lease Act into law, a rebooted World War II-e...
05-09.text: Russo-Ukrainian War: United States President Joe Biden signs the 2022 Lend-Lease Act into law, a rebooted World War II-era policy expediting American equipment to Ukraine and other Eastern European countries.

05-10.title: First Sunspot Recorded
05-10.subtitle: Chinese astronomers spot a blemish on the sun
05-10.text: During the Han dynasty, Chinese astronomers peered at the sun and recorded what appears to be the first precisely dated observation of a sunspot in human history. Using jade rings and other filtering methods, they documented a dark mark on the solar disc, noting it with meticulous care. This observation, made more than two thousand years ago, demonstrates the extraordinary sophistication of ancient Chinese astronomy and began humanity's long quest to understand our nearest star.

05-10.title: Cecilia Payne Was Born
05-10.subtitle: She revealed what the stars are made of
05-10.text: In Wendover, England, Cecilia Helena Payne was born -- the woman who would make one of the most brilliant discoveries in astrophysics. Her 1925 doctoral thesis at Harvard demonstrated that stars are composed primarily of hydrogen and helium, overturning the prevailing assumption that their composition mirrored Earth's. Initially dismissed, her conclusion was later proven correct and is now considered one of the most important findings in the history of astronomy.

05-10.title: Sony
05-10.subtitle: Sony introduces the Betamax videocassette recorder.
05-10.text: Sony introduces the Betamax videocassette recorder.

05-10.title: Nelson Mandela
05-10.subtitle: Nelson Mandela is inaugurated as South Africa's first black president.
05-10.text: Nelson Mandela is inaugurated as South Africa's first black president.

05-10.title: Bae Jin-young Was Born
05-10.subtitle: A K-pop star emerges from the spotlight
05-10.text: In South Korea, Bae Jin-young was born into the golden age of K-pop. Rising to fame through the fiercely competitive reality show Produce 101, he finished tenth in the final rankings and joined the mega-group Wanna One. After the group's disbandment, he debuted with CIX, continuing to build a devoted fanbase across Asia and beyond. His journey from aspiring trainee to international performer reflects the rigorous, dream-driven world of Korean pop music.

05-10.title: Vladimir Arutyunian
05-10.subtitle: Vladimir Arutyunian attempted to assassinate U.
05-10.text: Vladimir Arutyunian attempted to assassinate U.S. president George W. Bush and Georgian president Mikheil Saakashvili in Tbilisi using a hand grenade, which failed to detonate.

05-10.title: One World Trade Center
05-10.subtitle: One World Trade Center (pictured) in New York City, the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere, was topped out at a height of 1,776 feet (541 m).
05-10.text: One World Trade Center, also known as One WTC and the Freedom Tower, is the main building of the rebuilt World Trade Center complex in Lower Manhattan, New York City. Designed by David Childs of Skidmore, Owings & Merrill, One World Trade Center is the tallest building in the United States, the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere, and the seventh-tallest in the world.

05-10.title: Syrian civil war
05-10.subtitle: Syrian civil war: The Syrian Democratic Forces, assisted by the U.
05-10.text: The Syrian civil war was an armed conflict that began with the Syrian revolution in March 2011, when popular discontent with the Ba'athist regime ruled by Bashar al-Assad triggered large-scale protests and pro-democracy rallies across Syria, as part of the wider Arab Spring. The Assad regime responded to the protests with lethal force, which led to a series of defections, the emergence of armed opposition groups, and the civilian uprising descending into a civil war.

05-10.title: Elizabeth II
05-10.subtitle: Queen Elizabeth II misses the State Opening of Parliament for the first time in 59 years.
05-10.text: Elizabeth II was Queen of the United Kingdom and other Commonwealth realms from 6 February 1952 until her death in 2022. She was queen regnant of 32 sovereign states during her lifetime and was the monarch of 15 realms at her death. Her reign of 70 years and 214 days is the longest of any British monarch, the second-longest of any sovereign state, and the longest of any queen regnant in history.
05-10.location: Queen of the United Kingdom from 1952 to 2022

05-11.title: Constantinople Dedicated
05-11.subtitle: A new Rome rises on the Bosphorus
05-11.text: Emperor Constantine the Great stood at the crossroads of Europe and Asia and dedicated his magnificent new capital, christening it New Rome -- though the world would know it forever by his name: Constantinople. The rebuilt and greatly expanded city of Byzantium, adorned with treasures plundered from across the empire, became the seat of Roman power in the East. For over a thousand years, its massive walls would guard the gateway between continents, making it the most coveted city on Earth.

05-11.title: Rose Auslander Was Born
05-11.subtitle: Poetry survives where empires do not
05-11.text: In Czernowitz, a city that would belong to four different nations during her lifetime, Rose Auslander was born into a thriving Jewish community. A poet of extraordinary lyrical power, she wrote in both German and English, her verses shaped by the trauma of the Holocaust and the rootlessness of exile. From the Austro-Hungarian Empire to Romania to America to West Germany, she carried her language like a homeland, proving that poetry can survive even when everything else is lost.

05-11.title: Deep Blue
05-11.subtitle: Deep Blue, a chess-playing supercomputer, defeats Garry Kasparov in the last game of the rematch, becoming the first com...
05-11.text: Deep Blue, a chess-playing supercomputer, defeats Garry Kasparov in the last game of the rematch, becoming the first computer to beat a world-champion chess player in a classic match format.

05-11.title: Chechen Ambush Strikes
05-11.subtitle: Separatist fighters strike beyond Chechnya's borders
05-11.text: In the Republic of Ingushetia, Chechen separatist fighters launched a devastating ambush against Russian paramilitary forces during the Second Chechen War. The attack demonstrated the separatists' ability to strike beyond Chechnya's borders, extending the conflict into neighboring regions of the North Caucasus. The ambush was part of a broader guerrilla campaign that would drag on for nearly a decade, defying Russia's overwhelming military superiority.

05-11.title: Space Shuttle Atlantis
05-11.subtitle: Space Shuttle Atlantis is launched on the final mission to service the Hubble Space Telescope.
05-11.text: Space Shuttle Atlantis is a retired Space Shuttle orbiter vehicle which belongs to NASA, the spaceflight and space exploration agency of the United States. Atlantis was manufactured by the Rockwell International company in Southern California and was delivered to the Kennedy Space Center in Eastern Florida in April 1985. Atlantis is the fourth operational and the second-to-last Space Shuttle built. Its maiden flight was STS-51-J made from October 3 to 7, 1985.

05-11.title: David Cameron
05-11.subtitle: David Cameron takes office as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom as the Conservatives and Liberal Democrats form the c...
05-11.text: David Cameron takes office as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom as the Conservatives and Liberal Democrats form the country's first coalition government since the Second World War.

05-11.title: 2011 Lorca earthquake
05-11.subtitle: An earthquake registering Mw 5.
05-11.text: An earthquake registering Mw 5.1, the worst to hit the region for more than 50 years, struck near Lorca, Spain.

05-11.title: Tatmadaw
05-11.subtitle: The Burmese military executes at least 37 villagers during the Mon Taing Pin massacre in Sagaing, Myanmar.
05-11.text: The Burmese military executes at least 37 villagers during the Mon Taing Pin massacre in Sagaing, Myanmar.

05-11.title: Eurovision Song Contest 2024
05-11.subtitle: The 68th edition of the Eurovision Song Contest is held in Malmö, Sweden.
05-11.text: The 68th edition of the Eurovision Song Contest is held in Malmö, Sweden. Nemo from Switzerland wins with their song "The Code", making them the contest's first non-binary winner.

05-12.title: Pope Stephen I Elected
05-12.subtitle: A new pope confronts an old heresy
05-12.text: In Rome, Stephen I succeeded Lucius I as the twenty-third pope of the Catholic Church and immediately took a firm stand against Novatianism, a rigorist movement that denied reconciliation to Christians who had lapsed during persecution. His three-year pontificate would be marked by heated disputes over the validity of baptisms performed by heretics -- a controversy that pitted him against the formidable Cyprian of Carthage and foreshadowed centuries of theological debate about sacramental authority.

05-12.title: Helene Weigel Was Born
05-12.subtitle: Brecht's partner in art and revolution
05-12.text: In Vienna, Helene Weigel was born -- the actress who would become the most important interpreter of Bertolt Brecht's theatrical revolution. As Brecht's wife and artistic partner, she brought his epic theater to life with performances of searing emotional truth, most famously as Mother Courage. After Brecht's death, she led the Berliner Ensemble, transforming it into one of the most important theater companies in the world and preserving his radical legacy for future generations.

05-12.title: World War II
05-12.subtitle: World War II: Second Battle of Kharkov: In eastern Ukraine, Red Army forces under Marshal Semyon Timoshenko launch a maj...
05-12.text: World War II or the Second World War was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers. Nearly all of the world's countries participated. Tanks and aircraft played major roles, the latter enabling the strategic bombing of cities and delivery of the only nuclear weapons used in war. World War II was the deadliest conflict in history, causing the death of 60 to 75 million people.

05-12.title: Spacecraft
05-12.subtitle: The Soviet spacecraft Luna 5 crashes on the Moon.
05-12.text: The Soviet spacecraft Luna 5 crashes on the Moon.

05-12.title: Vietnam War
05-12.subtitle: Vietnam War: The 1st Australian Task Force began the defence of Fire Support Base Coral in the largest unit-level action...
05-12.text: The Vietnam War was an armed conflict in Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia fought between North Vietnam and South Vietnam and their allies. North Vietnam was supported by the Soviet Union and China, while South Vietnam was supported by the United States and other anti-communist nations. The conflict was the second of the Indochina wars and a proxy war of the Cold War between the Soviet Union and US.
05-12.location: War in Southeast Asia from 1955 to 1975

05-12.title: Mayaguez incident
05-12.subtitle: The Cambodian navy seized the American container ship SS Mayaguez in what they claimed to be Cambodian territorial waters.
05-12.text: The Mayaguez incident took place between Kampuchea and the United States from 12 to 15 May 1975, less than a month after the Khmer Rouge took control of the capital Phnom Penh ousting the U.S.-backed Khmer Republic. After the Khmer Rouge seized the U.S. merchant vessel SS Mayaguez in a disputed maritime area, the U.S. mounted a hastily prepared rescue operation. U.S.
05-12.location: Last battle of American involvement in Southeast Asia

05-12.title: Coppergate Helmet
05-12.subtitle: The Coppergate Helmet (pictured), the best preserved of the six known Anglo-Saxon helmets, was discovered.
05-12.text: The Coppergate Helmet (pictured), the best preserved of the six known Anglo-Saxon helmets, was discovered.

05-12.title: Trisakti shootings
05-12.subtitle: Four students were shot and killed by Indonesian soldiers at Trisakti University in Jakarta, which led to widespread rio...
05-12.text: The Trisakti shootings, also known as the Trisakti tragedy, took place at Trisakti University, Jakarta, Indonesia, on 12 May 1998. At a demonstration demanding President Suharto's resignation, Indonesian Army soldiers opened fire on unarmed protestors. Four students, Elang Mulia Lesmana, Heri Hertanto, Hafidin Royan, and Hendriawan Sie, were killed and dozens more were injured. The shootings triggered a riot and nationwide revolutionary wave, leading to Suharto's resignation later the same month.

05-12.title: Issa Kabore Was Born
05-12.subtitle: Burkina Faso's fleet-footed defender arrives
05-12.text: In Burkina Faso, Issa Kabore was born -- a footballer whose blistering speed and relentless energy would carry him from the dusty pitches of West Africa to the elite levels of European football. Signed by Manchester City, he has developed through loan spells across England's football pyramid, bringing his explosive pace to the right-back position. His rise represents the growing pipeline of talent from West Africa to the world's top leagues.

05-12.title: Jimmy Carter
05-12.subtitle: Former US President Jimmy Carter arrives in Cuba for a five-day visit with Fidel Castro, becoming the first President of...
05-12.text: James Earl Carter Jr. was an American politician and humanitarian who served as the 39th president of the United States from 1977 to 1981. A member of the Democratic Party, Carter served from 1971 to 1975 as the 76th governor of Georgia and from 1963 to 1967 in the Georgia State Senate. He lived longer than any other president in U.S. history, reaching age 100.
05-12.location: President of the United States from 1977 to 1981

05-12.title: United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement
05-12.subtitle: U.
05-12.text: U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement conducts the largest-ever raid of a workplace in Postville, Iowa, arresting nearly 400 immigrants for identity theft and document fraud.

05-12.title: WannaCry ransomware attack
05-12.subtitle: The WannaCry ransomware attack impacts over 400,000 computers worldwide, targeting computers of the United Kingdom's Nat...
05-12.text: The WannaCry ransomware attack impacts over 400,000 computers worldwide, targeting computers of the United Kingdom's National Health Services and Telefónica computers.

05-13.title: Hugh of Cluny Was Born
05-13.subtitle: The monk who counseled popes and kings
05-13.text: In the castle of Semur-en-Brionnais, Hugh was born into the Burgundian nobility -- but he would trade his aristocratic inheritance for monastic robes. Becoming Abbot of Cluny at just twenty-five, he led the most powerful monastery in Christendom for sixty years. His counsel was sought by popes and kings alike, and under his leadership, the Cluniac reform movement reshaped the medieval Church, promoting liturgical beauty, monastic discipline, and papal independence from secular rulers.

05-13.title: India's Fifth President Born
05-13.subtitle: A lawyer destined for the highest office
05-13.text: In Havildar Patti, Assam, Fakhruddin Ali Ahmed was born into a prominent Muslim family. His path from law to politics would lead him to the presidency of India from 1974 to 1977, a tenure marked by the controversial declaration of Emergency by Prime Minister Indira Gandhi. As the constitutional head of state who signed the Emergency proclamation, his legacy remains intertwined with one of the most debated episodes in Indian democratic history.

05-13.title: 1950 British Grand Prix
05-13.subtitle: The inaugural Formula One World Championship race takes place at Silverstone Circuit.
05-13.text: The 1950 British Grand Prix, formally known as The Royal Automobile Club Grand Prix d'Europe Incorporating The British Grand Prix, was a Formula One motor race held on 13 May 1950 at the Silverstone Circuit in Silverstone, England. It was the first World Championship Formula One race, as well as the fifth British Grand Prix, and the third to be held at Silverstone after motor racing resumed after World War II.
05-13.location: Formula One motor race held in 1950

05-13.title: Rajya Sabha
05-13.subtitle: The Rajya Sabha, the upper house of the Parliament of India, holds its first sitting.
05-13.text: The Rajya Sabha, the upper house of the Parliament of India, holds its first sitting.

05-13.title: Ben Carlin
05-13.subtitle: Ben Carlin becomes the first (and only) person to circumnavigate the world by amphibious vehicle, having travelled over ...
05-13.text: Ben Carlin becomes the first (and only) person to circumnavigate the world by amphibious vehicle, having travelled over 17,000 kilometres (11,000 mi) by sea and 62,000 kilometres (39,000 mi) by land during a ten-year journey.

05-13.title: 13 May incident
05-13.subtitle: Sino-Malay race riots began in Kuala Lumpur, leaving at least 190 people dead, with the government declaring a state of ...
05-13.text: Sino-Malay race riots began in Kuala Lumpur, leaving at least 190 people dead, with the government declaring a state of emergency and suspending the Parliament of Malaysia until 1971.

05-13.title: The Troubles
05-13.subtitle: The Troubles: A car bomb planted by Ulster loyalists exploded outside a crowded pub in Belfast, Northern Ireland, beginn...
05-13.text: The Troubles were an ethno-nationalist conflict in Northern Ireland that lasted for about 30 years from the late 1960s to 1998. Also known internationally as the Northern Ireland conflict, it began in the late 1960s and is usually deemed to have ended with the Good Friday Agreement of 1998. Although the Troubles mostly took place in Northern Ireland, at times violence spilled over into parts of the Republic of Ireland, England, and mainland Europe.

05-13.title: Kosovo War
05-13.subtitle: Kosovo War: NATO bombs the village of Koriša, killing at least 87 people.
05-13.text: Kosovo War: NATO bombs the village of Koriša, killing at least 87 people.

05-13.title: Enschede Fireworks Blast
05-13.subtitle: A quiet Dutch city is ripped apart
05-13.text: In the peaceful Dutch city of Enschede, a fireworks storage facility erupted in a catastrophic explosion that killed twenty-three people, injured nearly a thousand, and destroyed four hundred homes. The blast sent a mushroom cloud over the city and was felt kilometers away. The disaster exposed failures in safety regulation and zoning that had allowed a massive stockpile of pyrotechnics to exist in a residential neighborhood, sparking national outrage and sweeping regulatory reform.

05-13.title: Ministry of Internal Affairs
05-13.subtitle: Uzbek Interior Ministry and State Security Service troops fired at protesters in Andijan, killing between 187 and 1,500 people.
05-13.text: Uzbek Interior Ministry and State Security Service troops fired at protesters in Andijan, killing between 187 and 1,500 people.

05-13.title: Indian Mujahideen
05-13.subtitle: Nine bombs placed by the Indian Mujahideen, then an unknown terrorist group, exploded in a 15-minute period in Jaipur, I...
05-13.text: Nine bombs placed by the Indian Mujahideen, then an unknown terrorist group, exploded in a 15-minute period in Jaipur, India, killing 80 people and injuring more than 200 others.

05-13.title: Eurovision Song Contest
05-13.subtitle: Eurovision Song Contest: Portugal won the contest for the first time, with the song "Amar pelos dois," performed by Salvador Sobral (pictured).
05-13.text: Eurovision Song Contest: Portugal won the contest for the first time, with the song "Amar pelos dois," performed by Salvador Sobral (pictured).

05-14.title: Henry Named Junior King
05-14.subtitle: A father secures his dynasty's future
05-14.text: In the royal court of France, King Robert II the Pious named his son Henry as junior King of the Franks, securing the Capetian succession. This act of political foresight, following the tradition established by Robert's own father Hugh Capet, ensured a smooth transfer of power and helped cement the Capetian dynasty's grip on the French throne. The practice of crowning heirs during a king's lifetime would sustain the dynasty for centuries.

05-14.title: Paris Olympics Begin
05-14.subtitle: The Games hide inside a World's Fair
05-14.text: Amid the splendor of the Paris Exposition Universelle, the World Amateur Championship opened -- an event later recognized as the 1900 Olympic Games, though few participants even knew they were competing in an Olympics. Spread across months and submerged within the larger fair, these Games were so disorganized that some athletes went to their graves unaware they had been Olympians. It remains one of the most chaotic and charming chapters in the history of the modern Games.

05-14.title: David Ben-Gurion
05-14.subtitle: David Ben-Gurion publicly read the Israeli Declaration of Independence at a building later called Independence Hall in Tel Aviv.
05-14.text: David Ben-Gurion publicly read the Israeli Declaration of Independence at a building later called Independence Hall in Tel Aviv.

05-14.title: Talyllyn Railway
05-14.subtitle: Trains ran on the Talyllyn Railway in Wales for the first time since its preservation, making it the first railway in th...
05-14.text: Trains ran on the Talyllyn Railway in Wales for the first time since its preservation, making it the first railway in the world to be operated by volunteers.

05-14.title: Cold War
05-14.subtitle: Cold War: Eight Communist bloc countries, including the Soviet Union, sign a mutual defense treaty called the Warsaw Pact.
05-14.text: The Cold War was a period of international geopolitical rivalry between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) and their respective allies, the capitalist Western Bloc and communist Eastern Bloc, which began in the aftermath of the Second World War and ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
05-14.location: 1947–1991 geopolitical rivalry between US and USSR

05-14.title: Civil rights movement
05-14.subtitle: Civil rights movement: A white mob twice attacks a Freedom Riders bus near Anniston, Alabama, before fire-bombing the bu...
05-14.text: Civil rights movement: A white mob twice attacks a Freedom Riders bus near Anniston, Alabama, before fire-bombing the bus and attacking the civil rights protesters who flee the burning vehicle.

05-14.title: Skylab
05-14.subtitle: Skylab, the United States' first space station, is launched.
05-14.text: Skylab, the United States' first space station, is launched.

05-14.title: Salvadoran Civil War
05-14.subtitle: Salvadoran Civil War: Refugees trying to flee El Salvador across the Sumpul River to Honduras were attacked by both Salv...
05-14.text: The Salvadoran Civil War was a twelve-year civil war in El Salvador that was fought between the government of El Salvador, backed by the United States, and the Farabundo Martí National Liberation Front (FMLN), a coalition of left-wing guerilla groups backed by Cuba under Fidel Castro as well as the Soviet Union. A coup on 15 October 1979 followed by government killings of anti-coup protesters is widely seen as the start of civil war.

05-14.title: Jack Hughes Was Born
05-14.subtitle: America's next hockey prodigy emerges
05-14.text: In Orlando, Florida -- an unlikely birthplace for a hockey star -- Jack Rowden Hughes entered the world. He would develop into the most coveted prospect in the 2019 NHL draft, selected first overall by the New Jersey Devils. A product of the U.S. National Development Team, his exceptional skating, vision, and playmaking ability have established him as one of the most dynamic centers in the NHL, carrying the hopes of a franchise on his young shoulders.

05-14.title: 2008 UEFA Cup final
05-14.subtitle: On the day of the UEFA Cup Final, violence erupted between football hooligan supporters of both teams and the Greater Ma...
05-14.text: On the day of the UEFA Cup Final, violence erupted between football hooligan supporters of both teams and the Greater Manchester Police, resulting in 39 arrests and 39 injured officers.

05-14.title: Space Shuttle Atlantis
05-14.subtitle: Space Shuttle Atlantis launches on the STS-132 mission to deliver the first shuttle-launched Russian ISS component — Rassvet.
05-14.text: Space Shuttle Atlantis is a retired Space Shuttle orbiter vehicle which belongs to NASA, the spaceflight and space exploration agency of the United States. Atlantis was manufactured by the Rockwell International company in Southern California and was delivered to the Kennedy Space Center in Eastern Florida in April 1985. Atlantis is the fourth operational and the second-to-last Space Shuttle built. Its maiden flight was STS-51-J made from October 3 to 7, 1985.

05-14.title: Zhurong
05-14.subtitle: China successfully lands Zhurong, the country's first Mars rover.
05-14.text: China successfully lands Zhurong, the country's first Mars rover.

05-15.title: Liu Bei Claims the Throne
05-15.subtitle: A sandal-weaver becomes an emperor
05-15.text: In the ancient Chinese city of Chengdu, Liu Bei -- a man who claimed descent from the Han imperial family but had started life weaving straw sandals -- proclaimed himself emperor of Shu Han, declaring his state the legitimate successor to the fallen Han dynasty. His rise from poverty to imperial power, aided by the legendary strategist Zhuge Liang and the oath-brothers Guan Yu and Zhang Fei, became one of the most celebrated stories in Chinese literature and culture.

05-15.title: Ida Rhodes Was Born
05-15.subtitle: A woman who taught machines to think
05-15.text: In a Jewish community in Ukraine, Ida Rhodes was born -- the mathematician who would become a pioneer of computer programming in the United States. Emigrating to America as a child, she rose to become one of the most influential women in early computing, working at the National Bureau of Standards where she helped develop the first computer program for the Social Security Administration. Her work laid the groundwork for the digital systems that now govern modern life.

05-15.title: Pinewood derby
05-15.subtitle: Don Murphy organized the first pinewood derby, an event for Cub Scouts of the Boy Scouts of America where wooden cars built by the scouts are raced.
05-15.text: Don Murphy organized the first pinewood derby, an event for Cub Scouts of the Boy Scouts of America where wooden cars built by the scouts are raced.

05-15.title: Malden Island
05-15.subtitle: At Malden Island in the Pacific Ocean, Britain tests its first hydrogen bomb in Operation Grapple.
05-15.text: At Malden Island in the Pacific Ocean, Britain tests its first hydrogen bomb in Operation Grapple.

05-15.title: Project Mercury
05-15.subtitle: Project Mercury: The launch of the final Mercury mission, Mercury-Atlas 9 with astronaut Gordon Cooper on board.
05-15.text: Project Mercury was the first human spaceflight program of the United States, running from 1958 through 1963. An early highlight of the Space Race, its goal was to put a man into Earth orbit and return him safely, ideally before the Soviet Union. Taken over from the US Air Force by the newly created civilian space agency NASA, it conducted 20 uncrewed developmental flights, and six successful flights by astronauts.

05-15.title: Tôn Thất Đính
05-15.subtitle: Disapproving of General Tôn Thất Đính's handling of the Buddhist Uprising, South Vietnamese prime minister Nguyễn Cao Kỳ...
05-15.text: Disapproving of General Tôn Thất Đính's handling of the Buddhist Uprising, South Vietnamese prime minister Nguyễn Cao Kỳ ordered an attack on his forces and ousted Đính from his post.

05-15.title: Ma'alot massacre
05-15.subtitle: Ma'alot massacre: Members of the Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine attack and take hostages at an Israeli...
05-15.text: Ma'alot massacre: Members of the Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine attack and take hostages at an Israeli school; a total of 31 people are killed, including 22 schoolchildren.

05-15.title: Vincent van Gogh
05-15.subtitle: Vincent van Gogh's Portrait of Dr.
05-15.text: Vincent van Gogh's Portrait of Dr. Gachet was sold at auction at Christie's in New York for US$82.5 million, making it the world's most expensive painting at the time.

05-15.title: Space Shuttle Atlantis
05-15.subtitle: The Space Shuttle Atlantis launches on STS-84 to dock with the Russian space station Mir.
05-15.text: Space Shuttle Atlantis is a retired Space Shuttle orbiter vehicle which belongs to NASA, the spaceflight and space exploration agency of the United States. Atlantis was manufactured by the Rockwell International company in Southern California and was delivered to the Kennedy Space Center in Eastern Florida in April 1985. Atlantis is the fourth operational and the second-to-last Space Shuttle built. Its maiden flight was STS-51-J made from October 3 to 7, 1985.

05-15.title: Dayana Yastremska Was Born
05-15.subtitle: Ukraine's tennis warrior takes her first swing
05-15.text: In Odesa, Ukraine, Dayana Yastremska was born with a tennis racket in her future and a warrior's heart in her chest. By nineteen, she had climbed to world number twenty-one and won three WTA titles. When Russia's invasion of Ukraine upended her world in 2022, she fled the country and reached the semifinals of the Australian Open in 2024 while carrying the weight of a nation's suffering. Her story transcends sport -- it is one of resilience against impossible odds.

05-15.title: Arsenal F.C.
05-15.subtitle: Arsenal became the first football team in England's top flight to finish a season undefeated since Preston North End did so in 1888–1889.
05-15.text: Arsenal became the first football team in England's top flight to finish a season undefeated since Preston North End did so in 1888–1889.

05-15.title: Jessica Watson
05-15.subtitle: Three days before her seventeenth birthday, Jessica Watson arrived in Sydney after sailing non-stop and unassisted around the world.
05-15.text: Three days before her seventeenth birthday, Jessica Watson arrived in Sydney after sailing non-stop and unassisted around the world.

05-16.title: Emperor Suzaku Abdicates
05-16.subtitle: A Japanese emperor steps aside for his brother
05-16.text: In the imperial court of Kyoto, Emperor Suzaku abdicated the Chrysanthemum Throne in favor of his younger brother, who became Emperor Murakami -- the sixty-second emperor of Japan. Suzaku's decision to step aside was influenced by political pressures from the powerful Fujiwara clan, who dominated court politics during the Heian period. The transition marked another chapter in the complex dance between imperial authority and aristocratic power that defined medieval Japan.

05-16.title: Henry Fonda Was Born
05-16.subtitle: America's everyman is born in Nebraska
05-16.text: In Grand Island, Nebraska, Henry Jaynes Fonda was born -- the actor who would become the conscience of American cinema. From Tom Joad in 'The Grapes of Wrath' to the lone holdout juror in '12 Angry Men,' Fonda embodied the quiet integrity of the American everyman. His career spanned five decades, culminating in a long-overdue Academy Award for 'On Golden Pond' just months before his death. Few actors have so perfectly captured the moral core of a nation.

05-16.title: Lockheed F-104 Starfighter
05-16.subtitle: The Lockheed F-104 Starfighter, a supersonic interceptor aircraft, set a world flight airspeed record of 1,404.
05-16.text: The Lockheed F-104 Starfighter, a supersonic interceptor aircraft, set a world flight airspeed record of 1,404.012 mph (2,259.538 km/h).

05-16.title: Tritons' Fountain
05-16.subtitle: The Tritons' Fountain in Valletta, one of Malta's most important Modernist landmarks, was turned on for the first time.
05-16.text: The Tritons' Fountain in Valletta, one of Malta's most important Modernist landmarks, was turned on for the first time.

05-16.title: Theodore Maiman
05-16.subtitle: Theodore Maiman operates the first optical laser (a ruby laser), at Hughes Research Laboratories in Malibu, California.
05-16.text: Theodore Maiman operates the first optical laser (a ruby laser), at Hughes Research Laboratories in Malibu, California.

05-16.title: Park Chung Hee
05-16.subtitle: Park Chung Hee leads a coup d'état to overthrow the Second Republic of South Korea.
05-16.text: Park Chung Hee leads a coup d'état to overthrow the Second Republic of South Korea.

05-16.title: Venera program
05-16.subtitle: Venera program: Venera 5, a Soviet space probe, lands on Venus.
05-16.text: Venera program: Venera 5, a Soviet space probe, lands on Venus.

05-16.title: Chuck E. Cheese
05-16.subtitle: The first Chuck E.
05-16.text: The first Chuck E. Cheese location, the first family restaurant to integrate food, animated entertainment (example pictured), and an indoor arcade, opened in San Jose, California.

05-16.title: Luis Garcia Jr. Was Born
05-16.subtitle: A diamond from the Dominican Republic
05-16.text: In the Dominican Republic, Luis Victoriano Garcia was born into a country that has produced more Major League Baseball players per capita than any other. He would develop into a dynamic second baseman for the Washington Nationals, combining slick defensive skills with an electrifying bat. His rise from the Dominican amateur leagues to the major leagues embodies the enduring dream of countless young Caribbean athletes who see baseball as their pathway to a better life.

05-16.title: Kuwait
05-16.subtitle: Kuwait permits women's suffrage in a 35–23 National Assembly vote.
05-16.text: Kuwait permits women's suffrage in a 35–23 National Assembly vote.

05-16.title: STS-134
05-16.subtitle: STS-134 (ISS assembly flight ULF6), launched from the Kennedy Space Center on the 25th and final flight for Space Shuttle Endeavour.
05-16.text: STS-134 (ISS assembly flight ULF6), launched from the Kennedy Space Center on the 25th and final flight for Space Shuttle Endeavour.

05-17.title: Poet-Monk Jien Was Born
05-17.subtitle: A Buddhist voice in medieval Japan
05-17.text: In Kyoto, Jien was born into the powerful Fujiwara clan, destined to become one of Japan's most distinguished poet-monks. He would rise to lead the Tendai school of Buddhism while composing poetry of luminous beauty and writing 'Gukansho,' a groundbreaking historical work that interpreted Japanese history through Buddhist philosophy. His dual mastery of religious thought and literary art made him one of the most remarkable intellectuals of the Kamakura period.

05-17.title: Wizard of Oz Printed
05-17.subtitle: Dorothy and Toto begin their magical journey
05-17.text: The first copies of L. Frank Baum's 'The Wonderful Wizard of Oz' rolled off the presses, introducing the world to Dorothy, Toto, the Scarecrow, the Tin Man, and the Cowardly Lion. Illustrated by W. W. Denslow in vibrant color, the story of a Kansas girl swept by a cyclone into a magical land became an instant sensation and launched a literary franchise of fourteen Oz books. Its 1939 film adaptation would become one of the most beloved movies in cinema history.

05-17.title: Supreme Court of the United States
05-17.subtitle: The U.
05-17.text: The Supreme Court of the United States (SCOTUS) is the highest court in the federal judiciary of the United States. It has ultimate appellate jurisdiction over all U.S. federal court cases, and over state court cases that turn on questions of U.S. constitutional or federal law. It also has original jurisdiction over a narrow range of cases, specifically "all Cases affecting Ambassadors, other public Ministers and Consuls, and those in which a State shall be Party".
05-17.location: Highest court of jurisdiction in the US

05-17.title: Six-Day War
05-17.subtitle: Six-Day War: President Gamal Abdel Nasser of Egypt demands dismantling of the peace-keeping UN Emergency Force in Egypt.
05-17.text: Six-Day War: President Gamal Abdel Nasser of Egypt demands dismantling of the peace-keeping UN Emergency Force in Egypt.

05-17.title: The Troubles
05-17.subtitle: The Troubles: The Ulster Volunteer Force detonated a series of car bombs in Dublin and Monaghan, Ireland, killing 34 peo...
05-17.text: The Troubles were an ethno-nationalist conflict in Northern Ireland that lasted for about 30 years from the late 1960s to 1998. Also known internationally as the Northern Ireland conflict, it began in the late 1960s and is usually deemed to have ended with the Good Friday Agreement of 1998. Although the Troubles mostly took place in Northern Ireland, at times violence spilled over into parts of the Republic of Ireland, England, and mainland Europe.

05-17.title: Nolan Bushnell
05-17.subtitle: Nolan Bushnell opened the first Chuck E.
05-17.text: Nolan Kay Bushnell is an American businessman and electrical engineer. He established Atari, Inc., and the Chuck E. Cheese chain. He has been inducted into the Video Game Hall of Fame and the Consumer Electronics Association Hall of Fame, received the BAFTA Fellowship and the Nations Restaurant News "Innovator of the Year" award and was named one of Newsweek's "50 Men Who Changed America".

05-17.title: United States Department of Energy
05-17.subtitle: The U.
05-17.text: The U.S. Department of Energy declassifies documents showing world's largest mercury pollution event in Oak Ridge, Tennessee (ultimately found to be 4.2 million pounds [1.9 kt]), in response to the Appalachian Observer's Freedom of Information Act request.

05-17.title: USS Stark incident
05-17.subtitle: An Iraqi jet fired two Exocet missiles at the American frigate USS Stark, killing 37 personnel and injuring 21 others.
05-17.text: An Iraqi jet fired two Exocet missiles at the American frigate USS Stark, killing 37 personnel and injuring 21 others.

05-17.title: Gedhun Choekyi Nyima
05-17.subtitle: Six-year-old Gedhun Choekyi Nyima, the 11th Panchen Lama selected by the 14th Dalai Lama, was kidnapped by the Chinese g...
05-17.text: Six-year-old Gedhun Choekyi Nyima, the 11th Panchen Lama selected by the 14th Dalai Lama, was kidnapped by the Chinese government, who advocated a proxy.

05-17.title: Laurent-Désiré Kabila
05-17.subtitle: Troops of Laurent-Désiré Kabila march into Kinshasa.
05-17.text: Troops of Laurent-Désiré Kabila march into Kinshasa. Zaire is officially renamed Democratic Republic of the Congo.

05-17.title: UEFA Cup Final Riots
05-17.subtitle: Violence follows football to Copenhagen
05-17.text: On the day of the UEFA Cup Final in Copenhagen, the simmering fury from the deadly stabbing of two Leeds United fans in Istanbul the previous month boiled over. British and Turkish hooligans clashed in the streets of the Danish capital, turning a festive occasion into a scene of urban warfare. The violence underscored the failure of European football authorities to address fan violence and cast a shadow over the beautiful game that would take years to lift.

05-17.title: North Korea
05-17.subtitle: Trains from North and South Korea cross the 38th Parallel in a test-run agreed by both governments.
05-17.text: North Korea, officially the Democratic People's Republic of Korea (DPRK), is a country in East Asia. It constitutes the northern half of the Korean Peninsula and borders China and Russia to the north at the Yalu (Amnok) and Tumen rivers, and South Korea to the south at the Korean Demilitarized Zone (DMZ). The country's western border is formed by the Yellow Sea, while its eastern border is defined by the Sea of Japan.

05-18.title: Constantine Feeds His City
05-18.subtitle: An emperor ensures no Roman goes hungry
05-18.text: Emperor Constantine the Great announced free food distributions to the citizens of Constantinople, his newly established capital on the Bosphorus. Following the ancient Roman tradition of bread and circuses, the decree ensured that the growing population of the new capital would be well-fed and loyal. The grain dole, modeled on Rome's own system, became a cornerstone of Constantinople's civic life and helped transform a rebuilt Greek city into a true imperial metropolis.

05-18.title: Britain Claims Tonga
05-18.subtitle: The Pacific kingdom becomes a protectorate
05-18.text: The United Kingdom proclaimed a protectorate over the Polynesian Kingdom of Tonga, bringing the last independent Pacific island nation under European influence. While Tonga's monarchy was preserved and internal affairs remained largely self-governed, the protectorate gave Britain control over Tonga's foreign policy and defense -- a status that would endure until 1970. The arrangement reflected the era's colonial logic: small nations traded sovereignty for the protection of great powers.

05-18.title: First Indochina War
05-18.subtitle: First Indochina War: Viet Minh forces overran a French and Laotian garrison at Muong Khoua, leaving only four survivors.
05-18.text: First Indochina War: Viet Minh forces overran a French and Laotian garrison at Muong Khoua, leaving only four survivors.

05-18.title: Operation Passage to Freedom
05-18.subtitle: Operation Passage to Freedom, the evacuation of 310,000 Vietnamese civilians, soldiers and non-Vietnamese members of the...
05-18.text: Operation Passage to Freedom, the evacuation of 310,000 Vietnamese civilians, soldiers and non-Vietnamese members of the French Army from communist North Vietnam to South Vietnam following the end of the First Indochina War, ends.

05-18.title: Eli Cohen
05-18.subtitle: Eli Cohen, a spy who is credited with gathering significant intelligence used by Israel during the Six-Day War, was publicly hanged in Syria.
05-18.text: Eli Cohen, a spy who is credited with gathering significant intelligence used by Israel during the Six-Day War, was publicly hanged in Syria.

05-18.title: Nuclear weapons testing
05-18.subtitle: Nuclear weapons testing: Under project Smiling Buddha, India successfully detonates its first nuclear weapon becoming the sixth nation to do so.
05-18.text: Nuclear weapons tests are experiments carried out to determine the performance of nuclear weapons and the effects of their explosion. Over 2,000 nuclear weapons tests have been carried out since 1945. Nuclear testing is a sensitive political issue. Governments have often performed tests to signal strength. Because of their destruction and fallout, testing has seen opposition by civilians as well as governments, with international bans having been agreed on.
05-18.location: Controlled detonation of nuclear weapons for scientific or political purposes

05-18.title: Somalia
05-18.subtitle: Northern Somalia declares independence from the rest of Somalia as the Republic of Somaliland.
05-18.text: Northern Somalia declares independence from the rest of Somalia as the Republic of Somaliland.

05-18.title: Nørrebro
05-18.subtitle: Riots in Nørrebro, Copenhagen, caused by the approval of the four Danish exceptions in the Maastricht Treaty referendum.
05-18.text: Riots in Nørrebro, Copenhagen, caused by the approval of the four Danish exceptions in the Maastricht Treaty referendum. Police open fire against civilians for the first time since World War II and injure 11 demonstrators.

05-18.title: Republic of Ireland
05-18.subtitle: Ireland won the Eurovision song contest for the seventh time, the highest number of wins for any country before Sweden tied it in 2023.
05-18.text: Ireland, also known as the Republic of Ireland, is a country in Northwestern Europe. It consists of 26 of the 32 counties of the island of Ireland, with a population of about 5.4 million. Its capital and largest city is Dublin, on the eastern side of the island, with a population of over 1.5 million. The sovereign state shares its only land border with Northern Ireland, which is part of the United Kingdom.

05-18.title: Ryan Sessegnon Was Born
05-18.subtitle: A precocious left-footer lights up Fulham
05-18.text: In Roehampton, London, Ryan Sessegnon was born -- a footballer so precocious that by sixteen he was starting for Fulham in the Championship. His blistering pace, two-footed ability, and tactical versatility made him one of England's most exciting young talents, equally effective as a left-back or winger. His rise from south London to the Premier League and international recognition embodies the depth of talent that English football continues to produce.

05-18.title: Hubble Space Telescope
05-18.subtitle: A second photo from the Hubble Space Telescope confirms that Pluto has two additional moons, Nix and Hydra.
05-18.text: A second photo from the Hubble Space Telescope confirms that Pluto has two additional moons, Nix and Hydra.

05-18.title: Sri Lanka Army
05-18.subtitle: The Sri Lanka Army killed Velupillai Prabhakaran, the leader and founder of the Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam, to bri...
05-18.text: The Sri Lanka Army killed Velupillai Prabhakaran, the leader and founder of the Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam, to bring an end to the 26-year Sri Lankan civil war.

05-19.title: Assault on Jiucheng Palace
05-19.subtitle: A Turkic noble strikes at the Tang emperor
05-19.text: At the Jiucheng Palace in the mountains of Shaanxi, Ashina Jiesheshuai -- a Turkic noble and general serving the Tang dynasty -- launched a brazen assault on Emperor Taizong himself. The attack, born of personal grievance and political frustration, shook the imperial court to its foundations. Though the assault was repelled and Jiesheshuai killed, it exposed the tensions between the Tang court and its Turkic military allies that would simmer for generations.

05-19.title: Britain Annexes Tonga
05-19.subtitle: An empire swallows a Pacific kingdom whole
05-19.text: Great Britain formally annexed Tonga Island, completing its absorption of the Polynesian kingdom into the British sphere of influence. The move, following closely on the protectorate declaration, stripped the island nation of its remaining international autonomy while preserving the appearance of self-rule. For seventy years, Tonga would exist in this constitutional limbo -- nominally a kingdom, effectively a colony -- until independence was restored in 1970.

05-19.title: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk
05-19.subtitle: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk lands at Samsun on the Anatolian Black Sea coast, initiating what is later termed the Turkish War of Independence.
05-19.text: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk lands at Samsun on the Anatolian Black Sea coast, initiating what is later termed the Turkish War of Independence.

05-19.title: Breakup of Yugoslavia
05-19.subtitle: Breakup of Yugoslavia: With the local Serb population boycotting the referendum, Croatians voted in favour of independence from Yugoslavia.
05-19.text: After a period of political and economic crisis in the 1980s, the constituent republics of the Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia split apart in the early 1990s. Unresolved issues from the breakup caused a series of inter-ethnic Yugoslav Wars from 1991 to 2001 which primarily affected Bosnia and Herzegovina, neighbouring parts of Croatia and, some years later, Kosovo.

05-19.title: Space Shuttle program
05-19.subtitle: Space Shuttle program: Space Shuttle Endeavour is launched on mission STS-77.
05-19.text: The Space Shuttle program was the fourth human spaceflight program carried out by the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), which accomplished routine transportation for Earth-to-orbit crew and cargo from 1981 to 2011. Its official program name was Space Transportation System (STS), taken from a 1969 plan for a system of reusable spacecraft where it was the only item funded for development, as a proposed nuclear shuttle in the plan was cancelled in 1972.

05-19.title: Sierra Gorda
05-19.subtitle: The Sierra Gorda Biosphere, which encompasses the most ecologically diverse region in Mexico, was established as a result of grassroots efforts.
05-19.text: The Sierra Gorda Biosphere, which encompasses the most ecologically diverse region in Mexico, was established as a result of grassroots efforts.

05-19.title: Atlantis Resupplies ISS
05-19.subtitle: A shuttle delivers supplies to humanity's outpost
05-19.text: Space Shuttle Atlantis launched from Kennedy Space Center on mission STS-101, carrying critical supplies to the fledgling International Space Station. The mission represented the essential but unglamorous work of sustaining human life in orbit, delivering food, equipment, and spare parts to the station while performing maintenance that kept the orbital laboratory functioning. Each resupply mission was a reminder that humanity's presence in space depends on a constant lifeline from Earth.
05-19.location: Kennedy Space Center, Florida, United States

05-19.title: Royal Thai Armed Forces
05-19.subtitle: The Royal Thai Armed Forces concludes its crackdown on protests by forcing the surrender of United Front for Democracy Against Dictatorship leaders.
05-19.text: The Royal Thai Armed Forces concludes its crackdown on protests by forcing the surrender of United Front for Democracy Against Dictatorship leaders.

05-19.title: Refugio State Beach
05-19.subtitle: A corroded pipeline near Refugio State Beach, California, spilled 142,800 gallons (3,400 barrels) of crude oil onto the Gaviota Coast.
05-19.text: A corroded pipeline near Refugio State Beach, California, spilled 142,800 gallons (3,400 barrels) of crude oil onto the Gaviota Coast.

05-19.title: Wedding of Prince Harry and Meghan
05-19.subtitle: The wedding of Prince Harry and Meghan Markle (both pictured) took place at St George's Chapel in Windsor Castle, England.
05-19.text: The wedding of Prince Harry and Meghan Markle (both pictured) took place at St George's Chapel in Windsor Castle, England.

05-20.title: Council of Nicaea Opens
05-20.subtitle: Christianity defines itself for all time
05-20.text: In the lakeside city of Nicaea, Emperor Constantine the Great formally opened the first ecumenical council of the Christian Church, gathering over three hundred bishops from across the Roman world. Over two months of heated debate, they would produce the Nicene Creed -- the foundational statement of Christian belief that defined the relationship between God, Christ, and the Holy Spirit. The council's decisions shaped Christianity for seventeen centuries and still define orthodox theology today.

05-20.title: Sumitranandan Pant Was Born
05-20.subtitle: Hindi poetry finds its voice of nature
05-20.text: In the hill village of Kausani in Uttarakhand, Sumitranandan Pant was born amid the stunning beauty of the Himalayan foothills. He would grow to become one of the most celebrated poets of the Hindi language, crafting verses of exquisite lyricism inspired by nature, human beauty, and the rhythms of rural life. His romanticism and his later embrace of progressive humanism made him a towering figure in modern Hindi literature.

05-20.title: Treaty of Jeddah
05-20.subtitle: By the Treaty of Jeddah, the United Kingdom recognised the sovereignty of King Abdulaziz of Saudi Arabia (pictured) over...
05-20.text: By the Treaty of Jeddah, the United Kingdom recognised the sovereignty of King Abdulaziz of Saudi Arabia (pictured) over Hejaz and Nejd, which later merged to become Saudi Arabia.

05-20.title: World War II
05-20.subtitle: World War II: German paratroopers began the Battle of Heraklion on the island of Crete, capturing the airfield and port in Heraklion ten days later.
05-20.text: World War II or the Second World War was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers. Nearly all of the world's countries participated. Tanks and aircraft played major roles, the latter enabling the strategic bombing of cities and delivery of the only nuclear weapons used in war. World War II was the deadliest conflict in history, causing the death of 60 to 75 million people.

05-20.title: Cairo International Airport
05-20.subtitle: While attempting to land at Cairo International Airport, Pakistan International Airlines Flight 705 crashed for unknown ...
05-20.text: Cairo International Airport is the principal international airport of Cairo and the largest and busiest airport in Egypt. It serves as the primary hub for EgyptAir and Nile Air as well as several other airlines. The airport is located in Heliopolis, to the northeast of Cairo around fifteen kilometres from the business area of the city and has an area of approximately 37 km2 (14 sq mi).

05-20.title: Popular Movement of the Revolution
05-20.subtitle: The Popular Movement of the Revolution political party is established in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
05-20.text: The Popular Movement of the Revolution political party is established in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

05-20.title: HIV
05-20.subtitle: First publications of the discovery of the HIV virus that causes AIDS in the journal Science by a team of French scienti...
