10-01.subtitle: A tribunal was established to consider matters relating to the constitution of Singapore upon referral by the president.
10-01.text: A tribunal was established to consider matters relating to the constitution of Singapore upon referral by the president.

10-01.title: October Uprising Begins
10-01.subtitle: A twelve-year-old became a symbol of conflict
10-01.text: Across northern Israel, Palestinian communities erupted in protest following the killing of twelve-year-old Muhammad al-Durrah, whose death was broadcast worldwide. The demonstrations, which became known as the October 2000 events, marked a dramatic escalation in Israeli-Palestinian tensions and signaled the beginning of the Second Intifada's most turbulent chapter.

10-01.title: 2017 Catalan independence referendum
10-01.subtitle: An independence referendum, later declared illegal by the Constitutional Court of Spain, takes place in Catalonia.
10-01.text: An independence referendum, later declared illegal by the Constitutional Court of Spain, takes place in Catalonia.

10-01.title: 2022–23 Liga 1
10-01.subtitle: After losing a league home match to local rivals Persebaya Surabaya, some 3,000 Arema supporters invaded the stadium's p...
10-01.text: After losing a league home match to local rivals Persebaya Surabaya, some 3,000 Arema supporters invaded the stadium's pitch and met with police resistance, causing a stampede that killed 135.

10-02.title: Theophilos Takes the Throne
10-02.subtitle: The last emperor to shatter holy icons
10-02.text: When Michael II breathed his last in Constantinople, his son Theophilos ascended to the Byzantine throne. The young emperor would become the last champion of iconoclasm, waging a doomed campaign against religious imagery that had divided the empire for over a century. His reign blended cultural brilliance with theological stubbornness in equal measure.

10-02.title: Leela Roy Born
10-02.subtitle: Dhaka University's first daughter of defiance
10-02.text: In the tea-scented hills of Goalpara, Assam, Leela Roy came into a world she would spend her life reshaping. She became the first female student at Dhaka University and a close associate of Subhash Chandra Bose in India's independence movement. As politician, reformer, and champion of women's education, she blazed trails across colonial India.

10-02.title: Ukrainian War of Independence
10-02.subtitle: Ukrainian War of Independence: Mikhail Frunze orders the Red Army to immediately cease hostilities with the Revolutionary Insurgent Army of Ukraine.
10-02.text: Ukrainian War of Independence: Mikhail Frunze orders the Red Army to immediately cease hostilities with the Revolutionary Insurgent Army of Ukraine.

10-02.title: Thurgood Marshall
10-02.subtitle: Thurgood Marshall is sworn in as the first African-American justice of the United States Supreme Court.
10-02.text: Thurgood Marshall is sworn in as the first African-American justice of the United States Supreme Court.

10-02.title: Gustavo Díaz Ordaz
10-02.subtitle: Mexican President Gustavo Díaz Ordaz orders soldiers to suppress a demonstration of unarmed students, ten days before th...
10-02.text: Mexican President Gustavo Díaz Ordaz orders soldiers to suppress a demonstration of unarmed students, ten days before the start of the 1968 Summer Olympics.

10-02.title: Michael Myers
10-02.subtitle: Michael Myers becomes the first member of either chamber of Congress to be expelled since the Civil War.
10-02.text: Michael Myers becomes the first member of either chamber of Congress to be expelled since the Civil War.

10-02.title: Quadeca Is Born
10-02.subtitle: A future voice of internet-age artistry
10-02.text: Benjamin Lasky entered the world on a day that would eventually see him become Quadeca, one of the internet generation's most inventive musical voices. Starting with rap battles and YouTube sketches, he would evolve into a critically acclaimed singer-songwriter and producer, bridging the worlds of hip-hop, alternative music, and digital storytelling.

10-02.title: Parkrun
10-02.subtitle: The first parkrun, then known as the Bushy Park Time Trial, takes place in Bushy Park, London, UK.
10-02.text: Parkrun is a collection of 5-kilometre (3.1 mi) events for runners, walkers and volunteers that take place every Saturday morning at more than 2,000 locations in 23 countries across five continents.

10-02.title: Typhoon Longwang
10-02.subtitle: Typhoon Longwang made landfall in China as the deadliest tropical cyclone in that year to impact the country.
10-02.text: Typhoon Longwang made landfall in China as the deadliest tropical cyclone in that year to impact the country.

10-02.title: The Washington Post
10-02.subtitle: The Washington Post journalist Jamal Khashoggi was assassinated in the Saudi consulate in Istanbul, Turkey.
10-02.text: The Washington Post journalist Jamal Khashoggi was assassinated in the Saudi consulate in Istanbul, Turkey.

10-03.title: Korea's Mythic Founding
10-03.subtitle: Heaven touched earth on a sacred mountain
10-03.text: According to ancient Korean legend, the divine figure Hwanung descended from heaven to the peak of Mount Baekdu, bringing the gifts of civilization to humanity. This mythic moment, celebrated every October 3rd as Gaecheonjeol, marks the spiritual founding of the Korean nation. Thousands of years later, South Korea still honors this day as National Foundation Day.

10-03.title: Thomas Wolfe Born
10-03.subtitle: North Carolina's most eloquent literary son
10-03.text: In the mountain town of Asheville, North Carolina, Thomas Wolfe was born into a family whose turbulent dynamics would fuel some of American literature's most powerful autobiographical fiction. His debut novel, Look Homeward, Angel, would explode onto the literary scene in 1929, establishing him alongside Faulkner as a giant of the Southern Renaissance.

10-03.title: Major League Baseball
10-03.subtitle: In Major League Baseball, the New York Giants' Bobby Thomson hit the "Shot Heard 'Round the World", a game-winning home ...
10-03.text: In Major League Baseball, the New York Giants' Bobby Thomson hit the "Shot Heard 'Round the World", a game-winning home run, to win the National League pennant.

10-03.title: Project Mercury
10-03.subtitle: Project Mercury: US astronaut Wally Schirra, in Sigma 7, is launched from Cape Canaveral for a six-orbit flight.
10-03.text: Project Mercury was the first human spaceflight program of the United States, running from 1958 through 1963. An early highlight of the Space Race, its goal was to put a man into Earth orbit and return him safely, ideally before the Soviet Union. Taken over from the US Air Force by the newly created civilian space agency NASA, it conducted 20 uncrewed developmental flights, and six successful flights by astronauts.

10-03.title: Oswaldo López Arellano
10-03.subtitle: Oswaldo López Arellano replaced Honduran president Ramón Villeda Morales  in a violent coup, initiating two decades of military rule.
10-03.text: Oswaldo López Arellano replaced Honduran president Ramón Villeda Morales  in a violent coup, initiating two decades of military rule.

10-03.title: 1981 Irish hunger strike
10-03.subtitle: A hunger strike by Irish republican prisoners at HM Prison Maze outside Belfast, Northern Ireland, ended after seven months and ten deaths.
10-03.text: A hunger strike by Irish republican prisoners at HM Prison Maze outside Belfast, Northern Ireland, ended after seven months and ten deaths.

10-03.title: Space Shuttle Atlantis
10-03.subtitle: The Space Shuttle Atlantis makes its maiden flight, carrying two DSCS-III Satellites on STS-51-J.
10-03.text: Space Shuttle Atlantis is a retired Space Shuttle orbiter vehicle which belongs to NASA, the spaceflight and space exploration agency of the United States. Atlantis was manufactured by the Rockwell International company in Southern California and was delivered to the Kennedy Space Center in Eastern Florida in April 1985. Atlantis is the fourth operational and the second-to-last Space Shuttle built. Its maiden flight was STS-51-J made from October 3 to 7, 1985.

10-03.title: East Germany
10-03.subtitle: The German Democratic Republic is abolished and becomes part of the Federal Republic of Germany; the event is afterwards...
10-03.text: The German Democratic Republic is abolished and becomes part of the Federal Republic of Germany; the event is afterwards celebrated as German Unity Day.

10-03.title: Nadine Gordimer
10-03.subtitle: Nadine Gordimer is announced as the winner of the Nobel Prize in Literature.
10-03.text: Nadine Gordimer is announced as the winner of the Nobel Prize in Literature.

10-03.title: CJ Abrams Born
10-03.subtitle: A future All-Star takes his first breath
10-03.text: On this autumn day, CJ Abrams was born in Alpharetta, Georgia, destined to become one of baseball's brightest young talents. A natural athlete with blazing speed, he would be drafted by the San Diego Padres before finding his home with the Washington Nationals, where he earned his first All-Star selection in 2024.

10-03.title: Siegfried & Roy
10-03.subtitle: Roy Horn of the American entertainment duo Siegfried & Roy (both pictured) was mauled by a tiger during a performance at...
10-03.text: Roy Horn of the American entertainment duo Siegfried & Roy (both pictured) was mauled by a tiger during a performance at the Mirage on the Las Vegas Strip.

10-03.title: Svante Pääbo
10-03.subtitle: Svante Pääbo is awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.
10-03.text: Svante Pääbo is awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.

10-04.title: Rebels Sack Chang'an
10-04.subtitle: A peasant revolt topples an imperial capital
10-04.text: In the turbulent final days of the Xin dynasty, peasant rebels stormed the great Chinese capital of Chang'an, one of the ancient world's most magnificent cities. The sacking of the capital marked the violent end of Wang Mang's brief and troubled reign, clearing the path for the restoration of the Han dynasty under Emperor Guangwu.

10-04.title: Bona Arsenault Born
10-04.subtitle: A keeper of Quebec's ancestral memory
10-04.text: In the Gaspe Peninsula of Quebec, Bona Arsenault was born into the deep-rooted Acadian community. He would grow to become Canada's foremost genealogist, meticulously documenting the family histories of Quebec's founding families while simultaneously serving in both federal and provincial politics, bridging the worlds of heritage and governance.

10-04.title: First World War
10-04.subtitle: First World War: The Allies devastated the German defence at the Battle of Broodseinde, prompting a crisis among German ...
10-04.text: World War I, or the First World War, also known as the Great War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Central Powers. Major areas of conflict included Europe and the Middle East, as well as parts of Africa and the Asia-Pacific. The war saw important developments in weaponry including tanks, aircraft, artillery, machine guns, and chemical weapons.

10-04.title: First World War
10-04.subtitle: First World War: The Japanese merhant ship Hirano Maru was sunk by a German submarine in the Celtic Sea with the loss of 291 lives.
10-04.text: World War I, or the First World War, also known as the Great War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Central Powers. Major areas of conflict included Europe and the Middle East, as well as parts of Africa and the Asia-Pacific. The war saw important developments in weaponry including tanks, aircraft, artillery, machine guns, and chemical weapons.

10-04.title: Great Syrian Revolt
10-04.subtitle: Great Syrian Revolt: Rebels led by Fawzi al-Qawuqji captured the city of Hama from the French Mandate of Syria.
10-04.text: Great Syrian Revolt: Rebels led by Fawzi al-Qawuqji captured the city of Hama from the French Mandate of Syria.

10-04.title: Willie Gillis
10-04.subtitle: Willie Gillis, one of Norman Rockwell's trademark characters, debuted on the cover of The Saturday Evening Post.
10-04.text: Willie Gillis, one of Norman Rockwell's trademark characters, debuted on the cover of The Saturday Evening Post.

10-04.title: World War II
10-04.subtitle: World War II: Allied forces executed Operation Leader, an air raid against German shipping near Bodø, Norway.
10-04.text: World War II or the Second World War was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers. Nearly all of the world's countries participated. Tanks and aircraft played major roles, the latter enabling the strategic bombing of cities and delivery of the only nuclear weapons used in war. World War II was the deadliest conflict in history, causing the death of 60 to 75 million people.

10-04.title: Sputnik 1
10-04.subtitle: Sputnik 1 becomes the first artificial satellite to orbit the Earth.
10-04.text: Sputnik 1, often referred to as simply Sputnik, was the first artificial Earth satellite. It was launched into an elliptical low Earth orbit by the Soviet Union on 4 October 1957 as part of the Soviet space program. It sent a radio signal back to Earth for three weeks before its three silver-zinc batteries became depleted. Aerodynamic drag caused it to fall back into the atmosphere on 4 January 1958.

10-04.title: Richard Noble
10-04.subtitle: Richard Noble sets a new land speed record of 633.
10-04.text: Richard Noble sets a new land speed record of 633.468 miles per hour (1,019.468 km/h) at the Black Rock Desert in Nevada.

10-04.title: Battle of Mogadishu
10-04.subtitle: Battle of Mogadishu occurs killing 18 U.
10-04.text: Battle of Mogadishu occurs killing 18 U.S. Special Forces, two UN Peacekeepers and at least 600 Somalian militia men and civilians.

10-04.title: Siberia Airlines Shot Down
10-04.subtitle: An errant missile claims seventy-eight lives
10-04.text: Over the dark waters of the Black Sea, Siberia Airlines Flight 1812 was struck by an errant Ukrainian missile during a military exercise gone catastrophically wrong. All seventy-eight passengers and crew aboard the Tupolev Tu-154, traveling from Tel Aviv to Novosibirsk, perished instantly. The tragedy exposed fatal lapses in military-civilian coordination.

10-04.title: SpaceShipOne
10-04.subtitle: SpaceShipOne wins the Ansari X Prize for private spaceflight.
10-04.text: SpaceShipOne wins the Ansari X Prize for private spaceflight.

10-05.title: Heraclius Seizes Byzantium
10-05.subtitle: A tyrant falls, an emperor rises
10-05.text: Arriving by ship from North Africa, the general Heraclius stormed Constantinople and personally slew the despised Emperor Phocas, whose brutal reign had brought the Byzantine Empire to its knees. With Phocas dead, Heraclius claimed the purple and began one of the longest and most consequential reigns in Byzantine history, including the epic wars against Sasanian Persia.

10-05.title: Paris Defends the Boers
10-05.subtitle: Europe rallies against imperial overreach
10-05.text: In the grand halls of a Parisian peace congress, diplomats and activists united to condemn British imperial policy in South Africa. The congress formally asserted the Boer republics' right to self-determination, reflecting growing European unease with Britain's aggressive prosecution of the Second Boer War and its devastating impact on civilian populations.

10-05.title: James Bond
10-05.subtitle: The first of the James Bond film series, based on the novels by Ian Fleming, Dr.
10-05.text: The first of the James Bond film series, based on the novels by Ian Fleming, Dr. No, is released in Britain.

10-05.title: PBS
10-05.subtitle: The Public Broadcasting Service (PBS) is founded in the United States.
10-05.text: The Public Broadcasting Service (PBS) is founded in the United States.[citation needed]

10-05.title: Lloyd Bentsen
10-05.subtitle: During the United States vice-presidential debate, Democratic candidate Lloyd Bentsen told his opponent Dan Quayle, "Senator, you're no Jack Kennedy.
10-05.text: During the United States vice-presidential debate, Democratic candidate Lloyd Bentsen told his opponent Dan Quayle, "Senator, you're no Jack Kennedy."

10-05.title: Ladbroke Grove rail crash
10-05.subtitle: Two trains collided head-on in Ladbroke Grove, London, killing 31 people, injuring 417, and severely damaging public con...
10-05.text: Two trains collided head-on in Ladbroke Grove, London, killing 31 people, injuring 417, and severely damaging public confidence in the management and regulation of safety of Britain's privatised railway system.

10-05.title: The Bulldozer Revolution
10-05.subtitle: A wheel-loader became democracy's chariot
10-05.text: In the streets of Belgrade, Yugoslavian citizens rose against dictator Slobodan Milosevic following blatantly rigged elections. In a moment of dramatic symbolism, a protester drove a wheel-loader through the front of the state television building, giving the uprising its iconic name. The Bulldozer Revolution swept Milosevic from power and opened a new chapter for Serbia.

10-05.title: Mekong River massacre
10-05.subtitle: In the Mekong River massacre, two Chinese cargo boats are hijacked and 13 crew members murdered.
10-05.text: In the Mekong River massacre, two Chinese cargo boats are hijacked and 13 crew members murdered.

10-05.title: Jules Bianchi
10-05.subtitle: Formula One racing driver Jules Bianchi sustained fatal head injuries in a crash at the Japanese Grand Prix, dying the following year.
10-05.text: Formula One racing driver Jules Bianchi sustained fatal head injuries in a crash at the Japanese Grand Prix, dying the following year.

10-06.title: Disaster at Arausio
10-06.subtitle: Rome's worst defeat since Cannae
10-06.text: On the banks of the Rhone in southern Gaul, migrating Germanic and Celtic tribes annihilated a Roman army in one of the republic's most catastrophic defeats. The Battle of Arausio exposed Rome's vulnerability to the Cimbri and Teutones, threatening Italy itself. The disaster catalyzed military reforms under Gaius Marius that would reshape the Roman legions forever.

10-06.title: Willy Merkl Born
10-06.subtitle: A mountaineer destined for Nanga Parbat's ice
10-06.text: In Germany, Willy Merkl was born with a spirit that craved the world's highest peaks. He would become a pioneering Himalayan mountaineer, leading daring expeditions to the fearsome Nanga Parbat. Tragically, the mountain he loved most would claim his life in 1934 during a brutal storm, making him a legend of early alpine exploration.

10-06.title: World War I
10-06.subtitle: World War I: Combined Austro-Hungarian and German Central Powers, reinforced by the recently joined Bulgaria launched a ...
10-06.text: World War I, or the First World War, also known as the Great War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Central Powers. Major areas of conflict included Europe and the Middle East, as well as parts of Africa and the Asia-Pacific. The war saw important developments in weaponry including tanks, aircraft, artillery, machine guns, and chemical weapons.

10-06.title: Egyptian Armed Forces
10-06.subtitle: The Egyptian Armed Forces crossed the Suez Canal (pictured) to attack occupying Israeli forces at the Bar Lev Line in th...
10-06.text: The Egyptian Armed Forces crossed the Suez Canal (pictured) to attack occupying Israeli forces at the Bar Lev Line in the Sinai Peninsula, beginning the Yom Kippur War.

10-06.title: 6 October 1976 massacre
10-06.subtitle: Dozens are killed by Thai police and right-wing paramilitaries in the Thammasat University massacre; afterwards, the Sen...
10-06.text: Dozens are killed by Thai police and right-wing paramilitaries in the Thammasat University massacre; afterwards, the Seni Pramoj government is toppled in a military coup led by Sangad Chaloryu.

10-06.title: Anwar Sadat
10-06.subtitle: Egyptian president Anwar Sadat (pictured) was assassinated while attending a parade in Cairo to mark the eighth annivers...
10-06.text: Egyptian president Anwar Sadat (pictured) was assassinated while attending a parade in Cairo to mark the eighth anniversary of the Crossing of the Bar Lev Line at the start of the 1973 Arab-Israeli War.

10-06.title: Murder of Keith Blakelock
10-06.subtitle: Police constable Keith Blakelock was killed during rioting in the Broadwater Farm housing estate in Tottenham, London.
10-06.text: Police constable Keith Blakelock was killed during rioting in the Broadwater Farm housing estate in Tottenham, London.

10-06.title: Space Shuttle Discovery
10-06.subtitle: Space Shuttle Discovery is launched on STS-41, and deploys the Ulysses space probe to study the Sun's polar regions.
10-06.text: Space Shuttle Discovery is launched on STS-41, and deploys the Ulysses space probe to study the Sun's polar regions.

10-06.title: 51 Pegasi b
10-06.subtitle: The first planet orbiting another sun, 51 Pegasi b, is discovered.
10-06.text: The first planet orbiting another sun, 51 Pegasi b, is discovered.

10-06.title: Argentina's VP Resigns
10-06.subtitle: Corruption splits a fragile government
10-06.text: In a dramatic act of political conscience, Argentine Vice President Carlos Alvarez resigned his post, denouncing rampant corruption in President Fernando de la Rua's administration and the national Senate. His departure stripped the government of its moral authority and foreshadowed the catastrophic economic and political crisis that would engulf Argentina the following year.

10-06.title: MESSENGER
10-06.subtitle: The MESSENGER probe discovered Mercury's Rembrandt (pictured) – the second largest impact crater on the planet.
10-06.text: The MESSENGER probe discovered Mercury's Rembrandt (pictured) – the second largest impact crater on the planet.

10-06.title: Annie Ernaux
10-06.subtitle: Annie Ernaux is awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature.
10-06.text: Annie Ernaux is awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature.

10-07.title: The Hebrew Calendar Begins
10-07.subtitle: Time itself receives a sacred starting point
10-07.text: According to the calculation of the Hebrew calendar, this date marks the epoch reference point from which all Jewish time is measured. This lunisolar calendar, interweaving moon cycles with solar years, has guided Jewish religious observance for millennia. It determines the dates of holidays from Rosh Hashanah to Yom Kippur, anchoring an ancient people to the rhythm of the cosmos.

10-07.title: Heinrich Himmler Born
10-07.subtitle: The architect of humanity's darkest chapter
10-07.text: In Munich, Heinrich Himmler was born into an unremarkable middle-class family. He would rise to become the head of the SS and one of the chief architects of the Holocaust, overseeing a machinery of death that consumed six million Jewish lives. His name remains synonymous with the industrialized genocide that scarred the twentieth century beyond reckoning.

10-07.title: Luna 3
10-07.subtitle: The Soviet probe Luna 3 transmits the first-ever photographs of the far side of the Moon.
10-07.text: The Soviet probe Luna 3 transmits the first-ever photographs of the far side of the Moon.

10-07.title: Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty
10-07.subtitle: President Kennedy signs the ratification of the Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty.
10-07.text: President Kennedy signs the ratification of the Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty.

10-07.title: Point Barrow
10-07.subtitle: Near Point Barrow in Alaska, an Iñupiat hunter discovered three gray whales trapped in pack ice, which resulted in an in...
10-07.text: Near Point Barrow in Alaska, an Iñupiat hunter discovered three gray whales trapped in pack ice, which resulted in an international effort to free them.

10-07.title: Croatian War of Independence
10-07.subtitle: Croatian War of Independence: The Yugoslav People's Army conducted an air strike on Banski Dvori, the official residence...
10-07.text: Croatian War of Independence: The Yugoslav People's Army conducted an air strike on Banski Dvori, the official residence of the president of Croatia in Zagreb.

10-07.title: Hezbollah Border Raid
10-07.subtitle: Three soldiers vanish into the fog of war
10-07.text: In a brazen cross-border operation along the Israel-Lebanon frontier, Hezbollah militants ambushed and captured three Israeli Defense Force soldiers. The raid dramatically escalated tensions in the region and contributed to a deepening cycle of violence in the Israeli-Palestinian conflict, drawing Lebanon further into the volatile geopolitics of the Middle East.

10-07.title: Space Shuttle Atlantis
10-07.subtitle: The Space Shuttle Atlantis launches on STS-112 to continue assembly of the International Space Station.
10-07.text: Space Shuttle Atlantis is a retired Space Shuttle orbiter vehicle which belongs to NASA, the spaceflight and space exploration agency of the United States. Atlantis was manufactured by the Rockwell International company in Southern California and was delivered to the Kennedy Space Center in Eastern Florida in April 1985. Atlantis is the fourth operational and the second-to-last Space Shuttle built. Its maiden flight was STS-51-J made from October 3 to 7, 1985.

10-07.title: 2008 TC3
10-07.subtitle: 2008 TC3 exploded above the Nubian Desert in Sudan, in the first time that an asteroid impact had been predicted prior to atmospheric entry.
10-07.text: 2008 TC3 exploded above the Nubian Desert in Sudan, in the first time that an asteroid impact had been predicted prior to atmospheric entry.

10-07.title: Ales Bialiatski
10-07.subtitle: Ales Bialiatski, along with two organisations, Memorial & Center for Civil Liberties, are awarded the Nobel Peace Prize.
10-07.text: Ales Bialiatski, along with two organisations, Memorial & Center for Civil Liberties, are awarded the Nobel Peace Prize.

10-07.title: Hamas
10-07.subtitle: Hamas and several other Palestinian militant groups launch an attack into Israel, which results in the deaths of around ...
10-07.text: Hamas and several other Palestinian militant groups launch an attack into Israel, which results in the deaths of around 1,200, mostly civilians, and the taking of 251 hostages, including civilians and soldiers. The attack initiated the Gaza war and the larger Middle Eastern crisis.

10-08.title: Pyrrhus of Epirus Born
10-08.subtitle: Victory would forever carry his costly name
10-08.text: In the mountainous kingdom of Epirus in northwestern Greece, a prince was born who would become one of antiquity's greatest military minds. Pyrrhus would challenge early Rome itself, winning battles so devastating to his own forces that his very name became a byword for hollow triumph. The phrase 'Pyrrhic victory' endures as his most lasting legacy.

10-08.title: Eivind Groven Born
10-08.subtitle: Norway's folk melodies found a classical voice
10-08.text: In the rugged landscape of Vest-Telemark, Norway, Eivind Groven was born into a world steeped in traditional folk music. He would grow to become a composer and music theorist who bridged Norway's ancient musical heritage with classical composition, creating works that channeled the haunting melodies of his homeland into orchestral grandeur.

10-08.title: Weather Underground
10-08.subtitle: Demonstrations organized by the Weather Underground known as the Days of Rage began in Chicago, aimed at ending U.
10-08.text: Demonstrations organized by the Weather Underground known as the Days of Rage began in Chicago, aimed at ending U.S. involvement in the Vietnam War.

10-08.title: Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn
10-08.subtitle: Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn wins the Nobel Prize in literature.
10-08.text: Aleksandr Isayevich Solzhenitsyn was a Soviet and Russian author and dissident who helped to raise global awareness of political repression in the Soviet Union, especially the Gulag prison system. He was awarded the 1970 Nobel Prize in Literature "for the ethical force with which he has pursued the indispensable traditions of Russian literature". His nonfiction work The Gulag Archipelago "amounted to a head-on challenge to the Soviet state" and sold tens of millions of copies.

10-08.title: Franklin National Bank
10-08.subtitle: Franklin National Bank collapses due to fraud and mismanagement; at the time it is the largest bank failure in the history of the United States.
10-08.text: Franklin National Bank collapses due to fraud and mismanagement; at the time it is the largest bank failure in the history of the United States.

10-08.title: Croatian Army
10-08.subtitle: The Croatian Army and Croatian Defence Council launched Operation Southern Move, their last offensive in the Bosnian War.
10-08.text: The Croatian Army and Croatian Defence Council launched Operation Southern Move, their last offensive in the Bosnian War.

10-08.title: Oslo
10-08.subtitle: A new airport for Oslo, Norway, opened at Gardermoen, replacing a smaller one at the same location that had served as a ...
10-08.text: A new airport for Oslo, Norway, opened at Gardermoen, replacing a smaller one at the same location that had served as a backup to the city's previous main airport at Fornebu.

10-08.title: Linate Airport Disaster
10-08.subtitle: Fog, confusion, and 118 lives lost
10-08.text: On a fog-shrouded morning at Milan's Linate Airport, Scandinavian Airlines Flight 686 collided with a small Cessna business jet during takeoff, killing all aboard both aircraft and four people on the ground. The disaster, which claimed 118 lives in total, exposed critical failures in airport ground radar and communication systems across Europe.

10-08.title: 2005 Kashmir earthquake
10-08.subtitle: The 7.
10-08.text: The 7.6 Mw  Kashmir earthquake leaves 86,000–87,351 people dead, 69,000–75,266 injured, and 2.8 million homeless.

10-08.title: Yemen War
10-08.subtitle: Yemen War: A funeral in Sanaa was hit by two consecutive airstrikes  by a Saudi-led coalition, leaving 143–155 civilians...
10-08.text: Yemen War: A funeral in Sanaa was hit by two consecutive airstrikes  by a Saudi-led coalition, leaving 143–155 civilians dead and more than 525 injured.

10-08.title: Extinction Rebellion
10-08.subtitle: About 200 Extinction Rebellion activists block the gates of Leinster House (parliament) in the Republic of Ireland.
10-08.text: About 200 Extinction Rebellion activists block the gates of Leinster House (parliament) in the Republic of Ireland.

10-08.title: Second Nagorno-Karabakh War
10-08.subtitle: Second Nagorno-Karabakh War: Azerbaijan twice deliberately targeted the Church of the Holy Savior Ghazanchetsots of Shusha.
10-08.text: Second Nagorno-Karabakh War: Azerbaijan twice deliberately targeted the Church of the Holy Savior Ghazanchetsots of Shusha.

10-09.title: Two Kings Are Crowned
10-09.subtitle: Brothers share a throne that only one would keep
10-09.text: In a ceremony charged with dynastic tension, Carloman I and his elder brother Charlemagne were crowned co-kings of the Franks following the death of their father, Pepin the Short. The uneasy arrangement of shared rule would last only three years until Carloman's sudden death, leaving Charlemagne sole master of Francia and the future architect of a European empire.

10-09.title: Cook Islands Annexed
10-09.subtitle: Paradise claimed by a distant crown
10-09.text: Across the turquoise waters of the South Pacific, the fifteen islands of the Cook archipelago formally became a territory of the United Kingdom. The annexation brought these Polynesian islands, with their coral atolls and volcanic peaks, under the umbrella of the British Empire, beginning a colonial chapter that would eventually lead to self-governance in association with New Zealand.

10-09.title: Knowsley Hall shootings
10-09.subtitle: A footman shot and killed two colleagues and wounded the lady of the house at Knowsley Hall, England.
10-09.text: A footman shot and killed two colleagues and wounded the lady of the house at Knowsley Hall, England.

10-09.title: Nick Holonyak
10-09.subtitle: Nick Holonyak, an engineer for General Electric, gave the first public demonstration of a light-emitting diode.
10-09.text: Nick Holonyak, an engineer for General Electric, gave the first public demonstration of a light-emitting diode.

10-09.title: Vietnam War
10-09.subtitle: Vietnam War: the Republic of Korea Army commits the Binh Tai Massacre.
10-09.text: The Vietnam War was an armed conflict in Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia fought between North Vietnam and South Vietnam and their allies. North Vietnam was supported by the Soviet Union and China, while South Vietnam was supported by the United States and other anti-communist nations. The conflict was the second of the Indochina wars and a proxy war of the Cold War between the Soviet Union and US.
10-09.location: War in Southeast Asia from 1955 to 1975

10-09.title: Thomas & Friends
10-09.subtitle: The popular children's television show Thomas The Tank Engine & Friends, based on The Railway Series by the Reverend Wilbert Awdry, premieres on ITV.
10-09.text: The popular children's television show Thomas The Tank Engine & Friends, based on The Railway Series by the Reverend Wilbert Awdry, premieres on ITV.

10-09.title: The Phantom of the Opera
10-09.subtitle: The Phantom of the Opera, eventually the second longest running musical in London, opens at Her Majesty's Theatre.
10-09.text: The Phantom of the Opera, eventually the second longest running musical in London, opens at Her Majesty's Theatre.

10-09.title: Penei Sewell Born
10-09.subtitle: A future wall of gridiron greatness
10-09.text: On the island of American Samoa, Penei Sewell was born into a family of athletes. He would become one of football's most dominant offensive linemen, winning the Outland Trophy at Oregon before being drafted seventh overall by the Detroit Lions. Multiple Pro Bowl selections and All-Pro honors cemented his reputation as a generational talent.

10-09.title: Malala Yousafzai
10-09.subtitle: Pakistani activist Malala Yousafzai (pictured) was severely injured by a Taliban gunman in a failed assassination attempt.
10-09.text: Pakistani activist Malala Yousafzai (pictured) was severely injured by a Taliban gunman in a failed assassination attempt.

10-09.title: Arakan Rohingya Salvation Army
10-09.subtitle: The Arakan Rohingya Salvation Army launches its first attack on Myanmar security forces along the Bangladesh–Myanmar border.
10-09.text: The Arakan Rohingya Salvation Army launches its first attack on Myanmar security forces along the Bangladesh–Myanmar border.

10-09.title: Syrian civil war
10-09.subtitle: Syrian civil war: Turkish forces began an offensive into north-eastern Syria following the withdrawal of U.
10-09.text: The Syrian civil war was an armed conflict that began with the Syrian revolution in March 2011, when popular discontent with the Ba'athist regime ruled by Bashar al-Assad triggered large-scale protests and pro-democracy rallies across Syria, as part of the wider Arab Spring. The Assad regime responded to the protests with lethal force, which led to a series of defections, the emergence of armed opposition groups, and the civilian uprising descending into a civil war.

10-10.title: Germanicus Dies in Antioch
10-10.subtitle: Poison, power, and a dynasty's grief
10-10.text: Near the ancient city of Antioch, the beloved Roman general Germanicus succumbed to a mysterious illness he believed was poison administered by the Syrian governor Gnaeus Calpurnius Piso. His death at just thirty-three sent shockwaves through Rome, where crowds mourned a prince many had hoped would become emperor, and suspicion darkened the corridors of imperial power.

10-10.title: Helen Hayes Born
10-10.subtitle: The First Lady of American Theatre arrives
10-10.text: In Washington, D.C., Helen Hayes was born to a family that could not yet imagine her extraordinary destiny. She would become the first woman to achieve the EGOT, winning Emmy, Grammy, Oscar, and Tony awards across a career spanning seven decades. Known as the First Lady of American Theatre, she also received the Presidential Medal of Freedom.

10-10.title: World War II
10-10.subtitle: World War II: The Kempeitai, the military police arm of the Imperial Japanese Army, arrested and tortured fifty-seven ci...
10-10.text: World War II or the Second World War was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers. Nearly all of the world's countries participated. Tanks and aircraft played major roles, the latter enabling the strategic bombing of cities and delivery of the only nuclear weapons used in war. World War II was the deadliest conflict in history, causing the death of 60 to 75 million people.

10-10.title: Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty
10-10.subtitle: The Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty comes into effect.
10-10.text: The Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty comes into effect.

10-10.title: 1964 Summer Olympics
10-10.subtitle: The Tokyo Summer Olympics opening ceremony is the first to be relayed live by satellites.
10-10.text: The Tokyo Summer Olympics opening ceremony is the first to be relayed live by satellites.

10-10.title: Spiro Agnew
10-10.subtitle: U.
10-10.text: U.S. vice president Spiro Agnew  resigned after being charged with tax evasion.

10-10.title: Farabundo Martí National Liberation Front
10-10.subtitle: The Farabundo Martí National Liberation Front is founded in El Salvador.
10-10.text: The Farabundo Martí National Liberation Front is a Salvadoran political party and former guerrilla rebel group.
10-10.location: Salvadoran political party and former guerilla organization

10-10.title: Vidyasagar Setu
10-10.subtitle: After 20 years of construction, Vidyasagar Setu, the longest cable-stayed bridge in India, opened, joining Kolkata and Howrah.
10-10.text: After 20 years of construction, Vidyasagar Setu, the longest cable-stayed bridge in India, opened, joining Kolkata and Howrah.

10-10.title: Congress Authorizes Iraq War
10-10.subtitle: A vote that would reshape the Middle East
10-10.text: In the halls of the United States Capitol, Congress passed the Authorization for Use of Military Force Against Iraq, granting President George W. Bush broad powers to wage war. The vote, driven by claims of weapons of mass destruction, set the stage for the 2003 invasion of Iraq, a conflict that would consume American foreign policy for the next decade.

10-10.title: Murder of Huang Na
10-10.subtitle: Eight-year-old Huang Na was abducted and murdered; her body was found three weeks later after a search across Singapore and Malaysia.
10-10.text: Eight-year-old Huang Na was abducted and murdered; her body was found three weeks later after a search across Singapore and Malaysia.

10-10.title: Sheikh Muszaphar Shukor
10-10.subtitle: Sheikh Muszaphar Shukor becomes the first Malaysian in space on board Soyuz TMA-11.
10-10.text: Sheikh Muszaphar Shukor becomes the first Malaysian in space on board Soyuz TMA-11.

10-10.title: Ben Bernanke
10-10.subtitle: Ben S.
10-10.text: Ben S. Bernanke, Douglas W. Diamond and Philip H. Dybvig are jointly awarded the Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences.

10-11.title: Aleppo Earthquake Strikes
10-11.subtitle: The ancient citadel trembled and fell
10-11.text: A catastrophic earthquake struck the ancient Syrian city of Aleppo, reducing fortifications and homes to rubble in one of history's deadliest seismic events. Preceded by a smaller quake the day before, the main shock devastated the region and is frequently listed among the three deadliest earthquakes ever recorded, with casualties potentially reaching into the hundreds of thousands.

10-11.title: Masanobu Tsuji Born
10-11.subtitle: Brilliance and brutality in one man
10-11.text: In Japan, Masanobu Tsuji was born, destined to become one of the Imperial Army's most brilliant and controversial figures. As a tactical planner, he masterminded the stunning invasion of Malaya. As a war criminal, he ordered atrocities against prisoners. His postwar career as a politician added yet another layer to a life that embodied Japan's darkest contradictions.

10-11.title: CBS
10-11.subtitle: CBS's field-sequential color system for television was the first to be licensed for broadcast by the U.
10-11.text: CBS Broadcasting Inc., commonly known as CBS, is an American commercial broadcast television and radio network and the flagship property of the CBS Entertainment Group division of Paramount Skydance. It is one of Paramount Skydance's three flagship subsidiaries, along with Paramount Pictures and MTV.
10-11.location: American broadcast television and radio network

10-11.title: Pioneer 1
10-11.subtitle: NASA launches Pioneer 1, its first space probe, although it fails to achieve a stable orbit.
10-11.text: NASA launches Pioneer 1, its first space probe, although it fails to achieve a stable orbit.

10-11.title: Apollo 7
10-11.subtitle: NASA launches Apollo 7, the first successful crewed Apollo mission.
10-11.text: NASA launches Apollo 7, the first successful crewed Apollo mission.

10-11.title: Kathryn D. Sullivan
10-11.subtitle: Aboard the Space Shuttle Challenger, astronaut Kathryn D.
10-11.text: Aboard the Space Shuttle Challenger, astronaut Kathryn D. Sullivan becomes the first American woman to perform a space walk.

10-11.title: Second National March on Washington for
10-11.subtitle: An estimated 750,000 people attended the "Great March" in Washington, D.
10-11.text: An estimated 750,000 people attended the "Great March" in Washington, D.C., to demand greater civil rights for the LGBT community.

10-11.title: 100th Shuttle Mission
10-11.subtitle: Discovery marks a century of space flights
10-11.text: From Kennedy Space Center, Space Shuttle Discovery thundered into orbit on STS-92, marking a historic milestone as the 100th Space Shuttle mission. The crew's task was assembling the International Space Station, attaching critical structural components that would support humanity's permanent outpost in orbit. It was Discovery's 28th flight and a testament to sustained human spaceflight.

10-11.title: Myyrmanni bombing
10-11.subtitle: A bomb exploded in the Myyrmanni shopping center in Helsinki, Finland (aftermath pictured), resulting in seven deaths and 159 injuries.
10-11.text: A bomb exploded in the Myyrmanni shopping center in Helsinki, Finland (aftermath pictured), resulting in seven deaths and 159 injuries.

10-12.title: Babylon Falls to Cyrus
10-12.subtitle: The greatest empire crumbles without a fight
10-12.text: The armies of Cyrus the Great marched through the gates of Babylon, ending the Neo-Babylonian Empire in a conquest that was as much diplomatic as military. The Persian king, who would become founder of the Achaemenid Empire, famously freed the captive Jews and issued what many consider history's first charter of human rights. A new world order had begun.

10-12.title: The White House Gets Its Name
10-12.subtitle: A president gives a mansion its identity
10-12.text: President Theodore Roosevelt, never one for unnecessary formality, officially renamed the Executive Mansion to the White House. The change reflected Roosevelt's characteristically direct approach to governance and gave America's most famous residence the name by which the world would know it forever after. It was a small act with enduring resonance.

10-12.title: Nikita Khrushchev
10-12.subtitle: Soviet leader Nikita Khrushchev reportedly pounded his shoe on a desk during a meeting of the United Nations General Ass...
10-12.text: Soviet leader Nikita Khrushchev reportedly pounded his shoe on a desk during a meeting of the United Nations General Assembly in protest at a speech by Philippine delegate Lorenzo Sumulong.

10-12.title: Voskhod 1
10-12.subtitle: The Soviet Union launches the Voskhod 1 into Earth orbit as the first spacecraft with a multi-person crew, and the first...
10-12.text: Voskhod 1 was the seventh crewed Soviet space flight. Flown by cosmonauts Vladimir Komarov, Konstantin Feoktistov, and Boris Yegorov, it launched 12 October 1964, and returned on the 13th. Voskhod 1 was the first human spaceflight to carry more than one crewman into orbit, the first flight without the use of spacesuits, and the first to carry either an engineer or a physician into outer space. It also set a crewed spacecraft altitude record of 336 km (209 mi).

10-12.title: James Scott
10-12.subtitle: While in prison for murder, boxer James Scott defeated Eddie "The Flame" Gregory, a leading light heavyweight.
10-12.text: While in prison for murder, boxer James Scott defeated Eddie "The Flame" Gregory, a leading light heavyweight.

10-12.title: Typhoon Tip
10-12.subtitle: Typhoon Tip, the largest and most intense tropical cyclone ever recorded, reached a worldwide record-low sea-level pressure of 870 mbar (25.
10-12.text: Typhoon Tip, the largest and most intense tropical cyclone ever recorded, reached a worldwide record-low sea-level pressure of 870 mbar (25.69 inHg) in the western Pacific Ocean.

10-12.title: Provisional Irish Republican Army
10-12.subtitle: The Provisional Irish Republican Army detonated a bomb at the Grand Hotel in Brighton, England, in a failed attempt to a...
10-12.text: The Provisional Irish Republican Army detonated a bomb at the Grand Hotel in Brighton, England, in a failed attempt to assassinate British prime minister Margaret Thatcher and her cabinet.

10-12.title: New Zealand
10-12.subtitle: New Zealand holds its first general election under the new mixed-member proportional representation system, which led to...
10-12.text: New Zealand holds its first general election under the new mixed-member proportional representation system, which led to Jim Bolger's National Party forming a coalition government with Winston Peters's New Zealand First.

10-12.title: Abkhazia
10-12.subtitle: The former Autonomous Soviet Republic of Abkhazia declares its independence from Georgia.
10-12.text: The former Autonomous Soviet Republic of Abkhazia declares its independence from Georgia.

10-12.title: USS Cole Attacked
10-12.subtitle: Terror strikes an American warship at port
10-12.text: While refueling in the harbor of Aden, Yemen, the US Navy destroyer USS Cole was struck by a small boat laden with explosives piloted by two al-Qaeda suicide bombers. The blast tore a forty-foot hole in the ship's hull, killing seventeen sailors and wounding at least thirty-nine. The attack was a grim harbinger of the catastrophic terrorism to come.

10-12.title: Shenzhou 6
10-12.subtitle: The second Chinese human spaceflight, Shenzhou 6, is launched, carrying two cosmonauts in orbit for five days.
10-12.text: The second Chinese human spaceflight, Shenzhou 6, is launched, carrying two cosmonauts in orbit for five days.

10-12.title: European Union
10-12.subtitle: The European Union wins the 2012 Nobel Peace Prize.
10-12.text: The European Union wins the 2012 Nobel Peace Prize.

10-13.title: Claudius Poisoned, Nero Rises
10-13.subtitle: A mushroom dish ends one reign, starts another
10-13.text: Emperor Claudius died under mysterious circumstances in Rome, almost certainly poisoned, most likely by his ambitious wife Agrippina. Rather than his biological son Britannicus, it was his adopted son Nero who ascended to the imperial throne. At just sixteen, Nero inherited the most powerful position in the known world, beginning a reign that would end in infamy.

10-13.title: Arna Bontemps Born
10-13.subtitle: Harlem's quiet poet-librarian of the Renaissance
10-13.text: In Alexandria, Louisiana, Arna Bontemps was born into a world he would help transform through literature. As a poet, novelist, and librarian, he became a central figure of the Harlem Renaissance, capturing the African American experience in luminous prose and verse. His work at Fisk University's library preserved the cultural legacy of an entire artistic movement.

10-13.title: Micrograph
10-13.subtitle: The first electron micrograph of an Ebola virus is taken at the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention by Dr.
10-13.text: The first electron micrograph of an Ebola virus is taken at the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention by Dr. F. A. Murphy.

10-13.title: Lufthansa Flight 181
10-13.subtitle: Hijacking of Lufthansa Flight 181 by the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine.
10-13.text: Hijacking of Lufthansa Flight 181 by the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine.

10-13.title: The Troubles
10-13.subtitle: The Troubles: In a meeting at Fernhill House, Belfast, loyalist leader Gusty Spence announced that the Combined Loyalist...
10-13.text: The Troubles were an ethno-nationalist conflict in Northern Ireland that lasted for about 30 years from the late 1960s to 1998. Also known internationally as the Northern Ireland conflict, it began in the late 1960s and is usually deemed to have ended with the Good Friday Agreement of 1998. Although the Troubles mostly took place in Northern Ireland, at times violence spilled over into parts of the Republic of Ireland, England, and mainland Europe.

10-13.title: Caleb McLaughlin Born
10-13.subtitle: A future star of the Upside Down
10-13.text: In New York City, Caleb McLaughlin was born, destined to captivate audiences worldwide. Beginning his career as young Simba in Broadway's The Lion King, he would gain international fame as Lucas Sinclair in Netflix's Stranger Things, becoming one of his generation's most recognized young actors across both stage and screen.

10-13.title: 2010 Copiapó mining accident
10-13.subtitle: The mining accident in Copiapó, Chile ends as all 33 trapped miners arrive at the surface after a record 69 days underground.
10-13.text: The mining accident in Copiapó, Chile ends as all 33 trapped miners arrive at the surface after a record 69 days underground.

10-13.title: King of Bhutan
10-13.subtitle: The Dragon King of Bhutan, Jigme Khesar Namgyel Wangchuck (pictured) married Jetsun Pema at the Punakha Dzong.
10-13.text: The Dragon King of Bhutan, Jigme Khesar Namgyel Wangchuck (pictured) married Jetsun Pema at the Punakha Dzong.

10-13.title: Navaratri
10-13.subtitle: During the Hindu festival of Navaratri at a temple in Madhya Pradesh, India, rumours about an impending bridge collapse ...
10-13.text: During the Hindu festival of Navaratri at a temple in Madhya Pradesh, India, rumours about an impending bridge collapse caused a stampede that resulted in 115 deaths.

10-13.title: Monarchy of Thailand
10-13.subtitle: The King of Thailand, Bhumibol Adulyadej, died after 70 years on the throne, at which point was the longest-reigning current monarch.
10-13.text: The King of Thailand, Bhumibol Adulyadej, died after 70 years on the throne, at which point was the longest-reigning current monarch.

10-13.title: Brigid Kosgei
10-13.subtitle: Kenyan Brigid Kosgei sets a new world record for a woman runner with a time of 2:14:04 at the 2019 Chicago Marathon.
10-13.text: Kenyan Brigid Kosgei sets a new world record for a woman runner with a time of 2:14:04 at the 2019 Chicago Marathon.

10-14.title: The Battle of Hastings
10-14.subtitle: England's fate decided in a single afternoon
10-14.text: On a rain-soaked hillside near Hastings, William the Conqueror's Norman army clashed with the Anglo-Saxon forces of King Harold Godwinson. An arrow to the eye felled the last crowned Anglo-Saxon king, and England was forever changed. The Norman Conquest reshaped the nation's language, law, architecture, and aristocracy, marking one of history's true turning points.

10-14.title: W. Edwards Deming Born
10-14.subtitle: The man who taught the world quality
10-14.text: In Sioux City, Iowa, W. Edwards Deming was born into modest circumstances. He would become the father of the quality management movement, revolutionizing manufacturing first in post-war Japan and then worldwide. His statistical methods and management philosophy transformed how nations build, produce, and compete, earning him the title 'Dean of American Quality.'

10-14.title: Ariel Sharon
10-14.subtitle: Israeli military commander Ariel Sharon and his Unit 101 special forces attacked the village of Qibya on the West Bank, ...
10-14.text: Israeli military commander Ariel Sharon and his Unit 101 special forces attacked the village of Qibya on the West Bank, destroying 45 buildings, killing 42 villagers, and wounding 15 others.

10-14.title: B. R. Ambedkar
10-14.subtitle: B.
10-14.text: Bhimrao Ramji Ambedkar was an Indian jurist, economist, social reformer and politician who chaired the committee that drafted the Constitution of India based on the debates of the Constituent Assembly of India and the first draft of Sir Benegal Narsing Rau. Ambedkar served as Law and Justice minister in the first cabinet of Jawaharlal Nehru. He later renounced Hinduism and converted to Buddhism, inspiring the Dalit Buddhist movement.
10-14.location: Indian jurist, economist, politician and social reformer (1891–1956)

10-14.title: Martin Luther King Jr.
10-14.subtitle: Martin Luther King Jr.
10-14.text: Martin Luther King Jr. receives the Nobel Peace Prize for combating racial inequality through nonviolence.

10-14.title: Apollo program
10-14.subtitle: Apollo program: The first live television broadcast by American astronauts in orbit is performed by the Apollo 7 crew.
10-14.text: The Apollo program, also known as Project Apollo, was the United States human spaceflight program led by NASA, which landed the first humans on the Moon in 1969. Apollo was conceived during Project Mercury and executed after Project Gemini. It was conceived in 1960 as a three-person spacecraft during the presidency of Dwight D. Eisenhower. Apollo was later dedicated to President John F.

10-14.title: Aung San Suu Kyi
10-14.subtitle: Burmese opposition leader Aung San Suu Kyi is awarded the Nobel Peace Prize.
10-14.text: Aung San Suu Kyi is a Burmese politician, diplomat and author who served as State Counsellor of Myanmar and Minister of Foreign Affairs from 2016 to 2021. She has served as the general secretary of the National League for Democracy (NLD) since the party's founding in 1988 and was registered as its chairperson while it was a legal party from 2011 to 2023. She played a vital role in Myanmar's transition from military junta to partial democracy in the 2010s.

10-14.title: Yasser Arafat
10-14.subtitle: Yasser Arafat, Yitzhak Rabin and Shimon Peres receive the Nobel Peace Prize for their role in the establishment of the O...
10-14.text: Yasser Arafat, Yitzhak Rabin and Shimon Peres receive the Nobel Peace Prize for their role in the establishment of the Oslo Accords and the framing of future Palestinian self government.

10-14.title: Rowan Blanchard Born
10-14.subtitle: Disney Channel's girl who met the world
10-14.text: In Los Angeles, Rowan Blanchard was born, beginning a journey that would take her from child actress to cultural voice. Her breakout role as Riley Matthews in Disney Channel's Girl Meets World made her a household name, while her subsequent work in Snowpiercer and Crush showcased a maturing talent with range and depth.

10-14.title: Felix Baumgartner
10-14.subtitle: Felix Baumgartner jumped from a helium balloon in the stratosphere to become the first person to break the sound barrier without vehicular power.
10-14.text: Felix Baumgartner jumped from a helium balloon in the stratosphere to become the first person to break the sound barrier without vehicular power.

10-14.title: 2014 Nepal snowstorm disaster
10-14.subtitle: A snowstorm and series of avalanches occurred on and around the Himalayan peaks of Annapurna and Dhaulagiri, resulting i...
10-14.text: The 2014 Nepal snowstorm disaster occurred in central Nepal on 14 October 2014 and resulted in the deaths of at least 43 people of various nationalities, including at least 21 trekkers. Injuries and fatalities resulted from unusually severe snowstorms and avalanches on and around the mountains of Annapurna and Dhaulagiri. The incident was said to be Nepal's worst trekking disaster.

10-14.title: 2023 Australian Indigenous Voice referendum
10-14.subtitle: Australians vote to reject a constitutional amendment that would have established an Indigenous Voice to Parliament.
10-14.text: Australians vote to reject a constitutional amendment that would have established an Indigenous Voice to Parliament.

10-15.title: Virgil Is Born
10-15.subtitle: Rome's greatest poet enters the world
10-15.text: Near the village of Andes in Cisalpine Gaul, Publius Vergilius Maro was born into a farming family. He would become the supreme poet of the Roman Empire, composing the Eclogues, the Georgics, and the immortal Aeneid, an epic that gave Rome its founding myth and became the cornerstone of Western literature for two thousand years.

10-15.title: Enrique Jardiel Poncela Born
10-15.subtitle: Spain's master of absurd comedy arrives
10-15.text: In Madrid, Enrique Jardiel Poncela was born with a gift for making audiences laugh at the impossible. He would become one of Spain's most inventive playwrights and novelists, crafting humorous works that pushed the boundaries of the absurd. His comedic genius anticipated the Theatre of the Absurd decades before it became fashionable.

10-15.title: Hurricane Hazel
10-15.subtitle: Hurricane Hazel made landfall in the Carolinas in the United States before moving north to Toronto in Canada later the s...
10-15.text: Hurricane Hazel was the deadliest, second-costliest, and most intense hurricane of the 1954 Atlantic hurricane season. The storm killed at least 469 people in Haiti before it struck the United States near the border between North and South Carolina as a Category 4 hurricane. After causing 95 fatalities in the US, Hazel struck Canada as an extratropical storm, which raised the death toll by 81 people, mostly in Toronto.

10-15.title: Vietnam War protests
10-15.subtitle: Vietnam War protests: At an anti-war rally in New York City, David J.
10-15.text: Vietnam War protests: At an anti-war rally in New York City, David J. Miller burned his draft card (example pictured), the first such act to result in arrest under a new amendment to the Selective Service Act.

10-15.title: The Motherland Calls
10-15.subtitle: The Motherland Calls (depicted in coat of arms), a colossal statue in Volgograd, Russia, which commemorates the casualti...
10-15.text: The Motherland Calls is a colossal neoclassicist and socialist realist war memorial sculpture on Mamayev Kurgan in Volgograd, Russia. Designed primarily by sculptor Yevgeny Vuchetich with assistance from architect Yakov Belopolsky, the concrete sculpture commemorates the casualties of the Battle of Stalingrad, and is the predominant component of a monument complex, which includes several plazas and other sculptural works.

10-15.title: Mikhail Gorbachev
10-15.subtitle: Soviet Union leader Mikhail Gorbachev is awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for his efforts to lessen Cold War tensions and open up his nation.
10-15.text: Soviet Union leader Mikhail Gorbachev is awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for his efforts to lessen Cold War tensions and open up his nation.

10-15.title: Baltic states
10-15.subtitle: The leaders of the Baltic States, Arnold Rüütel of Estonia, Anatolijs Gorbunovs of Latvia and Vytautas Landsbergis of Li...
10-15.text: The leaders of the Baltic States, Arnold Rüütel of Estonia, Anatolijs Gorbunovs of Latvia and Vytautas Landsbergis of Lithuania, signed the OSCE Final Act in Helsinki, Finland.

10-15.title: Criminal Assets Bureau
10-15.subtitle: The Irish Criminal Assets Bureau was established following the gangland murders of Detective Garda Jerry McCabe and inve...
10-15.text: The Irish Criminal Assets Bureau was established following the gangland murders of Detective Garda Jerry McCabe and investigative journalist Veronica Guerin.

10-15.title: Galileo Buzzes Jupiter's Moon
10-15.subtitle: A spacecraft kisses Io's volcanic face
10-15.text: NASA's Galileo spacecraft swept within just 112 miles of Jupiter's volcanic moon Io, capturing unprecedented data about the most geologically active body in our solar system. The close flyby revealed Io's tortured landscape of erupting volcanoes and sulfur plains in stunning detail, pushing the aging spacecraft to its operational limits in the service of discovery.

10-15.title: Shenzhou 5
10-15.subtitle: China launches Shenzhou 5, its first crewed space mission.
10-15.text: China launches Shenzhou 5, its first crewed space mission.

10-15.title: New Zealand Police
10-15.subtitle: New Zealand Police conducted several anti-terrorism raids in relation to the discovery of an alleged paramilitary traini...
10-15.text: New Zealand Police conducted several anti-terrorism raids in relation to the discovery of an alleged paramilitary training camp in the Urewera mountain ranges, arresting 17 people and seizing four guns and 230 rounds of ammunition.

10-16.title: Ricimer Seizes the West
10-16.subtitle: A general becomes Rome's puppet master
10-16.text: At Piacenza, the Germanic general Ricimer defeated Emperor Avitus in battle and seized control of the Western Roman Empire. Rather than claim the purple himself, Ricimer chose to rule through a succession of puppet emperors, becoming the true power behind the crumbling western throne. His sixteen-year dominance marked Rome's twilight as a sovereign state.

10-16.title: Edward Ardizzone Born
10-16.subtitle: The man who painted childhood's golden shores
10-16.text: In Haiphong, French Indochina, Edward Ardizzone was born to an Italian father and English mother. He would become one of Britain's most beloved children's book illustrators, creating the iconic Little Tim series. His watercolors captured the magic and peril of childhood adventure, earning him the inaugural Kate Greenaway Medal and a place in the hearts of generations.

10-16.title: The Chronicles of Narnia
10-16.subtitle: The first novel of the The Chronicles of Narnia series by C.
10-16.text: The Chronicles of Narnia is a series of seven portal fantasy novels by British author C. S. Lewis. Illustrated by Pauline Baynes and originally published between 1950 and 1956, the series is set in the fictional realm of Narnia, a fantasy world of magic, mythical beasts, and talking animals. It narrates the adventures of various children who play central roles in the unfolding history of the Narnian world.
10-16.location: Series of children's novels by C. S. Lewis

10-16.title: Cuba
10-16.subtitle: Cuban revolutionary Fidel Castro delivers his "History Will Absolve Me" speech, and is sentenced to 15 years' imprisonme...
10-16.text: Cuba, officially the Republic of Cuba, is an island country in the Caribbean. It comprises the eponymous main island as well as 4,195 islands, islets, and cays. Situated at the convergence of the Caribbean Sea, Gulf of Mexico, and Atlantic Ocean, Cuba is located east of the Yucatán Peninsula, south of both Florida and the Bahamas, west of Hispaniola, and north of Jamaica and the Cayman Islands. Havana is the largest city and capital.

10-16.title: Yasunari Kawabata
10-16.subtitle: Yasunari Kawabata becomes the first Japanese person to be awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature.
10-16.text: Yasunari Kawabata becomes the first Japanese person to be awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature.

10-16.title: Henry Kissinger
10-16.subtitle: Henry Kissinger and Lê Đức Thọ are awarded the Nobel Peace Prize.
10-16.text: Henry Kissinger and Lê Đức Thọ are awarded the Nobel Peace Prize.

10-16.title: October 1978 conclave
10-16.subtitle: Cardinal Karol Wojtyła is elected to the papacy as Pope John Paul II, he becomes the first non-Italian pontiff since 1523.
10-16.text: Cardinal Karol Wojtyła is elected to the papacy as Pope John Paul II, he becomes the first non-Italian pontiff since 1523.

10-16.title: Desmond Tutu
10-16.subtitle: Desmond Tutu is awarded the Nobel Peace Prize.
10-16.text: Desmond Tutu is awarded the Nobel Peace Prize.

10-16.title: Luby's
10-16.subtitle: A man drove his vehicle through the window of a Luby's Cafeteria in Killeen, Texas, and opened fire, killing 23 people b...
10-16.text: A man drove his vehicle through the window of a Luby's Cafeteria in Killeen, Texas, and opened fire, killing 23 people before fatally shooting himself.

10-16.title: Willian Pacho Born
10-16.subtitle: Ecuador's defensive wall takes shape
10-16.text: In Ecuador, Willian Pacho was born, destined to become one of South American football's finest defenders. Rising through Ecuadorian youth ranks, he would eventually command the backline at Paris Saint-Germain in France's Ligue 1 while anchoring the Ecuador national team, proving that world-class talent knows no borders.

10-16.title: Lao Airlines Flight 301
10-16.subtitle: In Laos's deadliest air accident, Lao Airlines Flight 301 crashed into the Mekong River, resulting in the deaths of all 49 people aboard.
10-16.text: In Laos's deadliest air accident, Lao Airlines Flight 301 crashed into the Mekong River, resulting in the deaths of all 49 people aboard.

10-16.title: Daphne Caruana Galizia
10-16.subtitle: The Maltese journalist and anti-corruption activist Daphne Caruana Galizia was killed in a car bomb attack in Bidnija.
10-16.text: The Maltese journalist and anti-corruption activist Daphne Caruana Galizia was killed in a car bomb attack in Bidnija.

10-17.title: Vietnam's First Emperor Born
10-17.subtitle: A rebel magistrate would forge a nation
10-17.text: Ly Bon was born in the territory then under Chinese Liang dynasty control. He would rise from local magistrate to revolutionary leader, founding the Early Ly dynasty and becoming Vietnam's first emperor under the name Ly Nam De. His establishment of the kingdom of Van Xuan represented one of Vietnam's earliest assertions of independence from Chinese rule.

10-17.title: Cox van Asch van Wijck Born
10-17.subtitle: A sculptor's brief, brilliant life
10-17.text: In the Netherlands, Cornelie Caroline van Asch van Wijck was born into Dutch nobility. Known affectionately as Cox, she would become a talented artist and sculptor, studying under master Toon Dupuis. Though her life was tragically brief, ending at just thirty-two, her sculptures captured a creative spirit that burned intensely in the Dutch art world of the interwar years.

10-17.title: 17 October affair
10-17.subtitle: Indonesian Army elements surrounded the Merdeka Palace demanding President Sukarno disband the Provisional People's Representative Council.
10-17.text: Indonesian Army elements surrounded the Merdeka Palace demanding President Sukarno disband the Provisional People's Representative Council.

10-17.title: Sellafield
10-17.subtitle: The first commercial nuclear power station is officially opened by Queen Elizabeth II in Sellafield, England.
10-17.text: The first commercial nuclear power station is officially opened by Queen Elizabeth II in Sellafield, England.

10-17.title: Israeli apartheid
10-17.subtitle: The first attempt of the apartheid analogy by Ahmad Shukeiri.
10-17.text: The first attempt of the apartheid analogy by Ahmad Shukeiri.

10-17.title: Yom Kippur War
10-17.subtitle: Yom Kippur War: Egyptian forces retreated from the Battle of the Chinese Farm, allowing Israeli forces to build their fi...
10-17.text: Yom Kippur War: Egyptian forces retreated from the Battle of the Chinese Farm, allowing Israeli forces to build their first bridge across the Suez Canal.

10-17.title: Baton Rouge, Louisiana
10-17.subtitle: Having gone to the wrong house in Baton Rouge, Louisiana, for a Halloween party, Japanese exchange student Yoshihiro Hat...
10-17.text: Having gone to the wrong house in Baton Rouge, Louisiana, for a Halloween party, Japanese exchange student Yoshihiro Hattori was shot and killed by the homeowner.

10-17.title: Hatfield Rail Crash
10-17.subtitle: Broken rails shattered public trust
10-17.text: Near Hatfield in Hertfordshire, a high-speed train derailed when fatigue-cracked rails gave way beneath it, killing four passengers and injuring over seventy. The crash exposed systemic neglect of Britain's rail infrastructure under private management and triggered sweeping speed restrictions across the entire network, ultimately bringing down the management group Railtrack.

10-17.title: Rehavam Ze'evi
10-17.subtitle: Rehavam Ze'evi, the Israeli minister of tourism, was assassinated in revenge for the killing of PFLP leader Abu Ali Mustafa.
10-17.text: Rehavam Ze'evi, the Israeli minister of tourism, was assassinated in revenge for the killing of PFLP leader Abu Ali Mustafa.

10-17.title: Syrian civil war
10-17.subtitle: Syrian civil war: The Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF) capture the last foothold of the Islamic State of Iraq and the Leva...
10-17.text: The Syrian civil war was an armed conflict that began with the Syrian revolution in March 2011, when popular discontent with the Ba'athist regime ruled by Bashar al-Assad triggered large-scale protests and pro-democracy rallies across Syria, as part of the wider Arab Spring. The Assad regime responded to the protests with lethal force, which led to a series of defections, the emergence of armed opposition groups, and the civilian uprising descending into a civil war.

10-18.title: Agrippina Starves to Death
10-18.subtitle: A mother's grief becomes her final rebellion
10-18.text: On the desolate island of Pandateria, Agrippina the Elder, granddaughter of Augustus and mother to a future emperor, died of self-inflicted starvation. Banished by the paranoid Emperor Tiberius and heartbroken by the deaths of her sons Nero and Drusus, she chose death over submission, turning her body into a final act of defiance against imperial tyranny.

10-18.title: Germany Gets a New Chancellor
10-18.subtitle: Weltpolitik finds its champion
10-18.text: Count Bernhard von Bulow assumed the chancellorship of the German Empire, inheriting Bismarck's creation and steering it toward global ambition. A fervent advocate of Weltpolitik, he would transform Germany into a colonial and naval power, but his bellicose foreign policy alienated France, Britain, and Russia, planting seeds of the catastrophe that would erupt in 1914.

10-18.title: Regency TR-1
10-18.subtitle: Texas Instruments announces the Regency TR-1, the first mass-produced transistor radio.
10-18.text: Texas Instruments announces the Regency TR-1, the first mass-produced transistor radio.

10-18.title: Félicette
10-18.subtitle: Félicette, a black and white female Parisian stray cat, becomes the first cat launched into space.
10-18.text: Félicette, a black and white female Parisian stray cat, becomes the first cat launched into space.

10-18.title: Soviet Union
10-18.subtitle: The Soviet probe Venera 4 reaches Venus and becomes the first spacecraft to measure the atmosphere of another planet.
10-18.text: The Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (USSR), also known as the Soviet Union, was a transcontinental country that spanned much of Eurasia from 1922 until its dissolution in 1991. It was the world's third-most populous country, largest by area, and bordered twelve countries. A diverse multinational state, it was organized as a federal union of national republics, the largest and most populous being the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic. In practice, its government and economy were highly centralized.

10-18.title: Space Shuttle Atlantis
10-18.subtitle: The Space Shuttle Atlantis launches on STS-34 to deploy the Jupiter-bound Galileo space probe.
10-18.text: Space Shuttle Atlantis is a retired Space Shuttle orbiter vehicle which belongs to NASA, the spaceflight and space exploration agency of the United States. Atlantis was manufactured by the Rockwell International company in Southern California and was delivered to the Kennedy Space Center in Eastern Florida in April 1985. Atlantis is the fourth operational and the second-to-last Space Shuttle built. Its maiden flight was STS-51-J made from October 3 to 7, 1985.

10-18.title: Nihilism
10-18.subtitle: Nihilism, the first professional runway show from the British designer Alexander McQueen, was staged in London.
10-18.text: Nihilism, the first professional runway show from the British designer Alexander McQueen, was staged in London.

10-18.title: Sophie Thatcher Born
10-18.subtitle: A star who emerged from the wilderness
10-18.text: In Chicago, Sophie Thatcher was born, destined to become one of Hollywood's most compelling young talents. From her debut in the sci-fi film Prospect to her breakthrough in the survival drama Yellowjackets, she would establish herself as an actress of remarkable range, equally at home in horror, drama, and genre-bending storytelling.

10-18.title: NASA Astronaut Corps
10-18.subtitle: NASA Astronauts Jessica Meir and Christina Koch take part in the first all-female spacewalk when they venture out of the...
10-18.text: NASA Astronauts Jessica Meir and Christina Koch take part in the first all-female spacewalk when they venture out of the International Space Station to replace a power controller.

10-19.title: The Battle of Zama
10-19.subtitle: Hannibal's legend ends in African dust
10-19.text: On the sun-scorched plains near Carthage, Roman legions under Scipio Africanus finally defeated the legendary Hannibal Barca in the decisive Battle of Zama. The victory ended the seventeen-year Second Punic War and broke Carthage's power forever. Scipio's triumph secured Rome's dominance over the western Mediterranean and earned him the immortal surname Africanus.

10-19.title: Planck Discovers His Law
10-19.subtitle: A formula that would shatter classical physics
10-19.text: In his Berlin study, Max Planck arrived at a mathematical formula describing black-body radiation that perfectly matched experimental data. His discovery of what became Planck's law introduced the revolutionary concept of energy quanta, tiny packets of energy that classical physics could not explain. This quiet breakthrough would ignite the quantum revolution.

10-19.title: Juan Federico Ponce Vaides
10-19.subtitle: A coup is launched against Juan Federico Ponce Vaides, beginning the ten-year Guatemalan Revolution.
10-19.text: A coup is launched against Juan Federico Ponce Vaides, beginning the ten-year Guatemalan Revolution.

10-19.title: Korean War
10-19.subtitle: Korean War: The Battle of Pyongyang ends in a United Nations victory.
10-19.text: The Korean War was an armed conflict on the Korean peninsula between North Korea and South Korea and their allies. North Korea was supported by China and the Soviet Union, while South Korea was supported by the United Nations Command (UNC) led by the United States. The war, which ended in stalemate, was the first great clash of arms of the Cold War and was one of, if not its deadliest proxy war.

10-19.title: Iran–Iraq War
10-19.subtitle: Iran–Iraq War: U.
10-19.text: Iran–Iraq War: U.S. Navy forces destroyed two Iranian oil platforms in the Persian Gulf in response to an Iranian missile attack on a Kuwaiti oil tanker three days earlier.

10-19.title: 1988–1994 British broadcasting voice restrictions
10-19.subtitle: The British government imposes a broadcasting ban on television and radio interviews with members of Sinn Féin and eleve...
10-19.text: The British government imposes a broadcasting ban on television and radio interviews with members of Sinn Féin and eleven Irish republican and Ulster loyalist paramilitary groups.

10-19.title: Mother Teresa Beatified
10-19.subtitle: A saint of the gutters ascends toward heaven
10-19.text: In a grand ceremony at St. Peter's Square, Pope John Paul II beatified Mother Teresa of Calcutta, bringing the Albanian-Indian nun one step closer to sainthood. For decades she had served the poorest of the poor in the slums of Kolkata, founding the Missionaries of Charity. Her beatification recognized a life of radical compassion that inspired the world.

10-19.title: Hurricane Wilma
10-19.subtitle: Hurricane Wilma becomes the most intense Atlantic hurricane on record with a minimum pressure of 882 mb.
10-19.text: Hurricane Wilma was the most intense tropical cyclone in the Atlantic basin and the second-most intense tropical cyclone in the Western Hemisphere, both based on barometric pressure, after Hurricane Patricia in 2015. Wilma's rapid intensification led to a 24-hour pressure drop of 97 mbar (2.9 inHg), setting a new basin record. At its peak, Hurricane Wilma's eye contracted to a record minimum diameter of 2.3 mi (3.7 km).

10-19.title: YouTube
10-19.subtitle: British YouTube collective the Sidemen were formed as a Rockstar Games Social Club group in Grand Theft Auto Online.
10-19.text: British YouTube collective the Sidemen were formed as a Rockstar Games Social Club group in Grand Theft Auto Online.

10-19.title: Robert Weryk
10-19.subtitle: Canadian astronomer Robert Weryk discovered ʻOumuamua (depicted), the first known interstellar object detected passing through the Solar System.
10-19.text: Canadian astronomer Robert Weryk discovered ʻOumuamua (depicted), the first known interstellar object detected passing through the Solar System.

10-19.title: House of Lords
10-19.subtitle: Members of Parliament met at the House of Lords to discuss the United Kingdom's Brexit deal, this was the first Saturday...
10-19.text: Members of Parliament met at the House of Lords to discuss the United Kingdom's Brexit deal, this was the first Saturday sitting in Parliament since 3 April 1982 during the Falklands War.

10-20.title: Emperor Zhu Youzhen Born
10-20.subtitle: A future ruler of the Later Liang dynasty
10-20.text: In the turbulent twilight of China's Tang dynasty, Zhu Youzhen was born, destined to become the final emperor of the Later Liang. His reign would unfold during the chaotic Five Dynasties period, when China fragmented into competing kingdoms. Though his rule ended in defeat and death in 923, he remains a figure emblematic of an era of extraordinary upheaval.

10-20.title: Ismail al-Azhari Born
10-20.subtitle: Sudan's first prime minister draws breath
10-20.text: In Omdurman, Sudan, Ismail al-Azhari was born into the currents of Sudanese nationalism that would carry him to the highest office. He became Sudan's first Prime Minister, leading the nation through its transition from Anglo-Egyptian condominium to independence. Later serving as head of state, he shaped Sudan's early identity as a sovereign nation.

10-20.title: College football
10-20.subtitle: African-American college football player Johnny Bright was the victim of an on-field assault, eventually leading to chan...
10-20.text: African-American college football player Johnny Bright was the victim of an on-field assault, eventually leading to changes in NCAA football rules that mandated the use of more protective helmets with face guards.

10-20.title: Soviet Navy
10-20.subtitle: The Soviet Navy performs the first armed test of a submarine-launched ballistic missile, launching an R-13 from a Golf-class submarine.
10-20.text: The Soviet Navy performs the first armed test of a submarine-launched ballistic missile, launching an R-13 from a Golf-class submarine.

10-20.title: Watergate scandal
10-20.subtitle: Watergate scandal: "Saturday Night Massacre": United States President Richard Nixon fires U.
10-20.text: The Watergate scandal, or simply Watergate, was a political scandal in the United States involving the administration of President Richard Nixon. On June 17, 1972, operatives associated with Nixon's 1972 re-election campaign were caught burglarizing and planting listening devices in the Democratic National Committee headquarters at Washington, D.C.'s Watergate complex. Nixon's efforts to conceal his administration's involvement led to an impeachment process and his resignation in August 1974.

10-20.title: Brink's
10-20.subtitle: Two police officers and a Brink's armored car guard are killed during an armed robbery carried out by members of the Bla...
