11-07.text: The Cold War was a period of international geopolitical rivalry between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) and their respective allies, the capitalist Western Bloc and communist Eastern Bloc, which began in the aftermath of the Second World War and ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
11-07.location: 1947–1991 geopolitical rivalry between US and USSR

11-07.title: Mass Rapid Transit
11-07.subtitle: Singapore's first Mass Rapid Transit line opened, with train services running between Yio Chu Kang and Toa Payoh.
11-07.text: Singapore's first Mass Rapid Transit line opened, with train services running between Yio Chu Kang and Toa Payoh.

11-07.title: Mary Robinson
11-07.subtitle: Mary Robinson becomes the first woman to be elected President of the Republic of Ireland.
11-07.text: Mary Robinson becomes the first woman to be elected President of the Republic of Ireland.

11-07.title: WXYC
11-07.subtitle: WXYC, the student radio station of the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, launches the world's first internet radio broadcast.
11-07.text: WXYC, the student radio station of the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, launches the world's first internet radio broadcast.

11-07.title: NASA
11-07.subtitle: NASA launched the Mars Global Surveyor from the Kennedy Space Center at Cape Canaveral, Florida.
11-07.text: The National Aeronautics and Space Administration is an independent agency of the U.S. federal government responsible for the United States' civil space program and for research in aeronautics and space exploration. Headquartered in Washington, D.C., NASA operates ten field centers across the US and is organized into mission directorates for Science, Space Operations, Exploration Systems Development, Space Technology, Aeronautics Research, and Mission Support.

11-07.title: Hillary Clinton Elected Senator
11-07.subtitle: A first lady makes history at the ballot box
11-07.text: Hillary Clinton made history by winning a United States Senate seat from New York, becoming the first sitting first lady ever elected to public office. The victory launched a political career that would see her serve as Secretary of State and become the first woman nominated for president by a major party, reshaping the boundaries of American political life.

11-08.title: Emperor Nerva Born
11-08.subtitle: The wise old man who saved the succession
11-08.text: In the town of Narni in central Italy, Marcus Cocceius Nerva was born into a distinguished senatorial family. He would become Roman emperor at age sixty-five, inheriting a throne stained by the assassination of Domitian. His brief but pivotal reign established the precedent of adoptive succession, choosing Trajan as his heir and inaugurating Rome's golden age of the Five Good Emperors.

11-08.title: Margaret Mitchell Born
11-08.subtitle: Tomorrow was always another day for her
11-08.text: In Atlanta, Georgia, Margaret Mitchell was born into a family steeped in stories of the Old South. She would channel those stories into a single towering novel, Gone with the Wind, published in 1936. The epic tale of Scarlett O'Hara during the Civil War won the Pulitzer Prize and became one of the best-selling novels of all time, defining a literary era.

11-08.title: Massachusetts
11-08.subtitle: Former Massachusetts attorney general Edward Brooke became the first African American elected to the United States Senate since Reconstruction.
11-08.text: Former Massachusetts attorney general Edward Brooke became the first African American elected to the United States Senate since Reconstruction.

11-08.title: Provisional Irish Republican Army
11-08.subtitle: A Provisional Irish Republican Army bomb exploded during a Remembrance Sunday ceremony in Enniskillen, Northern Ireland,...
11-08.text: A Provisional Irish Republican Army bomb exploded during a Remembrance Sunday ceremony in Enniskillen, Northern Ireland, killing 12 people and injuring 63 others.

11-08.title: Jade Pettyjohn Born
11-08.subtitle: A young talent with range beyond her years
11-08.text: In Los Angeles, Jade Pettyjohn was born, beginning a journey through Hollywood that would showcase her versatility. From playing American Girl McKenna Brooks to rocking out on Nickelodeon's School of Rock to the dramatic intensity of Hulu's Little Fires Everywhere, she demonstrated a depth and range that belied her age, marking her as a talent to watch.

11-08.title: Typhoon Haiyan
11-08.subtitle: Typhoon Haiyan made landfall in the Visayas region of the Philippines, killing at least 6,300 people, making it the dead...
11-08.text: Typhoon Haiyan, locally known as Yolanda, was an extremely powerful and catastrophic tropical cyclone that is among the most intense tropical cyclones ever recorded. Upon making landfall, Haiyan devastated portions of Southeast Asia, particularly the Philippines during early November 2013. It is one of the deadliest typhoons on record in the Philippines, killing at least 6,300 people in the region of Visayas alone.

11-08.title: Louvre Abu Dhabi
11-08.subtitle: The Louvre Abu Dhabi was inaugurated by the French president Emmanuel Macron and then-crown prince of Abu Dhabi Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan.
11-08.text: The Louvre Abu Dhabi was inaugurated by the French president Emmanuel Macron and then-crown prince of Abu Dhabi Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan.

11-08.title: Second Nagorno-Karabakh War
11-08.subtitle: Second Nagorno-Karabakh War: Azerbaijani forces defeated the self-proclaimed Republic of Artsakh in the Battle of Shusha...
11-08.text: Second Nagorno-Karabakh War: Azerbaijani forces defeated the self-proclaimed Republic of Artsakh in the Battle of Shusha, reclaiming the town after 28 years.

11-09.title: Visigothic Jews Condemned
11-09.subtitle: A king weaponizes faith against an entire people
11-09.text: At the Seventeenth Council of Toledo, Visigothic King Egica leveled devastating accusations against the Jewish population of Hispania, charging them with conspiring with Muslim invaders. The council sentenced all Jews to slavery, a decree of breathtaking cruelty that reflected the deepening religious intolerance of the late Visigothic kingdom in the years before its fall.

11-09.title: Russia Occupies Manchuria
11-09.subtitle: A hundred thousand troops seize the east
11-09.text: With one hundred thousand soldiers spread across the vast plains and mountains of Manchuria, Russia completed its military occupation of the strategically vital Chinese territory. The invasion, launched in the chaos following the Boxer Rebellion, was a bold expansion of Russian imperial ambitions in East Asia that would lead directly to the Russo-Japanese War of 1904-05.

11-09.title: Apollo program
11-09.subtitle: Apollo program: NASA launches the unmanned Apollo 4 test spacecraft, atop the first Saturn V rocket, from Florida's Cape Kennedy.
11-09.text: The Apollo program, also known as Project Apollo, was the United States human spaceflight program led by NASA, which landed the first humans on the Moon in 1969. Apollo was conceived during Project Mercury and executed after Project Gemini. It was conceived in 1960 as a three-person spacecraft during the presidency of Dwight D. Eisenhower. Apollo was later dedicated to President John F.

11-09.title: Cold War
11-09.subtitle: Cold War: Nuclear false alarm: The NORAD computers and the Alternate National Military Command Center in Fort Ritchie, M...
11-09.text: The Cold War was a period of international geopolitical rivalry between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) and their respective allies, the capitalist Western Bloc and communist Eastern Bloc, which began in the aftermath of the Second World War and ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
11-09.location: 1947–1991 geopolitical rivalry between US and USSR

11-09.title: Garry Kasparov
11-09.subtitle: At age 22, Garry Kasparov became the then-youngest World Chess Champion by defeating then-champion Anatoly Karpov.
11-09.text: At age 22, Garry Kasparov became the then-youngest World Chess Champion by defeating then-champion Anatoly Karpov.

11-09.title: Cold War
11-09.subtitle: Cold War: Fall of the Berlin Wall: East Germany opens checkpoints in the Berlin Wall, allowing its citizens to travel to West Berlin.
11-09.text: Cold War: Fall of the Berlin Wall: East Germany opens checkpoints in the Berlin Wall, allowing its citizens to travel to West Berlin.

11-09.title: Bosnian War
11-09.subtitle: Bosnian War: Croatian Defence Council forces destroyed the Stari Most, a 16th-century bridge crossing the river Neretva ...
11-09.text: The Bosnian War was an international armed conflict that took place in Bosnia and Herzegovina between 1992 and 1995. Following several earlier violent incidents, the war is commonly seen as having started on 6 April 1992 when the newly independent Republic of Bosnia and Herzegovina was internationally recognized. It ended on 21 November 1995 when the Dayton Accords were initialed.
11-09.location: 1992–1995 armed conflict in Bosnia and Herzegovina

11-09.title: Uttarakhand Becomes a State
11-09.subtitle: The Himalayan foothills gain a voice of their own
11-09.text: Carved from thirteen districts of northwestern Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand officially became the twenty-seventh state of India. Nestled in the Himalayan foothills with Dehradun as its capital, the new state encompassed some of India's most sacred pilgrimage sites and pristine mountain landscapes, finally giving the people of the Garhwal and Kumaon regions their own governance.

11-09.title: Venus Express
11-09.subtitle: The Venus Express mission of the European Space Agency is launched from the Baikonur Cosmodrome in Kazakhstan.
11-09.text: The Venus Express mission of the European Space Agency is launched from the Baikonur Cosmodrome in Kazakhstan.

11-09.title: Alabama Crimson Tide football
11-09.subtitle: The Alabama Crimson Tide and LSU Tigers football teams, both with undefeated records thus far that season, played in a "Game of the Century".
11-09.text: The Alabama Crimson Tide and LSU Tigers football teams, both with undefeated records thus far that season, played in a "Game of the Century".

11-09.title: Second Nagorno-Karabakh War
11-09.subtitle: Second Nagorno-Karabakh War: An armistice agreement is signed by Armenia, Azerbaijan and Russia.
11-09.text: Second Nagorno-Karabakh War: An armistice agreement is signed by Armenia, Azerbaijan and Russia.

11-10.title: Child Emperor Leo II Dies
11-10.subtitle: Ten months of reign, a lifetime of what-ifs
11-10.text: After a reign of just ten months, the child Emperor Leo II of the Eastern Roman Empire died under circumstances that remain debated by historians. His father Zeno, who had been elevated as co-emperor, became sole ruler of the Byzantine Empire. The transition marked the beginning of Zeno's turbulent but consequential reign over the eastern half of the Roman world.

11-10.title: Josef Kramer Born
11-10.subtitle: The beast who would haunt Bergen-Belsen
11-10.text: In Munich, Josef Kramer was born into a world that would soon be consumed by the ideology he would come to serve. As an SS officer, he rose through the ranks to become commandant of both Auschwitz-Birkenau and Bergen-Belsen concentration camps. Dubbed 'The Beast of Belsen,' he was directly responsible for thousands of deaths before being hanged for war crimes in 1945.

11-10.title: National Educational Television
11-10.subtitle: National Educational Television (the predecessor to the Public Broadcasting Service) in the United States debuts Sesame Street.
11-10.text: National Educational Television (the predecessor to the Public Broadcasting Service) in the United States debuts Sesame Street.

11-10.title: Southern Airways
11-10.subtitle: Southern Airways Flight 49 from Birmingham, Alabama is hijacked and, at one point, is threatened with crashing into the ...
11-10.text: Southern Airways Flight 49 from Birmingham, Alabama is hijacked and, at one point, is threatened with crashing into the nuclear installation at the Oak Ridge National Laboratory. After two days, the plane lands in Havana, Cuba, where the hijackers are jailed by Fidel Castro.

11-10.title: MPLA
11-10.subtitle: In Angola, the MPLA decisively defeated the FNLA in the Battle of Quifangondo, while Portugal simultaneously withdrew al...
11-10.text: In Angola, the MPLA decisively defeated the FNLA in the Battle of Quifangondo, while Portugal simultaneously withdrew all its colonial and military personnel from Luanda.

11-10.title: Ken Saro-Wiwa
11-10.subtitle: Writer and environmental activist Ken Saro-Wiwa and eight others from the Movement for the Survival of the Ogoni People ...
11-10.text: Writer and environmental activist Ken Saro-Wiwa and eight others from the Movement for the Survival of the Ogoni People were executed by the Nigerian military regime led by Sani Abacha.

11-10.title: Mackenzie Foy Born
11-10.subtitle: A child star who reached for the stars
11-10.text: In Los Angeles, Mackenzie Foy was born, destined to light up the silver screen. From her breakout role as Renesmee in The Twilight Saga to playing young Murph in Christopher Nolan's Interstellar, where her scenes with Matthew McConaughey moved audiences to tears, she proved that the most powerful performances sometimes come from the youngest performers.

11-10.title: Veteran's Day Weekend Tornado Outbreak
11-10.subtitle: Veteran's Day Weekend Tornado Outbreak: A tornado outbreak stretching from Northern Ohio to the Gulf Coast, one of the l...
11-10.text: Veteran's Day Weekend Tornado Outbreak: A tornado outbreak stretching from Northern Ohio to the Gulf Coast, one of the largest outbreaks recorded in November.

11-10.title: National Museum of the Marine Corps
11-10.subtitle: The National Museum of the Marine Corps in Quantico, Virginia is opened and dedicated by U.
11-10.text: The National Museum of the Marine Corps in Quantico, Virginia is opened and dedicated by U.S. President George W. Bush, who announces that Marine Corporal Jason Dunham will posthumously receive the Medal of Honor.

11-10.title: Mars
11-10.subtitle: Over five months after landing on Mars, NASA declares the Phoenix mission concluded after communications with the lander were lost.
11-10.text: Mars is the fourth planet from the Sun. It is also known as the "Red Planet", for its orange-red appearance. Mars is a desert-like rocky planet with a tenuous atmosphere that is primarily carbon dioxide. At the average surface level the atmospheric pressure is a few thousandths of Earth's, atmospheric temperature ranges from −153 to 20 °C, and cosmic radiation is high.

11-10.title: Daecheong incident
11-10.subtitle: A skirmish occurred between South Korean and North Korean naval ships off Daecheong Island in the Yellow Sea.
11-10.text: A skirmish occurred between South Korean and North Korean naval ships off Daecheong Island in the Yellow Sea.

11-10.title: Land mines in the Falkland Islands
11-10.subtitle: The British government announced that it had removed the last land mine from the Falkland Islands, laid by Argentine for...
11-10.text: The British government announced that it had removed the last land mine from the Falkland Islands, laid by Argentine forces during the 1982 Falklands War.

11-11.title: Conference at Carnuntum
11-11.subtitle: Three emperors try to end Rome's civil wars
11-11.text: At the ancient legionary fortress of Carnuntum on the Danube, the retired Emperor Diocletian met with Galerius and Maximianus in a desperate attempt to resolve the civil wars tearing the Roman Tetrarchy apart. The conference, convened amid the ruins of a once-mighty garrison town, represented the last serious effort to salvage Diocletian's grand experiment in shared imperial power.

11-11.title: Maria Babanova Born
11-11.subtitle: Meyerhold's greatest actress enters the wings
11-11.text: In Moscow, Maria Babanova was born with a talent for transformation that would make her the supreme actress of Vsevolod Meyerhold's revolutionary theater. Her performances electrified Soviet audiences and critics alike, earning her the title of People's Artist of the USSR. She bridged the worlds of avant-garde experimentation and classical artistry with unmatched grace.

11-11.title: 1960 South Vietnamese coup attempt
11-11.subtitle: A coup attempt by the Army of the Republic of Vietnam against President Ngô Đình Diệm was crushed after he falsely promi...
11-11.text: A coup attempt by the Army of the Republic of Vietnam against President Ngô Đình Diệm was crushed after he falsely promised reform, allowing loyalists to rescue him.

11-11.title: Kindu atrocity
11-11.subtitle: Thirteen Italian Air Force servicemen, deployed to the Congo as a part of the UN peacekeeping force, are massacred by a mob in Kindu.
11-11.text: Thirteen Italian Air Force servicemen, deployed to the Congo as a part of the UN peacekeeping force, are massacred by a mob in Kindu.

11-11.title: Rhodesia
11-11.subtitle: Rhodesia, led by Prime Minister Ian Smith (pictured), unilaterally declared independence from the United Kingdom.
11-11.text: Rhodesia, led by Prime Minister Ian Smith (pictured), unilaterally declared independence from the United Kingdom.

11-11.title: Australian constitutional crisis of 1975
11-11.subtitle: Australian constitutional crisis of 1975: Australian Governor-General Sir John Kerr dismisses the government of Gough Wh...
11-11.text: The 1975 Australian constitutional crisis, also called the Dismissal, culminated with the dismissal of the prime minister, Gough Whitlam of the Australian Labor Party (ALP), by Sir John Kerr, the governor-general of Australia, on 11 November 1975. Kerr then commissioned the leader of the opposition, Malcolm Fraser of the Liberal Party, as prime minister on the condition that he advise a new election. It has been described as the greatest political and constitutional crisis in Australian history.
11-11.location: Dismissal of Prime Minister Gough Whitlam

11-11.title: Space Shuttle Columbia
11-11.subtitle: Space Shuttle Columbia launches from the Kennedy Space Center on STS-5, the first operational mission of the Space Shuttle program.
11-11.text: Space Shuttle Columbia (OV-102) was a Space Shuttle orbiter manufactured by Rockwell International and operated by NASA. Named after the first American ship to circumnavigate the globe, and the female personification of the United States, Columbia was the first of five Space Shuttle orbiters to fly in space, debuting the Space Shuttle launch vehicle on its maiden flight on April 12, 1981 and becoming the first spacecraft to be re-used after its first flight when it launched on STS-2 on

11-11.title: House of Lords Act 1999
11-11.subtitle: The House of Lords Act was given royal assent, removing most hereditary peers from the British House of Lords.
11-11.text: The House of Lords Act was given royal assent, removing most hereditary peers from the British House of Lords.

11-11.title: Kaprun Cable Car Disaster
11-11.subtitle: Fire in an alpine tunnel kills 155 skiers
11-11.text: In the Austrian Alps, a cable car ascending to the Kitzsteinhorn glacier caught fire inside a tunnel near Kaprun, trapping and killing 155 skiers and snowboarders. The cause was traced to a faulty fan heater that ignited hydraulic fluid. The disaster remains the deadliest rail accident in Austrian history and led to sweeping safety reforms across European mountain transport.

11-11.title: Grigori Perelman
11-11.subtitle: Russian mathematician Grigori Perelman posts the first of three preprint texts with his proof of the Poincaré conjecture.
11-11.text: Russian mathematician Grigori Perelman posts the first of three preprint texts with his proof of the Poincaré conjecture. It remains the only of the Millennium Prize Problems in mathematics to be solved. He later refused both the prize money from Clay Mathematics Institute as well as the Fields Medal for his work.

11-11.title: Palestine Liberation Organization
11-11.subtitle: The Palestine Liberation Organization confirms the death of Yasser Arafat from unidentified causes.
11-11.text: The Palestine Liberation Organization confirms the death of Yasser Arafat from unidentified causes. Mahmoud Abbas is elected chairman of the PLO minutes later.

11-11.title: Maumoon Abdul Gayoom
11-11.subtitle: After 30 years in power, Maumoon Abdul Gayoom was succeeded by Mohamed Nasheed as president of the Maldives.
11-11.text: After 30 years in power, Maumoon Abdul Gayoom was succeeded by Mohamed Nasheed as president of the Maldives.

11-12.title: Young Lothair Crowned King
11-12.subtitle: A thirteen-year-old inherits a fading dynasty
11-12.text: At the Abbey of Saint-Remi in Reims, the thirteen-year-old Lothair III was crowned king of the West Frankish Kingdom. As the penultimate Carolingian king, he inherited a realm where royal authority was increasingly challenged by powerful nobles. His long reign of thirty-two years would prove to be a last gasp of the dynasty that Charlemagne had made legendary.

11-12.title: Jack Oakie Born
11-12.subtitle: Hollywood's great comic foil arrives
11-12.text: In Sedalia, Missouri, Lewis Delaney Offield was born, later reinventing himself as Jack Oakie. He would become one of Hollywood's most beloved character actors, starring in dozens of films. His portrayal of the dictator Napaloni opposite Charlie Chaplin in The Great Dictator earned him an Academy Award nomination and secured his place in cinema history.

11-12.title: Suez Crisis
11-12.subtitle: Suez Crisis: During an invasion of Rafah, Israeli soldiers shot and killed an estimated 111 Palestinian refugees and local inhabitants.
11-12.text: Suez Crisis: During an invasion of Rafah, Israeli soldiers shot and killed an estimated 111 Palestinian refugees and local inhabitants.

11-12.title: NASA
11-12.subtitle: The NASA space probe Voyager I makes its closest approach to Saturn and takes the first images of its rings.
11-12.text: The National Aeronautics and Space Administration is an independent agency of the U.S. federal government responsible for the United States' civil space program and for research in aeronautics and space exploration. Headquartered in Washington, D.C., NASA operates ten field centers across the US and is organized into mission directorates for Science, Space Operations, Exploration Systems Development, Space Technology, Aeronautics Research, and Mission Support.

11-12.title: Space Shuttle program
11-12.subtitle: Space Shuttle program: Mission STS-2, utilizing the Space Shuttle Columbia, marks the first time a crewed spacecraft is launched into space twice.
11-12.text: The Space Shuttle program was the fourth human spaceflight program carried out by the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), which accomplished routine transportation for Earth-to-orbit crew and cargo from 1981 to 2011. Its official program name was Space Transportation System (STS), taken from a 1969 plan for a system of reusable spacecraft where it was the only item funded for development, as a proposed nuclear shuttle in the plan was cancelled in 1972.

11-12.title: Santa Cruz massacre
11-12.subtitle: Indonesian forces opened fire on student demonstrators protesting the occupation of East Timor in the capital Dili, killing at least 250 people.
11-12.text: Indonesian forces opened fire on student demonstrators protesting the occupation of East Timor in the capital Dili, killing at least 250 people.

11-12.title: Erdut Agreement
11-12.subtitle: Erdut Agreement regarding the peaceful resolution to the Croatian War of Independence is reached.
11-12.text: Erdut Agreement regarding the peaceful resolution to the Croatian War of Independence is reached.

11-12.title: Saudia
11-12.subtitle: A Saudi Arabian Airlines Boeing 747 and a Kazakhstan Airlines Ilyushin Il-76 collided in mid-air near New Delhi, killing...
11-12.text: A Saudi Arabian Airlines Boeing 747 and a Kazakhstan Airlines Ilyushin Il-76 collided in mid-air near New Delhi, killing 349 people, the deadliest such collision in history.

11-12.title: Flight 587 Crashes in Queens
11-12.subtitle: A neighborhood becomes a crash site
11-12.text: Just minutes after takeoff from JFK International Airport, American Airlines Flight 587 plummeted into the residential neighborhood of Belle Harbor in Queens, killing all 260 people aboard and five on the ground. Coming just two months after September 11th, the crash sent shockwaves through an already traumatized New York City, though it was ultimately attributed to pilot error.

11-12.title: Shanghai maglev train
11-12.subtitle: Shanghai Transrapid sets a new world speed record of 501 kilometres per hour (311 mph) for commercial railway systems, w...
11-12.text: Shanghai Transrapid sets a new world speed record of 501 kilometres per hour (311 mph) for commercial railway systems, which remains the fastest for unmodified commercial rail vehicles.

11-12.title: South Ossetia
11-12.subtitle: Although the Georgian government declared it illegal, South Ossetia held a referendum on independence, with more than 99...
11-12.text: South Ossetia, formally known as the State of Alania since 2017, or originally the Republic of South Ossetia is a partially recognised state in the South Caucasus. It has an officially stated population of just over 56,500 people (2022), who live in an area of 3,900 square kilometres (1,500 sq mi), with 33,000 living in the capital city, Tskhinvali. It borders only Russia and Georgia.
11-12.location: Partially recognized state in the South Caucasus

11-12.title: European Space Agency
11-12.subtitle: The European Space Agency's lander Philae  touched down on 67P/Churyumov–Gerasimenko, becoming the first spacecraft to land on a comet.
11-12.text: The European Space Agency (ESA), pronounced 'ee-sah', is a 23-member international organisation devoted to space exploration. It has its headquarters in Paris and a staff of around 2,547 people globally as of 2023. ESA was founded in 1975 in the context of European integration. Its 2026 annual budget was around US$10 billion.
11-12.location: European organisation dedicated to space exploration

11-13.title: Augustine of Hippo Born
11-13.subtitle: The restless heart that shaped Western thought
11-13.text: In the North African town of Thagaste, Aurelius Augustinus was born to a pagan father and devout Christian mother. His intellectual journey from hedonism to faith would produce the Confessions, The City of God, and other works that profoundly shaped Western philosophy and Christian theology. No single thinker has left a deeper imprint on the Western tradition.

11-13.title: Edward Buzzell Born
11-13.subtitle: The director who guided the Marx Brothers
11-13.text: In Brooklyn, New York, Edward Buzzell was born with show business in his blood. Starting as an actor, he transitioned to directing and helmed some of Hollywood's most beloved comedies, including the Marx Brothers' films At the Circus and Go West. His career spanned the golden age of American cinema, from silent films to lavish Technicolor musicals.

11-13.title: World War I
11-13.subtitle: World War I: beginning of the First Battle of Monte Grappa (in Italy known as the "First Battle of the Piave").
11-13.text: World War I: beginning of the First Battle of Monte Grappa (in Italy known as the "First Battle of the Piave"). The Austro-Hungarian Armed Forces, despite help from the German Alpenkorps and numerical superiority, will fail their offensive against the Italian Army now led by its new chief of staff Armando Diaz.

11-13.title: Great Britain national rugby league team
11-13.subtitle: Great Britain defeats France to capture the first ever Rugby League World Cup in Paris in front of around 30,000 spectators.
11-13.text: Great Britain defeats France to capture the first ever Rugby League World Cup in Paris in front of around 30,000 spectators.

11-13.title: Arab–Israeli conflict
11-13.subtitle: Arab–Israeli conflict: In response to a Fatah landmine incident, the Israeli military conducted a large cross-border ass...
11-13.text: Arab–Israeli conflict: In response to a Fatah landmine incident, the Israeli military conducted a large cross-border assault on the Jordanian-controlled West Bank village of Samu.

11-13.title: Ronald DeFeo Jr.
11-13.subtitle: Ronald DeFeo Jr.
11-13.text: Ronald DeFeo Jr. killed six members of his family in Amityville, New York, events that later inspired the book The Amityville Horror and a subsequent media franchise.

11-13.title: Kim Duk-koo
11-13.subtitle: South Korean boxer Kim Duk-koo suffered fatal brain injuries during a match with American Ray Mancini in Las Vegas, lead...
11-13.text: South Korean boxer Kim Duk-koo suffered fatal brain injuries during a match with American Ray Mancini in Las Vegas, leading to significant rule changes in the sport.

11-13.title: Nevado del Ruiz
11-13.subtitle: The volcano Nevado del Ruiz (pictured) erupted, causing a volcanic mudslide that buried the town of Armero, Colombia, ki...
11-13.text: The volcano Nevado del Ruiz (pictured) erupted, causing a volcanic mudslide that buried the town of Armero, Colombia, killing approximately 23,000 people.

11-13.title: Mozambique
11-13.subtitle: Mozambique becomes the first state to join the Commonwealth of Nations without having been part of the former British Empire.
11-13.text: Mozambique becomes the first state to join the Commonwealth of Nations without having been part of the former British Empire.

11-13.title: Great Internet Mersenne Prime Search
11-13.subtitle: As part of the Great Internet Mersenne Prime Search (GIMPS) project, Joel Armengaud discovers the project's first Mersen...
11-13.text: As part of the Great Internet Mersenne Prime Search (GIMPS) project, Joel Armengaud discovers the project's first Mersenne prime number,

11-13.title: Estrada Impeachment Advances
11-13.subtitle: A president faces the people's judgment
11-13.text: In Manila, Philippine House Speaker Manny Villar transmitted the articles of impeachment against President Joseph Estrada to the Senate, setting in motion one of the most dramatic constitutional crises in Philippine history. The charges of corruption and betrayal of public trust would ultimately lead to Estrada's removal and the People Power II revolution.

11-13.title: War on terror
11-13.subtitle: War on terror: In the first such act since World War II, US President George W.
11-13.text: War on terror: In the first such act since World War II, US President George W. Bush signs an executive order allowing military tribunals against foreigners suspected of connections to terrorist acts or planned acts on the United States.

11-13.title: November 2015 Paris attacks
11-13.subtitle: Coordinated terrorist attacks in Paris perpetrated by the Islamic State killed 130 people and injured 413 others.
11-13.text: Coordinated terrorist attacks in Paris perpetrated by the Islamic State killed 130 people and injured 413 others.

11-14.title: Alexander Crowned Pharaoh
11-14.subtitle: A Greek king becomes a living god in Egypt
11-14.text: In the ancient temple at Memphis, Alexander the Great was crowned pharaoh of Egypt, adding one of civilization's oldest titles to his growing list of conquests. The young Macedonian king, just twenty-four years old, was welcomed by the Egyptians as a liberator from Persian rule. His founding of Alexandria on the Nile's delta would create one of the ancient world's greatest cities.

11-14.title: Aaron Copland Born
11-14.subtitle: The sound of America found its composer
11-14.text: In Brooklyn, New York, Aaron Copland was born to Lithuanian Jewish immigrants. He would become the Dean of American Music, composing works that captured the vast American landscape in orchestral sound. Ballets like Appalachian Spring, Billy the Kid, and Rodeo defined what many consider the quintessential American musical voice, open and spacious as the frontier itself.

11-14.title: Ruby Bridges
11-14.subtitle: Ruby Bridges becomes the first black child to attend an all-white elementary school in Louisiana.
11-14.text: Ruby Bridges becomes the first black child to attend an all-white elementary school in Louisiana.

11-14.title: Apollo program
11-14.subtitle: Apollo program: NASA launches Apollo 12, the second crewed mission to the surface of the Moon.
11-14.text: The Apollo program, also known as Project Apollo, was the United States human spaceflight program led by NASA, which landed the first humans on the Moon in 1969. Apollo was conceived during Project Mercury and executed after Project Gemini. It was conceived in 1960 as a three-person spacecraft during the presidency of Dwight D. Eisenhower. Apollo was later dedicated to President John F.

11-14.title: German reunification
11-14.subtitle: After German reunification, the Federal Republic of Germany and Poland sign a treaty confirming the Oder–Neisse line as ...
11-14.text: After German reunification, the Federal Republic of Germany and Poland sign a treaty confirming the Oder–Neisse line as the border between Germany and Poland.

11-14.title: Cyclone Forrest
11-14.subtitle: In poor conditions caused by Cyclone Forrest, Vietnam Airlines Flight 474 crashed near Nha Trang, killing 30 people.
11-14.text: In poor conditions caused by Cyclone Forrest, Vietnam Airlines Flight 474 crashed near Nha Trang, killing 30 people.

11-14.title: Bill Clinton
11-14.subtitle: As a result of budget conflicts between President Bill Clinton and the United States Congress led by Newt Gingrich, the ...
11-14.text: As a result of budget conflicts between President Bill Clinton and the United States Congress led by Newt Gingrich, the federal government was forced to shut down non-essential services.

11-14.title: Xiyeon Born
11-14.subtitle: A child star of K-pop's new generation
11-14.text: In South Korea, Park Jung-hyun was born, destined to become Xiyeon, a multitalented entertainer who would train for nine years under Pledis Entertainment. As a former child actress, singer, and model, she debuted with the girl group Pristin in 2017 and co-hosted Show! Music Core, showcasing the dedication and versatility that define K-pop's most polished performers.

11-14.title: 2001 Kunlun earthquake
11-14.subtitle: A magnitude 7.
11-14.text: A magnitude 7.8 earthquake strikes a remote part of the Tibetan Plateau. It has the longest known surface rupture recorded on land (~400 km) and is the best documented example of a supershear earthquake.

11-14.title: Michael E. Brown
11-14.subtitle: Astronomers Michael E.
11-14.text: Michael E. Brown is an American astronomer, who has been professor of planetary astronomy at the California Institute of Technology (Caltech) since 2003. His team has discovered many trans-Neptunian objects (TNOs), including the dwarf planet Eris, which was originally thought to be bigger than Pluto, triggering a debate on the definition of a planet.

11-14.title: Space Shuttle Endeavour
11-14.subtitle: Space Shuttle Endeavour launches on STS-126 to continue assembly of the International Space Station.
11-14.text: Space Shuttle Endeavour is a retired orbiter from NASA's Space Shuttle program and the fifth and final operational Shuttle built. It embarked on its first mission, STS-49, in May 1992 and its 25th and final mission, STS-134, in May 2011. STS-134 was expected to be the final mission of the Space Shuttle program, but with the authorization of STS-135 by the United States Congress, Atlantis became the last shuttle to fly.

11-14.title: Red Bull Racing
11-14.subtitle: Red Bull Racing's Sebastian Vettel won the Drivers' Championship after winning the final race of the season, becoming th...
11-14.text: Red Bull Racing's Sebastian Vettel won the Drivers' Championship after winning the final race of the season, becoming the youngest Formula One champion.

11-15.title: Battle of the Winwaed
11-15.subtitle: England's last pagan king meets his end
11-15.text: On the rain-swollen banks of the Winwaed River in Yorkshire, King Oswiu of Northumbria defeated the combined forces of Penda of Mercia and Aethelhere of East Anglia. Penda, the last great pagan king of Anglo-Saxon England, fell in the battle, effectively ending organized pagan resistance to Christianity in England and reshaping the island's political landscape.

11-15.title: Stewie Dempster Born
11-15.subtitle: New Zealand cricket's pioneering batsman
11-15.text: In Wellington, New Zealand, Stewart Dempster was born with the hand-eye coordination that would make him his country's first international cricket star. As a Test batsman and later coach, he represented New Zealand, Wellington, and several English counties, establishing the foundation upon which generations of New Zealand cricketers would build.

11-15.title: The Holocaust
11-15.subtitle: The Holocaust: In the Romani Holocaust, Nazi official Heinrich Himmler ordered that the Romani were to be put "on the sa...
11-15.text: The Holocaust, known in Hebrew as the Shoah, was the genocide of European Jews during World War II. From 1941 to 1945, Nazi Germany and its collaborators systematically murdered around six million Jews across German-occupied Europe, around two-thirds of Europe's Jewish population. The murders were committed primarily through mass shootings across Eastern Europe and poison gas chambers in extermination camps, chiefly Auschwitz-Birkenau, Treblinka, Belzec, Sobibor, Chełmno and Majdanek death camps in occupied Poland.
11-15.location: Genocide of European Jews by Nazi Germany

11-15.title: Clutter family murders
11-15.subtitle: Two men murdered a family in Holcomb, Kansas; the events became the subject of Truman Capote's non-fiction novel In Cold...
11-15.text: Two men murdered a family in Holcomb, Kansas; the events became the subject of Truman Capote's non-fiction novel In Cold Blood, a pioneering work of the true crime genre.

11-15.title: Intel
11-15.subtitle: Intel releases the world's first commercial single-chip microprocessor, the 4004.
11-15.text: Intel releases the world's first commercial single-chip microprocessor, the 4004.

11-15.title: René Lévesque
11-15.subtitle: René Lévesque and the Parti Québécois take power to become the first Quebec government of the 20th century clearly in favor of independence.
11-15.text: René Lévesque and the Parti Québécois take power to become the first Quebec government of the 20th century clearly in favor of independence.

11-15.title: Fair trade certification
11-15.subtitle: The first Fairtrade label, Max Havelaar, is launched in the Netherlands.
11-15.text: The first Fairtrade label, Max Havelaar, is launched in the Netherlands.

11-15.title: Space Shuttle Atlantis
11-15.subtitle: Space Shuttle Atlantis is launched on STS-38, a classified mission for the Department of Defense.
11-15.text: Space Shuttle Atlantis is a retired Space Shuttle orbiter vehicle which belongs to NASA, the spaceflight and space exploration agency of the United States. Atlantis was manufactured by the Rockwell International company in Southern California and was delivered to the Kennedy Space Center in Eastern Florida in April 1985. Atlantis is the fourth operational and the second-to-last Space Shuttle built. Its maiden flight was STS-51-J made from October 3 to 7, 1985.

11-15.title: 1994 Mindoro earthquake
11-15.subtitle: A magnitude 7.
11-15.text: A magnitude 7.1 earthquake hits the central Philippine island of Mindoro, killing 78 people, injuring 430 and triggering a tsunami up to 8.5 m (28 ft) high.

11-15.title: Edoardo Agnelli Found Dead
11-15.subtitle: A dynasty's heir lost beneath a Turin bridge
11-15.text: Edoardo Agnelli, eldest son of Fiat patriarch Gianni Agnelli, was found dead beneath a bridge on the outskirts of Turin. The scion of Italy's most powerful industrial dynasty had converted to Islam and changed his name, living a life at odds with his family's expectations. His mysterious death at forty-six sent shockwaves through Italian society.

11-15.title: Xi Jinping
11-15.subtitle: Xi Jinping replaced Hu Jintao as General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party, succeeding as the paramount leader of China.
11-15.text: Xi Jinping replaced Hu Jintao as General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party, succeeding as the paramount leader of China.

11-16.title: Emperor Tiberius Born
11-16.subtitle: Augustus' reluctant heir enters the world
11-16.text: In Rome, Tiberius Julius Caesar was born into a family that would become the most powerful in the ancient world. Though one of Rome's most successful generals, he was Augustus' reluctant choice as successor, elevated only after the deaths of preferred heirs. His reign as emperor combined military brilliance with increasing paranoia, and he spent his final years in self-imposed exile on Capri.

11-16.title: Eliska Junkova Born
11-16.subtitle: The first woman to win a Grand Prix
11-16.text: In Olomouc, Moravia, Eliska Junkova was born with a need for speed that would shatter gender barriers in motorsport. She became the first woman to win a Grand Prix event, competing fearlessly against the best male drivers of her era in the dangerous world of 1920s motor racing. Her courage and skill made her a legend of automotive history.

11-16.title: The Sound of Music
11-16.subtitle: The Sound of Music, a musical by Rodgers and Hammerstein based on The Story of the Trapp Family Singers, opened on Broad...
11-16.text: The Sound of Music, a musical by Rodgers and Hammerstein based on The Story of the Trapp Family Singers, opened on Broadway at the Lunt-Fontanne Theatre.

11-16.title: Venera program
11-16.subtitle: Venera program: The Soviet Union launches the Venera 3 space probe toward Venus, which will be the first spacecraft to r...
11-16.text: Venera program: The Soviet Union launches the Venera 3 space probe toward Venus, which will be the first spacecraft to reach the surface of another planet.

11-16.title: Skylab program
11-16.subtitle: Skylab program: NASA launches Skylab 4 with a crew of three astronauts from Cape Canaveral, Florida for an 84-day mission.
11-16.text: Skylab program: NASA launches Skylab 4 with a crew of three astronauts from Cape Canaveral, Florida for an 84-day mission.

11-16.title: Luke and Laura
11-16.subtitle: About 30 million people watched the fictional couple Luke Spencer and Laura Webber wed on the television show General Ho...
11-16.text: Luke and Laura Spencer are fictional characters, and the signature supercouple from the American daytime drama General Hospital. Luke is portrayed by Anthony Geary, and Laura is portrayed by Genie Francis. Though other supercouples came before them, Luke and Laura are the best known outside of the soap opera medium and are credited with defining the term supercouple and leading other soap operas to try to duplicate their success.

11-16.title: Supreme Soviet
11-16.subtitle: The Supreme Soviet of the Estonian Soviet Socialist Republic declares that Estonia is "sovereign" but stops short of declaring independence.
11-16.text: The Supreme Soviet of the Estonian Soviet Socialist Republic declares that Estonia is "sovereign" but stops short of declaring independence.

11-16.title: Suffolk
11-16.subtitle: In Suffolk, England, a local man found the largest hoard of Roman silver and gold in Britain (sample pictured), includin...
11-16.text: In Suffolk, England, a local man found the largest hoard of Roman silver and gold in Britain (sample pictured), including the largest collection of 4th- and 5th-century gold and silver coins ever discovered within the former Roman Empire.

11-16.title: Josh Green Born
11-16.subtitle: Australia's gift to the NBA hardwood
11-16.text: In Sydney, Australia, Josh Green was born with the athletic versatility that would carry him to the NBA. After playing college basketball at Arizona, he was drafted by the Dallas Mavericks and later joined the Charlotte Hornets, becoming one of Australia's most prominent basketball exports and representing his country on the international stage.

11-16.title: 2002–2004 SARS outbreak
11-16.subtitle: The first case of SARS, a zoonotic respiratory coronavirus disease, was recorded in Guangdong, China.
11-16.text: The first case of SARS, a zoonotic respiratory coronavirus disease, was recorded in Guangdong, China.

11-16.title: Space Shuttle program
11-16.subtitle: Space Shuttle program: Space Shuttle Atlantis is launched on mission STS-129 to the International Space Station.
11-16.text: The Space Shuttle program was the fourth human spaceflight program carried out by the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), which accomplished routine transportation for Earth-to-orbit crew and cargo from 1981 to 2011. Its official program name was Space Transportation System (STS), taken from a 1969 plan for a system of reusable spacecraft where it was the only item funded for development, as a proposed nuclear shuttle in the plan was cancelled in 1972.

11-16.title: Artemis Program
11-16.subtitle: Artemis Program: NASA launches Artemis 1 on the first flight of the Space Launch System, the start of the program's future missions to the moon.
11-16.text: Artemis Program: NASA launches Artemis 1 on the first flight of the Space Launch System, the start of the program's future missions to the moon.

11-17.title: Emperor Vespasian Born
11-17.subtitle: A soldier's son who would restore an empire
11-17.text: In a small village in the Sabine country northeast of Rome, Titus Flavius Vespasianus was born into a family of modest equestrian rank. He would rise through military service to become the founder of the Flavian dynasty, restoring stability after the chaos of the Year of the Four Emperors. His reign brought the Colosseum, fiscal sanity, and a new era of Roman confidence.

11-17.title: Walter Hallstein Born
11-17.subtitle: The man who built Europe's common market
11-17.text: In Mainz, Germany, Walter Hallstein was born with the diplomatic vision that would help forge a united Europe from the ashes of war. As the first president of the European Economic Community's Commission, he laid the institutional foundations of what would become the European Union. His legacy endures in the Brussels institutions that shape European life today.

11-17.title: John Bardeen
11-17.subtitle: American scientists John Bardeen and Walter Houser Brattain observe the basic principles of the transistor, a key elemen...
11-17.text: American scientists John Bardeen and Walter Houser Brattain observe the basic principles of the transistor, a key element for the electronics revolution of the 20th century.

11-17.title: NBC
11-17.subtitle: NBC controversially cut away from an American football game between the Oakland Raiders and New York Jets to broadcast H...
11-17.text: The National Broadcasting Company (NBC) is an American commercial broadcast television and radio network, serving as the flagship property of NBC Entertainment, a division of NBCUniversal, which is a subsidiary of Comcast. It is one of NBCUniversal's two flagship subsidiaries, alongside Universal Studios. It is the first and oldest major broadcast network in the United States.

11-17.title: Luna programme
11-17.subtitle: Luna programme: The Soviet Union lands Lunokhod 1 on Mare Imbrium (Sea of Rains) on the Moon.
11-17.text: The Luna programme, sometimes called Lunik by western media, was a series of robotic spacecraft missions sent to the Moon by the Soviet Union between 1959 and 1976. The programme accomplished many firsts in space exploration, including first flyby of the Moon, first impact of the Moon and first photos of the far side of the Moon. Each mission was designed as either an orbiter or lander.
11-17.location: Robotic spacecraft missions to the Moon by the Soviet Union (1958–1976)

11-17.title: Zapatista Army of National Liberation
11-17.subtitle: The Zapatista Army of National Liberation is founded in Mexico.
11-17.text: The Zapatista Army of National Liberation is founded in Mexico.

11-17.title: Cold War
11-17.subtitle: Cold War: Velvet Revolution begins: In Czechoslovakia, a student demonstration in Prague is quelled by riot police.
11-17.text: Cold War: Velvet Revolution begins: In Czechoslovakia, a student demonstration in Prague is quelled by riot police. This sparks an uprising aimed at overthrowing the communist government (it succeeds on December 29).

11-17.title: Luxor
11-17.subtitle: In Luxor, Egypt, 62 people are killed by six Islamic militants outside the Temple of Hatshepsut, known as Luxor massacre.
11-17.text: In Luxor, Egypt, 62 people are killed by six Islamic militants outside the Temple of Hatshepsut, known as Luxor massacre.

11-17.title: Slovenia Landslide Disaster
11-17.subtitle: A mountain gives way, burying a village
11-17.text: A catastrophic landslide struck the village of Log pod Mangartom in the Julian Alps of Slovenia, killing seven people and causing devastating damage. Triggered by heavy rainfall, the massive flow of rock and mud was one of the worst natural catastrophes in Slovenia in a century, reshaping the landscape and forcing a complete reassessment of alpine building regulations.

11-17.title: University of East Anglia
11-17.subtitle: Administrators at the University of East Anglia's Climatic Research Unit discovered that their servers had been hacked, ...
11-17.text: Administrators at the University of East Anglia's Climatic Research Unit discovered that their servers had been hacked, and thousands of emails and files on climate change had been stolen.

11-17.title: Tatarstan Airlines Flight 363
11-17.subtitle: Tatarstan Airlines Flight 363 crashed during an aborted landing at Kazan International Airport, Russia, killing all 50 p...
11-17.text: Tatarstan Airlines Flight 363 crashed during an aborted landing at Kazan International Airport, Russia, killing all 50 people on board and leading to the revocation of the airline's operating certificate.

11-18.title: Old St. Peter's Consecrated
11-18.subtitle: Christianity's greatest church rises over a circus
11-18.text: Pope Sylvester I consecrated the old St. Peter's Basilica in Rome, built on the site of the Circus of Nero where tradition held that the apostle Peter had been martyred. Commissioned by Emperor Constantine, the basilica became Christendom's most sacred church, a pilgrimage destination for over a millennium until its replacement by the current Renaissance masterpiece.

11-18.title: Hay-Pauncefote Treaty Signed
11-18.subtitle: Britain steps aside for America's canal dream
11-18.text: Britain and the United States signed the Hay-Pauncefote Treaty, nullifying earlier agreements that had prevented American control of a Central American canal. The treaty cleared the diplomatic path for the construction of the Panama Canal, one of the greatest engineering feats in history. Britain's graceful withdrawal acknowledged America's growing dominance in the Western Hemisphere.

11-18.title: Bell Telephone Company
11-18.subtitle: The Bell Telephone Company introduces the first push-button telephone.
11-18.text: The Bell Telephone Company introduces the first push-button telephone.

11-18.title: Jim Jones
11-18.subtitle: Jim Jones led more than 900 members of the Peoples Temple to mass murder/suicide in Jonestown, Guyana, hours after some of its members assassinated U.
11-18.text: James Warren Jones was an American cult leader, preacher, and mass murderer who founded and led the Peoples Temple between 1955 and 1978. Jones and the members of his inner circle planned and orchestrated a mass murder–suicide that resulted in the deaths of over 900 people including 304 children, which he described as "revolutionary suicide", a term coined by Huey P. Newton, in his remote jungle commune at Jonestown, Guyana on November 18, 1978, including the assassination of U.S.
11-18.location: American cult leader and mass murderer (1931–1978)

11-18.title: Croatian Republic of Herzeg-Bosnia
11-18.subtitle: The autonomous Croatian Community of Herzeg-Bosnia, which would in 1993 become a republic, was established in Bosnia and Herzegovina.
11-18.text: The autonomous Croatian Community of Herzeg-Bosnia, which would in 1993 become a republic, was established in Bosnia and Herzegovina.

11-18.title: Texas A&M University
11-18.subtitle: Texas A&M University's Aggie Bonfire collapsed (aftermath pictured), killing 12 people and injuring 27 others, and causi...
11-18.text: Texas A&M University's Aggie Bonfire collapsed (aftermath pictured), killing 12 people and injuring 27 others, and causing the university to officially declare a hiatus on the 90-year-old annual event.

11-18.title: Caleb Williams Born
11-18.subtitle: A Heisman Trophy winner takes his first snap
11-18.text: In Washington, D.C., Caleb Williams was born with the arm talent and competitive fire that would make him college football's most electrifying quarterback. After transferring from Oklahoma to USC, he won the 2022 Heisman Trophy by setting single-season school records in passing yards and touchdowns, then became the Chicago Bears' franchise quarterback in the NFL.

11-18.title: Iraq disarmament crisis
11-18.subtitle: Iraq disarmament crisis: United Nations weapons inspectors led by Hans Blix arrive in Iraq.
11-18.text: Iraq disarmament crisis: United Nations weapons inspectors led by Hans Blix arrive in Iraq.

11-18.title: Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court
11-18.subtitle: The Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court rules 4–3 in Goodridge v.
11-18.text: The Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court rules 4–3 in Goodridge v. Department of Public Health that the state's ban on same-sex marriage is unconstitutional and gives the state legislature 180 days to change the law making Massachusetts the first state in the United States to grant marriage rights to same-sex couples.

11-18.title: Cyclone Numa
11-18.subtitle: Cyclone Numa, a rare "medicane", made landfall in Greece to become the worst weather event that the country had experienced since 1977.
11-18.text: Cyclone Numa, a rare "medicane", made landfall in Greece to become the worst weather event that the country had experienced since 1977.

11-19.title: A Shadow Emperor Crowned
11-19.subtitle: Ricimer pulls the strings from behind the throne
11-19.text: Libius Severus was declared emperor of the Western Roman Empire, but real power remained firmly in the hands of the magister militum Ricimer. A native of Lucania, Severus became the latest in a line of shadow emperors who ruled in name only while Germanic generals controlled the military and dictated policy. The Western Empire was an empire in name alone.

11-19.title: Anna Seghers Born
11-19.subtitle: War's moral weight captured in exile's ink
11-19.text: In Mainz, Germany, Netty Reiling was born into a Jewish family. Writing under the pen name Anna Seghers, she would become one of Germany's most important novelists, exploring the moral experience of World War II with unflinching honesty. Fleeing Nazi persecution, she found refuge in Mexico before returning to East Germany, where her works became cornerstones of anti-fascist literature.

11-19.title: TVB
11-19.subtitle: The establishment of TVB, the first wireless commercial television station in Hong Kong.
11-19.text: Television Broadcasts Limited is a Hong Kong television network that began broadcasting on 19 November 1967. It operates five free-to-air terrestrial television channels, with TVB Jade as its main Cantonese service, and TVB Pearl as its main English service. Since 2003, TVB has been headquartered at TVB City in the Tseung Kwan O Industrial Estate.

11-19.title: Apollo program
11-19.subtitle: Apollo program: Apollo 12 astronauts Pete Conrad and Alan Bean land at Oceanus Procellarum (the "Ocean of Storms") and b...
11-19.text: The Apollo program, also known as Project Apollo, was the United States human spaceflight program led by NASA, which landed the first humans on the Moon in 1969. Apollo was conceived during Project Mercury and executed after Project Gemini. It was conceived in 1960 as a three-person spacecraft during the presidency of Dwight D. Eisenhower. Apollo was later dedicated to President John F.

11-19.title: Cold War
11-19.subtitle: Cold War: In Geneva, U.
11-19.text: The Cold War was a period of international geopolitical rivalry between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) and their respective allies, the capitalist Western Bloc and communist Eastern Bloc, which began in the aftermath of the Second World War and ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
11-19.location: 1947–1991 geopolitical rivalry between US and USSR

11-19.title: Luis Miguel
11-19.subtitle: Mexican singer Luis Miguel released the album Romance, which led to a revival of interest in bolero music.
11-19.text: Mexican singer Luis Miguel released the album Romance, which led to a revival of interest in bolero music.

11-19.title: Space Shuttle program
11-19.subtitle: Space Shuttle program: Columbia is launched on STS-80, which would become the longest mission in the program at 17 days.
11-19.text: The Space Shuttle program was the fourth human spaceflight program carried out by the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), which accomplished routine transportation for Earth-to-orbit crew and cargo from 1981 to 2011. Its official program name was Space Transportation System (STS), taken from a 1969 plan for a system of reusable spacecraft where it was the only item funded for development, as a proposed nuclear shuttle in the plan was cancelled in 1972.

11-19.title: Shenzhou 1
11-19.subtitle: Shenzhou 1: The People's Republic of China launches its first Shenzhou spacecraft.
11-19.text: Shenzhou 1 was the first uncrewed test flight of the Shenzhou spacecraft and the Long March 2F rocket, launched on 19 November 1999. The spacecraft lacked a life-support system but included an emergency escape system. After 14 orbits, the command to begin reentry was sent by the Yuan Wang 3 off the coast of Namibia at 18:49 UTC.

11-19.title: The TSA Is Born
11-19.subtitle: Airport security transforms overnight
11-19.text: In the aftermath of the September 11th attacks, the 107th United States Congress enacted the Aviation and Transportation Security Act, creating the Transportation Security Administration. The legislation fundamentally transformed airport security across America, federalizing screening operations and establishing the security protocols that millions of travelers navigate to this day.

11-19.title: MV Prestige
11-19.subtitle: The Greek oil tanker Prestige split in two and sank off the coast of Galicia after spilling 420 thousand barrels (17.
11-19.text: The Greek oil tanker Prestige split in two and sank off the coast of Galicia after spilling 420 thousand barrels (17.8 million US gallons) of oil, in the worst environmental disaster in Spanish and Portuguese history.

11-19.title: Pike River Mine disaster
11-19.subtitle: The first of four explosions occurred at the Pike River Mine in the West Coast in New Zealand's worst mining disaster in nearly a century.
11-19.text: The first of four explosions occurred at the Pike River Mine in the West Coast in New Zealand's worst mining disaster in nearly a century.

11-20.title: Emperor Maximinus II Born
11-20.subtitle: The last pharaoh and persecutor of Christians
11-20.text: In the rugged countryside of the Danube provinces, the future Emperor Maximinus Daza was born. Rising through the military ranks under his uncle Galerius, he would claim the title of Augustus and become the last Roman emperor to persecute Christians systematically and the last person to hold the ancient title of Pharaoh of Egypt, a figure straddling two dying worlds.

11-20.title: Sarah Bernhardt Returns to NYC
11-20.subtitle: The divine Sarah enchants America once more
11-20.text: At the Savoy Hotel in New York, the legendary French actress Sarah Bernhardt received the press at the outset of her first American visit since 1896. With a troupe of over fifty performers, she announced plans to tour the country and to play the title role in Hamlet. Hugo had praised her golden voice, and Rostand called her the queen of the pose. America was ready to be enchanted again.

11-20.title: Native Americans in the United States
11-20.subtitle: A group of Native American activists began a 19-month occupation (graffiti pictured) of Alcatraz Island in San Francisco Bay.
11-20.text: A group of Native American activists began a 19-month occupation (graffiti pictured) of Alcatraz Island in San Francisco Bay.

11-20.title: Egypt
11-20.subtitle: Egyptian President Anwar Sadat becomes the first Arab leader to officially visit Israel, when he meets Israeli prime min...
11-20.text: Egyptian President Anwar Sadat becomes the first Arab leader to officially visit Israel, when he meets Israeli prime minister Menachem Begin and speaks before the Knesset in Jerusalem, seeking a permanent peace settlement.

11-20.title: Grand Mosque seizure
11-20.subtitle: Armed insurgents attacked and took over the Masjid al-Haram in Mecca, declaring that one of their leaders was the Mahdi,...
11-20.text: The Grand Mosque seizure took place between 20 November and 4 December 1979 at the Grand Mosque of Mecca in Mecca, Saudi Arabia, the holiest site in Islam. The attack was carried out by up to 600 militants led by Juhayman al-Otaybi, a Saudi Islamist opposed to the monarchy, belonging to the Otaibah tribe.
11-20.location: 1979 radical Islamic insurgency in Mecca, Saudi Arabia

11-20.title: Microsoft
11-20.subtitle: Microsoft Windows 1.
11-20.text: Microsoft Windows 1.0, the first graphical personal computer operating environment developed by Microsoft, is released.

11-20.title: Andrei Chikatilo
11-20.subtitle: Andrei Chikatilo, one of the Soviet Union's most prolific serial killers, was arrested in Novocherkassk.
11-20.text: Andrei Chikatilo, one of the Soviet Union's most prolific serial killers, was arrested in Novocherkassk.

11-20.title: First Nagorno-Karabakh War
11-20.subtitle: First Nagorno-Karabakh War: An Azerbaijani military helicopter carrying a peacekeeping mission team was shot down in Nag...
11-20.text: The First Nagorno-Karabakh War was an ethnic and territorial conflict that took place from February 1988 to May 1994 in the enclave of Nagorno-Karabakh in southwestern Azerbaijan between the majority ethnic Armenians of Nagorno-Karabakh backed by Armenia, and the Republic of Azerbaijan with support from Turkey. As the war progressed, Armenia and Azerbaijan, both former Soviet republics, entangled themselves in protracted, undeclared mountain warfare in the mountainous heights of Karabakh as Azerbaijan attempted to curb the Karabakh Movement.

11-20.title: Savings and loan crisis
11-20.subtitle: Savings and loan crisis: The United States Senate Ethics Committee issues a stern censure of California senator Alan Cra...
11-20.text: Savings and loan crisis: The United States Senate Ethics Committee issues a stern censure of California senator Alan Cranston for his "dealings" with savings-and-loan executive Charles Keating.

11-20.title: Zarya
11-20.subtitle: The first space station module component, Zarya, for the International Space Station is launched from the Baikonur Cosmodrome in Kazakhstan.
11-20.text: The first space station module component, Zarya, for the International Space Station is launched from the Baikonur Cosmodrome in Kazakhstan.

11-20.title: Connie Talbot Born
11-20.subtitle: A child's voice that conquered the world
11-20.text: In Sutton Coldfield, England, Connie Talbot was born with a voice that would capture hearts worldwide. At just six years old, she became the runner-up on the first series of Britain's Got Talent, stunning audiences with her rendition of Over the Rainbow. Her debut album reached number one in three countries, launching an extraordinary career before she was even in school.

11-20.title: 2003 Istanbul bombings
11-20.subtitle: Suicide bombers blew up the British consulate and the headquarters of HSBC Bank in Istanbul, killing 31 people, includin...
11-20.text: Suicide bombers blew up the British consulate and the headquarters of HSBC Bank in Istanbul, killing 31 people, including consul general Roger Short and actor Kerem Yılmazer.

11-20.title: 2022 FIFA World Cup
11-20.subtitle: The 2022 FIFA World Cup begins in Qatar.
11-20.text: The 2022 FIFA World Cup begins in Qatar. This is the first time the tournament was held in the Middle East.

11-21.title: The Temple Rededicated
11-21.subtitle: A miracle of light in the darkness of occupation
11-21.text: After driving the Seleucid Empire's forces from Jerusalem, Judas Maccabeus rededicated the desecrated Temple, restoring its sacred rites. According to tradition, a single day's supply of oil miraculously burned for eight days. This moment of triumph and faith is commemorated each year in the festival of Hanukkah, a celebration of light, resistance, and divine providence.

11-21.title: Monet Exhibited in Paris
11-21.subtitle: Impressionism's master returns to his gallery
11-21.text: At the Gallery Durand-Ruel in Paris, a new exhibition of Claude Monet's paintings opened its doors to an eager public. The founder of Impressionism and the most consistent practitioner of painting nature as perceived rather than as known, Monet unveiled works that continued to push the boundaries of light and color. The show reaffirmed his status as the movement's towering figure.

11-21.title: Irish War of Independence
11-21.subtitle: Irish War of Independence: On "Bloody Sunday" in Dublin, the Irish Republican Army (IRA) assassinated a group of British...
11-21.text: Irish War of Independence: On "Bloody Sunday" in Dublin, the Irish Republican Army (IRA) assassinated a group of British Intelligence agents, and British forces killed 14 civilians at a Gaelic football match at Croke Park.

11-21.title: Disc jockey
11-21.subtitle: American disc jockey Alan Freed (pictured), who popularized the term rock and roll, was fired from WABC-AM for his role in the payola scandal.
11-21.text: American disc jockey Alan Freed (pictured), who popularized the term rock and roll, was fired from WABC-AM for his role in the payola scandal.

11-21.title: Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge
11-21.subtitle: The Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge, connecting Staten Island and Brooklyn in New York City, opened to traffic as the longest ...
11-21.text: The Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge, connecting Staten Island and Brooklyn in New York City, opened to traffic as the longest suspension bridge in the world at the time.

11-21.title: ARPANET
11-21.subtitle: The first permanent ARPANET link is established between UCLA and SRI.
11-21.text: The first permanent ARPANET link is established between UCLA and SRI.

11-21.title: Vietnam War
11-21.subtitle: Vietnam War: American forces raided the North Vietnamese Sơn Tây prison camp in an attempt to rescue 61 American POWs wh...
11-21.text: The Vietnam War was an armed conflict in Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia fought between North Vietnam and South Vietnam and their allies. North Vietnam was supported by the Soviet Union and China, while South Vietnam was supported by the United States and other anti-communist nations. The conflict was the second of the Indochina wars and a proxy war of the Cold War between the Soviet Union and US.
11-21.location: War in Southeast Asia from 1955 to 1975

11-21.title: Park Chung Hee
11-21.subtitle: Voters in South Korea overwhelmingly approve a new constitution, giving legitimacy to Park Chung Hee and the Fourth Republic.
11-21.text: Voters in South Korea overwhelmingly approve a new constitution, giving legitimacy to Park Chung Hee and the Fourth Republic.

11-21.title: Birmingham pub bombings
11-21.subtitle: Bombs exploded in two pubs in central Birmingham, England, killing 21 people and leading to the imprisonment of six people who were later exonerated.
11-21.text: Bombs exploded in two pubs in central Birmingham, England, killing 21 people and leading to the imprisonment of six people who were later exonerated.

11-21.title: God Defend New Zealand
11-21.subtitle: "God Defend New Zealand" became New Zealand's second national anthem, on equal standing with "God Save the King", which ...
11-21.text: "God Defend New Zealand" became New Zealand's second national anthem, on equal standing with "God Save the King", which had been the traditional anthem since 1840.

11-21.title: Isabel May Born
11-21.subtitle: The frontier's storyteller for a new generation
11-21.text: In Santa Monica, California, Isabel May was born, destined to bring compelling stories to life on screen. From her co-lead role on Netflix's Alexa and Katie to her breakout performance as Elsa Dutton in Paramount's 1883, she demonstrated a gift for portraying strong women navigating unforgiving worlds. Her role in the Scream franchise showed she could conquer any genre.

11-21.title: NATO
11-21.subtitle: NATO invites Bulgaria, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Romania, Slovakia and Slovenia to become members.
11-21.text: The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) is an intergovernmental military alliance between 32 member states—30 in Europe and 2 in North America. Founded in the aftermath of World War II, NATO was established with the signing of the North Atlantic Treaty in 1949. The organization serves as a system of collective security, whereby its independent member states agree to mutual defence in response to an attack by any outside party.

11-21.title: Tesla, Inc.
11-21.subtitle: Tesla launches the SUV Cybertruck.
11-21.text: Tesla launches the SUV Cybertruck. A gaffe occurs during the launch event when its "unbreakable" windows shatter during demonstration.

11-22.title: Two Popes Elected at Once
11-22.subtitle: Rome splits its loyalty between rival basilicas
11-22.text: Following the death of Pope Anastasius II, the Roman clergy split into two factions. Symmachus was elected pope in the Lateran Palace while Laurentius was simultaneously elected in Santa Maria Maggiore. The resulting Laurentian schism tore the Roman church apart, a crisis that would require years and imperial intervention to resolve, revealing deep theological and political fractures.

11-22.title: Joaquin Rodrigo Born
11-22.subtitle: Aranjuez's gardens sang through his fingers
11-22.text: In Sagunto, Spain, Joaquin Rodrigo was born, blinded by diphtheria at age three but gifted with extraordinary musical vision. His Concierto de Aranjuez for guitar and orchestra became the most performed Spanish concerto in history, its haunting adagio evoking the gardens of the royal palace. The composition elevated the classical guitar to the concert stage and made Rodrigo immortal.

11-22.title: John F. Kennedy
11-22.subtitle: U.
11-22.text: John Fitzgerald Kennedy, also known as JFK, was the 35th president of the United States, serving from 1961 until his assassination in 1963. He was the youngest person elected president at 43 years, as well as the first Catholic president. Kennedy served at the height of the Cold War, and the majority of his foreign policy concerned relations with the Soviet Union and Cuba.
11-22.location: President of the United States from 1961 to 1963

11-22.title: United Nations Security Council Resolution 242
11-22.subtitle: UN Security Council Resolution 242 is adopted, establishing a set of the principles aimed at guiding negotiations for an...
11-22.text: UN Security Council Resolution 242 is adopted, establishing a set of the principles aimed at guiding negotiations for an Arab–Israeli peace settlement.

11-22.title: Cairngorm Plateau disaster
11-22.subtitle: In Britain's worst mountaineering disaster, five teenage students and one of their leaders were found dead from exposure...
11-22.text: In Britain's worst mountaineering disaster, five teenage students and one of their leaders were found dead from exposure on the Cairngorm Plateau in the Scottish Highlands.

11-22.title: Francisco Franco
11-22.subtitle: Two days after the death of Francisco Franco, Juan Carlos I (pictured) was declared King of Spain according to the law o...
11-22.text: Two days after the death of Francisco Franco, Juan Carlos I (pictured) was declared King of Spain according to the law of succession promulgated by Franco.

11-22.title: Mike Tyson
11-22.subtitle: Mike Tyson defeated Trevor Berbick to win the World Boxing Council title, becoming the youngest heavyweight champion in history.
11-22.text: Michael Gerard Tyson is an American former professional boxer who competed between 1985 and 2024. Nicknamed "Iron Mike" and "Kid Dynamite" in his early career, and later known as "the Baddest Man on the Planet", Tyson is widely regarded as one of the greatest heavyweight boxers of all time, and one of the most intimidating men in boxing history. He reigned as the undisputed world heavyweight champion from 1987 to 1990.

11-22.title: Northrop B-2 Spirit
11-22.subtitle: The B-2 Spirit stealth bomber of the United States Air Force was first displayed in public at Air Force Plant 42 in Palmdale, California.
11-22.text: The B-2 Spirit stealth bomber of the United States Air Force was first displayed in public at Air Force Plant 42 in Palmdale, California.

11-22.title: NASA
11-22.subtitle: NASA launches Space Shuttle Discovery on STS-33, a classified mission for the United States Department of Defense.
11-22.text: The National Aeronautics and Space Administration is an independent agency of the U.S. federal government responsible for the United States' civil space program and for research in aeronautics and space exploration. Headquartered in Washington, D.C., NASA operates ten field centers across the US and is organized into mission directorates for Science, Space Operations, Exploration Systems Development, Space Technology, Aeronautics Research, and Mission Support.

11-22.title: Trans World Airlines
11-22.subtitle: A Trans World Airlines McDonnell Douglas MD-80 and Cessna 441 Conquest II aircraft collide on the runway at St.
11-22.text: A Trans World Airlines McDonnell Douglas MD-80 and Cessna 441 Conquest II aircraft collide on the runway at St. Louis Lambert International Airport in Bridgeton, Missouri, killing two people and injuring eight.

11-22.title: Auli'i Cravalho Born
11-22.subtitle: The voice that sailed across the ocean
11-22.text: In Kohala, Hawaii, Chloe Auli'i Cravalho was born, carrying the spirit of the Pacific Islands in her voice. At just fourteen, she was chosen from hundreds of auditioners to voice the titular character in Disney's Moana, becoming the youngest actress to voice a Disney princess. Her performance resonated with audiences worldwide and celebrated Polynesian heritage on the global stage.

11-22.title: Orange Revolution
11-22.subtitle: The Orange Revolution begins in Ukraine, resulting from the presidential elections.
11-22.text: The Orange Revolution begins in Ukraine, resulting from the presidential elections.

11-23.title: Thespis Invents Acting
11-23.subtitle: One man steps out of the chorus and into history
11-23.text: In ancient Athens, Thespis of Icaria became the first recorded person to portray a character on stage, stepping out of the chorus to speak as an individual. This revolutionary act, which earned him the title 'Inventor of Tragedy,' gave birth to the art of acting itself. Every actor who has ever taken a stage owes their craft to this single, daring moment of theatrical innovation.

11-23.title: Bennie Osler Born
11-23.subtitle: South Africa's rugby general takes the field
11-23.text: In Aliwal North, South Africa, Benjamin Osler was born with the tactical mind and boot accuracy that would make him South African rugby's most influential fly-half. Playing for both Western Province and the Springboks, he pioneered a kicking-based game plan that dominated international rugby in the late 1920s and early 1930s, forever changing how the position was played.

11-23.title: An Unearthly Child
11-23.subtitle: The first episode of Doctor Who, the world's longest-running science fiction television show, was broadcast on BBC telev...
11-23.text: The first episode of Doctor Who, the world's longest-running science fiction television show, was broadcast on BBC television, starring William Hartnell as the first incarnation of the title role.

11-23.title: 1980 Irpinia earthquake
11-23.subtitle: An earthquake struck the Irpinia region of Italy, killing at least 2,483 people, injuring more than 7,700 and leaving 250,000 homeless.
11-23.text: An earthquake struck the Irpinia region of Italy, killing at least 2,483 people, injuring more than 7,700 and leaving 250,000 homeless.

11-23.title: Budapest Cybercrime Convention
11-23.subtitle: The first global treaty for the digital age
11-23.text: In Budapest, Hungary, representatives from nations around the world signed the Convention on Cybercrime, the first international treaty aimed at addressing internet and computer crime. The landmark agreement sought to harmonize national laws, improve investigative techniques, and increase cooperation among nations in the fight against an entirely new category of criminal activity.

11-23.title: Rose Revolution
11-23.subtitle: Rose Revolution: Eduard Shevardnadze resigned as President of Georgia following weeks of mass protests over disputed election results.
11-23.text: Rose Revolution: Eduard Shevardnadze resigned as President of Georgia following weeks of mass protests over disputed election results.

11-23.title: Esmael Mangudadatu
11-23.subtitle: A crowd of people on their way to register Esmael Mangudadatu's candidacy for governor of Maguindanao, Philippines, were...
11-23.text: A crowd of people on their way to register Esmael Mangudadatu's candidacy for governor of Maguindanao, Philippines, were kidnapped and killed by supporters of his rival, resulting in 58 deaths.

11-23.title: Arab Spring
11-23.subtitle: Arab Spring: After months of protests in Yemen, President Ali Abdullah Saleh agreed to transfer power to Vice President Abdrabbuh Mansur Hadi.
11-23.text: The Arab Spring was a series of pro-democracy anti-government protests, uprisings, and armed rebellions that spread across much of the Arab world in the early 2010s. It began in Tunisia in response to the death of Mohamed Bouazizi by self-immolation. From Tunisia, the protests initially spread to five other countries: Libya, Egypt, Yemen, Syria and Bahrain.
11-23.location: 2010s protests and revolutions in the Arab world

11-24.title: Theodosius Enters Constantinople
11-24.subtitle: The last ruler of a united Rome arrives
11-24.text: Emperor Theodosius I made his formal entry, or adventus, into Constantinople, the eastern capital of the Roman Empire. He would become the last emperor to rule both halves of the Roman world, establishing Nicene Christianity as the empire's official faith and ending the Gothic War. After his death, the empire would be permanently divided between East and West.

11-24.title: Ross Tilley Born
11-24.subtitle: The surgeon who rebuilt broken airmen
11-24.text: In Bowmanville, Ontario, Albert Ross Tilley was born, destined to become a pioneer of wartime plastic surgery. During World War II, he developed revolutionary techniques for treating severely burned Allied airmen, restoring both their faces and their hope. His compassionate innovation saved hundreds of lives and advanced the field of reconstructive surgery immeasurably.

11-24.title: World War II
11-24.subtitle: World War II: The First Slovak Republic becomes a signatory to the Tripartite Pact, officially joining the Axis powers.
11-24.text: World War II or the Second World War was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers. Nearly all of the world's countries participated.
